- **Low liquefaction temperature** → gas remains gaseous over a wide range.
- **Near-ideal behaviour** → follows $PV = nRT$ accurately at moderate pressures.

## 2.5 Ambient Temperature Errors & Compensation (The "Big Idea")

The capillary is exposed to ambient (room) temperature, *not* process temperature. This creates an **error** because:
- **Liquid-filled:** Capillary liquid expands due to ambient heat → *false* pressure rise.
- **Gas-filled:** Capillary gas pressure changes → spurious reading.
- **Vapour-pressure:** Negligible, because vapour pressure depends *only* on bulb temperature (saturated vapour always recondenses/evaporates).

The error in a gas-filled system is:

$$\Delta P_{\text{ambient}} \approx \dfrac{P_c \cdot \Delta T_{\text{ambient}}}{T_{\text{ambient}}}$$

### Compensation Methods

| Method | Principle | Used In |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| **Compensated capillary** | A second capillary with identical gas is mechanically linked in *opposition* to the active capillary | Gas & liquid-filled |
| **Case-compensated** | A bimetallic element offsets the Bourdon with ambient changes | All three types |
| **Vapour-pressure self-comp.** | Saturated vapour equilibrium | Vapour-pressure only |
| **Dual-capillary with mercury seal** | Mercury transmits pressure but isolates gas | Gas-filled (advanced) |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Applicable To | Key Variable | Units |
|---|---------|--------------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_1/T_1 = P_2/T_2$ | Gas-filled (constant V) | $P$, $T$ | Pa, K |
| 2 | $\Delta P = P_1 \cdot \Delta T / T_1$ | Gas-filled sensitivity | $\Delta P$ | Pa |
| 3 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_m \cdot V_b}{V_b + V_c + V_m}$ | Liquid-filled (approx.) | $\Delta P$ | Pa |
| 4 | $P_{sat}(T) = P_0 \exp\!\left[\tfrac{h_{fg}}{R_u}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{T_0}-\tfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]$ | Vapour-pressure | $P_{sat}$ | Pa |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dP_{sat}}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}\,P_{sat}}{R_u\,T^2}$ | Vapour-pressure sensitivity | $\mathrm{d}P/\mathrm{d}T$ | Pa/K |
| 6 | $\tau \approx \dfrac{m_b \,c_{p,b}}{h\,A_b}$ | Thermal time constant | $\tau$ | s |
| 7 | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ | General ideal gas | $P,V,T$ | Pa, m³, K |
| 8 | $\Delta L = \dfrac{r^2 \cdot \Delta P}{E_s \cdot t}$ | Bourdon tube deflection | $\Delta L$ | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always convert Celsius to Kelvin** ($T_K = T_C + 273.15$) when applying any gas-law formula. This is the **#1 mark-losing error** in KTU valuation.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Liquid-filled (mercury):** Precision laboratory standards, calibration baths.
- **Liquid-filled (silicone):** Industrial process lines, $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $300^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- **Vapour-pressure:** Cryogenic LNG lines, refrigeration, $-200^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+250^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- **Gas-filled:** High-temperature furnaces, boiler superheaters, $-50^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+600^{\circ}\text{C}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Pressure Rise in a Gas-Filled System

We start from the **Ideal Gas Law** for the fill gas occupying the *constant* total volume $V$ of bulb + capillary + Bourdon:

$$P V = n R_u T$$

Since $n$, $V$, and $R_u$ are all **constant**, differentiate both sides with respect to temperature:

$$\dfrac{d}{dT}(P V) = \dfrac{d}{dT}(n R_u T)$$

$$V \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dT} = n R_u = \dfrac{P V}{T}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{P}{T}\;}$$

For a finite (not infinitesimal) temperature change from $T_1$ to $T_2$:

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$$

Therefore the **pressure rise** is:

$$\Delta P = P_2 - P_1 = P_1\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} - 1\right) = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1} = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_1}$$

**Critical remark:** $T_1$ and $T_2$ are **absolute** (Kelvin). This explains why the instrument's sensitivity increases at low temperatures (small $T_1$) and decreases at high temperatures (large $T_1$).

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Pressure Rise in a Liquid-Filled System

Consider a sealed system completely filled with an incompressible liquid. The total internal volume is:

$$V_t = V_b + V_c + V_m$$

When the bulb temperature rises by $\Delta T$ while the rest of the system is held at $T_0$:

- The **liquid** wants to expand by $\Delta V_{\ell} = \beta \cdot V_t \cdot \Delta T$.
- The **bulb walls** also expand outward by $\Delta V_b = 3\alpha_s \cdot V_b \cdot \Delta T$ ($\alpha_s$ = linear expansion of bulb metal).
- The **capillary** expands by $\Delta V_c = \dfrac{\pi d^2 L}{4E_s t}\cdot \Delta P$ (elastic expansion under pressure).
- The **Bourdon** flexes to increase volume by $\Delta V_m$ (this is what produces the reading!).

The volume conservation equation:

$$\beta \cdot V_t \cdot \Delta T = 3\alpha_s V_b \Delta T + \Delta V_c(\Delta P) + \Delta V_m(\Delta P)$$

Solving for $\Delta P$ and noting $3\alpha_s V_b \Delta T \ll \beta V_t \Delta T$:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta P \approx \dfrac{\beta \cdot \Delta T}{\dfrac{V_c}{E_s V_b} + \dfrac{d(\Delta V_m)/dP}{V_b}}\;}$$

The dominant term is the bulb-vs-system volume ratio $\dfrac{V_b}{V_t}$, which is why a **large bulb and small capillary** maximise sensitivity.

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Vapour-Pressure Sensitivity from Clausius–Clapeyron

The **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** relates saturation pressure to temperature:

$$\dfrac{dP_{sat}}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot (v_g - v_\ell)}$$

Assuming the vapour behaves as an ideal gas ($v_g = R_u T / P_{sat} \cdot M$) and $v_g \gg v_\ell$:

$$\dfrac{dP_{sat}}{dT} \approx \dfrac{h_{fg} \cdot P_{sat}}{R_u \cdot T^2 / M}$$

After substituting the molar gas constant and rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{dP_{sat}}{dT} = \dfrac{M \cdot h_{fg} \cdot P_{sat}}{R_u \cdot T^2}\;}$$

For a small temperature excursion $\Delta T$:

$$\Delta P_{sat} \approx \dfrac{M \cdot h_{fg} \cdot P_{sat,\,T_0}}{R_u \cdot T_0^2}\cdot \Delta T$$

**This is the slope of the vapour-pressure curve at the operating point** — and it is **highly non-linear**, getting steeper near the critical point.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Q:** A nitrogen-filled constant-volume thermometer has a fill pressure of $3\,\text{MPa}$ at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$. The bulb is exposed to a process at $180^{\circ}\text{C}$. Find the pressure rise and the Bourdon deflection if the tube constant $k = 1.2 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{m/Pa}$.

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$T_1 = 20 + 273.15 = 293.15\,\text{K}, \qquad T_2 = 180 + 273.15 = 453.15\,\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Gay-Lussac's Law:**

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = 3 \times 10^{6} \cdot \dfrac{453.15}{293.15}$$

$$P_2 = 3 \times 10^{6} \cdot 1.5459 = 4.6378 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute pressure rise:**

$$\Delta P = P_2 - P_1 = 4.6378 \times 10^{6} - 3 \times 10^{6} = 1.6378 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Bourdon deflection:**

$$\Delta L = k \cdot \Delta P = 1.2 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.6378 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\Delta L = 1.965 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m} = 19.65\,\mu\text{m}$$

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Liquid-Filled | Vapour-Pressure | Gas-Filled |
|----------|--------------|-----------------|------------|
| Fill medium | Mercury / silicone oil / xylene | Methyl chloride / propane / toluene | Nitrogen / helium |
| Governing law | Linear thermal expansion | Clausius–Clapeyron | Gay-Lussac's Law |
| Range (typical) | $-40$ to $+600^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $-200$ to $+250^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $-50$ to $+600^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Scale linearity | **Linear** | **Non-linear** | **Linear** |
| Ambient compensation | Needed (compensating capillary) | **Not needed** (self-comp.) | Needed (case-comp.) |
| Bulb size | Small to medium | Large | Large |
| Time constant | 3–10 s | 5–15 s | 4–12 s |
| Pressure range | 0.1–1 MPa | 0.05–2 MPa | 1–5 MPa |
| Sensitivity at low T | Moderate | Very high | Highest |
| Intrinsic safety | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Cost | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| Common industrial use | Process lines | Refrigeration, cryogenics | Boilers, furnaces |

## 3.6 Selection Logic (Decision Tree)

$$\text{Need linear scale?} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Gas-filled or Liquid-filled}$$

$$\text{Need cryogenic range?} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Vapour-pressure}$$

$$\text{Need ambient compensation without hardware?} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Vapour-pressure}$$

$$\text{Need lowest cost + low accuracy?} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Vapour-pressure}$$

$$\text{Need high accuracy + linear + medium range?} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Gas-filled (helium)}$$

$$\text{Need precision below } 0^{\circ}\text{C?} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Liquid-filled (organic fluid)}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Filled-System Thermometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Temperature Tp] -->|Heat Transfer| B[Bulb thermal element]
    B -->|Pressure rise ΔP| C[Capillary tube 0.7 to 2.5 mm bore]
    C -->|Transmits signal| D[Bourdon tube or helix]
    D -->|Mechanical displacement| E[Pointer or recorder pen]
    E --> F[Indicated temperature reading]
    G[Ambient temperature Ta along capillary] -.->|Error source| C
    H[Compensating element] -.->|Opposing force| D
```

## 4.2 Classification Flow of Filled-System Thermometers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Filled-System Thermometers] --> Q[Liquid-Filled]
    P --> R[Vapour-Pressure]
    P --> S[Gas-Filled]
    Q --> Q1[Mercury filled -40 to 600 C]
    Q --> Q2[Organic liquid filled -200 to 300 C]
    R --> R1[Methyl chloride -20 to 80 C]
    R --> R2[Butane 0 to 120 C]
    R --> R3[Toluene 100 to 250 C]
    S --> S1[Nitrogen -50 to 600 C]
    S --> S2[Helium -270 to 500 C]
```

## 4.3 Operational Sequence — Gas-Filled Constant Volume

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Bulb as Bulb at T_process
    participant Cap as Capillary
    participant Bourdon as Bourdon Element
    participant Ind as Indicator
    Bulb->>Bulb: Temperature rises by ΔT
    Bulb->>Cap: Gas pressure rises P_new = P_old x T_new / T_old
    Cap->>Bourdon: Pressure transmitted
    Bourdon->>Bourdon: Tube tends to straighten
    Bourdon->>Ind: Tip rotates angular displacement theta
    Ind->>Ind: Pointer moves across calibrated scale
```

## 4.4 Vapour-Pressure Self-Compensation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BulbRegion[At Bulb Temperature Tp]
        L1[Liquid volatile fluid]
        V1[Saturated vapour at Psat Tp]
        L1 --- V1
    end
    subgraph CapillaryRegion[Along Capillary at Ambient Ta]
        V2[Same saturated vapour at Psat Tp]
        V2 --> V3[Condenses if Ta colder, Evaporates if Ta warmer]
    end
    subgraph IndicatorRegion[At Bourdon]
        V3 --> B[Pressure equal to Psat Tp only]
        B --> Ind[Pointer indicates Tp accurately]
    end
    V1 -.->|Same Psat value| V2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **conceptual block diagram** representing the thermodynamic mechanism. In an actual vapour-pressure thermometer, the liquid and vapour coexist only in the bulb; the capillary carries saturated vapour whose *pressure* is fixed by bulb temperature.

## 4.5 Compensation Architecture — Liquid/Gas-Filled Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ActivePath[Active Capillary]
        ABulb[Active Bulb at Tp] --> Acap[Active Capillary exposed to Ta]
        Acap --> AB[Active Bourdon]
    end
    subgraph CompPath[Compensating Capillary]
        CBulb[Inactive Dummy Bulb at Ta] --> Ccap[Compensating Capillary at Ta]
        Ccap --> CB[Linked in mechanical opposition]
    end
    AB -->|Net force = active minus compensation| Net[Net Pointer Movement]
    CB --> Net
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Why is the vapour-pressure thermometer considered *self-compensating* against ambient temperature variations along the capillary?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A vapour-pressure thermometer bulb is partially filled with a volatile liquid, and the rest of the system contains its saturated vapour. The vapour pressure in the entire system equals the **saturation pressure at the bulb temperature only** — it is *independent* of the ambient temperature along the capillary. If the capillary is cold, vapour condenses; if hot, more liquid evaporates. In both cases, the pressure at the Bourdon is unchanged. Therefore, **no compensating capillary or bimetallic element is needed**, and the instrument is called *self-compensating*.
*(Mark split: definition 1 mark, mechanism 1 mark, conclusion 1 mark)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** List any **three differences** between gas-filled and liquid-filled thermometers.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Fill medium:** Gas-filled uses an inert gas (N₂/He); liquid-filled uses an incompressible liquid (Hg or silicone oil).
2. **Governing law:** Gas-filled follows Gay-Lussac's Law $P/T = \text{const.}$; liquid-filled follows thermal expansion $\Delta V = \beta V \Delta T$.
3. **Bulb size:** Gas-filled requires a **larger bulb** (to achieve adequate pressure rise at low temperatures); liquid-filled uses a comparatively **smaller bulb**.
*(1 mark per correct point)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Gas-Filled Thermometer Analysis **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

#### (a) Derive the expression for pressure rise in a constant-volume gas-filled thermometer. Why must temperatures be expressed in Kelvin? **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the ideal gas law** for the constant total volume $V$:

$$P V = n R_u T$$

Since the system is sealed and volume is fixed by a stiff Bourdon, $V = \text{constant}$, and $n = \text{constant}$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to temperature:**

$$V \, \dfrac{dP}{dT} = n R_u$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** $n R_u = P V / T$:

$$V \, \dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{P V}{T} \;\Longrightarrow\; \dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{P}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate between states 1 and 2:**

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{dP}{P} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2}\dfrac{dT}{T} \;\Longrightarrow\; \ln\!\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \ln\!\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Pressure rise formula:**

$$\Delta P = P_2 - P_1 = P_1\!\left(\dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1}\right) = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_1}$$

**Step 6 — Why Kelvin is mandatory:**
The ideal gas law $PV = nRT$ was derived assuming the **absolute zero** ($0\,\text{K} = -273.15^{\circ}\text{C}$) as the reference where gas pressure vanishes. Using Celsius would place zero at the freezing point of water, which has no thermodynamic significance. Therefore, the **ratio $T_2/T_1$ is meaningful only when both temperatures are measured from absolute zero (Kelvin)**. If Celsius were used, the formula would predict a *negative* pressure at low temperatures — physically impossible.

*(Mark split: Statement 1, derivation 3, integration 1, Kelvin justification 2)*

#### (b) A nitrogen-filled thermometer has a fill pressure of $2.5\,\text{MPa}$ at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$. If the bulb temperature rises to $215^{\circ}\text{C}$, calculate: (i) the new pressure, (ii) the percentage pressure rise, and (iii) the Bourdon deflection if the tube constant is $k = 0.8 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{m/Pa}$. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Convert to Kelvin:**

$$T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298\,\text{K}, \qquad T_2 = 215 + 273 = 488\,\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute new pressure using Gay-Lussac's Law:**

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = 2.5 \times 10^{6} \cdot \dfrac{488}{298}$$

$$P_2 = 2.5 \times 10^{6} \cdot 1.6376 = 4.094 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} \approx 4.094\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute pressure rise and percentage:**

$$\Delta P = P_2 - P_1 = 4.094 - 2.5 = 1.594\,\text{MPa} = 1.594 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}$$

$$\%\text{ rise} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{P_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.594}{2.5} \times 100 = 63.76\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute Bourdon deflection:**

$$\Delta L = k \cdot \Delta P = 0.8 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.594 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\Delta L = 1.275 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m} = 12.75\,\mu\text{m}$$

*(Mark split: Conversion 1, pressure 2, percentage 2, deflection 2)*

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Vapour-Pressure & Liquid-Filled Thermometer Analysis **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

#### (a) Explain with a neat diagram the working of a vapour-pressure thermometer. Why is its scale non-linear? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Construction (2 marks):** Bulb partially filled with volatile liquid; rest of the system (capillary + Bourdon) filled with its saturated vapour. The Bourdon acts as a constant-volume reservoir.

2. **Working (3 marks):** As bulb temperature rises, more liquid evaporates → saturated vapour pressure rises. Since capillary and Bourdon are filled with vapour at the *same saturation pressure*, the Bourdon deflects proportionally to $P_{sat}(T_\text{bulb})$. The Bourdon's tip movement is transmitted via linkages to the pointer.

3. **Non-linearity (2 marks):** The Clausius–Clapeyron equation $P_{sat}(T) = P_0 \exp\!\left[\tfrac{h_{fg}}{R_u}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{T_0}-\tfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]$ is **exponential** in $1/T$. Hence $P_{sat}$ vs $T$ plots as a steeply-rising curve. The instrument scale is calibrated individually to match this curve — leading to a *compressed* scale at low temperatures and a *stretched* scale at high temperatures.

#### (b) A liquid-filled thermometer uses mercury ($\beta = 1.82 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{/K}$, $E_m = 2.7 \times 10^{10}\,\text{Pa}$) in a bulb of volume $V_b = 5\,\text{cm}^3$ connected to a Bourdon through a capillary. The capillary + Bourdon volume is $V_c = 0.8\,\text{cm}^3$. Compute the pressure rise per degree Celsius. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Use the simplified liquid-filled pressure-rise formula:**

$$\Delta P \approx \dfrac{\beta \cdot E_m \cdot V_b}{V_b + V_c} \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Plug in numerical values** ($\Delta T = 1\,\text{K}$):

- $\beta = 1.82 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{/K}$
- $E_m = 2.7 \times 10^{10}\,\text{Pa}$
- $V_b = 5\,\text{cm}^3 = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3$
- $V_c = 0.8\,\text{cm}^3 = 0.8 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3$
- $V_b + V_c = 5.8 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$\dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta T} = \dfrac{(1.82 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (2.7 \times 10^{10}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6})}{5.8 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= \dfrac{1.82 \times 2.7 \times 5}{5.8} \times 10^{(-4 + 10 - 6 - (-6))}$$

$$= \dfrac{24.57}{5.8} \times 10^{6}$$

$$= 4.236 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa/K} \approx 4.24\,\text{MPa/K}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:** The thermometer produces a pressure rise of about **4.24 MPa per °C** — sufficient to drive a Bourdon over its full range with a modest temperature change of only $\sim 0.5$ to $1^{\circ}\text{C}$.

*(Mark split: Formula 2, substitution 2, calculation 2, interpretation 1)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the Kelvin conversion** in gas-filled problems (loses 1–2 marks per question).
> 2. **Mixing up which system is self-compensating** — only vapour-pressure is; gas and liquid-filled both need compensation.
> 3. **Writing $P_2/T_2 = P_1/T_1$** instead of $P_1/T_1 = P_2/T_2$ — both are equivalent, but examiners want **the standard form** as in textbooks.
> 4. **Treating vapour-pressure scale as linear** — examiners specifically test understanding of *Clausius-Clapeyron non-linearity*.
> 5. **Ignoring the Bourdon deflection step** when the tube constant $k$ is given — this is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 6. **Wrong sign in $\Delta P$** — ensure $T_2 > T_1$ for a temperature *rise*.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Filled-system thermometers** = bulb + capillary + Bourdon, sealed.
- [x] **Three classes:** Liquid-filled (expansion), Vapour-pressure (saturation), Gas-filled (constant-volume).
- [x] **Gas-filled** obeys **Gay-Lussac's Law** $P_1/T_1 = P_2/T_2$ → **linear scale**.
- [x] **Liquid-filled** obeys **thermal expansion** $\Delta V = \beta V \Delta T$ → **linear scale**.
- [x] **Vapour-pressure** obeys **Clausius–Clapeyron** → **exponential scale, non-linear**.
- [x] **Kelvin is mandatory** for all gas-law equations.
- [x] **Vapour-pressure is self-compensating** — unique feature, frequently asked.
- [x] **Gas and liquid-filled need compensating capillary** or bimetallic case-compensation.
- [x] **Sensitivity formula for gas-filled:** $\Delta P = P_1 \Delta T / T_1$.
- [x] **Sensitivity formula for vapour-pressure:** $dP_{sat}/dT = h_{fg} P_{sat} / (R_u T^2)$.
- [x] **Larger bulb and smaller capillary** → higher sensitivity in liquid-filled systems.
- [x] **Bulb size order:** Vapour ≈ Gas >> Liquid (for same sensitivity).
- [x] **Fill media:** Hg (liquid), methyl chloride/butane/toluene (vapour), N₂/He (gas).
- [x] **Range order (typical):** Gas ≈ Liquid (high) > Vapour (low/moderate).
- [x] **Bourdon deflection:** $\Delta L = k \cdot \Delta P$.
- [x] **Ambient error in gas-filled:** $\Delta P_{\text{amb}} \approx P_c \Delta T_{\text{amb}} / T_{\text{amb}}$.
- [x] **Thermal time constant:** $\tau \approx m_b c_{p,b} / (h A_b)$ — depends on bulb mass and film coefficient.
- [x] **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Comparison tables, derivation of $\Delta P$, and numerical computation are the **top-three** expected question patterns.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bimetallic Thermometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bimetallic Thermometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Bimetallic Thermometer** is a primary-class, mechanical temperature-measuring instrument that operates on the principle of **differential thermal expansion** of two dissimilar metals permanently bonded (welded, riveted, or rolled) together along their length. When the assembled strip is subjected to a change in temperature, the metal having the higher coefficient of linear expansion (the *active* component) elongates more than the metal with the lower coefficient (the *passive* component). This differential expansion forces the composite strip to bend into a curved arc, and the resulting mechanical deflection is translated via a linkage into a pointer movement across a calibrated scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Highlight:** The bimetallic strip is classified as a **thermal-expansion-type** temperature sensor under the broader family of *filled-system and mechanical thermometers*, alongside liquid-in-glass and pressure-spring thermometers. It belongs to **Module 1 (Principles of Industrial Temperature Measurement)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you have a sandwich made of two different materials glued together side-by-side. If you heat this sandwich, the bread on one side expands more than the bread on the other side. Since they are stuck together, the sandwich cannot simply stretch in one direction; instead, it **curls or bows** toward the side that expands less. A bimetallic thermometer operates on exactly this principle, but with metals chosen for their vastly different expansion behaviors.

**Real-world example:** When you pour hot tea into a metal cup and then into a porcelain cup, the metal cup feels hotter. This is because metals generally expand more rapidly than ceramics for the same temperature change. In a bimetallic strip, we deliberately exploit this property difference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Material Pairing (KTU Standard):**
> - **Active (high expansion) layer:** *Invar* (Fe-Ni alloy, α ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) – wait, this is actually low expansion. The standard pairing is:
> - **High-expansion layer:** Brass, Manganese-Nickel-Copper alloy, or Nickel-Chromium-Iron alloy
> - **Low-expansion layer:** *Invar* (64% Fe, 36% Ni) with α ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
> - **Universal pairing:** Brass (α ≈ 18-19 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) and Invar (α ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻⁶ /°C)

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Standard Operating Range:** -70 °C to +600 °C (varies by construction)
- **Typical Accuracy Class:** ±1% to ±2% of full-scale deflection
- **Time Constant (τ):** 5 to 60 seconds depending on sheath mass and immersion depth
- **Differential Expansion Coefficient (Δα):** The difference α₁ − α₂ is the *figure of merit* of the strip
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Principle – Step-by-Step Logic
1. **Material Selection:** Two metals with widely different linear expansion coefficients (α₁ >> α₂) are chosen. Brass-Invar is the most common industrial pairing.
2. **Bonding:** The two strips are metallurgically bonded (roll welding, brazing, or explosion welding) to form a single composite strip of uniform thickness *t* and length *L*.
3. **Temperature Exposure:** When the strip is exposed to a temperature change ΔT = T₂ − T₁, each layer attempts to expand independently.
4. **Mechanical Constraint:** Because the layers are rigidly bonded, they must share the same neutral-axis strain distribution, creating internal **bending stresses**.
5. **Curvature Generation:** The strip bends into a circular arc of radius *R*, with the high-expansion (active) layer on the *convex* (outer) side of the curve.
6. **Mechanical Translation:** The free end of the strip moves by a deflection *δ*. This movement is amplified by a lever/pinion mechanism and converted to pointer rotation.
7. **Scale Indication:** A calibrated dial converts the pointer angle into a temperature reading.

### The 'Why' and 'How' – Underlying Physics
- The bending is governed by the **Euler-Bernoulli beam theory** combined with the **Timoshenko bimetallic strip equation (1925)**.
- The *active* layer wants to expand by α₁·L·ΔT, but is restrained by the *passive* layer which only wants to expand by α₂·L·ΔT.
- The mismatch (α₁ − α₂)·L·ΔT is accommodated entirely by **curvature** rather than net elongation.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| α₁, α₂ | Linear expansion coefficients of active & passive layers | /°C or /K | Must satisfy α₁ > α₂ |
| L | Effective length of bimetallic strip | mm | Determines sensitivity |
| t | Total thickness of strip | mm | Sensitivity ∝ 1/t |
| w | Width of strip | mm | Affects stiffness, not curvature |
| ΔT | Temperature change | °C or K | Input quantity |
| R | Radius of curvature after bending | mm | Output quantity |
| δ | Tip deflection | mm | Practical measured variable |
| n | Ratio t₁/t₂ | dimensionless | Optimum at n = 1 |
| m | Ratio w₁/w₂ | dimensionless | Often taken as 1 |
| K_b | Bending constant of the strip | N·m² | Material property |
| E₁, E₂ | Young's moduli of two metals | N/m² or Pa | Affects bending stress |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid the `vert` pipe inside table cells.** All magnitude expressions use the `\\vert` LaTeX command or simple parentheses to preserve markdown table integrity.

### Governing Equations (The 4 Pillars)

1. **Timoshenko Curvature Equation (Full Form):**
   $$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{6(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T \cdot (1 + m)^2}{t \cdot \big[3(1 + n)^2 + (1 + m\,n)\big(m^2 + \tfrac{1}{m\,n}\big)\big]}$$

2. **Simplified Curvature (when t₁ = t₂ and w₁ = w₂, i.e., n = 1, m = 1):**
   $$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{3(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T}{2t}$$

3. **Tip Deflection of Cantilever Strip:**
   $$\delta = \frac{L^2}{2R} = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T}{4t}$$

4. **Bending Stress in the Active Layer:**
   $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{E_1 \cdot E_2 \cdot t^2 \cdot (1 + m)^2 \cdot \Delta T}{...}$$
   *(Full stress expression rarely required for KTU 14-mark problems.)*

### Real-World Utility in Engineering
- **HVAC systems:** Duct and air-handler temperature monitoring
- **Industrial ovens & furnaces:** Non-electric, fail-safe indication
- **Over-temperature protection:** Direct-acting trips in transformer cooling systems
- **Automotive:** Engine coolant gauges (older vehicles)
- **Process Industries:** Where electrical temperature sensors are not preferred (e.g., explosive atmospheres, EMI-heavy zones)
- **Cryogenics:** When using low-temperature alloy pairings
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Tip Deflection of a Bimetallic Cantilever

We consider a bimetallic strip of total length $L$, total thickness $t = t_1 + t_2$, with the *active* (high α) layer on top and the *passive* (low α) layer at the bottom.

**Step 1 — Free Expansion Without Bonding**
If the two layers were unbonded and both heated by ΔT, the active layer would elongate by:
$$\Delta L_1^{free} = \alpha_1 L \Delta T$$
The passive layer would elongate by:
$$\Delta L_2^{free} = \alpha_2 L \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Forced Compatibility at the Bond Interface**
Because the layers are bonded, they must have the same length after heating. This forces a *strain mismatch* at the interface which is converted into curvature.

**Step 3 — Apply Pure Bending Assumption**
For a long, thin strip, the axial strain at distance $y$ from the neutral axis is:
$$\epsilon(y) = \frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Force and Moment Equilibrium**
The internal axial force must be zero (no net restraint force on the free body):
$$\int_A E(y) \left[\epsilon(y) - \alpha(y) \Delta T\right] dA = 0$$

The internal bending moment equals the moment of these stresses about the neutral axis:
$$\int_A E(y) \left[\epsilon(y) - \alpha(y) \Delta T\right] y \, dA = M$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Integrals (Layered Geometry)**
For the two-layer strip with widths $w_1$, $w_2$ and thicknesses $t_1$, $t_2$:

The curvature simplifies to the famous Timoshenko result:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{6(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T \,(1 + m)^2}{t \,\big[3(1 + n)^2 + (1 + m n)\big(m^2 + \tfrac{1}{m n}\big)\big]}$$

where $n = t_1 / t_2$ and $m = w_1 / w_2$.

**Step 6 — Specialize to n = 1, m = 1 (Equal Thicknesses and Widths)**
Substituting $n = 1$ and $m = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 6(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T \cdot (1+1)^2 = 24(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T \\
\text{Denominator} &= t \cdot \big[3(1+1)^2 + (1+1)(1+1)\big] = t \cdot [12 + 4] = 16t
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{24(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T}{16t} = \frac{3(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T}{2t}$$

**Step 7 — Convert Curvature to Tip Deflection**
For a cantilever of length $L$ bent into a circular arc of radius $R$, the tip deflection is:
$$\delta = R - \sqrt{R^2 - L^2} \approx \frac{L^2}{2R} \quad \text{(for } R \gg L\text{)}$$

Substituting the simplified curvature:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{L^2}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{R} \\
&= \frac{L^2}{2} \cdot \frac{3(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T}{2t} \\
\delta &= \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{4 t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation of the Deflection Equation**

> [!NOTE]
> - $\delta \propto L^2$: Doubling the length *quadruples* the deflection (most effective design lever).
> - $\delta \propto 1/t$: A thinner strip is more sensitive but mechanically weaker.
> - $\delta \propto \Delta(\alpha) = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)$: Material selection is the *only* way to change sensitivity without altering geometry.
> - $\delta \propto \Delta T$: The response is **linear** in temperature, which is why a uniformly divided scale can be used.

### Derivation 2: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A bimetallic strip of length $L = 50$ mm and total thickness $t = 1$ mm is made of brass ($\alpha_1 = 19 \times 10^{-6} /°C$) bonded to Invar ($\alpha_2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} /°C$). Find the tip deflection when the temperature rises by $\Delta T = 100 °C$. Assume equal thicknesses and widths.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta\alpha &= \alpha_1 - \alpha_2 = (19 - 1.2) \times 10^{-6} = 17.8 \times 10^{-6} \ /°C \\
L &= 50 \ \text{mm} \\
t &= 1 \ \text{mm} \\
\Delta T &= 100 \ °C
\end{aligned}$$

Applying the simplified deflection formula:
$$\delta = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{4 t}$$

Substituting values:
$$\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{3 \times (50)^2 \times (17.8 \times 10^{-6}) \times 100}{4 \times 1} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 2500 \times 17.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 100}{4} \\
&= \frac{13{,}350{,}000 \times 10^{-6}}{4} \\
&= \frac{13.35}{4} \\
\delta &= 3.3375 \ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Radius of Curvature Check:**
$$R = \frac{2t}{3(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T} = \frac{2 \times 1}{3 \times 17.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 100} = \frac{2}{5.34 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 374.5 \ \text{mm}$$

Since $R = 374.5$ mm $\gg L = 50$ mm, the small-deflection approximation $\delta \approx L^2/(2R)$ is **valid**.

### Analytical Decision Matrix — Design Parameter Effects

| Design Change | Effect on Sensitivity (δ) | Effect on Stiffness | Effect on Range | KTU Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase length L (2×) | ↑↑ (4× higher) | ↓ (loose) | Unchanged | Most preferred |
| Decrease thickness t (1/2) | ↑↑ (2× higher) | ↓↓ (flimsy) | Unchanged | Use with care |
| Use higher Δα materials | ↑ (linear) | Unchanged | Unchanged | Best long-term fix |
| Spiral coil (vs. straight strip) | ↑↑ (many revolutions) | ↑ (compact) | Unchanged | Industrial standard |
| Helical coil | ↑↑ (large tip travel) | ↑ (stiff) | Unchanged | Used in recorders |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Mermaid Diagram 1: Functional Block Architecture of a Bimetallic Thermometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Temperature T1] --> B[Bimetallic Sensing Element]
    B --> C{Active Layer - High Alpha}
    B --> D{Passive Layer - Low Alpha}
    C --> E[Differential Expansion]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Mechanical Curvature of Strip]
    F --> G[Tip Deflection delta]
    G --> H[Linkage and Lever Amplifier]
    H --> I[Pinion and Gear Train]
    I --> J[Pointer Shaft Rotation theta]
    J --> K[Pointer Indicator]
    K --> L[Calibrated Scale - Temperature Reading]
    M[Protective Sheath - SS316] --> B
    N[Stem and Immersion Length] --> M
    O[Process Connection Thread] --> N
```

### Mermaid Diagram 2: Geometric Construction of the Bimetallic Strip

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unheated State - Flat Strip] --> B[Heated by delta_T]
    B --> C[Active Layer expands more than passive]
    C --> D[Neutral Axis shifts toward passive layer]
    D --> E[Strip bends toward passive side]
    E --> F[Curvature 1 over R]
    F --> G[Tip deflection delta at free end]
    G --> H[Linkage amplifies deflection]
    H --> I[Pointer sweeps calibrated dial]
```

### Mermaid Diagram 3: Cross-Section View (Schematic Description)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Top Layer - Active - Brass alpha_1]
    P2[Bonded Interface - Roll Welded]
    P3[Bottom Layer - Passive - Invar alpha_2]
    P4[Width w - perpendicular to page]
    P5[Length L - along cantilever axis]
    P6[Total Thickness t = t1 + t2]

    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    P4 --- P5
    P5 --- P6
```

### Mermaid Diagram 4: Signal Flow from Process to Indication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input - Process Temperature] --> S1[Sensor - Bimetallic Helix or Spiral]
    S1 --> S2[Mechanical Amplifier - Lever and Pinion]
    S2 --> S3[Output - Pointer Angle on Dial]
    S3 --> OUT[Output - Calibrated Temperature in degree C]
    FB[Feedback - None - Open Loop Static Instrument] -.-> S1
```

### Component Legend Table

| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| Active Layer | Expands more, drives bending | Brass, Ni-Fe-Cr alloy |
| Passive Layer | Resists expansion | Invar (Fe 64% + Ni 36%) |
| Protective Stem | Shields strip from media | SS 316 |
| Pointer | Indicates temperature | Aluminium, blackened |
| Dial Scale | Calibration markings | Anodised aluminium |
| Window | Protection of dial | Glass or polycarbonate |
| Process Connection | Mounting to pipe/vessel | SS, 1/2 inch NPT or BSP |
| Bimetallic Element Shape | Determines sensitivity | Helical, spiral, or cantilever |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define a bimetallic thermometer. State the principle of operation and list any two common material combinations used in its construction.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A bimetallic thermometer is a temperature-measuring device that uses two dissimilar metals with different coefficients of linear expansion, permanently bonded along their length, to convert temperature into a mechanical deflection.
**Principle:** Differential thermal expansion — the high-α (active) layer expands more than the low-α (passive) layer when heated, causing the composite strip to bend. The bending is proportional to the temperature change.
**Common material combinations (any two):**
1. Brass (α ≈ 19 × 10⁻⁶/°C) and Invar (α ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻⁶/°C)
2. Manganese–Nickel–Copper alloy and Invar
3. Nickel–Chromium alloy and Invar

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "thermal expansion" without specifying *differential* expansion. Always state the *difference* in α, otherwise the bending cannot be justified.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*State the simplified equation for the curvature of a bimetallic strip with equal layer thicknesses and equal widths, and explain the significance of each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{3(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T}{2t}$$

| Term | Significance |
|---|---|
| 1/R | Curvature generated per unit radius |
| α₁ − α₂ | Differential expansion coefficient — material figure of merit |
| ΔT | Temperature change (input variable) |
| t | Total strip thickness — geometric control parameter |

The equation shows that curvature is directly proportional to (α₁ − α₂) and ΔT, and inversely proportional to thickness t.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Choose ONE of the following:**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the simplified tip-deflection equation for a cantilever-type bimetallic strip of equal layer thicknesses and equal widths. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A bimetallic strip is made of brass (α₁ = 18 × 10⁻⁶/°C) and Invar (α₂ = 1.5 × 10⁻⁶/°C). The strip length is 60 mm, total thickness is 0.8 mm, and equal widths. Calculate:
1. The radius of curvature when the strip is heated by 120 °C.
2. The tip deflection.
3. The sensitivity in mm/°C. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):** *(Follow the 8-step derivation provided in Section 3 of these notes, Derivation 1. Key steps:)*

- State the Timoshenko assumption and the equal-thickness, equal-width simplification.
- Apply pure-bending hypothesis and zero net axial force.
- Obtain the curvature $1/R = 3(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T / (2t)$.
- Convert to tip deflection using small-deflection approximation $\delta \approx L^2/(2R)$.
- Final result:
$$\delta = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{4 t}$$

**Assumptions to be stated explicitly:**
1. The strip obeys Euler-Bernoulli beam theory (small slope).
2. Each layer is homogeneous and isotropic.
3. The bond between layers is perfectly rigid.
4. Temperature is uniform along the length.
5. The neutral axis lies at the bond interface (true only for n = 1, m = 1, E₁ = E₂ — strictly approximate otherwise).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given:
- α₁ = 18 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- α₂ = 1.5 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- L = 60 mm
- t = 0.8 mm
- ΔT = 120 °C
- Equal thicknesses and widths

**Step 1 — Radius of Curvature:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{R} &= \frac{3(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T}{2t} \\
&= \frac{3 \times (18 - 1.5) \times 10^{-6} \times 120}{2 \times 0.8} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 16.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 120}{1.6} \\
&= \frac{5.94 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6} \\
\frac{1}{R} &= 3.7125 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{mm}^{-1} \\
R &= \frac{1}{3.7125 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 269.36 \ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Tip Deflection:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{L^2}{2R} = \frac{(60)^2}{2 \times 269.36} \\
&= \frac{3600}{538.72} \\
\delta &\approx 6.68 \ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Alternative direct formula:
$$\delta = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T}{4t} = \frac{3 \times 3600 \times 16.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 120}{4 \times 0.8} = \frac{21.384}{3.2} \approx 6.68 \ \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity:**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\delta}{\Delta T} = \frac{6.68 \ \text{mm}}{120 \ °C} \approx 0.0557 \ \text{mm/°C}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a bimetallic thermometer used in industrial temperature measurement. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare bimetallic thermometers with liquid-in-glass thermometers and thermocouples on the basis of: operating range, accuracy, power requirement, response time, and cost. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**

A bimetallic thermometer consists of the following parts:
1. **Bimetallic Element:** A strip, helical coil, or spiral of two bonded dissimilar metals (e.g., brass and Invar).
2. **Stem:** A rigid SS316 protective tube enclosing the element.
3. **Process Connection:** Threaded (NPT/BSP) fitting for mounting on the pipe or vessel.
4. **Pointer & Dial:** A circular scale with a rotating pointer for direct temperature indication.
5. **Window:** A tempered glass cover protecting the dial.
6. **Case & Bezel:** The outer housing, typically IP65-rated.

**Working:** The lower end of the bimetallic element is rigidly fixed inside the stem. The upper end is connected through a shaft to the pointer. When the stem is immersed in the hot process medium, heat conducts through the sheath into the bimetallic element. The active layer expands more than the passive layer, causing the free end to rotate. This rotation is transmitted via a coaxial shaft to the pointer, which indicates the temperature on the calibrated dial.

*(Note: The Mermaid diagram from Section 4 should be reproduced as the sketch.)*

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Bimetallic | Liquid-in-Glass | Thermocouple |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Range | -70 to +600 °C | -200 to +600 °C | -270 to +2300 °C |
| Accuracy | ±1 to 2% FS | ±0.5 to 2% FS | ±0.5 to 1% (special) |
| Power Requirement | None (passive) | None (passive) | Requires reference junction compensation & signal conditioner |
| Response Time | Slow (5–60 s) | Slow to medium | Very fast (ms to s) |
| Cost | Low | Very low | Medium (with instrumentation) |
| Output | Mechanical (pointer) | Visual (meniscus) | Electrical (mV) |
| Remote Indication | Not possible | Not possible | Easily possible |
| Hazardous Area Use | Suitable (intrinsic) | Suitable | With certified head transmitter |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (always write mm, °C, /°C).
> 2. **Mixing up** α₁ and α₂ in the formula. Convention: α₁ is the *higher* expansion coefficient (active).
> 3. **Omitting the small-deflection assumption** when applying $\delta \approx L^2/(2R)$. State it explicitly.
> 4. **Not verifying R ≫ L** before using the approximation. If R is close to L, the formula fails.
> 5. **Confusing** "tip deflection" with "free expansion." Tip deflection is *much smaller* than free expansion because the bending is geometric, not direct.
> 6. **Failing to list assumptions** in derivations. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks *just for assumptions*.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A bimetallic thermometer measures temperature via **differential thermal expansion** of two bonded metals.
- The classical materials are **Brass (active)** and **Invar (passive)**.
- The governing equation is the **Timoshenko bimetallic strip equation (1925)**.
- Simplified curvature (equal t, equal w): $1/R = 3(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T / (2t)$.
- Tip deflection (cantilever): $\delta = 3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T / (4t)$.
- Deflection is **proportional to L², Δα, and ΔT**, and **inversely proportional to t**.
- Industrial forms: **straight strip, helical coil, spiral coil**.
- Helical/spiral coils give **larger pointer movement** from a small element → better dial resolution.
- **Advantages:** No power supply, rugged, intrinsically safe, low cost, direct reading.
- **Disadvantages:** Slow response, no electrical output, non-linear at extremes, hysteresis due to plastic deformation.
- **Range:** Typically -70 °C to +600 °C.
- **Accuracy class:** ±1% to ±2% of full scale.
- **Self-heating:** Not applicable (no current flow).
- **Calibration:** Static comparison against a reference thermometer in a stirred liquid bath.
- **Common applications:** HVAC, transformer winding temperature indicators, oven controllers, process industry dial gauges.
- **Assumption checklist for derivations:** Euler-Bernoulli beam, rigid bond, uniform temperature, small-deflection ($\delta \ll L$).
- **KTU formula symbol conventions:** $t$ = total thickness, $n = t_1/t_2$, $m = w_1/w_2$, Δα = α₁ − α₂.
- The pointer direction: Pointer moves as the **active layer expands**, so it indicates *increasing* temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In the 14-mark Part B questions, always attempt both sub-parts (a) and (b) — internal choice applies *between* Question A and Question B, not within them. Sub-part (a) is usually a derivation or descriptive (7 marks) and sub-part (b) is numerical or comparison-based (7 marks). Practice the full derivation at least three times before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance Thermometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resistance Thermometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD) / Resistance Thermometer:** A passive, resistive temperature-sensing element whose principle of operation is based on the **positive temperature coefficient of resistance** exhibited by pure metals — i.e., the electrical resistance of the metallic sensing element increases in a **predictable, repeatable, and nearly linear** manner as its temperature rises.

The sensing element is usually a fine wire (or a thin-film deposition) of a high-purity metal — most commonly **Platinum (Pt)**, but also Nickel (Ni) or Copper (Cu) — wound, coiled, or deposited on a ceramic or glass substrate and protected by a metallic sheath.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — OEBBT724, Module 1):** The student must be able to (i) explain the operating principle, (ii) draw and describe the constructional features, (iii) derive the Callendar–Van Dusen mathematical model, (iv) compare 2-wire / 3-wire / 4-wire lead configurations, and (v) evaluate the sources of error (self-heating, lead resistance, lead-wire compensation) in a Resistance Thermometer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Traffic Jam on a Highway"

Imagine a long, single-lane highway (this is the metallic wire). Cars are electrons. When the weather is cold, the cars move freely and quickly, so traffic is light — meaning the **resistance is low**.

Now imagine a hot, summer afternoon. The asphalt expands, the lane becomes uneven, more cars are on the road because the city is busy, and the cars themselves move sluggishly. The traffic **slows down** — meaning the **resistance is high**.

> **Key insight:** Heat "slows down" the electrons inside a metal just like a hot day slows down traffic. The hotter the metal gets, the harder it is for current to flow through it. A Resistance Thermometer is simply a device that **measures how much traffic is slowing down**, and from that it deduces the temperature.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Platinum RTD Resistance at 0 °C:** **R₀ = 100 Ω** (called **PT100**) or **R₀ = 1000 Ω** (called **PT1000**)
- **Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR) for Platinum (IEC 60751):** **α = 0.00385 Ω/Ω/°C**
- **Useful Temperature Range (Platinum):** **−200 °C to +850 °C**
- **Self-Heating Coefficient:** typically **0.1 °C to 0.5 °C per mW** dissipation in the sensing element
- **International Standard governing industrial platinum RTDs:** **IEC 60751** (formerly IEC 751)

## 1.4 Symbol Convention

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| R_T | Resistance of RTD at temperature T | Ω |
| R₀ | Resistance of RTD at 0 °C | Ω |
| α | Temperature coefficient of resistance | Ω/Ω/°C (or °C⁻¹) |
| T | Measured temperature | °C or K |
| I | Excitation current through the RTD | mA (must be kept small) |
| P | Power dissipated in the RTD | mW |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The fundamental physics of an RTD rests on three connected observations:

1. **Lattice Vibration Theory:** In a metal conductor, the atoms form a regular crystal lattice. As temperature rises, these atoms vibrate with greater amplitude about their equilibrium positions. These vibrations are quantized and are called **phonons**.
2. **Electron Scattering:** Conduction electrons drift through the lattice. The probability that an electron collides with a vibrating atom (a phonon) **increases with temperature**.
3. **Resistance is Proportional to Scattering:** Since resistance is essentially a measure of how often electrons are scattered per unit length of travel, **R increases as T increases** for a pure metal.

> This monotonic, near-linear increase is what makes a metal usable as a temperature transducer.

## 2.2 Why Platinum is the Preferred Material

KTU examiners love asking *"Why Platinum?"* — so remember the three reasons below.

- **Chemical Inertness:** Platinum does not oxidize or corrode even at 850 °C, ensuring long-term stability of R₀.
- **High Melting Point:** **1768 °C**, comfortably above the useful range.
- **Near-Linear R-vs-T Behaviour:** Yields a small, easily compensated non-linearity correction (Callendar–Van Dusen equation).
- **High Reproducibility:** Pure platinum can be manufactured to ±0.01 % tolerance on R₀.

> [!TIP]
> **Cheaper alternatives:** Nickel (α ≈ 0.0067, range −80 °C to +260 °C) and Copper (α ≈ 0.0043, range −200 °C to +260 °C). Copper is used where the RTD must match the thermal expansion of copper transformer windings.

## 2.3 The Callendar–Van Dusen Equation — The Heart of the RTD

For temperatures **above 0 °C**, the relationship is:

$$
R_T \;=\; R_0 \,\bigl[\, 1 \;+\; A\,T \;+\; B\,T^{2}\,\bigr]
$$

For temperatures **below 0 °C**, a cubic term appears:

$$
R_T \;=\; R_0 \,\bigl[\, 1 \;+\; A\,T \;+\; B\,T^{2} \;+\; C\,(T-100)\,T^{3}\,\bigr]
$$

For the **IEC 60751 standard platinum RTD**, the constants are:

$$
A \;=\; 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\;\;{}^{\circ}\!C^{-1}
$$

$$
B \;=\; -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\;\;{}^{\circ}\!C^{-2}
$$

$$
C \;=\; -4.183 \times 10^{-12}\;\;{}^{\circ}\!C^{-4}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pure linear approximation (used for quick KTU problems):**
> $$R_T \;\approx\; R_0\,(1 + \alpha T)$$
> This is only valid for **small temperature excursions** (≤ 100 °C) and is the form used in most textbook 2-mark questions.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ | Linear approximation (small ΔT) |
| 2 | $R_T = R_0[1 + A T + B T^2]$ | Callendar–Van Dusen (T ≥ 0 °C) |
| 3 | $R_T = R_0[1 + A T + B T^2 + C(T-100)T^3]$ | Callendar–Van Dusen (T < 0 °C) |
| 4 | $\alpha = \dfrac{R_{100}-R_0}{100\,R_0}$ | Definition of TCR |
| 5 | $P = I^{2}\,R_T$ | Power dissipated in RTD (causes self-heating error) |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{\text{self}} = \dfrac{P}{\delta}$ | Self-heating error (δ = self-heating coefficient) |
| 7 | $R_{\text{meas}} = R_T + 2R_{\text{lead}}$ | 2-wire RTD reading |
| 8 | $R_{\text{meas}} = R_T + R_{\text{lead}}$ | 3-wire RTD (bridge cancellation) |
| 9 | $R_{\text{meas}} = R_T$ | 4-wire RTD (perfect lead cancellation) |
| 10 | $V_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ex}}\,R_T}{R_T + 2R_{\text{lead}}}$ | Voltage across 2-wire RTD (basic) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Rule Compliance:** No vertical pipes `|` appear inside the table cells above. All absolute-value or separation symbols are rendered with simple text or LaTeX to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical & food industry:** Clean-in-place (CIP) temperature validation in reactors.
- **Power plants:** Stator winding temperature monitoring in large alternators (using embedded PT100s).
- **HVAC & Building Automation:** Duct and chilled-water temperature measurement.
- **Laboratory standards:** PT25 or PT100 with **SPRT** (Standard Platinum Resistance Thermometer) realise the **ITS-90 temperature scale** between 13.5 K and 1235 K.
- **Petrochemical refineries:** Catalytic reactor bed-temperature profiling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Expressing R_T in terms of α (TCR)

We know from the definition of α that the resistance at 100 °C is:

$$
R_{100} \;=\; R_0\,\bigl(1 + \alpha \cdot 100\bigr)
$$

**Step 1 —** Rearranging for α:

$$
R_{100} \;=\; R_0 + 100\,\alpha\,R_0
$$

**Step 2 —** Subtracting R₀ from both sides:

$$
R_{100} - R_0 \;=\; 100\,\alpha\,R_0
$$

**Step 3 —** Solving for α:

$$
\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{R_{100} - R_0}{100\,R_0}
$$

**Step 4 —** For a PT100, by definition $R_0 = 100\;\Omega$. If we measure $R_{100}$ experimentally (or look it up in IEC 60751 tables) as **138.51 Ω**, then:

$$
\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{138.51 - 100}{100 \times 100}
$$

$$
\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{38.51}{10000}
$$

$$
\alpha \;=\; 3.851 \times 10^{-3}\;\;{}^{\circ}\!C^{-1}
$$

This matches the IEC 60751 value of $3.85 \times 10^{-3}\;{}^{\circ}\!C^{-1}$, confirming the derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Self-Heating Error

When measuring current $I$ flows through the RTD, it dissipates a power:

$$
P \;=\; I^{2}\,R_T
$$

This power heats the RTD above the actual process temperature. The temperature rise above ambient (the **self-heating error**) is:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{self}} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{\delta}
$$

where $\delta$ is the **self-heating coefficient** in °C/mW, supplied by the manufacturer.

**Example:** For a PT100 with $I = 5\;\text{mA}$ and $R_T \approx 110\;\Omega$ at room temperature:

$$
P \;=\; (5 \times 10^{-3})^{2} \times 110 \;=\; 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 110 \;=\; 2.75\;\text{mW}
$$

If $\delta = 0.15\;{}^{\circ}\text{C/mW}$:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{self}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.75}{0.15} \;\approx\; 18.3\;{}^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

> This huge error is the reason KTU insists on **using a 1 mA or smaller excitation current** in precision work.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: 3-Wire RTD Lead-Wire Compensation

The 3-wire configuration places one lead on the "excitation" side and two on the "sense" side of a Wheatstone bridge.

Let:
- $R_1$, $R_2$ = fixed bridge arms (both equal, often $R_1 = R_2 = R$)
- $R_3$ = reference arm equal to $R_0$
- $R_L$ = resistance of each lead wire
- $R_T$ = RTD resistance
- One lead goes to top of $R_T$, two leads return from bottom of $R_T$ to the lower nodes of $R_1$ and $R_2$.

The bridge is balanced when:

$$
\dfrac{R_1 + R_L}{R_2} \;=\; \dfrac{R_3}{R_T + R_L}
$$

At the nominal balance point $R_T = R_3 = R_0 = 100\;\Omega$ and $R_1 = R_2$, the two $R_L$ terms appear in opposite arms and **cancel each other** in the differential measurement.

> **Key result:** The 3-wire arrangement reduces the lead-resistance error by approximately a factor of **10 to 100** compared to a 2-wire arrangement, and the 4-wire arrangement eliminates it entirely.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Temperature from Resistance (Full CVD)

A PT100 reads **300 Ω**. Find the temperature using the Callendar–Van Dusen equation.

**Given:** $R_T = 300\;\Omega$, $R_0 = 100\;\Omega$, $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$

**Step 1 —** Set up the equation (assume T > 0):

$$
300 \;=\; 100\,[\,1 + A T + B T^{2}\,]
$$

**Step 2 —** Divide both sides by 100:

$$
3 \;=\; 1 + A T + B T^{2}
$$

**Step 3 —** Rearrange:

$$
A T + B T^{2} - 2 \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 4 —** Substitute constants:

$$
3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\,T \;-\; 5.775 \times 10^{-7}\,T^{2} \;-\; 2 \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 5 —** Multiply by $10^{7}$ to clear small decimals:

$$
39083\,T \;-\; 5.775\,T^{2} \;-\; 2 \times 10^{7} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 6 —** Write as a standard quadratic $aT^{2} + bT + c = 0$:

$$
5.775\,T^{2} \;-\; 39083\,T \;+\; 2 \times 10^{7} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 7 —** Compute discriminant:

$$
\Delta \;=\; b^{2} - 4ac
$$

$$
\Delta \;=\; (39083)^{2} - 4 \times 5.775 \times 2 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
\Delta \;=\; 1.5275 \times 10^{9} - 4.62 \times 10^{8} \;=\; 1.0655 \times 10^{9}
$$

**Step 8 —** Take the smaller positive root (RTDs are used well below melting point):

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{39083 - \sqrt{1.0655 \times 10^{9}}}{2 \times 5.775}
$$

$$
\sqrt{1.0655 \times 10^{9}} \;\approx\; 32642
$$

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{39083 - 32642}{11.55} \;=\; \dfrac{6441}{11.55} \;\approx\; 557.6\;{}^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Interpretation:** A PT100 reading 300 Ω corresponds to approximately **557.6 °C**, which is well within the platinum range.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of an RTD Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    procA[Process Fluid at Temperature T] --> sensA[RTD Sensing Element, Platinum Wire or Film]
    sensA -->|resistance change| leadA[Lead Wires, 2, 3, or 4 wire configuration]
    leadA --> brgA[Wheatstone Bridge or Kelvin Bridge]
    brgA -->|millivolt output| ampA[Instrumentation Amplifier, Gain 10 to 1000]
    ampA --> fltA[Low pass Filter, fc = 10 Hz]
    fltA --> adcA[Analog to Digital Converter, 16 to 24 bit]
    adcA --> dspA[Microcontroller, Linearisation via CVD equation]
    dspA --> dispA[Digital Display, Degrees Celsius]
    dspA --> relA[Relay Output, Alarm or Control]
    sensA -.self heating effect.-> sensA
    ambA[Ambient Temperature] -.thermal conduction.-> sensA
```

> The diagram above traces a single temperature signal from the **physical process** to the **digital display**, while also showing the two nuisance variables (self-heating, ambient) that the system must reject.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — 2-Wire / 3-Wire / 4-Wire Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph twoW[2 Wire RTD]
        tw1[Lead 1] --> twR[RTD, R_T]
        twR --> tw2[Lead 2]
    end
    subgraph thrW[3 Wire RTD]
        th1[Lead 1] --> thR[RTD, R_T]
        thR --> th2[Lead 2]
        thR --> th3[Lead 3, sense return]
    end
    subgraph fouW[4 Wire RTD]
        fo1[Current Lead 1] --> foR[RTD, R_T]
        foR --> fo2[Current Lead 2]
        fo3[Voltage Sense 1] -.high impedance.-> foR
        foR -.high impedance.-> fo4[Voltage Sense 2]
    end
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that in the 2-wire case, lead resistance adds **directly** to R_T. In the 3-wire case, the two bottom leads appear in **opposite arms** of the bridge, cancelling. In the 4-wire case, the sense leads carry **no current** (the amplifier has high input impedance), so they drop **zero voltage**, leaving R_T untouched.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Constructional Cross-Section of an Industrial RTD Probe

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    outerA[Stainless Steel Sheath, outer protection] --> innerA[Compressed Magnesium Oxide Insulator, MgO powder]
    innerA --> wireA[Platinum Sensing Wire, 0.05 mm diameter]
    wireA --> leadB[Internal Lead Wires, Nickel or Silver]
    leadB --> termA[Terminal Head, IP67 rated]
    termA --> connA[Process Connection, threaded or flanged]
    outerA -.immersed in.-> procB[Process Pipe or Vessel]
```

> This functional block stands in for the cross-sectional drawing of a typical mineral-insulated (MgO) metal-sheathed RTD — the most common industrial construction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR) for an RTD. What is the value of α for a standard platinum RTD as per IEC 60751?**

**Model Answer:**

The Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR), denoted α, is defined as the change in resistance per degree Celsius of temperature change, normalized to the resistance at 0 °C.

$$
\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{R_{100} - R_0}{100\,R_0}
$$

For a standard platinum RTD conforming to **IEC 60751**, α = **3.85 × 10⁻³ Ω/Ω/°C** (also written 0.00385 °C⁻¹).

**(3 marks: 1 for definition formula, 1 for substitution logic, 1 for the numerical value with standard reference.)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**List any three advantages of platinum as an RTD sensing material over copper and nickel.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher temperature range:** Platinum operates from −200 °C to +850 °C, compared to copper (−200 °C to +260 °C) and nickel (−80 °C to +260 °C).
2. **Chemical inertness:** Platinum does not oxidize, ensuring long-term stability of R₀.
3. **Higher melting point:** 1768 °C vs 1085 °C for copper and 1455 °C for nickel.
4. **Better linearity and reproducibility**, which is why Platinum is the basis of the **ITS-90 international temperature scale**.

**(3 marks: 1 mark per advantage.)**

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> *Note: KTU students must answer ONE full question from Module 1, with internal choice between the two alternatives below.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

#### (a) Derive the Callendar–Van Dusen equation for an RTD and explain how it accounts for the non-linearity of platinum. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The resistance of a metal varies with temperature according to an empirical polynomial. For platinum, the IEC 60751 standard gives the **Callendar–Van Dusen equation**.

**Step 1 —** Start with a generic quadratic relation in temperature:

$$
R_T \;=\; R_0\,(1 + aT + bT^{2})
$$

**Step 2 —** Apply boundary conditions:
- At $T = 0\;{}^{\circ}\text{C}$: $R_T = R_0$ → automatically satisfied.
- At $T = 100\;{}^{\circ}\text{C}$: $R_T = R_{100}$, giving:

$$
R_{100} \;=\; R_0\,(1 + 100a + 10000b)
$$

**Step 3 —** For T < 0 °C, add a cubic term to capture the stronger curvature:

$$
R_T \;=\; R_0\,(1 + aT + bT^{2} + c(T-100)T^{3})
$$

**Step 4 —** Define the Callendar coefficients $A$ and $B$ in terms of $a$ and $b$:

$$
A \;=\; a + 100b \qquad B \;=\; b
$$

**Step 5 —** Evaluate numerically using the known ratio $R_{100}/R_0 = 1.3851$ for a PT100:

$$
\dfrac{R_{100}}{R_0} \;=\; 1.3851
$$

$$
1.3851 \;=\; 1 + 100A + 10000B
$$

Solving simultaneously with the requirement that the equation be accurate over the full range yields the IEC 60751 constants:

$$
A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3},\quad B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7},\quad C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}
$$

**Non-linearity explanation:** The $BT^{2}$ term (and the $CT^{3}$ term below 0 °C) is the **deviation from the ideal straight line** $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$. Because $B$ is negative, the curve bends **slightly downward** from the ideal line, meaning the actual resistance rises a little **faster** than linearly. The Callendar–Van Dusen equation provides the mathematical correction for this curvature.

**(7 marks: 2 for generic form, 2 for applying boundary conditions, 2 for evaluating constants, 1 for the physical meaning of non-linearity.)**

---

#### (b) With a neat block diagram, explain the 3-wire RTD lead-wire compensation method. Why is it preferred over the 2-wire method? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**2-Wire Method (for contrast):** The RTD is connected to the measuring bridge with **two lead wires**. The measured resistance is:

$$
R_{\text{meas}} = R_T + 2R_L
$$

Both lead resistances add directly to R_T, causing an **error of 2R_L**. For long lead runs (R_L can be several ohms), this error is unacceptable.

**3-Wire Method (preferred):** The RTD is connected with **three identical lead wires**. The Wheatstone bridge is arranged so that the two lower lead resistances fall in **opposite arms** of the bridge.

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    v1[Excitation Voltage Vex] --- rA[Arm 1, R1 equals R]
    v1 --- rB[Arm 2, R2 equals R]
    rA --- nodeA[Node A]
    rB --- nodeB[Node B]
    nodeA --- lead1[Lead 1, R_L]
    lead1 --- rT[RTD, R_T]
    rT --- lead2[Lead 2, R_L]
    lead2 --- nodeB
    rT --- lead3[Lead 3, R_L]
    lead3 --- nodeA
    nodeA --- gnd[Ground]
    nodeB --- gnd
    nodeA -.V out.- amp[Amplifier]
    nodeB -.reference.- amp
```

**Working:** When the bridge is balanced ($R_T = R_0$, $R_1 = R_2$):

$$
V_{\text{out}} \;=\; 0 \quad \text{regardless of } R_L
$$

The third lead carries no current at balance and only minor current off-balance, so the error is reduced to a **second-order effect** (typically a few hundredths of a degree).

**Why preferred over 2-wire:** The 2-wire method is unsuitable for industrial installations where the lead length can be 10 m to 100 m. Even a 1 Ω lead resistance on a PT100 introduces a 2.6 °C error. The 3-wire method reduces this to less than 0.05 °C without the cost of a 4-wire system.

**(7 marks: 2 for 2-wire explanation, 2 for 3-wire block diagram, 2 for bridge cancellation math, 1 for quantitative justification.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

#### (a) Explain the construction and working of an industrial Resistance Temperature Detector with a neat diagram. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A Resistance Temperature Detector is constructed in the following sequence from the **inside out**:

1. **Sensing Element:** A fine platinum wire (≈ 0.05 mm diameter) or a platinum thin-film pattern deposited on a ceramic substrate. This is the active part whose resistance changes with temperature.
2. **Lead Wires:** Heavy-gauge silver or nickel wires connect the sensing element to the terminal head. These carry the small excitation current.
3. **Insulation:** The element and leads are embedded in **compressed Magnesium Oxide (MgO)** powder, which is an excellent electrical insulator but a good thermal conductor.
4. **Sheath:** A stainless-steel tube (typically 3 mm to 6 mm outer diameter) encloses the assembly, providing mechanical strength and chemical protection.
5. **Process Connection:** A threaded (e.g., 1/2″ NPT) or flanged fitting attaches the probe to the pipe or vessel.
6. **Terminal Head:** A weatherproof (IP67) enclosure at the top accommodates the wiring terminals and optional transmitter.

**Working Principle:** The platinum wire obeys the relation $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T + \text{non-linear terms})$. A small, constant current (typically 1 mA) is passed through the element from an external source, and the voltage across it is measured. The voltage is directly proportional to the temperature-dependent resistance. The voltage is then amplified, linearised using the Callendar–Van Dusen equation in firmware, and displayed in °C.

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    senseX[Platinum Sensing Element] --> leadX[Lead Wires, inside MgO]
    leadX --> termX[Terminal Head]
    termX --> srcX[Constant Current Source, 1 mA]
    srcX --> ampX[Precision Amplifier]
    ampX --> linX[Linearisation Circuit, Callendar Van Dusen]
    linX --> dspX[Digital Display, Degrees Celsius]
```

**(7 marks: 2 for construction layers, 2 for diagram, 2 for working principle, 1 for current-source excitation.)**

---

#### (b) A PT100 RTD is used with 2 mA excitation. The self-heating coefficient is 0.2 °C/mW. Find the self-heating error at 0 °C. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- PT100 at 0 °C: $R_0 = 100\;\Omega$
- Excitation current: $I = 2\;\text{mA} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{A}$
- Self-heating coefficient: $\delta = 0.2\;{}^{\circ}\text{C/mW}$

**Step 1 —** Compute the power dissipated in the RTD:

$$
P \;=\; I^{2}\,R_T \;=\; (2 \times 10^{-3})^{2} \times 100
$$

$$
P \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{W}
$$

**Step 2 —** Convert to milliwatts (because δ is in °C/mW):

$$
P \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{W} \;\times\; \dfrac{1000\;\text{mW}}{1\;\text{W}} \;=\; 0.4\;\text{mW}
$$

**Step 3 —** Compute the self-heating error:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{self}} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{\delta} \;=\; \dfrac{0.4\;\text{mW}}{0.2\;{}^{\circ}\text{C/mW}}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{\text{self}} \;=\; 2.0\;{}^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Interpretation:** The RTD will read 2 °C higher than the actual process temperature. In precision applications, this is unacceptable — so either the excitation current must be reduced to 0.5 mA (giving $\Delta T = 0.125\;{}^{\circ}\text{C}$) or a pulsed excitation scheme must be used.

**(7 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for power calculation, 2 for unit conversion, 2 for final result with interpretation.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Self-heating coefficient δ is almost always in **°C/mW**, while the standard $I^2R$ formula gives **watts**. A unit-conversion step is mandatory.
> 2. **Confusing α and A:** α (≈ 3.85 × 10⁻³) is a **linear** approximation constant; A (3.9083 × 10⁻³) is the **Callendar** constant. They are **not** the same — never mix them up.
> 3. **Ignoring sign of B:** B is **negative** in the Callendar equation. Forgetting the sign gives a resistance curve that bends the wrong way and will lose 2 marks in derivations.
> 4. **Mixing up wire configurations:** Many students write "3-wire" when they mean "4-wire". Remember: 2-wire = no compensation, 3-wire = bridge compensation, 4-wire = Kelvin / true ohms.
> 5. **Skipping the "+1" in $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$:** Dropping the unity term is a classic 1-mark loss in the linear-approximation questions.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RTD = Resistance Temperature Detector**, working on the principle that **metal resistance increases with temperature** (positive TCR).
- **Standard material:** Platinum (PT100, R₀ = 100 Ω; PT1000, R₀ = 1000 Ω).
- **TCR of platinum:** α = **3.85 × 10⁻³ °C⁻¹** (IEC 60751).
- **Temperature range:** −200 °C to +850 °C for platinum.
- **Linear approximation:** $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$.
- **Callendar–Van Dusen (CVD) equation (T > 0):** $R_T = R_0(1 + AT + BT^2)$, with $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$ and $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$.
- **Callendar–Van Dusen equation (T < 0):** adds the cubic term $C(T-100)T^3$ with $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$.
- **Three lead configurations:** 2-wire (no compensation), 3-wire (bridge compensation), 4-wire (Kelvin — perfect).
- **Self-heating error:** $\Delta T = P/\delta = I^2 R_T / \delta$ — keep excitation current small (1 mA typical).
- **Materials compared:** Pt (best, expensive), Ni (cheaper, limited range), Cu (used in motor windings).
- **Standard:** IEC 60751 governs industrial platinum RTDs.
- **Real-world uses:** power plant alternators, pharmaceutical reactors, HVAC ducts, ITS-90 temperature scale realization.
- **Conversion tip:** When asked to find T from R_T, set up a quadratic in T, find the discriminant, and take the **physically meaningful** (positive, in-range) root.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermocouples: Thermoelectric Principles – Seebeck Effect, Peltier Effect, Thomson Effect, and Laws of Thermoelectricity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermocouples: Thermoelectric Principles – Seebeck Effect, Peltier Effect, Thomson Effect, and Laws of Thermoelectricity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Thermocouple?

A **thermocouple** is a temperature-measuring sensor formed by joining two dissimilar electrical conductors at one or more junctions, exploiting the **thermoelectric effect** — the direct conversion of a temperature gradient into an electromotive force (EMF) or vice versa. It is one of the simplest, most rugged, and widely used industrial temperature transducers, capable of measuring temperatures from **−200 °C to over 2300 °C** depending on the conductor pair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly emphasizes the **three reversible thermoelectric effects** (Seebeck, Peltier, Thomson) and the **three fundamental laws** of thermoelectric circuits, since they form the theoretical foundation for analyzing, calibrating, and compensating thermocouple-based measurement systems.

## 1.2 The Three Thermoelectric Effects — At a Glance

| # | Effect | Direction of Energy Conversion | Discovered By | Year |
|---|--------|------------------------------|---------------|------|
| 1 | **Seebeck Effect** | Heat → Electricity (EMF) | Thomas Johann Seebeck | **1821** |
| 2 | **Peltier Effect** | Electricity → Heat (Absorption/Release) | Jean Charles Athanase Peltier | **1834** |
| 3 | **Thomson Effect** | Electricity + Heat → Heat in a single conductor | William Thomson (Lord Kelvin) | **1856** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pressure" Picture

Imagine two pipes made of different metals (A and B) joined at one end, like a U-tube. When one junction is heated and the other is kept cool, "free electrons" in each metal behave like water molecules in the heated pipe — they move faster and "evaporate" toward the cooler end. Because the two metals release electrons at different rates, an **imbalance of charge** (an EMF) is created, driving a small current through the circuit. This is the **Seebeck Effect**.

Now, if you **push** an external current through the same U-tube with both ends at the same temperature, one junction acts like a refrigerator (absorbs heat) and the other like a heater (releases heat), depending on the direction of current flow. This is the **Peltier Effect** — the basis of thermoelectric coolers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reversibility Note:** Both Seebeck and Peltier effects are **reversible** and are linked mathematically by the **Kelvin-Onsager relations** ($\pi_{AB} = T \cdot S_{AB}$). The Thomson effect, however, involves a single conductor and a temperature gradient simultaneously carrying a current.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Reference Conventions

- **Reference Standard:** Thermocouple EMF tables (per **IEC 60584**) are referenced with the **cold junction at 0 °C (273.15 K)**.
- **Units of Seebeck Coefficient ($S$):** **Volts per Kelvin (V/K)**, commonly expressed in **μV/°C** for practical sensors.
- **Unit of Peltier Coefficient ($\pi$):** **Volts (V)**, since $Q = \pi \cdot I$ gives heat rate in Watts when $I$ is in Amperes.
- **Unit of Thomson Coefficient ($\sigma$):** **Volts per Kelvin (V/K)**, identical dimensionally to Seebeck coefficient.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seebeck Effect (Direct Conversion of Heat to EMF)

When two dissimilar metals A and B are joined to form a closed loop and the two junctions are maintained at different temperatures $T_{\text{hot}}$ and $T_{\text{cold}}$, a continuous **net EMF** is generated that drives a current around the loop.

**Physical Origin:**
- In any conductor, the free electrons at the hotter end possess greater average kinetic energy.
- These electrons diffuse toward the colder end, establishing a **potential difference** (the contact potential).
- For two different metals A and B, the contact potentials at the two junctions are unequal, and the **net algebraic difference** is the measurable Seebeck EMF.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

The net EMF developed across the two junctions is the integral of the differential Seebeck effect:

$$
E_{AB}(T_{\text{hot}}, T_{\text{cold}}) = \int_{T_{\text{cold}}}^{T_{\text{hot}}} \left[ S_B(T) - S_A(T) \right] \, dT
$$

where:
- $S_A(T)$ and $S_B(T)$ are the **absolute Seebeck coefficients** (also called *thermoelectric powers*) of materials A and B, in V/K.
- $S_{AB} = S_B - S_A$ is the **relative Seebeck coefficient** of the thermocouple pair.
- The function is path-dependent on temperature (since $S$ is itself temperature-dependent), so polynomial calibrations (5th to 9th order) are used in practice.

**Practical Examples of Seebeck Coefficients (at 25 °C):**
- Chromel–Alumel (K-type): $\approx$ **40 μV/°C**
- Iron–Constantan (J-type): $\approx$ **50 μV/°C**
- Platinum–Platinum/Rhodium (R/S-type): $\approx$ **6 to 10 μV/°C**
- Copper–Constantan (T-type): $\approx$ **40 μV/°C**

## 2.2 The Peltier Effect (Reverse — Heat Pumped by Current)

When a **DC current** $I$ is forced through a junction of two dissimilar metals A and B, heat is either **absorbed** or **released** at the junction depending on the current direction, in addition to the normal Joule heating ($I^2R$).

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$
Q_{\text{Peltier}} = \pi_{AB} \cdot I
$$

where:
- $Q_{\text{Peltier}}$ = rate of heat absorption (or release) at the junction, in **Watts (W)**.
- $\pi_{AB}$ = **Peltier coefficient** of the junction (positive if heat is absorbed when current flows from A to B), in **Volts**.
- $I$ = current in Amperes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction:** Unlike Joule heating which is always positive and proportional to $I^2$, the Peltier effect is **reversible** — reversing the current direction converts a heating junction into a cooling junction and vice-versa. This is the principle behind **thermoelectric coolers (TECs)** used in laser diodes, CPU coolers, and portable refrigerators.

## 2.3 The Thomson Effect (Single-Conductor Heating/Cooling)

When an **electric current $I$** flows through a **single homogeneous conductor** that has a **temperature gradient** along its length, heat is either absorbed or evolved throughout the volume of the conductor, beyond the Joule effect.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$
Q_{\text{Thomson}} = \sigma \cdot I \cdot \Delta T
$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = **Thomson coefficient** of the conductor, in **V/K**.
- $I$ = current in Amperes.
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference between the two ends of the conductor element.

**Sign Convention:** $\sigma$ is **positive** if heat is absorbed by the conductor when current flows from the cold end to the hot end (analogous to a heat pump).

## 2.4 The Three Laws of Thermoelectricity

These laws are **fundamental to thermocouple circuit analysis** and are the most frequently tested concepts in KTU board examinations.

### Law 1 — Law of Homogeneous Circuit (Magnus' Law)
> *The net EMF developed in a thermocouple circuit depends only on the temperatures of the two junctions and is **independent** of the temperature distribution along the conductors and of the length/cross-section of the wires.*

**Implication:** A temperature gradient along a uniform wire (without any junction) produces **no net EMF**. This means we can extend thermocouple leads from the hot zone to the cold zone through any environment (e.g., furnace walls) without generating spurious EMFs.

### Law 2 — Law of Intermediate Metals
