> *If a third metal C is introduced into a thermocouple circuit (A–B), the net EMF of the circuit remains unchanged, **provided** the two new junctions formed with metal C are maintained at the **same temperature**.*

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$
E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) = E_{AC}(T_1, T_2) + E_{CB}(T_1, T_2)
$$

**Implication:** This law justifies the use of **soldering, brazing, welding, or terminal blocks** of any third metal (copper, brass, etc.) in the thermocouple circuit, as long as both contact points are isothermal. This is the basis for the practical **cold-junction compensation (CJC)** hardware at the instrument terminals.

### Law 3 — Law of Intermediate Temperatures
> *The EMF developed by a thermocouple between temperatures $T_1$ and $T_3$ is equal to the algebraic sum of the EMFs developed between $T_1$ and $T_2$ and between $T_2$ and $T_3$.*

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$
E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)
$$

**Implication:** This is the foundation of **reference-junction compensation** and **standard thermocouple tables**. The reference tables are universally tabulated with cold junction at 0 °C, but the actual cold junction may be at any other temperature; the law allows accurate software/hardware compensation.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary/Condition |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Seebeck EMF (integral form) | $E_{AB} = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} (S_B - S_A)\, dT$ | $S$: V/K ; $T$: K | Cold junction reference at 0 °C |
| 2 | Differential Seebeck | $dE = S_{AB}\, dT$ | $S_{AB}$: μV/°C | $S_{AB} = S_B - S_A$ |
| 3 | Peltier Heat | $Q = \pi_{AB} \cdot I$ | $\pi_{AB}$: V ; $I$: A | Reversible, junction-only |
| 4 | Thomson Heat | $Q = \sigma \cdot I \cdot \Delta T$ | $\sigma$: V/K ; $\Delta T$: K | Single conductor, distributed |
| 5 | Kelvin Relation 1 | $\pi_{AB} = T \cdot S_{AB}$ | T in Kelvin | Couples Peltier & Seebeck |
| 6 | Kelvin Relation 2 | $\dfrac{dS_{AB}}{dT} = \dfrac{\sigma_B - \sigma_A}{T}$ | σ in V/K | Couples all three effects |
| 7 | Law of Intermediate Metals | $E_{AB} = E_{AC} + E_{CB}$ (at same $T_1, T_2$) | All in volts | Both new junctions isothermal |
| 8 | Law of Intermediate Temps | $E_{AB}(T_1,T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1,T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2,T_3)$ | All in volts | Requires $T_1 < T_2 < T_3$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Generation:** **Radioisotope Thermoelectric Generators (RTGs)** in spacecraft (e.g., Voyager, Curiosity rover) use the Seebeck effect to convert heat from decaying Pu-238 into electricity.
- **Cooling & Heating:** **Thermoelectric Coolers (TECs)** in medical refrigerators, infrared detectors, and CPU cooling use the Peltier effect.
- **Process Industries:** Refineries, power plants, and steel mills use K-type and S-type thermocouples for continuous high-temperature monitoring because of their ruggedness and **wide range**.
- **Cold-Junction Compensation Electronics:** The Law of Intermediate Metals is what allows simple **transistor-based or IC-based CJC** modules to work accurately.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Seebeck and Peltier Coefficients (Kelvin Relation 1)

### Statement to Prove:
$$
\pi_{AB} = T \cdot S_{AB}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Set up the thermodynamic system:**
Consider a thermocouple formed by two metals A and B with junctions at absolute temperatures $T$ (hot) and $T_0$ (cold). The net Seebeck EMF is:

$$
E = \int_{T_0}^{T} S_{AB}(T')\, dT'
$$

**Step 2 — Treat the thermocouple as a reversible heat engine:**
If a current $I$ is allowed to flow (from the Seebeck EMF), the circuit delivers electrical power $P = E \cdot I$ to an external load. By the Second Law of Thermodynamics, the total entropy change in a reversible cycle must be zero.

**Step 3 — Entropy flow from the hot junction:**
At the hot junction, heat $Q_h$ is absorbed. The rate of entropy intake at the hot junction is:

$$
\Delta S_{\text{hot}} = \frac{Q_{\text{Peltier, hot}}}{T} = \frac{\pi_{AB} \cdot I}{T}
$$

**Step 4 — Entropy flow at the cold junction:**
At the cold junction, the same magnitude of Peltier heat is rejected (with opposite sign of entropy):

$$
\Delta S_{\text{cold}} = -\frac{\pi_{AB} \cdot I}{T_0}
$$

**Step 5 — Add the entropy flow from the Thomson effect along the wires:**
The Thomson heat along the wires contributes an entropy term $\int (\sigma_B - \sigma_A) \cdot I\, dT / T$, which is independent of $\pi_{AB}$ for this step.

**Step 6 — Apply reversibility (zero net entropy change):**
For a fully reversible cycle (no net entropy production):

$$
\frac{\pi_{AB} \cdot I}{T} - \frac{\pi_{AB} \cdot I}{T_0} + \text{(Thomson terms)} = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Equate electrical work to total heat absorbed:**
The First Law for the reversible cycle gives:

$$
E \cdot I = Q_{\text{hot}} - Q_{\text{cold}}
$$

Substituting the integrals and simplifying through the combined First & Second Law constraints yields the **Kelvin relation**:

$$
\pi_{AB}(T) = T \cdot S_{AB}(T)
$$

This is one of the most important relations in thermoelectric theory and is the cornerstone of modern thermoelectric material science.

## 3.2 Derivation: Kelvin Relation 2 (Linking Thomson to Seebeck)

### Statement to Prove:
$$
\frac{dS_{AB}}{dT} = \frac{\sigma_B - \sigma_A}{T}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Differentiate Kelvin Relation 1 with respect to T:**

$$
\frac{d\pi_{AB}}{dT} = S_{AB}(T) + T \cdot \frac{dS_{AB}}{dT}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Second Law to the entire thermoelectric circuit:**
For a reversible cyclic process, considering the Thomson heat contributions in wires A and B over a temperature interval $dT$:

$$
\frac{d\pi_{AB}}{dT} - (\sigma_B - \sigma_A) = S_{AB}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $d\pi_{AB}/dT$:**

$$
S_{AB} + T \cdot \frac{dS_{AB}}{dT} - (\sigma_B - \sigma_A) = S_{AB}
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $S_{AB}$ from both sides:**

$$
T \cdot \frac{dS_{AB}}{dT} = \sigma_B - \sigma_A
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the final result:**

$$
\frac{dS_{AB}}{dT} = \frac{\sigma_B - \sigma_A}{T}
$$

**Engineering Significance:** This relation tells us that the **temperature sensitivity of a thermocouple's Seebeck coefficient** is directly determined by the difference in Thomson coefficients of the two wires. K-type thermocouples have nearly linear response (constant $S_{AB}$) because $\sigma_B \approx \sigma_A$ for Chromel and Alumel.

## 3.3 Derivation: Law of Intermediate Metals (Full Proof)

### Statement to Prove:
If metal C is inserted at a point where temperature is $T_m$, with both its junctions at $T_m$:

$$
E_{ABC}(T_1, T_2) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2)
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Write the EMF contributions in the modified circuit (A–C–B):**

$$
E_{\text{new}} = \int_{T_1}^{T_m} (S_C - S_A)\, dT + \int_{T_m}^{T_2} (S_B - S_C)\, dT
$$

**Step 2 — Combine the integrals over the full range $[T_1, T_2]$:**

$$
E_{\text{new}} = \int_{T_1}^{T_m} (S_C - S_A)\, dT + \int_{T_m}^{T_2} (S_B - S_C)\, dT
$$

Splitting the $S_C$ terms:

$$
E_{\text{new}} = \int_{T_1}^{T_m} S_C\, dT - \int_{T_1}^{T_m} S_A\, dT + \int_{T_m}^{T_2} S_B\, dT - \int_{T_m}^{T_2} S_C\, dT
$$

**Step 3 — Reorder the $S_C$ integrals:**

$$
\int_{T_1}^{T_m} S_C\, dT - \int_{T_m}^{T_2} S_C\, dT = -\int_{T_1}^{T_2} S_C\, dT
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$
E_{\text{new}} = -\int_{T_1}^{T_2} S_C\, dT - \int_{T_1}^{T_m} S_A\, dT + \int_{T_m}^{T_2} S_B\, dT
$$

Rearranging the $S_A$ and $S_B$ terms to form one continuous integral:

$$
E_{\text{new}} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (S_B - S_A)\, dT - \int_{T_1}^{T_2} S_C\, dT + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} S_C\, dT
$$

**Step 5 — The $S_C$ integrals cancel exactly:**

$$
E_{\text{new}} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (S_B - S_A)\, dT = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2)
$$

**Conclusion:** The net EMF is **unchanged** regardless of the third metal, as long as both junctions of metal C are at the same temperature $T_m$. This elegant proof underpins the use of copper extension wires and connector blocks in practical thermocouple instrumentation.

## 3.4 Derivation: Law of Intermediate Temperatures (Full Proof)

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Write the EMF between $T_1$ and $T_3$:**

$$
E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = \int_{T_1}^{T_3} S_{AB}(T)\, dT
$$

**Step 2 — Split the integral at $T_2$:**

$$
E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} S_{AB}(T)\, dT + \int_{T_2}^{T_3} S_{AB}(T)\, dT
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the two sub-integrals as separate EMFs:**

$$
E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)
$$

This result is the **foundation of reference junction compensation** algorithms in modern digital thermocouple instruments, where the cold-junction temperature is measured by a separate sensor (e.g., a silicon diode or RTD) and the corresponding EMF is added algebraically to the measured thermocouple EMF.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A Chromel–Alumel (K-type) thermocouple has a mean Seebeck coefficient of **40 μV/°C** in the range 0–600 °C. If the hot junction is at 600 °C and the cold junction is at 50 °C, calculate the net Seebeck EMF. (Assume linearity for simplicity.)

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the temperature difference:**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}} = 600 - 50 = 550\,°C
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Seebeck equation (linear approximation):**

$$
E = S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
E = (40 \times 10^{-6}\, \text{V/°C}) \times (550\,°C)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$
E = 22000 \times 10^{-6}\, \text{V} = 0.022\, \text{V} = 22\, \text{mV}
$$

**Answer:** The thermocouple develops an EMF of **22 mV** for a 550 °C temperature difference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** The standard K-type EMF at 600 °C (with cold junction at 0 °C) is approximately **24.905 mV**, and at 50 °C it is **2.023 mV**. Subtracting gives **22.882 mV** — close to our linear approximation of 22 mV, with a small deviation due to the actual nonlinearity of $S_{AB}(T)$. For higher accuracy, students should use the **NIST polynomial coefficients** (ITS-90) rather than a constant mean coefficient.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper, B.Tech Instrumentation]**
**State and explain the Seebeck Effect. Derive the expression for the net Seebeck EMF developed in a thermocouple circuit.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **Seebeck Effect** is the direct conversion of a temperature difference into an electromotive force (EMF) at the junction of two dissimilar metals. Discovered by **Thomas Johann Seebeck in 1821**, it is the fundamental principle of thermocouple temperature measurement.

When two dissimilar metals A and B are joined to form a closed loop, and the two junctions are maintained at different temperatures $T_{\text{hot}}$ and $T_{\text{cold}}$, a net EMF is induced that drives a continuous current in the loop. The mathematical expression is:

$$
E_{AB} = \int_{T_{\text{cold}}}^{T_{\text{hot}}} \left[ S_B(T) - S_A(T) \right]\, dT
$$

where $S_A(T)$ and $S_B(T)$ are the absolute Seebeck coefficients of the two metals. The net EMF depends only on the junction temperatures and the material properties, in accordance with the **Law of Homogeneous Circuits**.

*(Examiner's Allocation: 1 mark definition + 1 mark expression/derivation + 1 mark explanation of $S_{AB}$)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Distinguish between the Peltier Effect and the Thomson Effect. Give the governing equations for both.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Parameter | Peltier Effect | Thomson Effect |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| Location | At the **junction** of two dissimilar metals | Along the **body** of a single conductor |
| Cause | Current flow across junction | Current + temperature gradient in same conductor |
| Heat Equation | $Q = \pi_{AB} \cdot I$ | $Q = \sigma \cdot I \cdot \Delta T$ |
| Coefficient Unit | Volt (V) | Volt per Kelvin (V/K) |
| Reversibility | Reversible | Reversible |
| Discovery | **Peltier, 1834** | **Thomson (Lord Kelvin), 1856** |

The **Peltier Effect** occurs only at the boundary between two materials, while the **Thomson Effect** is distributed throughout a single material that has both current flow and a thermal gradient.

*(Examiner's Allocation: 1.5 marks for distinction table + 1 mark each for both equations = 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester Examination, B.Tech]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams and governing equations, explain the **Seebeck Effect, Peltier Effect, and Thomson Effect** in detail. How are the three effects thermodynamically related? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **Type K (Chromel–Alumel)** thermocouple has its hot junction in a furnace and cold junction maintained at 25 °C. The voltmeter at the cold junction reads **20.10 mV**. Using the standard EMF table values (E_K(600 °C, 0 °C) = 24.905 mV; E_K(25 °C, 0 °C) = 1.000 mV), determine the **furnace temperature** using the Law of Intermediate Temperatures. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:

**1. Seebeck Effect (2.5 marks):**
- Definition: Direct conversion of temperature difference into EMF at a junction of two dissimilar metals.
- Equation: $E_{AB} = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} (S_B - S_A)\, dT$
- Diagram: Standard thermocouple loop with hot and cold junctions, voltmeter connected.

**2. Peltier Effect (1.5 marks):**
- Definition: Reversible heating/cooling at a junction when a DC current is forced through it.
- Equation: $Q = \pi_{AB} \cdot I$
- Application: Thermoelectric coolers (TECs), portable refrigerators.

**3. Thomson Effect (1 mark):**
- Definition: Heat absorbed/released in a single conductor carrying current and having a temperature gradient.
- Equation: $Q = \sigma \cdot I \cdot \Delta T$

**4. Thermodynamic Relations (2 marks):**
- **Kelvin Relation 1:** $\pi_{AB} = T \cdot S_{AB}$ (couples Peltier and Seebeck)
- **Kelvin Relation 2:** $\dfrac{dS_{AB}}{dT} = \dfrac{\sigma_B - \sigma_A}{T}$ (couples Thomson to Seebeck)
- These follow from the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics applied to a reversible thermoelectric cycle.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:

**Given Data:**
- Measured EMF: $E_{\text{meas}} = 20.10$ mV
- Cold junction temperature: $T_c = 25$ °C
- Reference table value at hot junction (assuming 600 °C): $E_K(600\,°C, 0\,°C) = 24.905$ mV
- Reference table value at cold junction: $E_K(25\,°C, 0\,°C) = 1.000$ mV

**Step 1 — Apply the Law of Intermediate Temperatures:**

The actual EMF measured is between the hot junction at $T_h$ and the cold junction at 25 °C. According to the law:

$$
E_{AB}(T_h, T_c) = E_{AB}(T_h, 0\,°C) - E_{AB}(T_c, 0\,°C)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:**

$$
20.10\, \text{mV} = E_{AB}(T_h, 0\,°C) - 1.000\, \text{mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the unknown reference EMF:**

$$
E_{AB}(T_h, 0\,°C) = 20.10 + 1.000 = 21.10\, \text{mV}
$$

**Step 4 — Use interpolation in the standard K-type table (NIST ITS-90):**

From the K-type EMF table, the EMF of 21.10 mV corresponds to approximately:

- At 500 °C: $E = 20.640$ mV
- At 520 °C: $E = 21.493$ mV (approximately)

**Step 5 — Linear interpolation:**

$$
T_h = 500 + \left(\frac{21.10 - 20.640}{21.493 - 20.640}\right) \times (520 - 500)
$$

$$
T_h = 500 + \left(\frac{0.460}{0.853}\right) \times 20 = 500 + 10.78
$$

$$
\boxed{T_h \approx 510.8\,°C}
$$

**Answer:** The furnace temperature is approximately **511 °C**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes (KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Sign Error:** Students often forget to **subtract** the cold-junction EMF (i.e., they write $E_h = E_{\text{meas}} + E_c$ instead of $E_h = E_{\text{meas}} + E_c$ when $E_c$ is referenced to 0 °C). Always draw the **thermoelectric circuit diagram** before writing equations.
> 2. **Interpolation Sloppiness:** Linear interpolation is acceptable for small ranges; for KTU 14-mark problems, students should clearly show **two bracketing table values** and apply the interpolation formula.
> 3. **Unit Confusion:** The Seebeck coefficient is in **μV/°C** or **V/K** — never mix up the two.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Examination, B.Tech Instrumentation]**

**(a)** State and prove the **three laws of thermoelectricity**. Explain their practical significance in thermocouple instrumentation with appropriate examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **Type J (Iron–Constantan)** thermocouple is used with a **copper** extension wire to a remote indicator. The hot junction is at **400 °C**, the cold junction (at the indicator terminals) is at **30 °C**, and the indicator is connected via copper wires. The voltmeter reads **19.787 mV**. Verify whether this reading is consistent with the **Law of Intermediate Metals** and compute the **true hot-junction temperature** if the indicator's internal CJC measures 30 °C, given $E_J(30\,°C, 0\,°C) = 1.527$ mV. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:

**1. Law of Homogeneous Circuit (2 marks):**
- Statement: The net EMF depends only on the temperatures of the two junctions, not on the temperature distribution along the wires.
- **Practical Significance:** Thermocouple leads can be routed through any environment (e.g., furnace walls) without generating spurious EMFs from temperature gradients along the wire.

**2. Law of Intermediate Metals (2.5 marks):**
- Statement: Inserting a third metal C into the circuit does not change the net EMF, provided the two new junctions are at the same temperature.
- **Mathematical Form:** $E_{AB} = E_{AC} + E_{CB}$ (at same $T_1, T_2$)
- **Proof:** Already provided in Section 3.3 above. The key insight is that the $S_C$ terms cancel in the combined integral.
- **Practical Significance:** Justifies the use of **solder joints, terminal blocks, copper extension wires, and connector pins** in thermocouple circuits.

**3. Law of Intermediate Temperatures (2.5 marks):**
- Statement: $E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)$
- **Proof:** Already provided in Section 3.4 above by splitting the integral at $T_2$.
- **Practical Significance:** Foundation of **cold-junction compensation (CJC)**. Reference tables are at 0 °C, but actual cold junctions are at ambient; this law allows us to algebraically compensate for the difference.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:

**Given Data:**
- Type J thermocouple (Iron–Constantan)
- Hot junction: 400 °C; Cold junction: 30 °C
- Voltmeter reading: 19.787 mV
- Standard EMF value: $E_J(30\,°C, 0\,°C) = 1.527$ mV
- Copper extension wires are used (third metal inserted)

**Step 1 — Apply the Law of Intermediate Metals:**
Since copper is introduced as the third metal at the terminal block, both new junctions (Iron–Copper and Constantan–Copper) are at the **same temperature (30 °C)**. Therefore, the voltmeter reading **must equal** the EMF of the original J-type thermocouple between 400 °C and 30 °C.

**Step 2 — Apply the Law of Intermediate Temperatures:**

$$
E_{\text{meas}} = E_J(T_h, 0\,°C) - E_J(T_c, 0\,°C)
$$

$$
19.787\, \text{mV} = E_J(400\,°C, 0\,°C) - 1.527\, \text{mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify by checking the standard table value:**

From the J-type ITS-90 table:

$$
E_J(400\,°C, 0\,°C) = 21.846\, \text{mV}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the expected voltmeter reading:**

$$
E_{\text{expected}} = 21.846 - 1.527 = 20.319\, \text{mV}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare and conclude:**

The actual voltmeter reading is **19.787 mV**, which differs from the expected **20.319 mV** by approximately **0.532 mV**. This discrepancy (about 2.6%) could be due to:
- Instrument calibration error
- Cold-junction temperature inaccuracy
- Lead-wire resistance effects
- Reference junction drift

**Step 6 — Calculate the corrected hot-junction temperature assuming the measured EMF is correct:**

$$
E_J(T_h, 0\,°C) = 19.787 + 1.527 = 21.314\, \text{mV}
$$

From the J-type table:
- At 390 °C: $E = 21.298$ mV (approximately)
- At 391 °C: $E \approx 21.347$ mV

By interpolation, the corrected hot-junction temperature is:

$$
T_h \approx 390.4\,°C
$$

**Conclusion:** The Law of Intermediate Metals is verified to hold (the EMF is independent of the copper extension), and the true hot-junction temperature is **approximately 390 °C**, slightly lower than the nominal 400 °C.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Universal for Module 1 Thermoelectric Problems):**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention:** Always state explicitly which junction is HOT and which is COLD, and adopt the convention $E_{AB} = E(T_{\text{hot}}) - E(T_{\text{cold}})$.
> 2. **Ignoring the Law of Intermediate Metals:** When extension wires or connectors are used, students often introduce fictitious EMFs. The law **proves** that no such EMF exists if the connector is isothermal.
> 3. **Using the wrong table:** Different thermocouple types (K, J, T, R, S, B) have **different polynomial coefficients**. Mixing up tables leads to massive errors. Always cite the type clearly.
> 4. **Mixing up Peltier and Thomson:** The Peltier effect is at a **junction**; the Thomson effect is in a **single conductor**. KTU questions frequently test this distinction.
> 5. **Neglecting units in Seebeck coefficient:** Always state the unit (μV/°C or V/K) when quoting the coefficient.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 – Thermoelectric Principles):**

- ⭐ **Seebeck Effect:** Heat → EMF; equation $E_{AB} = \int (S_B - S_A)\, dT$; discovered 1821.
- ⭐ **Peltier Effect:** Current → Heat at junction; equation $Q = \pi_{AB} \cdot I$; discovered 1834; reversible.
- ⭐ **Thomson Effect:** Current + ΔT → Heat in single conductor; equation $Q = \sigma \cdot I \cdot \Delta T$; discovered 1856.
- ⭐ **Kelvin Relation 1:** $\pi_{AB} = T \cdot S_{AB}$ (Peltier–Seebeck coupling).
- ⭐ **Kelvin Relation 2:** $\dfrac{dS_{AB}}{dT} = \dfrac{\sigma_B - \sigma_A}{T}$ (Thomson–Seebeck coupling).
- ⭐ **Law 1 (Homogeneous Circuit):** EMF independent of wire length and temperature profile.
- ⭐ **Law 2 (Intermediate Metals):** Third metal OK if both new junctions isothermal; $E_{AB} = E_{AC} + E_{CB}$.
- ⭐ **Law 3 (Intermediate Temperatures):** $E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)$.
- ⭐ **Typical Seebeck Coefficients:** Type K ≈ 40 μV/°C; Type J ≈ 50 μV/°C; Type S ≈ 6–10 μV/°C.
- ⭐ **Reference Standard:** All NIST EMF tables assume **0 °C cold-junction reference**.
- ⭐ **Practical Range:** Thermocouples cover **−200 °C to 2300 °C** depending on type.
- ⭐ **KTU Key Insight:** The three laws together justify the use of **extension wires, connectors, and reference-junction compensation** in real instruments.

**Key Formulas at a Glance (Quick-Fire Revision):**

$$
E_{AB} = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} (S_B - S_A)\, dT
$$

$$
Q_{\text{Peltier}} = \pi_{AB} \cdot I
$$

$$
Q_{\text{Thomson}} = \sigma \cdot I \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\pi_{AB} = T \cdot S_{AB}
$$

$$
\frac{dS_{AB}}{dT} = \frac{\sigma_B - \sigma_A}{T}
$$

**Cross-Connections to Other Modules:**
- **Module 2 (Signal Conditioning):** Thermocouple outputs (mV) require **amplification** and **linearization** using look-up tables.
- **Module 3 (ADC and Microcontrollers):** EMF is digitized, and **polynomial evaluation** of the inverse ITS-90 function gives temperature.
- **Module 4 (Industrial Standards):** ISA, IEC 60584, and NIST mandate the use of standardized calibration curves.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermocouple Output Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermocouple Output Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **thermocouple** is a passive, rugged, and widely used temperature-sensing transducer that converts a temperature difference between two junctions of dissimilar conductors into a measurable electromotive force (EMF), based on the **Seebeck Effect**. The junction physically exposed to the unknown/process temperature is called the **Hot (Measuring) Junction (HJ)**, while the other junction maintained at a known, stable temperature is called the **Cold (Reference) Junction (CJ)**. The net DC millivolt output is a continuous, near-monotonic function of the temperature difference $(T_{HJ} - T_{CJ})$.

$$E_{AB}(T_{HJ}, T_{CJ}) = \int_{T_{CJ}}^{T_{HJ}} \big(S_A(T) - S_B(T)\big)\, dT$$

where $S_A(T)$ and $S_B(T)$ are the **Seebeck coefficients** (in $\mu V/^{\circ}C$) of materials A and B as functions of temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** "A thermocouple is a thermoelectric transducer consisting of two dissimilar thermoelements joined at one end (hot junction); the EMF generated is proportional to the temperature difference between the hot and reference junctions, in accordance with the Seebeck Effect."

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you hold two different metal wires (say, copper and iron) and twist one end of each together. Now heat the twisted end with a match while keeping the other open ends in a glass of ice water. Connect a sensitive voltmeter to the open ends — you will see a tiny but stable DC voltage.

What you have just built is a **thermocouple**.

- The **flame** is the *Hot Junction* — the unknown temperature $T_{HJ}$.
- The **ice water** is the *Cold Junction* — the *known reference* $T_{CJ}$.
- The **voltmeter reading** is the *EMF* that encodes the temperature difference.

Think of the thermocouple as a **"thermal battery"**: instead of chemical energy producing voltage, it is the *flow of electrons caused by a temperature gradient along two different metals* that produces the voltage. The bigger the temperature difference, the larger the EMF — roughly speaking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant to Memorize:** The Seebeck coefficient of a standard **K-type (Chromel-Alumel)** thermocouple is approximately **$41 \mu V/^{\circ}C$** near room temperature. Typical full-scale outputs are in the **millivolt (mV) range** — usually **0 mV to 50 mV**.

---

### 1.3 KTU-Style Classification of Thermoelectric Effects

For full marks in any KTU Part B question, you should know the three foundational thermoelectric phenomena. Only the first is operational in a thermocouple; the other two are reverse/companion effects.

| # | Effect | Discovery | Direction | KTU Significance |
|---|--------|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Seebeck Effect** | Thomas Seebeck, 1821 | Heat → EMF | The principle behind thermocouple **measurement** |
| 2 | **Peltier Effect** | Jean Peltier, 1834 | Current → Heat/Cooling | Basis of thermoelectric **coolers** |
| 3 | **Thomson Effect** | William Thomson, 1851 | Current + Temp. gradient → Heat | Explains small corrections in thermocouple wire |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Memory Trick:** *"Seebeck Senses Heat → Voltage, Peltier Pumps Voltage → Heat, Thomson Thinks about both."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Why Does a Thermocouple Produce EMF?

When two dissimilar metals A and B are joined at the hot junction at temperature $T_{HJ}$ and the open ends (CJ) are at $T_{CJ}$, three microscopic phenomena simultaneously occur along each wire:

1. **Charge Carrier Diffusion:** Free electrons in the hotter end gain kinetic energy and diffuse toward the colder end, producing an electric potential.
2. **Contact (Peltier) EMF:** Because the Fermi levels of A and B differ at the HJ, electrons crossing the junction gain or lose energy, producing a contact potential.
3. **Thomson EMF:** A temperature gradient along a single uniform wire produces a small voltage proportional to the local Thomson coefficient.

When you sum the EMF contributions around the closed loop A → HJ → B → CJ → A, only the **net Seebeck EMF** between the two junctions survives, yielding the integral form shown in Section 1.1.

---

### 2.2 Fundamental Laws of Thermocouples (KTU High-Yield)

The following three laws form the analytical backbone of every KTU numerical on thermocouples. **Memorize the names verbatim** — examiners award marks for proper terminology.

#### Law 1 — Law of Homogeneous Circuit
> *"An EMF generated in a thermocouple circuit depends only on the materials and the temperatures of the two junctions; it is independent of the temperature distribution along the wires and of the cross-section / length of the thermoelements."*

**Implication:** We can extend the thermocouple to the CJ using *extension/compensating leads* without changing the EMF.

#### Law 2 — Law of Intermediate Metals
> *"Inserting a third metal C into either thermoelement (A or B) does not change the net EMF of the circuit, provided both new junctions introduced by C are held at the same temperature."*

**Implication:** We can introduce a measuring instrument, terminal blocks, or solder joints without error — a huge practical advantage.

#### Law 3 — Law of Intermediate (Successive) Temperatures
> *"The EMF generated by a thermocouple with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ is equal to the algebraic sum of EMFs that would be generated by thermocouples with junctions at $T_1$–$T_2$ and $T_2$–$T_3$."*

$$E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)$$

**Implication:** This is the foundation of **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)** and reference-table corrections.

---

### 2.3 Cold Junction Compensation (CJC) — The Heart of the Topic

A thermocouple *only* measures a **temperature difference**, not an absolute temperature. The standard reference tables (IPTS-68 / ITS-90) tabulate $E(T, 0^{\circ}C)$. So if the cold junction is *not* at $0^{\circ}C$, you must correct.

**The Reference-Junction Correction Equation (Law 3 applied):**

Let $T_{HJ}$ = true hot-junction temperature, $T_{CJ}$ = actual cold-junction temperature, $T_{REF} = 0^{\circ}C$.

$$E_{MEAS} = E(T_{HJ}, T_{CJ}) = E(T_{HJ}, T_{REF}) - E(T_{CJ}, T_{REF})$$

Therefore the corrected (true) EMF referred to $0^{\circ}C$ is:

$$E_{TRUE}(T_{HJ}, 0^{\circ}C) = E_{MEAS} + E(T_{CJ}, 0^{\circ}C)$$

In words: **add the tabulated EMF corresponding to the actual CJ temperature to the measured EMF** to get the corrected EMF, which you then look up in the standard tables to find $T_{HJ}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Juggler:** Most Part B problems state "the CJ is at $T_X^{\circ}C$". You must (i) convert $T_X$ into mV from the thermocouple table, (ii) add it to the measured mV, (iii) convert the total back into ${}^{\circ}C$.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Seebeck EMF (integral form) | $E = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} \big(S_A(T) - S_B(T)\big)\, dT$ | mV | A, B are the two thermoelements |
| 2 | Approx. linear form | $E \approx (S_A - S_B)\,\Delta T$ | mV | Valid over small $\Delta T$ only |
| 3 | Law of Intermediate Temperatures | $E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$ | mV | Foundation of CJC |
| 4 | Reference-junction correction | $E_{TRUE} = E_{MEAS} + E(T_{CJ}, 0^{\circ}C)$ | mV | Add CJ EMF, then look up tables |
| 5 | K-type sensitivity (avg) | $S \approx 41\ \mu V/^{\circ}C$ | $\mu V/^{\circ}C$ | Near $0^{\circ}C$–$100^{\circ}C$ |
| 6 | T-type sensitivity (avg) | $S \approx 40\ \mu V/^{\circ}C$ | $\mu V/^{\circ}C$ | Best for cryogenics |
| 7 | J-type sensitivity (avg) | $S \approx 50\ \mu V/^{\circ}C$ | $\mu V/^{\circ}C$ | Iron-Constantan |
| 8 | S-type sensitivity (avg) | $S \approx 6\ \mu V/^{\circ}C$ | $\mu V/^{\circ}C$ | Pt-Pt/Rh, high-temp |
| 9 | Open-circuit voltage of standard thermocouple | $E \approx 0\ \text{to}\ 50$ | mV | Full industrial range |
| 10 | Thermoelectric Sensitivity (K-type, $0$–$1000^{\circ}C$) | $S_{avg} \approx 40\ \mu V/^{\circ}C$ | $\mu V/^{\circ}C$ | Use this in Part B numerics |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of Thermocouple Output Measurement

- **Industrial Furnaces & Boilers:** K-type and S-type thermocouples monitor combustion temperatures in power plants.
- **Aerospace Engine Testing:** Bare-wire thermocouples measure turbine blade temperatures exceeding $1200^{\circ}C$.
- **Petrochemical Reactors:** Sheathed thermocouples in thermowells provide explosion-proof temperature monitoring.
- **Cryogenics & LNG Storage:** T-type (copper-constantan) thermocouples operate down to $-200^{\circ}C$.
- **HVAC & Building Automation:** Low-cost K-type probes with CJC electronics inside the transmitter head.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reference-Junction Correction Formula (from Law of Intermediate Temperatures)

**Goal:** Show that if the cold junction sits at $T_{CJ}$ instead of $0^{\circ}C$, the true temperature $T_{HJ}$ can be recovered from the measured EMF using a single tabulated correction.

**Step 1 — Define the measured EMF.** The voltmeter reads the EMF generated between the two junctions at $T_{HJ}$ and $T_{CJ}$:

$$E_{MEAS} = E(T_{HJ},\, T_{CJ})$$

**Step 2 — Introduce an intermediate temperature $T_{REF} = 0^{\circ}C$ between the two actual junctions.** By the Law of Intermediate Temperatures:

$$E(T_{HJ},\, 0) = E(T_{HJ},\, T_{CJ}) + E(T_{CJ},\, 0)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the EMF that would have been read if the CJ were at $0^{\circ}C$.** This is what the standard thermocouple tables tabulate:

$$E(T_{HJ},\, 0) = E_{MEAS} + E(T_{CJ},\, 0)$$

**Step 4 — Look up $T_{HJ}$ from the standard tables** using the value $E(T_{HJ}, 0)$. This is the *true* hot-junction temperature in ${}^{\circ}C$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Used:** *Measured mV + tabulated mV of the CJ = Total mV referred to 0 °C → Look up in standard table to get the true temperature.*

---

### 3.2 Worked KTU-Style Numerical (Full Step-by-Step Solution)

**Problem Statement:**
A K-type (Chromel-Alumel) thermocouple is used to measure the temperature of a furnace. The cold junction is held at $T_{CJ} = 40^{\circ}C$ (not at $0^{\circ}C$). The voltmeter across the CJ reads $E_{MEAS} = 20.643$ mV. From the standard K-type reference tables (ITS-90):

- $E_{K}(40^{\circ}C,\, 0^{\circ}C) = 1.612$ mV
- $E_{K}(500^{\circ}C,\, 0^{\circ}C) = 20.643$ mV

**Find the true furnace (hot-junction) temperature.**

---

**Step 1 — Write the reference-junction correction equation:**

$$E_{TRUE}(T_{HJ},\, 0) = E_{MEAS} + E_{K}(T_{CJ},\, 0)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{TRUE}(T_{HJ},\, 0) &= 20.643\ \text{mV} + 1.612\ \text{mV} \\
&= 22.255\ \text{mV}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Now look up the K-type table for the EMF 22.255 mV.** From the standard K-type table (ITS-90):

- $E_{K}(540^{\circ}C,\, 0^{\circ}C) = 22.255$ mV (tabulated)

**Step 4 — Therefore, the true furnace temperature is:**

$$T_{HJ} = 540^{\circ}C$$

---

**Conversion Logic Recap Table:**

| Step | Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|------|----------|--------|-------|
| 1 | Measured EMF | $E_{MEAS}$ | $20.643$ mV |
| 2 | Cold junction tabulated EMF | $E_{K}(40^{\circ}C, 0^{\circ}C)$ | $+1.612$ mV |
| 3 | Corrected EMF referred to $0^{\circ}C$ | $E_{TRUE}$ | $22.255$ mV |
| 4 | True hot-junction temperature | $T_{HJ}$ | $540^{\circ}C$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Why we add, not subtract:** The CJ at $40^{\circ}C$ produces a *positive* mV of its own. Without correction, this mV would have been *added to* the furnace mV had the CJ been at $0^{\circ}C$. So we *add* the CJ EMF back to recover the original $0^{\circ}C$-based reading.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Comparison of Standard Thermocouples

| ANSI Code | + Leg | − Leg | Sensitivity (avg, $\mu V/^{\circ}C$) | Range (${}^{\circ}C$) | Typical Application |
|-----------|-------|-------|--------------------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **J** | Iron | Constantan | $50$ | $-210$ to $760$ | Plastics, food |
| **K** | Chromel | Alumel | $41$ | $-270$ to $1370$ | General purpose, furnaces |
| **T** | Copper | Constantan | $40$ | $-270$ to $400$ | Cryogenics, labs |
| **E** | Chromel | Constantan | $60$ | $-270$ to $870$ | Highest sensitivity |
| **R** | Pt-13%Rh | Pure Pt | $6$ | $-50$ to $1480$ | High-temp labs |
| **S** | Pt-10%Rh | Pure Pt | $6$ | $-50$ to $1480$ | Industry standard high-temp |
| **B** | Pt-30%Rh | Pt-6%Rh | $4$ | $0$ to $1700$ | Glass, steel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trap:** Type B thermocouple output is essentially **0 mV below $50^{\circ}C$** because both legs are platinum-rhodium alloys with very similar Seebeck coefficients — this is why its cold-junction correction is *negligible* in many practical cases.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Thermocouple Measurement — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Junction T_HJ] -->|Seebeck EMF| B[Thermocouple Wires A and B]
    B --> C[Extension or Compensating Leads]
    C --> D[Cold Junction T_CJ at Terminal Block]
    D --> E[Cold Junction Compensation IC e.g. AD594]
    E --> F[Amplifier and Linearization Circuit]
    F --> G[ADC]
    G --> H[Microcontroller with Lookup Table]
    H --> I[Display or 4-20 mA Output]
    D -.Measured mV.-> J[Voltmeter]
    K[RTD or Semiconductor Sensor at Terminal Block] -.Auxiliary T_CJ reading.-> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Process temperature flows left to right: heat → EMF → amplified signal → digital temperature in ${}^{\circ}C$ → display. The dashed lines represent *measurement feedback* — the auxiliary RTD/sensor reads the actual CJ temperature so the compensation IC can compute $E(T_{CJ}, 0)$ in real time.

---

### 4.2 Cold Junction Compensation Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start] --> P2[Measure E_MEAS across Cold Junction]
    P3[Sense T_CJ via on-board sensor] --> P4[Lookup E_CJ in thermocouple table]
    P2 --> P5[Compute E_TRUE equals E_MEAS plus E_CJ]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Lookup T_HJ corresponding to E_TRUE]
    P6 --> P7[Display or Transmit T_HJ]
    P7 --> P8[Stop]
```

---

### 4.3 Thermocouple Laws — Conceptual Summary Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Thermocouple Laws] --> L1[Law 1 Homogeneous Circuit]
    L1 --> L1a[EMF independent of wire shape and length]
    L --> L2[Law 2 Intermediate Metals]
    L2 --> L2a[Third metal C ok if its two ends at same T]
    L --> L3[Law 3 Intermediate Temperatures]
    L3 --> L3a[E T1 T3 equals E T1 T2 plus E T2 T3]
    L3 --> L3b[Foundation of Cold Junction Compensation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the Seebeck Effect. Explain how it forms the working principle of a thermocouple.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Seebeck Effect** is the direct conversion of a temperature difference into an electromotive force (EMF) at the junction of two dissimilar conductors. Discovered by Thomas Johann Seebeck in 1821, it states that when two different metals A and B are joined at two junctions held at different temperatures $T_H$ and $T_C$, a net DC EMF $E_{AB}$ circulates in the closed loop.

In a thermocouple:
- The hot junction at $T_H$ is the *measuring* junction exposed to the unknown process temperature.
- The cold junction at $T_C$ is maintained at a known reference temperature.
- The generated EMF is a near-monotonic function of $\Delta T = T_H - T_C$.
- For small ranges, $E_{AB} \approx (S_A - S_B)\,\Delta T$, where $S_A, S_B$ are the Seebeck coefficients of the two materials.
- This small mV-level EMF is then amplified, linearized, and converted to a temperature reading using standardized reference tables (ITS-90).

*(1 mark – correct definition; 1 mark – working explanation; 1 mark – equation + table reference.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State and explain the Law of Intermediate Metals in thermocoucles.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Law of Intermediate Metals** states that *introducing a third metal C into a thermocouple circuit does not change the net thermoelectric EMF, provided the two new junctions introduced by C are maintained at the same temperature.*

**Explanation:** Practically, this means we can insert:
- Solder joints,
- Terminal blocks,
- Connecting leads,
- Measuring instruments (voltmeter, amplifier),

…between the two thermoelements without introducing any error in the measured EMF, as long as those insertion points are isothermal (at the same temperature). This is the reason thermocouples can be extended to remote instrumentation using terminal blocks and connectors.

*(1 mark – statement; 1 mark – practical implication; 1 mark – one valid example.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a thermocouple. Define the terms *Hot Junction*, *Cold Junction*, and *Seebeck Coefficient*. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A K-type thermocouple is used with its cold junction at $30^{\circ}C$. The voltmeter reads $11.357$ mV. Using the reference data:
$E_K(30^{\circ}C, 0^{\circ}C) = 1.203$ mV and $E_K(290^{\circ}C, 0^{\circ}C) = 11.357$ mV,
find the true hot-junction temperature. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution

##### (a) Construction and Working \[7 Marks\]

**Construction:**
A thermocouple consists of two dissimilar thermoelements (wires), labelled A and B, joined at one end by welding, brazing, or twisting. The joined end is the **Hot Junction (HJ)** placed inside a protective sheath (stainless steel or Inconel) for industrial use. The free ends form the **Cold Junction (CJ)**, terminated at a terminal block often called the *reference junction*.

**Working:**
When HJ is exposed to the unknown temperature $T_H$ and CJ is held at $T_C$ (often $0^{\circ}C$ in tabulated data, or a known compensated value in modern transmitters):

1. Charge carrier diffusion along each wire produces a temperature-dependent voltage.
2. Contact EMF arises at the HJ due to differing Fermi levels of A and B.
3. The net loop EMF is $E_{AB}(T_H, T_C) = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} (S_A - S_B)\, dT$.
4. This millivolt signal is amplified and converted to a digital temperature.

**Definitions:**
- **Hot Junction:** The measuring junction exposed to the process; the temperature of interest is $T_H$.
- **Cold Junction (Reference Junction):** The second junction held at a known, stable temperature $T_C$.
- **Seebeck Coefficient $S$:** The EMF generated per unit temperature difference ($\mu V/^{\circ}C$) for a single material relative to platinum.

**Schematic (text):**

$$
\text{[Process at } T_H\text{]} \;\to\; \bullet \text{HJ} \;\to\; \text{Wire A} \;\to\; \text{Terminal Block at } T_C \;\to\; \text{Voltmeter} \;\to\; \text{Wire B} \;\to\; \text{HJ}
$$

##### (b) Numerical — Reference Junction Correction \[7 Marks\]

**Step 1 — Apply the correction formula:**

$$E_{TRUE}(T_H, 0) = E_{MEAS} + E_K(T_{CJ}, 0)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{TRUE} &= 11.357\ \text{mV} + 1.203\ \text{mV} \\
&= 12.560\ \text{mV}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Look up the K-type table for 12.560 mV.** The standard table gives:

$$E_K(320^{\circ}C,\, 0^{\circ}C) = 12.560\ \text{mV (tabulated)}$$

**Step 4 — True hot-junction temperature:**

$$\boxed{T_H = 320^{\circ}C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **subtract** instead of adding the CJ EMF — this gives a *lower* (wrong) temperature. **Always ADD.**
> - Students forget to look up the *corrected* mV in the table and report the cold-junction temperature. **Final answer must be in ${}^{\circ}C$.**
> - Do not assume CJ EMF is negligible — even a $10^{\circ}C$ warm terminal block introduces roughly **0.4 mV error**, which can translate to **~10 °C** in the final reading for a K-type thermocouple.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the three fundamental laws of thermocouples. Why is the Law of Intermediate Temperatures critical for cold junction compensation? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A J-type thermocouple has its cold junction held at $50^{\circ}C$. The instrument reads $6.359$ mV. From standard tables:
$E_J(50^{\circ}C, 0^{\circ}C) = 2.585$ mV and $E_J(150^{\circ}C, 0^{\circ}C) = 6.359$ mV.
Determine the true process temperature. State two advantages and two disadvantages of J-type thermocouples. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution

##### (a) Three Laws of Thermocouples \[7 Marks\]

1. **Law of Homogeneous Circuit (1.5 marks):**
   *"The EMF generated in a thermocouple circuit depends only on the materials of the thermoelements and the temperatures of the two junctions — not on the temperature distribution along the wires, their length, or cross-section."*
   Implication: extension leads can be long without changing the reading.

2. **Law of Intermediate Metals (1.5 marks):**
   *"Inserting a third metal C anywhere in the circuit does not change the net EMF, provided its two new junctions are at the same temperature."*
   Implication: terminal blocks, connectors, and the measuring instrument may be inserted without introducing error.

3. **Law of Intermediate Temperatures (2 marks):**
   *"The EMF generated between junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ equals the sum of EMFs generated between $(T_1, T_2)$ and $(T_2, T_3)$."*
   $E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$

**Why Law 3 is critical for CJC (2 marks):**
Because standard tables list EMF relative to a $0^{\circ}C$ reference, but the actual cold junction is at some other temperature $T_{CJ}$. By setting $T_1 = T_H$, $T_2 = T_{CJ}$, $T_3 = 0$, we get $E(T_H, 0) = E(T_H, T_{CJ}) + E(T_{CJ}, 0)$, i.e., we can algebraically recover the $0^{\circ}C$-referred EMF by adding the tabulated CJ EMF. Without Law 3, CJC would be impossible analytically.

##### (b) Numerical — J-type Thermocouple \[7 Marks\]

**Step 1 — Apply correction:**

$$E_{TRUE}(T_H, 0) = E_{MEAS} + E_J(T_{CJ}, 0)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{TRUE} &= 6.359\ \text{mV} + 2.585\ \text{mV} \\
&= 8.944\ \text{mV}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Look up J-type table for 8.944 mV:**

$$E_J(200^{\circ}C, 0^{\circ}C) = 8.944\ \text{mV (tabulated)}$$

**Step 4 — True process temperature:**

$$\boxed{T_H = 200^{\circ}C}$$

**Advantages of J-type (1 mark):**
- Higher Seebeck coefficient ($\sim 50\,\mu V/^{\circ}C$) than K-type → better sensitivity and resolution.
- Lower cost due to use of iron instead of nickel-based Chromel.

**Disadvantages of J-type (1 mark):**
- Iron leg oxidizes rapidly above $540^{\circ}C$, limiting useful range.
- Susceptible to corrosion in reducing atmospheres; not suitable for sulfurous environments.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Mixing up thermocouple codes (e.g., using K-type tables for a J-type junction) is the **single most common KTU mistake** — table values differ by tens of millivolts at high temperatures.
> - Forgetting to mention the **assumed reference temperature** ($0^{\circ}C$) when stating the tabulated EMFs costs 1 mark in Part B.
> - Stating advantages without pairing them with a matching disadvantage loses the comparison marks — examiners look for **balanced evaluation**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Thermocouple Output Measurement**
>
> - **Core principle:** Seebeck Effect → EMF $\propto \Delta T$ between Hot and Cold junctions.
> - **Three thermoelectric effects to know:** Seebeck (Heat → EMF), Peltier (Current → Heat), Thomson (Current + gradient → Heat).
> - **Three Laws (must quote names + equations):**
>   1. Homogeneous Circuit.
>   2. Intermediate Metals.
>   3. Intermediate Temperatures: $E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$.
> - **Master Correction Formula:** $E_{TRUE}(T_H, 0) = E_{MEAS} + E(T_{CJ}, 0)$. **Always ADD the CJ tabulated EMF** — never subtract.
> - **Workflow for any CJC numerical:** Read $E_{MEAS}$ → Find $E(T_{CJ}, 0)$ from table → **Add** → Look up total in table → Report $T_H$.
> - **Typical output range:** $0$ to $\sim 50$ mV; sensitivities in tens of $\mu V/^{\circ}C$.
> - **Highest sensitivity:** E-type ($\sim 60\,\mu V/^{\circ}C$); **Lowest:** B-type ($\sim 4\,\mu V/^{\circ}C$).
> - **Most common industrial grade:** K-type (Chromel-Alumel), range $-270^{\circ}C$ to $1370^{\circ}C$.
> - **Common KTU pitfalls to avoid:**
>   * Subtracting instead of adding the CJ EMF.
>   * Mixing up thermocouple table codes.
>   * Forgetting the third law's role in justifying CJC.
>   * Reporting the CJ temperature instead of the corrected $T_H$.
> - **Practical CJC in modern instruments:** Done electronically using a semiconductor or RTD sensor at the terminal block and a compensation IC (e.g., AD594, AD595, MAX31855) that performs the correction automatically.
> - **ITS-90 reference standard:** All modern thermocouple tables (K, J, T, E, R, S, B, N) are defined relative to $0^{\circ}C$ with platinum as the reference electrode.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Methods: Radiation and Optical Pyrometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Radiation Methods: Radiation and Optical Pyrometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Radiation Pyrometry — Formal Definition

**Radiation Pyrometry** is a class of non-contact temperature measurement techniques that infer the surface temperature of a body by detecting the **thermal electromagnetic radiation** it spontaneously emits due to its temperature (i.e., without any external excitation or physical contact with the target).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Radiation pyrometers measure the total radiant energy emitted by a hot body across a broad band of wavelengths (typically in the infrared and visible regions of the electromagnetic spectrum). The output is functionally related to the **fourth power of the absolute temperature** of the target, following the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**.

These devices are fundamentally important when:
- The target is **moving** (e.g., rotating rollers, molten metal streams).
- The target is **chemically reactive**, electrically charged, or **physically inaccessible**.
- Temperatures **exceed the range of thermocouples** (typically > 1300 °C, up to 3500 °C).

### 1.2 Optical Pyrometry — Formal Definition

**Optical Pyrometry** is a refined subclass of radiation pyrometry in which the measurement is performed by **visually comparing the brightness (luminous intensity) of the target's emitted radiation** at a **single narrow wavelength band** against a calibrated internal reference source. The classical implementation is the **Disappearing Filament Optical Pyrometer (DFOP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A radiation (total) pyrometer receives energy from **all wavelengths** emitted by the body. An optical (brightness) pyrometer senses only the radiation emitted within a **narrow bandpass filter** (typically around 0.65 µm red light), and is matched to the **human eye's sensitivity curve**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine standing at a safe distance from a glowing iron rod being heated by a blacksmith:

- The **color of the glow** tells you how hot it is — a faint cherry red is cooler than a bright yellow-white. This is the principle behind **optical pyrometry** (brightness comparison at a chosen color).
- The **overall heat you feel on your face** from across the room is from invisible infrared radiation — this is the principle behind **total radiation pyrometry**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Box — "The Sun and the Shadow":** A black asphalt road on a hot day feels scorching because it absorbs and re-emits radiation across all wavelengths. A mirror reflects most radiation and feels cool. The body that **emits** radiation proportional to its temperature behaves like the asphalt — that is the **blackbody** concept central to pyrometry.

### 1.4 Core Physical Constants (Bolded for Memorization)

| Symbol | Constant | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\sigma$ | **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** | $5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ | W·m$^{-2}$·K$^{-4}$ |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ | m·s$^{-1}$ |
| $h$ | **Planck's constant** | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| $k$ | **Boltzmann's constant** | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | J·K$^{-1}$ |
| $b$ | **Wien's displacement constant** | $2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | m·K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** The constant $\sigma$ is numerically derived from $h$, $c$, and $k$. Its unit (W·m$^{-2}$·K$^{-4}$) directly telegraphs that radiated power scales with $T^4$. This is the single most-tested fact in radiation pyrometry numerical problems.

### 1.5 Blackbody, Greybody, and Emissivity — Foundational Concepts

A **blackbody** is a hypothetical perfect emitter that absorbs **all incident radiation** and re-emits energy at the maximum possible rate for its temperature. Its emissivity $\varepsilon = 1$.

A **real body** (greybody) emits only a fraction of blackbody radiation, characterized by its **emissivity $\varepsilon$**, where $0 < \varepsilon < 1$.

$$E_{\text{real}} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma T^{4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emissivity is the single largest source of error in industrial radiation pyrometry.** Polished metals ($\varepsilon \approx 0.05$–$0.2$) are notoriously difficult to measure accurately with radiation pyrometers unless corrected for emissivity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Thermal Radiation

#### Pillar 1 — Planck's Law of Blackbody Radiation

Planck's law gives the **spectral emissive power** $E_{\lambda}$ (W·m$^{-2}$·µm$^{-1}$) of a blackbody at absolute temperature $T$ and wavelength $\lambda$:

$$E_{\lambda b}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_{1}}{\lambda^{5} \left[ \exp\!\left( \dfrac{C_{2}}{\lambda T} \right) - 1 \right]}$$

where:
- $C_{1} = 2\pi h c^{2} = 3.7415 \times 10^{8}$ W·µm$^{4}$·m$^{-2}$
- $C_{2} = \dfrac{h c}{k} = 1.4388 \times 10^{4}$ µm·K

**Why it matters:** This is the *master equation* from which **all other radiation laws** (Wien, Rayleigh-Jeans, Stefan-Boltzmann, Wien's displacement) are derived by integration or differentiation.

#### Pillar 2 — Wien's Displacement Law

The wavelength at which a blackbody emits maximum radiation is **inversely proportional** to its absolute temperature:

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

**Engineering use:** Determines which detector (silicon photodiode, InGaAs, thermopile, HgCdTe) to select for a given temperature range. For example, a furnace at 1500 K peaks at $\lambda_{\max} \approx 1.93$ µm (near-IR).

#### Pillar 3 — Stefan–Boltzmann Law

The total energy emitted per unit area per unit time across **all wavelengths** (integration of Planck's law over $\lambda = 0 \to \infty$):

$$E_{b} = \sigma T^{4} \text{ W·m}^{-2}$$

**This is the foundational equation for total radiation pyrometers.**

#### Pillar 4 — Kirchhoff's Law

At thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a body equals its absorptivity: $\varepsilon_{\lambda} = \alpha_{\lambda}$. A good absorber is a good emitter. This is why **blackbody cavities** (the interior of furnaces) have $\varepsilon \approx 1$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Law / Equation | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use / Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Planck's Law | $E_{\lambda b} = \dfrac{C_{1}}{\lambda^{5}[\exp(C_{2}/\lambda T) - 1]}$ | Master spectral equation; basis of all optical pyrometers |
| 2 | Stefan–Boltzmann Law | $E_{b} = \sigma T^{4}$ | Total radiation pyrometer (thermopile-based) |
| 3 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{\max} T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | Selecting detector wavelength band |
| 4 | Kirchhoff's Law | $\varepsilon_{\lambda} = \alpha_{\lambda}$ | Emissivity correction in non-blackbody targets |
| 5 | Real-body radiation | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | Greybody correction |
| 6 | Brightness temperature relation | $\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_{b}} = \dfrac{\lambda}{C_{2}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{\lambda}}\right)$ | Optical pyrometer emissivity correction |
| 7 | Effective wavelength relation | $\varepsilon_{\lambda} = \dfrac{E_{\lambda}}{E_{\lambda b}}$ | Spectral emissivity definition |
| 8 | Narrow-band (monochromatic) | $E_{\lambda} = \varepsilon_{\lambda} \cdot C_{1} \lambda^{-5} e^{-C_{2}/\lambda T}$ | Optical pyrometer fundamental relation (Wien approx.) |
| 9 | Radiance temperature | $T = \left[ \dfrac{E_{\text{measured}}}{\varepsilon \sigma} \right]^{1/4}$ | Total radiation pyrometer corrected reading |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** For optical pyrometers using a **red filter near 0.65 µm**, the **Wien approximation** $\exp(C_{2}/\lambda T) \gg 1$ holds for $T < 3000$ K. This simplifies Planck's law to $E_{\lambda b} \approx C_{1} \lambda^{-5} \exp(-C_{2}/\lambda T)$, and after taking logarithms, gives a **linear** relationship between $\ln E_{\lambda b}$ and $1/T$.

### 2.3 Operational Steps — How a Total Radiation Pyrometer Works

1. **Collection:** A concave mirror or lens collects radiation from the target and focuses it on a thermal detector (thermopile, bolometer, or thermistor).
2. **Filtering (optional):** Some instruments use a sapphire or quartz window that passes IR while blocking visible light.
3. **Detection:** The absorbed radiation raises the detector's temperature, generating a small EMF (thermopile) or resistance change (bolometer).
4. **Signal Conditioning:** The millivolt output is amplified, linearized (since it follows $T^{4}$), and displayed.
5. **Emissivity Compensation:** The user inputs the target's $\varepsilon$, and the instrument corrects the displayed temperature.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Steel plants | Molten metal stream, ladle, tundish | 1500–1700 °C |
| Glass furnaces | Molten glass surface | 1200–1500 °C |
| Cement kilns | Burning zone | 1200–1400 °C |
| Semiconductor | Wafer processing (rapid thermal annealing) | 600–1200 °C |
| Power plants | Boiler tube and flame temperature | 800–1600 °C |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Stefan–Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law

**Goal:** Integrate $E_{\lambda b}(\lambda, T)$ over all wavelengths from $\lambda = 0$ to $\lambda = \infty$ to obtain total emissive power $E_{b}$.

**Step 1:** Set up the integral of spectral emissive power over the full spectrum:

$$E_{b} = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{\lambda b} \, d\lambda = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{C_{1}}{\lambda^{5} \left[ \exp\!\left( \dfrac{C_{2}}{\lambda T} \right) - 1 \right]} d\lambda$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $x = \dfrac{C_{2}}{\lambda T}$, so $d\lambda = -\dfrac{C_{2}}{T x^{2}} dx$. When $\lambda \to 0$, $x \to \infty$; when $\lambda \to \infty$, $x \to 0$. The limits flip and absorb the negative sign:

$$E_{b} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{C_{1}}{\left(\dfrac{C_{2}}{x T}\right)^{5} \left[ e^{x} - 1 \right]} \cdot \frac{C_{2}}{T x^{2}} \, dx$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the powers of $\lambda$:

$$\left( \frac{C_{2}}{x T} \right)^{5} = \frac{C_{2}^{5}}{x^{5} T^{5}}$$

Therefore:

$$E_{b} = \int_{0}^{\infty} C_{1} \cdot \frac{x^{5} T^{5}}{C_{2}^{5} (e^{x} - 1)} \cdot \frac{C_{2}}{T x^{2}} \, dx$$

**Step 4:** Combine the constants and powers of $x$:

$$E_{b} = \frac{C_{1} T^{5}}{C_{2}^{5}} \cdot C_{2} T^{-1} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx = \frac{C_{1} T^{4}}{C_{2}^{4}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the definite integral. Using the series expansion $\dfrac{1}{e^{x}-1} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} e^{-nx}$:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} dx = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \int_{0}^{\infty} x^{3} e^{-nx} dx$$

Each integral is a gamma function: $\int_{0}^{\infty} x^{3} e^{-nx} dx = \dfrac{3!}{n^{4}} = \dfrac{6}{n^{4}}$.

**Step 6:** The sum $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n^{4}} = \dfrac{\pi^{4}}{90}$ (a Riemann zeta function identity). Therefore:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} dx = 6 \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{90} = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute back:

$$E_{b} = \frac{C_{1} T^{4}}{C_{2}^{4}} \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} = \frac{\pi^{4} C_{1}}{15 C_{2}^{4}} \cdot T^{4}$$

**Step 8:** Define the Stefan–Boltzmann constant:

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}$$

(Note: the factor of 2 comes from the original $C_{1} = 2\pi h c^{2}$ accounting for both polarizations of light.)

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{E_{b} = \sigma T^{4}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law

**Goal:** Find $\lambda_{\max}$ that maximizes $E_{\lambda b}(\lambda, T)$.

**Step 1:** Differentiate Planck's law with respect to $\lambda$ and set to zero. Using the Wien approximation for the maximum (valid for the peak region):

$$\frac{d E_{\lambda b}}{d\lambda} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Using the Wien-approximated form $E_{\lambda b} \approx C_{1} \lambda^{-5} \exp(-C_{2}/\lambda T)$:

$$\frac{d E_{\lambda b}}{d\lambda} = C_{1} \left[ -5\lambda^{-6} \exp(-C_{2}/\lambda T) + \lambda^{-5} \cdot \frac{C_{2}}{\lambda^{2} T} \exp(-C_{2}/\lambda T) \right] = 0$$

**Step 3:** Factor out the common terms:

$$\exp(-C_{2}/\lambda T) \left[ -5\lambda^{-6} + \frac{C_{2}}{\lambda^{7} T} \right] = 0$$

The exponential is never zero, so:

$$\frac{C_{2}}{\lambda^{7} T} = \frac{5}{\lambda^{6}} \Rightarrow \frac{C_{2}}{\lambda T} = 5$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\lambda_{\max}$:

$$\lambda_{\max} T = \frac{C_{2}}{5} = \frac{h c}{5 k} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

This is **Wien's displacement constant** $b$. (The exact value using full Planck's law gives $4.9651\, kT/hc$, yielding essentially the same numerical constant.)

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Optical Pyrometer Calibration Equation

For an optical pyrometer using the **Wien approximation** at effective wavelength $\lambda$:

$$E_{\lambda b}(T) = C_{1} \lambda^{-5} \exp\!\left(-\frac{C_{2}}{\lambda T}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Take natural log of both sides:

$$\ln E_{\lambda b} = \ln(C_{1} \lambda^{-5}) - \frac{C_{2}}{\lambda T}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange into a linear form $\ln E_{\lambda b} = A - B/T$:

$$\boxed{\ln E_{\lambda b} = \underbrace{\ln(C_{1} \lambda^{-5})}_{A} - \underbrace{\frac{C_{2}}{\lambda}}_{B} \cdot \frac{1}{T}}$$

**Step 3:** This linear relationship is the foundation of the **two-point calibration** of optical pyrometers. Two known temperature standards (typically the freezing points of gold at 1337.58 K and copper at 1357.77 K, or simply a tungsten-strip lamp at certified temperatures) yield slope $B$ and intercept $A$.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Total Radiation Pyrometer

**Problem:** A total radiation pyrometer with a thermopile of responsivity $R = 0.4$ µV/W measures an EMF of $V = 12$ mV from a target of emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.8$. If the collecting lens has area $A = 10^{-4}$ m² and the optical geometry collects radiation from a solid angle such that the effective view factor is unity, find the target temperature.

**Step 1:** The power received by the detector:

$$P = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-3}}{0.4 \times 10^{-6}} = 30{,}000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Power emitted per unit area by the target:

$$E = \frac{P}{A \cdot \varepsilon} = \frac{30{,}000}{10^{-4} \times 0.8} = 3.75 \times 10^{8} \text{ W·m}^{-2}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Stefan–Boltzmann law:

$$T = \left( \frac{E}{\sigma} \right)^{1/4} = \left( \frac{3.75 \times 10^{8}}{5.670 \times 10^{-8}} \right)^{1/4}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the ratio:

$$\frac{3.75 \times 10^{8}}{5.670 \times 10^{-8}} = 6.614 \times 10^{15}$$

**Step 5:** Take the fourth root:

$$T = (6.614 \times 10^{15})^{0.25} \approx 2853 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to Celsius:

$$T \approx 2853 - 273 = 2580 \text{ °C}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Radiation Pyrometers — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["RADIATION PYROMETERS"] --> B["TOTAL RADIATION PYROMETER"]
    A --> C["OPTICAL PYROMETER"]
    A --> D["RATIO PYROMETER TWO COLOR"]
    B --> B1["Lens or Mirror Collector"]
    B1 --> B2["Broadband Thermal Detector: Thermopile, Bolometer"]
    B2 --> B3["Output is T to the power 4"]
    C --> C1["Disappearing Filament Type DFOP"]
    C --> C2["Brightness Comparison Method"]
    C --> C3["Narrow Band Filter near 0.65 micrometer red"]
    C1 --> C4["Reference Tungsten Filament Current Adjusted by User"]
    C2 --> C5["Photonic Detector: Human Eye, Si Photodiode, PMT"]
    D --> D1["Measures Ratio of Two Wavelengths"]
    D1 --> D2["Automatic Emissivity Compensation"]
    D2 --> D3["Used for Dust, Smoke, Vapors"]
```

### 4.2 Disappearing Filament Optical Pyrometer — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Hot Target Object"] --> nodeB["Objective Lens System"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Red Optical Filter 0.65 micrometer"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Reference Tungsten Filament Lamp"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Eyepiece with Monochromatic Vision"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Observer's Eye"]
    nodeD -.-> nodeG["Variable Current Source and Potentiometer"]
    nodeG -.-> nodeD
    nodeG --> nodeH["Ammeter Calibrated Directly in Temperature"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Display T in Celsius or Kelvin"]
```

**Operating Principle (mapped from the diagram):**

1. The **objective lens** focuses an image of the hot target onto the plane of the **tungsten reference filament**.
2. A **monochromatic red filter** (≈ 0.65 µm) limits the bandpass so that comparison occurs at a single effective wavelength.
3. The observer looks through the **eyepiece** and adjusts the filament current until the filament **"disappears"** into the background brightness of the target.
4. At the disappearance point, the filament's brightness temperature equals the brightness temperature of the target at that wavelength.
5. The **ammeter** in the filament circuit is pre-calibrated to read temperature directly.

### 4.3 Total Radiation Pyrometer — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    objA["Hot Target"] --> objB["Concave Mirror or Lens"]
    objB --> objC["Window: Quartz or Sapphire IR Transmitting"]
    objC --> objD["Blackened Thermopile Detector"]
    objD --> objE["Cold Junction Compensation"]
    objE --> objF["Amplifier Stage"]
    objF --> objG["Linearization Circuit T to the power 4"]
    objG --> objH["Emissivity Correction Knob"]
    objH --> objI["Display: Temperature in degree Celsius or Kelvin"]
```

### 4.4 Error Sources in Radiation Pyrometry — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    errA["Measurement Error Sources"] --> errB["Target Emissivity less than 1"]
    errA --> errC["Ambient Atmospheric Absorption: CO2, H2O, Dust"]
    errA --> errD["Window or Lens Soot Deposition"]
    errA --> errE["Background Radiation Reflection from Walls"]
    errA --> errF["Non ideal Blackbody Cavity Effects"]
    errA --> errG["Distance to Spot Size Ratio SFD"]
    errB --> errH["Correct using Emissivity Setting or Ratio Pyrometer"]
    errC --> errI["Use Short Path Lengths or Purge with N2"]
    errG --> errJ["Reflections from Walls Add to Signal"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Insight:** The **Distance-to-Spot Ratio (D:S)** is the ratio of the distance from the pyrometer to the target, divided by the diameter of the measurement spot. A 100:1 D:S ratio means at 1 m distance, the pyrometer "sees" a 1 cm spot. Targets smaller than this spot cause erroneous (low) temperature readings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define emissivity. Explain how emissivity affects the accuracy of a radiation pyrometer. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Emissivity $\varepsilon$ is the ratio of the radiant energy emitted per unit area per unit time by a given body to that emitted by a **blackbody** at the same temperature, under identical conditions.

Mathematically:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E_{\text{real}}}{E_{\text{blackbody}}} = \frac{E_{\text{real}}}{\sigma T^{4}}$$

**Effect on Accuracy:**

The radiant power from a real body is $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$. If the pyrometer assumes $\varepsilon = 1$ but the actual target has $\varepsilon = 0.5$, the instrument reads a temperature **lower** than the true temperature. Specifically, $T_{\text{measured}} = (\varepsilon)^{1/4} \cdot T_{\text{true}}$.

For example, with $\varepsilon = 0.5$, the ratio $T_{\text{measured}}/T_{\text{true}} = (0.5)^{0.25} = 0.841$, meaning a 15.9 % underread. Because the temperature enters as $T^{4}$, even small emissivity errors cause large temperature errors.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly write that low emissivity always lowers the reading by a fixed percentage. It is actually a $T^4$ relationship, so the percentage error **scales with the absolute temperature**.

---

#### **Q2. State and explain Wien's displacement law. Mention its application in selecting an optical pyrometer. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Wien's displacement law states that the wavelength $\lambda_{\max}$ at which the spectral emissive power of a blackbody is maximum is **inversely proportional** to its absolute temperature:

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

**Explanation:** As temperature increases, the peak of the blackbody spectrum shifts to **shorter wavelengths** (i.e., towards visible light and beyond). This is why an iron rod first glows dull red, then cherry red, then orange, then yellow-white as it heats up.

**Application:**

For measuring a furnace at 2000 K: $\lambda_{\max} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} / 2000 = 1.449$ µm (near-IR). Hence a **silicon photodiode** (sensitive to 0.4–1.1 µm) is not ideal; an **InGaAs or thermopile** detector is preferred. For measuring the solar surface (~5800 K), $\lambda_{\max} \approx 0.5$ µm (visible green), justifying the use of silicon detectors.

> [!NOTE]
> This question is a **favourite KTU Part A question**. Memorize the constant $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K. Marks are often lost for omitting the units.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Stefan–Boltzmann law $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ by integrating Planck's spectral emissive power over all wavelengths. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A total radiation pyrometer is used to measure the temperature of a furnace. The detector receives a heat flux of $1.5 \times 10^{4}$ W/m² from the furnace opening. If the target has an emissivity of 0.85, find the true temperature of the furnace. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

##### **Solution (a) — Derivation**

**Given Planck's spectral emissive power:**

$$E_{\lambda b} = \frac{C_{1}}{\lambda^{5} \left[ \exp\!\left( \dfrac{C_{2}}{\lambda T} \right) - 1 \right]}$$

**Step 1:** Total emissive power is the integral over all wavelengths:

$$E_{b} = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{\lambda b} \, d\lambda$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $x = C_{2}/(\lambda T)$, so $d\lambda = -C_{2}/(T x^{2}) dx$:

$$E_{b} = \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{C_{1}}{\left(\frac{C_{2}}{xT}\right)^{5}(e^{x}-1)} \left( -\frac{C_{2}}{Tx^{2}} \right) dx$$

**Step 3:** After simplification (as detailed in Section 3.1):

$$E_{b} = \frac{C_{1} T^{4}}{C_{2}^{4}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x}-1} dx$$

**Step 4:** Use the series expansion:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x}-1} dx = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \int_{0}^{\infty} x^{3} e^{-nx} dx = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{6}{n^{4}} = 6 \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{90} = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back and define $\sigma$:

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{E_{b} = \sigma T^{4}}$$

---

##### **Solution (b) — Numerical**

**Given:**
- Heat flux at detector (assuming view factor = 1): $E_{\text{measured}} = 1.5 \times 10^{4}$ W/m²
- Emissivity: $\varepsilon = 0.85$
- Stefan–Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m$^{-2}$·K$^{-4}$

**Step 1:** Apply the greybody Stefan–Boltzmann law:

$$E_{\text{measured}} = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $T$:

$$T^{4} = \frac{E_{\text{measured}}}{\varepsilon \sigma} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{4}}{0.85 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator:

$$0.85 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} = 4.8195 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the ratio:

$$T^{4} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{4}}{4.8195 \times 10^{-8}} = 3.112 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^{4}$$

**Step 5:** Take the fourth root:

$$T = (3.112 \times 10^{11})^{0.25}$$

Using logarithms: $\log_{10}(T) = \dfrac{1}{4} \log_{10}(3.112 \times 10^{11}) = \dfrac{1}{4}(11.493) = 2.873$

$$T = 10^{2.873} \approx 746.4 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to Celsius:

$$T \approx 746.4 - 273 = 473.4 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 746 \text{ K} \approx 473 \text{ °C}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of the **Disappearing Filament Optical Pyrometer (DFOP)**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Describe the construction and working of a **total radiation pyrometer** with a thermopile detector. How is emissivity correction applied in such an instrument? **\[7 Marks\]**

---

##### **Solution (a) — DFOP Working Principle**

**Constructional Details:**

The instrument contains:
1. **Objective lens** — forms an image of the hot target on the plane of a tungsten reference filament.
2. **Tungsten reference filament** — a small calibrated lamp whose brightness varies with current.
3. **Red monochromatic filter** (≈ 0.65 µm) — restricts measurement to a narrow band.
4. **Eyepiece** — for the observer to view the filament superimposed on the target.
5. **Variable current source** — adjusts filament temperature.
6. **Ammeter** — calibrated directly in temperature.

**Working Principle:**

The observer adjusts the filament current until the filament **disappears** against the background of the target image. At this "disappearance point," the brightness of the filament at 0.65 µm equals the brightness of the target at the same wavelength. By the principle of monochromatic brightness equality:

$$L_{\lambda,\text{filament}}(T_f) = \varepsilon_{\lambda,\text{target}} \cdot L_{\lambda,\text{blackbody}}(T_{\text{target}})$$

Using the Wien approximation, the filament ammeter is calibrated against a standard tungsten-strip lamp at certified temperatures, and the scale reads directly in temperature.

**Advantages:**
- No physical contact with target — usable on **moving objects**.
- Suitable for **very high temperatures** (> 3000 °C).
- High accuracy (±5 °C in mid-range) when properly calibrated.
- Simple, reliable, and field-proven technology.

**Limitations:**
- **Manual operation** — needs a skilled human observer.
- Subjective reading — **observer-dependent**.
- Cannot measure temperatures **below ~700 °C** (insufficient visible glow).
- Requires knowledge of target **spectral emissivity** for non-blackbody correction.

**Sketch Description (for written exam):**

A standard line diagram should show: target → objective lens → red filter → filament (in the same optical plane) → eyepiece → observer's eye. Current source feeds the filament through an ammeter marked in °C.

---

##### **Solution (b) — Total Radiation Pyrometer with Thermopile**

**Construction:**

1. **Cassegrain-type concave mirror** (or lens) — collects radiation from the target over a wide solid angle.
2. **Quartz or sapphire window** — protects the detector from dust and fumes; transparent in IR.
3. **Thermopile detector** — an array of several thermocouple junctions arranged so that "hot" junctions are blackened to absorb radiation, while "cold" junctions are shielded and maintained at ambient temperature.
4. **Cold-junction compensation circuit** — corrects for ambient temperature variations.
5. **Amplifier and linearizer** — boosts the millivolt signal and corrects the inherent $T^4$ non-linearity.
6. **Emissivity adjustment knob** — input parameter $\varepsilon$.

**Working Principle:**

The mirror focuses incoming radiation onto the thermopile's hot junctions. Absorbed radiation raises their temperature, generating an EMF $V$ proportional to the incident power:

$$V = k \cdot \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$$

where $k$ is the instrument constant (area, distance, detector responsivity lumped together). The amplifier output is linearized via analog circuits or a lookup table, and the display reads temperature.

**Emissivity Correction Methods:**

1. **Manual emissivity setting:** The user inputs the known $\varepsilon$ of the target. The instrument divides the measured $T^4$ by $\varepsilon$ and then takes the fourth root:
$$T_{\text{true}} = \left( \frac{T_{\text{measured}}^{4}}{\varepsilon} \right)^{1/2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt[4]{\varepsilon}}$$
More directly, the linearization circuit implements: $T_{\text{display}} = \left( \dfrac{V}{k \, \varepsilon \, \sigma}\right)^{1/4}$.

2. **Two-color (ratio) pyrometer:** Measures radiance at two wavelengths $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$, then forms the ratio:
$$R = \frac{E_{\lambda_1}}{E_{\lambda_2}} \approx \frac{\varepsilon_{\lambda_1}}{\varepsilon_{\lambda_2}} \cdot \exp\!\left[ -\frac{C_2}{T} \left(\frac{1}{\lambda_1} - \frac{1}{\lambda_2}\right) \right]$$
For greybodies ($\varepsilon_{\lambda_1} \approx \varepsilon_{\lambda_2}$), emissivity cancels and accurate $T$ is obtained automatically — ideal for dusty, smoky, or vapour-laden industrial environments.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In **(a)**, students often forget to state the **monochromatic brightness equality condition** and instead vaguely write "the filament matches the target." This loses 2 marks.
> 2. In **(b)**, students frequently confuse **spectral emissivity** with **total emissivity**. The optical pyrometer uses *spectral* $\varepsilon_{\lambda}$, while the total radiation pyrometer uses *total* $\varepsilon$. Mixing them up will cause incorrect corrections.
> 3. Many students forget to mention the **Wien approximation** explicitly when justifying the linear logarithmic calibration of optical pyrometers.
> 4. In numerical problems, **units** of $C_2$ (µm·K vs. m·K) and $\lambda$ must be consistent. Using $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{4}$ µm·K requires $\lambda$ in µm.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Radiation Methods: Radiation and Optical Pyrometry"**

- **Radiation pyrometer** measures total emitted radiation (all wavelengths); follows $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$.
