(Use the exact equation.) ### Given Data - $V = 500 \text{ cm}^3 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$ - $A = 0.5 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$ - $a = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$ - $h = 8 \text{ mm}$ of Hg ### Step 1 — Convert $h$ to Consistent Units Since $P$ is asked in Torr, keep $h$ in **mmHg** directly (Torr = mmHg): $$h = 8 \text{ mmHg} = 8 \text{ Torr}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Exact McLeod Equation $$P = \frac{h \cdot a \cdot A}{V - aA}$$ Calculate the term $aA$: $$aA = (0.02) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-7}) = 1 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3 = 0.01 \text{ cm}^3$$ Denominator: $$V - aA = 500 \text{ cm}^3 - 0.01 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 499.99 \text{ cm}^3$$ ### Step 3 — Compute P in mmHg (Torr) $$P = \frac{(8 \text{ mm}) \cdot (0.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2) \cdot (0.02 \text{ m})}{499.99 \text{ cm}^3}$$ Convert all to consistent units (use $\text{mm}$ and $\text{mm}^2$ for $a$ and $A$): $$P = \frac{(8) \cdot (0.5) \cdot (20)}{500 \times 10^3} = \frac{80}{500{,}000} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mmHg}$$ $$\boxed{\, P = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Torr} \,}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert to Pascal $$P = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Torr} \times 133.322 \text{ Pa/Torr}$$ $$\boxed{\, P = 2.13 \times 10^{-2} \text{ Pa} \,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The approximation $P \approx \frac{aAh}{V}$ gives **identical** result here because $aA/V = 0.01/500 = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ is negligible. Students may use either form, but in the KTU 2024 scheme, examiners prefer the **exact form** stated first, then justified approximation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — McLeod Gauge The McLeod gauge has a precise physical structure that cannot easily be drawn in pure Mermaid. The following **block-level functional architecture flow** maps out the energy/signal/physical interaction chain instead. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Vacuum System
Unknown Pressure P] --> B[Open End
of Capillary] B --> C[Mercury Reservoir
Raised by Operator] C --> D[Mercury Level Rises
Trapping Gas Volume V] D --> E[Closed Bulb
Volume V calibrated] E --> F[Gas Compressed
into Closed Capillary] F --> G[Capillary Cross-Section A
Trapped Length a] G --> H[Open Comparison
Capillary Reference] H --> I[Mercury Height
Difference h Read on Scale] I --> J[Boyle's Law
Equation Solved] J --> K[Output: Pressure P
in Torr or Pascal] style A fill:#e1f5ff style E fill:#fff4e1 style G fill:#ffe1e1 style I fill:#e1ffe1 style K fill:#f0e1ff ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Kenetometer (Radiometer Gauge) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Vacuum Chamber
Low Pressure P] --> B[Hot Vane
Heated to T1] A --> C[Cold Vane
At T2] B --> D[Fast Molecules
Leave Hot Vane] C --> E[Slow Molecules
Leave Cold Vane] D --> F[Net Momentum
Transfer to Vane] E --> F F --> G[Knudsen
Pressure PK] G --> H[Force on
Vane F] H --> I[Torsion Fiber
Deflection Theta] I --> J[Optical Lever
Mirror + Lamp] J --> K[Scaled Output
Pressure P] style B fill:#ffe1e1 style C fill:#e1f0ff style G fill:#fff4e1 style I fill:#e1ffe1 style K fill:#f0e1ff ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Pirani (Thermal Conductivity) Gauge ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Vacuum System
Pressure P] --> B[Sealed Gauge
Chamber] B --> C[Heated Tungsten
Filament Tf] C --> D[Gas Molecules
Carry Heat Away] D --> E[Heat Loss Q
Proportional to P] E --> F[Filament Temp
Tf Changes] F --> G[Filament
Resistance Rf] G --> H[Wheatstone
Bridge Unbalance] H --> I[Output Voltage
Delta V] I --> J[Calibrated
Pressure Reading] style C fill:#ffe1e1 style D fill:#fff4e1 style G fill:#e1ffe1 style H fill:#f0e1ff style J fill:#e1f5ff ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Operational Block — All Three Gauges ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Low Pressure
Measurement Module 2] --> B[Primary Absolute
Gauge] A --> C[Secondary Absolute
Gauge] A --> D[Indirect
Secondary Gauge] B --> E[McLeod Gauge
10-1 to 10-6 Torr] C --> F[Kenetometer
10-3 to 10-7 Torr] D --> G[Pirani Gauge
10-4 to 1 Torr] E --> E1[Principle: Boyle's Law
Isothermal Compression] E --> E2[Mercury + Capillary
Direct Readout h, a] F --> F1[Principle: Radiometric
Knudsen Effect] F --> F2[Hot and Cold Vanes
Torsion Fiber + Mirror] G --> G1[Principle: Thermal
Conductivity vs P] G --> G2[Heated Filament
Wheatstone Bridge] style A fill:#f0e1ff style E fill:#ffe1e1 style F fill:#fff4e1 style G fill:#e1f5ff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank]** **State and explain Boyle's Law as applied to the McLeod gauge. Why is the McLeod gauge classified as an *absolute* gauge?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Boyle's Law Statement (1 mark):** At constant temperature, the pressure of a fixed mass of gas is inversely proportional to its volume. Mathematically, $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$. - **Application to McLeod (1 mark):** In the McLeod gauge, a known volume $V$ of gas at unknown pressure $P$ is isothermally compressed into a small capillary volume $aA$, raising the pressure to $(P+h)$. Equating: $P \cdot V = (P+h) \cdot aA$, giving $P = \frac{aAh}{V - aA} \approx \frac{aAh}{V}$. - **Why Absolute (1 mark):** The gauge needs **no prior calibration** because $P$ is calculated directly from measurable geometric quantities ($a, A, h, V$) and the universal Boyle's Law. It is therefore a **primary standard** for vacuum measurement. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank]** **Briefly explain why the thermal conductivity of a gas becomes pressure-dependent only at low pressures. How does the Pirani gauge exploit this?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Mechanism (1.5 marks):** At **high pressure**, gas molecules are densely packed. Heat is transferred primarily by **conduction through molecule-to-molecule collisions**, and the thermal conductivity is essentially **independent of pressure**. At **low pressure** (mean free path $\gg$ gap), molecules travel ballistically to the cold wall, so the heat transfer rate becomes **directly proportional to the number density of gas molecules**, i.e., **proportional to pressure $P$**. - **Pirani exploitation (1.5 marks):** The Pirani gauge operates in this low-pressure regime. The filament temperature $T_f$ (and hence resistance $R_f$) varies with pressure because heat loss $Q = k \lambda (T_f - T_c) \propto P$ changes. A **Wheatstone bridge** measures the resistance change, giving a calibrated pressure readout. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. In McLeod gauge derivations, **forgetting to state the Boyle's Law assumption (isothermal compression)** costs **1 full mark**. > 2. In Kenetometer derivations, **mixing up $T_1$ (hot) and $T_2$ (cold)** is a guaranteed 1-mark cut. Always write "hot vane temperature $T_1 > T_2$ (cold vane)". > 3. In Pirani numericals, **students frequently forget to convert $h$ from mmHg to Torr** — they are equal, so the answer is straightforward, but show the step explicitly. > 4. **Never write $\frac{1}{2} P (\sqrt{T_1/T_2})$** — the *minus 1* is essential; omitting it loses 1 mark. --- ### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank]** **Derive the working equation of the McLeod gauge. Clearly state all assumptions and the final approximate form used in practice.** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions (1.5 marks):** 1. The trapped gas behaves ideally. 2. The compression is **isothermal** (slow process, gas in thermal equilibrium with surroundings). 3. The bulb volume $V$ is **calibrated** and **constant**. 4. Mercury is clean and dry; no gas is dissolved in it. 5. The capillary cross-section $A$ is **uniform** along its length. **Derivation (4.5 marks):** - Initial state: pressure $P$, volume $V$. So $P_1 V_1 = PV$. - Compressed state: pressure $(P+h)$, volume $aA$ (length $a$ of trapped gas in capillary of area $A$). So $P_2 V_2 = (P+h)(aA)$. - By Boyle's Law (isothermal): $PV = (P+h)(aA)$. - Expanding: $PV = PaA + haA$ $\Rightarrow$ $P(V - aA) = haA$. - Therefore: $\boxed{P = \dfrac{haA}{V - aA}}$ **Approximation (1 mark):** - Since $V \gg aA$ (the compressed volume is microscopic compared to the bulb): $V - aA \approx V$. - So $P \approx \dfrac{haA}{V}$, the practical working equation. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank]** **A McLeod gauge has a bulb of volume 250 cm³ and a capillary of cross-section 0.4 mm². When the mercury is raised, the trapped gas column length is 15 mm and the mercury level difference is 10 mm. Calculate the original pressure in (i) Torr and (ii) Pascal.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $V = 250 \text{ cm}^3$ - $A = 0.4 \text{ mm}^2$ - $a = 15 \text{ mm}$ - $h = 10 \text{ mm}$ **Step 1 — Calculate $aA$ in consistent units (use mm and mm²):** $$aA = 15 \times 0.4 = 6 \text{ mm}^3 = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^3$$ **Step 2 — Apply the exact McLeod equation (1.5 marks):** $$P = \frac{h \cdot a \cdot A}{V - aA} = \frac{10 \times 6}{250 \times 10^3 - 6}$$ (The denominator uses $V$ converted to mm³: $250 \text{ cm}^3 = 250 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3$.) $$P = \frac{60}{2.5 \times 10^5 - 6} = \frac{60}{249{,}994} \approx 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mmHg}$$ **Step 3 — Result in Torr (1 mark):** $$\boxed{P \approx 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Torr}}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to Pascal (1.5 marks):** $$P = 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 133.322 \text{ Pa} = 3.2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 5 — Practical approximation check (1 mark):** $$P \approx \frac{haA}{V} = \frac{60}{2.5 \times 10^5} = 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Torr (same to 5 sig. fig.)}$$ --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank]** **With a neat functional diagram, explain the working principle of the Kenetometer (Knudsen radiometer gauge). Derive the expression for the Knudsen pressure.** **Model Solution:** **Construction & Principle (2.5 marks):** - Two parallel vanes — one heated to $T_1$, the other cooled to $T_2$. - Suspended vane on a torsion fiber, deflection measured by an optical lever. - At very low pressure (molecular flow), gas molecules bouncing off the hot vane have a higher mean velocity than those off the cold vane. - This produces a net unidirectional force on the suspended vane. **Derivation (4.5 marks):** *(Steps 1–5 as in Section 3.1)* - $\bar{c}_1 = \sqrt{8RT_1/\pi M}$ and $\bar{c}_2 = \sqrt{8RT_2/\pi M}$ - Force per unit area from each side: $\frac{1}{4}\rho \bar{c}^2$ - Net Knudsen pressure: $P_K = \frac{1}{4}\rho(\bar{c}_1^2 - \bar{c}_2^2) = \frac{1}{2}P\left(\sqrt{T_1/T_2} - 1\right)$ **Final Result (1 mark for box, 0.5 mark for units discussion):** $$\boxed{P_K = \frac{1}{2} P \left( \sqrt{\frac{T_1}{T_2}} - 1 \right) \text{ N/m}^2}$$ --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank]** **Explain the construction and working of a Pirani (thermal conductivity) gauge. Why is it unsuitable for very high vacuum measurement (above 10⁻⁴ Torr)?** **Model Solution:** **Construction (1.5 marks):** - A fine **tungsten or platinum filament** stretched inside a glass/metal envelope connected to the vacuum. - The filament forms one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge**. - A **reference tube** (sealed at known low pressure) is often used in the adjacent arm for temperature compensation. **Working Principle (3 marks):** - Filament is heated by an electric current. - Heat loss $Q = k \lambda (T_f - T_c)$ depends on the gas thermal conductivity $\lambda$. - In the low-pressure regime, $\lambda \propto P$. - So $Q \propto P$, filament temperature $T_f$ depends on $P$. - Resistance $R_f = R_0[1 + \alpha(T_f - T_0)]$ changes, producing a **bridge unbalance voltage $\Delta V$** proportional to $P$. **Why Unsuitable Above 10⁻⁴ Torr (High Vacuum) (2.5 marks):** - Above $\sim 10^{-3}$ Torr, the gas mean free path becomes **smaller than the filament-to-wall gap**. - The thermal conductivity then **saturates** and becomes **independent of pressure** (the continuum regime). - Therefore the gauge **loses its pressure sensitivity** at very low pressures. - Additionally, the filament can burn out if operated at very high vacuum without thermal protection (no convective cooling). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this checklist before every KTU Module 2 exam.** - [ ] **Low Pressure Range:** Atmospheric = 760 Torr = $1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa; 1 Torr = 133.322 Pa. - [ ] **McLeod Gauge — Equation:** $P = \dfrac{haA}{V - aA} \approx \dfrac{haA}{V}$. - [ ] **McLeod Gauge — Assumptions:** Isothermal, ideal gas, $V \gg aA$, uniform capillary, clean mercury. - [ ] **McLeod Gauge — Type:** **Primary, Absolute**, range $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-6}$ Torr. - [ ] **McLeod Gauge — Limitations:** Cannot measure vapours that condense in mercury; not for continuous industrial use; manual operation. - [ ] **Kenetometer — Equation:** $P_K = \dfrac{1}{2} P \left(\sqrt{T_1/T_2} - 1\right)$ with $T_1$ = hot, $T_2$ = cold. - [ ] **Kenetometer — Type:** **Secondary, Absolute**, range $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-7}$ Torr; uses **molecular flow** (mean free path $\gg$ gap). - [ ] **Kenetometer — Readout:** Torsion fiber + optical lever (micronewton forces). - [ ] **Pirani Gauge — Equation:** $Q = k \lambda (T_f - T_c)$; $\lambda \propto P$ at low $P$. - [ ] **Pirani Gauge — Type:** **Indirect, Secondary**, range $10^{-4}$ to $1$ Torr; uses **Wheatstone bridge** on filament resistance. - [ ] **Pirani Gauge — Caveat:** Above $10^{-3}$ Torr, $\lambda$ becomes pressure-independent — **loses sensitivity**. Suitable for medium vacuum only. - [ ] **Industrial Pick:** Pirani = process control; McLeod = lab standard; Kenetometer = calibration reference. - [ ] **Conversion must-remember:** $1 \text{ mmHg} = 1 \text{ Torr} = 133.322 \text{ Pa}$. - [ ] **KTU board trap:** Never write $P_K = \frac{1}{2} P \sqrt{T_1/T_2}$ — the "$-1$" term is **mandatory**. - [ ] **KTU board trap:** In McLeod derivations, always state the isothermal assumption first; examiners deduct 1 mark if missing.

topics.title: Pressure Measurement Using Bourdon Tube, Flat and Corrugated Diaphragms, and Capsules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pressure Measurement Using Bourdon Tube, Flat and Corrugated Diaphragms, and Capsules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 2 — PRESSURE MEASUREMENT: ELASTIC TRANSDUCERS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Pressure" in Industrial Instrumentation?

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, **pressure** is defined as the normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area on a surface. Mathematically:

$$P = \frac{F}{A} \quad [\text{N/m}^2]$$

The **SI unit** is the **Pascal (Pa)**. However, in industrial practice, several other units are routinely used, and you must be fluent in converting between them for KTU exam problems.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 — Unit Conversion Master Table (Memorize!)**
> - **1 bar** = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa
> - **1 atm** = 1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa
> - **1 kgf/cm²** = 0.980665 bar (often taken as 1 kgf/cm² ≈ 1 bar in KTU problems)
> - **1 psi** = 6.895 kPa
> - **1 torr** = 1 mmHg = 133.322 Pa
> - **1 inH₂O** = 249.089 Pa

The instruments discussed in this module are all **mechanical/elastic deformation-type pressure sensors**. They convert pressure into a measurable **mechanical displacement**, which is then read by a pointer (analogue) or converted to an electrical signal (transducer).

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight (OEBBT724 - Module 2):**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly clusters the following under elastic-element pressure sensors:
> 1. **Bourdon Tubes** (C-type, helical, spiral)
> 2. **Flat Diaphragms**
> 3. **Corrugated Diaphragms**
> 4. **Capsules**
>
> Each belongs to the family of **"Deflection-type"** primary pressure sensors.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Balloon-Spring" Intuition

Imagine you are blowing air into a long, thin, party balloon that has been bent into a "C" shape. As the air pressure inside rises, the balloon **tries to straighten out** because the outer wall of the bend is being stretched more than the inner wall. This is *exactly* how a **Bourdon tube** works — a curved metal tube with a flattened cross-section wants to uncurl when pressurized.

Now take a **soft drink bottle cap** and press your thumb against it — the thin metal disc **bends inward** by a tiny amount. That is your **flat diaphragm**.

If you took that same cap and added a few **concentric ridges** (corrugations) to it, pressing on it would cause a much larger, more predictable bend. That is your **corrugated diaphragm**.

Finally, weld **two** such corrugated discs back-to-back, sealing the inside, and you have a **capsule** — so sensitive it can detect the weight of a feather on a postage stamp.

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight**
> All four devices work on the same universal principle: **an elastic element deforms in proportion to the applied pressure, and that deformation is read out as a measure of pressure.**

---

### 1.3 Classification of the Four Devices (KTU Exam Favourite)

| Device | Typical Pressure Range | Typical Application | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bourdon Tube (C-type)** | 0.6 bar → 7000 bar | Steam boilers, hydraulic presses, gas cylinders | Medium |
| **Helical/Spiral Bourdon** | 0.5 bar → 1000 bar | High-pressure gauges, aircraft instruments | High (large deflection) |
| **Flat Diaphragm** | 0.1 bar → 10 bar (approx.) | Low-pressure gauges, manometers | Low |
| **Corrugated Diaphragm** | 0.01 bar → 5 bar | Process control, differential pressure | High |
| **Capsule** | < 0.01 bar (very low) | Aneroid barometer, altimeter, draft gauges | Very High |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bourdon Tube — Engineering Anatomy

### 2.1.1 Construction

A Bourdon tube is essentially a **flattened (oval/elliptical cross-section) tube bent into an arc of approximately 250°–270°**. One end (the **fixed end / socket end**) is closed and connected to the pressure inlet. The other end (the **free end / tip**) is sealed and connected through a mechanical linkage (gear–pinion) to a pointer.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Insight**
> The C-type Bourdon has a single curve, the helical type has multiple turns around a central axis, and the spiral type is wound like a clock spring. **More turns → larger tip displacement for the same pressure → higher sensitivity and resolution.**

### 2.1.2 Working Principle — The "Why" Behind the Movement

When pressurized fluid enters the tube:
- The **outer wall of the curve** (longer path) is stretched by tensile hoop stress.
- The **inner wall** (shorter path) is compressed.
- The cross-section **tends to become circular** (because a circle is the shape with minimum perimeter for a given area — the *isoperimetric* principle).
- The combined effect **straightens the curved tube**, causing the free tip to move.

The tip displacement is transmitted via a **link–lever–pinion–gear train** to a pointer that sweeps across a calibrated dial.

### 2.1.3 Material Selection (Why This Matters for KTU Problems)

- **Phosphor Bronze** — low pressures, corrosive media, good fatigue life.
- **Beryllium Copper** — high elastic limit, suitable up to ~700 bar.
- **Stainless Steel (AISI 316)** — high pressure, high temperature, chemical industry.
- **Monel / Inconel** — extreme environments (acid, seawater, aerospace).

---

### 2.2 Flat Diaphragm — The Simplest Elastic Element

### 2.2.1 Geometry and Boundary Conditions

A **flat (circular) diaphragm** is a thin circular plate, rigidly clamped at its circumference (between two flanges), with the pressure acting on one face. The KTU board problem typically assumes:
- Radius = $R$
- Thickness = $t$
- Young's modulus = $E$
- Poisson's ratio = $\nu$

### 2.2.2 Working Principle

Pressure $P$ acting on the diaphragm produces:
1. A **central deflection** ($w_0$ or $w_{\max}$).
2. **Radial** and **tangential stresses** within the disc.

The **central deflection** is the quantity measured (via a mechanical pointer or LVDT/strain gauge bonded on the disc).

---

### 2.3 Corrugated Diaphragm — Engineering Improvement

A flat diaphragm is **non-linear** at large deflections and has limited travel. By adding **concentric corrugations** (ripples), engineers achieve:

1. **Larger deflection for the same pressure** (up to 5× more than flat).
2. **Improved linearity** between deflection and pressure.
3. **Reduced stress concentration** (corrugations absorb strain).
4. **Resistance to "oil-canning"** (buckling under overload).

> [!NOTE] **Types of Corrugations**
> - **Linear (symmetric) corrugations** — deflection ∝ pressure.
> - **Non-linear (asymmetric) corrugations** — deflection ∝ pressure² (used where output is to be linearized electrically, e.g., capsule-type barometers).

---

### 2.4 Capsule — Two Diaphragms Welded Back-to-Back

### 2.4.1 Construction

Two identical corrugated diaphragms are welded together at their outer edges, forming a sealed, hollow "pillow". Pressure is applied to the **outside** of the capsule; the **inside** is either evacuated (absolute pressure measurement) or sealed at a reference pressure (gauge or differential measurement).

### 2.4.2 Why Two Diaphragms?

Because each diaphragm deflects by $w$, the **total center deflection of the capsule** is $2w$. This **doubles the sensitivity**, allowing the measurement of very small pressures — down to fractions of a millibar.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Real-World Application — The Aneroid Barometer**
> A stack of multiple capsules (called a **capsule stack** or **aneroid cell**) is used in altimeters and barometers. A stack of 4–6 capsules can provide a deflection of several millimetres for a pressure change of just a few millibars.

---

### 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Save this table. At least 60% of KTU numerical problems will use one of these.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure (general) | $P = F/A$ | $P$: Pa, $F$: N, $A$: m² |
| 2 | Bourdon tip displacement (small angle) | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{P \cdot R^3 \cdot n \cdot (1-\nu^2)}{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}$ | $n$: number of turns |
| 3 | Flat diaphragm — central deflection | $w_0 = \dfrac{3 P R^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$ | $w_0$: m, $R$: m, $t$: m |
| 4 | Flat diaphragm — max radial stress (at edge) | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{3 P R^2}{4 t^2}$ | Pa |
| 5 | Flat diaphragm — max tangential stress (at centre) | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{3 P R^2 (1+\nu)}{8 t^2}$ | Pa |
| 6 | Corrugated diaphragm — approx. central deflection | $w_c \approx K \cdot w_0$ (where $K$ is the corrugation factor, 1.5 → 5) | — |
| 7 | Capsule total deflection | $w_{capsule} = 2 w_c$ | m |
| 8 | Capsule stack of $N$ capsules | $w_{stack} = N \cdot 2 w_c$ | m |
| 9 | Natural frequency of diaphragm | $f_n = \dfrac{2.56 t}{\pi R^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{3\rho(1-\nu^2)}}$ | Hz |
| 10 | Hoop stress in thin-walled Bourdon | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 t}$ | Pa (cylinder formula) |

> **Note on $\nu$:** Poisson's ratio for most metals ≈ **0.3**. For a flat diaphragm, the factor $(1-\nu^2)$ evaluates to **0.91** when $\nu = 0.3$. The board often **stipulates** to assume $\nu = 0.3$ or to drop the $(1-\nu^2)$ term altogether.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bourdon tube gauges** dominate the **boiler, process, and hydraulic industries** — every steam plant has dozens.
- **Diaphragm gauges** are mandatory where the fluid would damage a Bourdon (e.g., viscous slurries, sanitary/food-grade applications).
- **Capsules** form the heart of **altimeters, barometers, and differential pressure transmitters** in HVAC and aerospace.
- **Strain-gauge-bonded diaphragms** are the modern bridge between mechanical deflection and electrical signal (used in load cells and DP transmitters).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Examiner's Note:**
> The two derivations below are **the most frequently asked 7-mark problems** in KTU university exams on this module. Memorize the *steps* and the *logic*, not just the final expression.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Central Deflection of a Flat Circular Diaphragm

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A flat circular diaphragm of radius $R$ and thickness $t$ is rigidly clamped at its periphery. A uniform pressure $P$ acts perpendicular to one face. The material has Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$. Find the **central deflection** $w_0$.

### 3.1.2 Assumptions (State these first in your KTU answer!)

1. Deflection $w \ll t$ (small deflection theory).
2. The diaphragm behaves as a **thin plate** ($t \ll R$).
3. Plane stress state (no stress in the thickness direction).
4. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.

### 3.1.3 Governing Equation (Plate Theory)

The deflection of a thin circular plate is governed by the **biharmonic equation** in polar coordinates:

$$D \nabla^4 w = P$$

where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the plate:

$$D = \frac{E t^3}{12 (1-\nu^2)}$$

### 3.1.4 Integration Steps

**Step 1 — Apply Axisymmetry**

Because the pressure is uniform and the geometry is circular, the deflection depends only on the radial distance $r$ from the centre. The biharmonic operator in polar coordinates becomes:

$$\nabla^4 w = \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left\{r \frac{d}{dr}\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dw}{dr}\right)\right]\right\}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate Twice**

The general solution of $D \nabla^4 w = P$ (constant $P$) is:

$$w(r) = C_1 r^2 + C_2 \ln r + C_3 r^2 \ln r + C_4 + \frac{P r^4}{64 D}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Boundary Conditions**

- **Bounded at the centre ($r = 0$):** Deflection must be finite. This kills the singular $\ln r$ terms: $C_2 = 0$ and $C_3 = 0$.

- **Clamped at the periphery ($r = R$):** Deflection and slope are zero.
   - $w(R) = 0$
   - $\dfrac{dw}{dr}\bigg|_{r=R} = 0$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Constants**

The simplified solution reduces to:

$$w(r) = C_1 r^2 + C_4 + \frac{P r^4}{64 D}$$

Apply $w(R) = 0$:

$$0 = C_1 R^2 + C_4 + \frac{P R^4}{64 D}$$

Differentiate: $\dfrac{dw}{dr} = 2 C_1 r + \dfrac{P r^3}{16 D}$

Apply $\dfrac{dw}{dr}\bigg|_{r=R} = 0$:

$$0 = 2 C_1 R + \frac{P R^3}{16 D} \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_1 = -\frac{P R^2}{32 D}$$

Substitute back:

$$C_4 = -C_1 R^2 - \frac{P R^4}{64 D} = \frac{P R^4}{32 D} - \frac{P R^4}{64 D} = \frac{P R^4}{64 D}$$

**Step 5 — Central Deflection ($r = 0$)**

$$w_0 = w(0) = C_4 = \frac{P R^4}{64 D}$$

Substitute $D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12 (1-\nu^2)}$:

$$w_0 = \frac{P R^4}{64} \cdot \frac{12 (1-\nu^2)}{E t^3} = \frac{3 P R^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$$

### 3.1.5 ✅ Final Result (Box this in your KTU answer!)

$$\boxed{\,w_0 = \frac{3 P R^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E t^3}\,}$$

### 3.1.6 Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Q:** A steel diaphragm ($E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$) has $R = 25$ mm and $t = 0.5$ mm. Find the central deflection when $P = 0.5$ bar.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 0.5 \text{ bar} = 0.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 5 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} \\
R &= 25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m},\quad t = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \\
1-\nu^2 &= 1 - 0.09 = 0.91 \\
w_0 &= \frac{3 \times 5 \times 10^4 \times (25 \times 10^{-3})^4 \times 0.91}{16 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^3} \\
w_0 &= \frac{3 \times 5 \times 10^4 \times 3.906 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.91}{16 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-10}} \\
w_0 &= \frac{5.331 \times 10^{-1}}{4.0 \times 10^{2}} = 1.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \\
\therefore w_0 &\approx 1.33 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Stresses in a Flat Diaphragm

For a KTU problem, the **two critical locations** for stress are:
- **At the edge ($r = R$):** Maximum **radial** (bending) stress $\sigma_r$.
- **At the centre ($r = 0$):** Maximum **tangential** (hoop) stress $\sigma_t$.

The **bending moment per unit length** in a circular plate is related to the curvature. For a plate with deflection profile $w(r)$, the moments are:

$$M_r = -D\left(\frac{d^2w}{dr^2} + \frac{\nu}{r}\frac{dw}{dr}\right)$$

$$M_t = -D\left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{dw}{dr} + \nu \frac{d^2w}{dr^2}\right)$$

The **maximum bending stresses** are:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{6 M_r}{t^2}, \qquad \sigma_t = \frac{6 M_t}{t^2}$$

### 3.2.1 Compute $w(r)$ Derivatives

From the solution $w(r) = \dfrac{P}{64 D}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dw}{dr} &= \frac{P}{64 D} \cdot 2(R^2 - r^2)(-2r) = -\frac{P r (R^2 - r^2)}{16 D} \\
\frac{d^2w}{dr^2} &= -\frac{P}{16 D} \cdot \frac{d}{dr}\left[r R^2 - r^3\right] = -\frac{P}{16 D}(R^2 - 3r^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Maximum Radial Stress at the Edge ($r = R$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left.\frac{d^2w}{dr^2}\right|_{r=R} &= -\frac{P}{16 D}(R^2 - 3R^2) = \frac{P R^2}{8 D} \\
\left.\frac{dw}{dr}\right|_{r=R} &= 0 \quad (\text{clamped edge}) \\
M_r(R) &= -D \cdot \frac{P R^2}{8 D} = -\frac{P R^2}{8} \\
\sigma_r &= \frac{6 |M_r|}{t^2} = \frac{6 P R^2}{8 t^2} = \frac{3 P R^2}{4 t^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_r(\max) = \frac{3 P R^2}{4 t^2}\,}$$

### 3.2.3 Maximum Tangential Stress at the Centre ($r = 0$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left.\frac{d^2w}{dr^2}\right|_{r=0} &= -\frac{P R^2}{16 D} \\
\left.\frac{dw}{dr}\right|_{r=0} &= 0 \\
M_t(0) &= -D \left(-\frac{P R^2}{16 D}\right) = \frac{P R^2}{16} \\
\sigma_t &= \frac{6 P R^2}{16 t^2} = \frac{3 P R^2}{8 t^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

However, at $r = 0$ the **membrane stress** (in-plane stretching) also contributes. The total tangential stress at the centre is:

$$\sigma_t(\text{total}) = \frac{3 P R^2}{8 t^2}(1 + \nu)$$

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_t(\max) = \frac{3 P R^2 (1 + \nu)}{8 t^2}\,}$$

### 3.2.4 Design Rule (KTU favourite)

$$\frac{\sigma_r(\max)}{\sigma_t(\max)} = \frac{3PR^2/4t^2}{3PR^2(1+\nu)/8t^2} = \frac{2}{1+\nu}$$

For $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_r$ is **~1.54× larger** than $\sigma_t$. **The edge is the critical design point for radial stress.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bourdon Tip Displacement

A widely-used engineering approximation for the angular tip displacement $\Delta\theta$ of a C-Bourdon tube is:

$$\Delta\theta \approx \frac{P \cdot R^3 \cdot n \cdot (1-\nu^2)}{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}$$

where:
- $R$ = radius of curvature of the tube centreline
- $n$ = number of turns (for helical/spiral)
- $b$ = minor (width) dimension of the flattened cross-section
- $t$ = wall thickness

> [!NOTE] **Insight:** Tip displacement is **proportional to $R^3$** and **inversely proportional to $t^3$**. This is why:
> - Doubling the tube thickness reduces the deflection by **8×** (huge!).
> - Increasing the radius of curvature dramatically increases sensitivity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE] **KTU Tip:** In a 14-mark question, a well-labelled diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** on its own. Always draw the diagram first, then the explanation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not confuse "radial stress" and "tangential stress"** in diaphragm problems. The radial stress peaks at the *edge*; tangential stress peaks at the *centre*. Drawing a wrong sign or location = **−3 marks**.
> - **Units trap:** If the radius is in mm and you forget to convert to metres, the deflection answer will be off by a factor of 10⁶. Always write units in every step.
> - **Bourdon cross-section trap:** Don't write "Bourdon tube is circular in cross-section." It is **flattened/elliptical** — that is the *core* reason it deflects.
> - **Capsule trap:** Capsule measures **external** pressure; the **inside is sealed**. Writing "pressure is applied internally" will lose **1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain with a neat sketch the working of a C-type Bourdon tube used for pressure measurement. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **C-type Bourdon tube** is a curved metallic tube of **flattened (elliptical) cross-section**, bent into an arc of about **250°–270°**. The fixed end is connected to the pressure line, and the free end is sealed and coupled through a **link–lever–pinion–gear mechanism** to a pointer.

**Working principle:** When fluid under pressure enters the tube, the **hoop stress** in the outer wall of the curve is greater than that in the inner wall. The cross-section tends to become circular (since a circle has minimum perimeter for a given area). This causes the tube to **straighten partially**, displacing the free tip. The tip motion is geared to the pointer, which indicates pressure on a calibrated dial.

> **Key points (each = 1 mark):**
> 1. Sketch of C-Bourdon with linkage.
> 2. Flattened/elliptical cross-section.
> 3. Pressure → tube straightens → tip displaces → pointer moves.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a flat diaphragm and a corrugated diaphragm. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Flat Diaphragm | Corrugated Diaphragm |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Plain circular plate | Concentric ripples/corrugations |
| **Deflection vs Pressure** | Non-linear | Approximately linear |
| **Deflection magnitude** | Small | 1.5×–5× larger |
| **Sensitivity** | Low | High |
| **Stress concentration** | Higher (edge effects) | Lower (ripples distribute strain) |
| **Application** | Low-range gauges, transducers | Process control, DP transmitters |

> Any **3 correct contrasting points** get full marks.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

> **KTU Rule:** You must answer **ONE full question** of 14 marks, choosing from the two alternatives provided. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) for **7 + 7 marks**.

---

### 🟦 Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### (a) Derive an expression for the central deflection of a flat circular diaphragm clamped at the periphery, when a uniform pressure P is applied. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **State the assumptions** (small deflection, thin plate, plane stress, axisymmetry, isotropic). *(1 mark)*

2. **Write the governing equation** for a thin circular plate: $D \nabla^4 w = P$, where $D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$. *(1 mark)*

3. **General solution** under axisymmetry:
$$w(r) = C_1 r^2 + C_2 \ln r + C_3 r^2 \ln r + C_4 + \frac{P r^4}{64 D}$$
*(1 mark)*

4. **Apply boundary conditions:**
   - Finite at $r=0$ → $C_2 = C_3 = 0$. *(1 mark)*
   - $w(R) = 0$ and $\dfrac{dw}{dr}\bigg|_R = 0$ → solve for $C_1$ and $C_4$. *(2 marks)*

5. **Final central deflection at $r=0$:**
$$w_0 = \frac{P R^4}{64 D} = \frac{3 P R^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$$
*(1 mark)*

#### (b) A steel diaphragm ($E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$) of diameter 50 mm and thickness 1 mm is used to measure a pressure of 2 bar. Calculate: (i) Central deflection, (ii) Maximum radial stress. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- Diameter = 50 mm → $R = 0.025$ m
- $t = 1$ mm = $0.001$ m
- $P = 2$ bar = $2 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $\nu = 0.3$ → $1-\nu^2 = 0.91$

**(i) Central Deflection:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_0 &= \frac{3 P R^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E t^3} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times (0.025)^4 \times 0.91}{16 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times (0.001)^3} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 3.906 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.91}{16 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= \frac{2.133 \times 10^{-1}}{3.2 \times 10^{3}} \\
&= 6.665 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \\
\therefore w_0 &\approx \mathbf{0.0667 \text{ mm}} \quad \mathbf{(3 \text{ marks})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Radial Stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r(\max) &= \frac{3 P R^2}{4 t^2} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times (0.025)^2}{4 \times (0.001)^2} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{3.75 \times 10^{2}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 9.375 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
\therefore \sigma_r &\approx \mathbf{93.75 \text{ MPa}} \quad \mathbf{(4 \text{ marks})}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 🟥 Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

#### (a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Bourdon tube pressure gauge. Compare C-type, helical, and spiral Bourdon tubes. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Sketch** of the gauge showing the curved tube, fixed end, free end, link, lever, pinion, and pointer. *(1 mark)*

2. **Construction:** Tube is metallic with **flattened (elliptical) cross-section**, bent into a curve. Fixed end connects to the process; free end is sealed. Materials include phosphor bronze, beryllium copper, and stainless steel. *(2 marks)*

3. **Working:** Pressure inside → hoop stress on outer wall > inner wall → cross-section tends to become circular → tube straightens → tip displaces → motion is amplified through the mechanical linkage → pointer moves over the dial. *(2 marks)*

4. **Comparison Table:** *(2 marks)*

| Parameter | C-Type | Helical | Spiral |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shape** | Single C-curve | Multiple turns around an axis | Clock-spring spiral |
| **Tip displacement** | Smallest | Medium | Largest |
| **Sensitivity** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Range** | Wide | Higher pressure | Highest pressure |
| **Application** | General purpose | High-pressure gauges, recorders | Aircraft altimeters, precision gauges |

#### (b) What is a capsule? Explain its construction, working, and applications. Why is it preferred for very low-pressure measurements? (7 Marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Definition:** A **capsule** is a pressure-sensing element formed by welding two corrugated diaphragms back-to-back at their edges, enclosing a sealed cavity. *(1 mark)*

2. **Construction:** Two identical diaphragms (usually corrugated) are seam-welded around the periphery. The internal cavity is either **evacuated** (absolute pressure capsule) or **sealed at a reference pressure** (differential capsule). A central stud transfers the deflection to a mechanical pointer or to a transducer (LVDT/strain gauge). *(2 marks)*

3. **Working:** External pressure compresses the capsule. Each diaphragm deflects inward by $w$, giving a **total central deflection of $2w$**. Multiple capsules stacked in series give $N \times 2w$ total deflection. *(2 marks)*

4. **Why preferred for very low pressure:** Doubling the deflection (and stacking) gives very high sensitivity, allowing measurement of pressure differences as small as **fractions of a millibar**. *(1 mark)*

5. **Applications:** Aneroid barometer, aircraft altimeter, draft gauge in boilers, HVAC differential pressure sensing, weather instruments. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Final 60-second revision checklist (memorize this!):**

- ✅ **Bourdon tube** = flattened cross-section, curved tube → **straightens** under pressure. **C-type / Helical / Spiral** = increasing sensitivity.
- ✅ **Bourdon tip displacement** ∝ $R^3$ and ∝ $1/t^3$. Triple-thick walls = **27× less** deflection.
- ✅ **Flat diaphragm central deflection formula:** $w_0 = \dfrac{3 P R^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$. Note: **fourth power of $R$** — radius is the most sensitive design parameter.
- ✅ **Max radial stress** in a flat diaphragm occurs at the **edge** and equals $\dfrac{3 P R^2}{4 t^2}$.
- ✅ **Max tangential stress** occurs at the **centre** and equals $\dfrac{3 P R^2 (1+\nu)}{8 t^2}$.
- ✅ **Corrugated diaphragm** = larger deflection, better linearity, lower stress. $w_{corrugated} = K \cdot w_{flat}$ with $K$ between 1.5 and 5.
- ✅ **Capsule** = two diaphragms back-to-back → deflection = $2w$ per capsule. **Stacks** are used for very low pressure.
- ✅ **Units:** $1$ bar = $10^5$ Pa. Always convert mm to m before substituting.
- ✅ **Poisson's ratio** for metals ≈ 0.3. The $(1-\nu^2)$ factor ≈ **0.91**.
- ✅ **Aneroid barometer** = a stack of capsules. The most iconic capsule application.
- ✅ **Bourdon gauge** cannot measure absolute pressure directly (it measures gauge pressure relative to atmosphere).
- ✅ **Capsule vs Diaphragm:** Capsule = sealed inside + outside pressure; Diaphragm = pressure on one side only.
- ✅ **Always state assumptions** before any diaphragm derivation in the KTU exam — this alone is worth 1 mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Transducers of Electrical and Mechanical Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transducers of Electrical and Mechanical Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — TRANSDUCERS OF ELECTRICAL AND MECHANICAL TYPE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

A **transducer** is a device that converts energy from one form to another. In the context of industrial instrumentation, a transducer is defined as a device that converts a physical quantity (measurand) — such as pressure, temperature, displacement, force, or flow — into a usable output signal, which is most commonly an electrical signal proportional to the input measurand.

The measurand is the physical quantity being measured. The transducer is the primary sensing element in any measurement chain, acting as the bridge between the physical world and the electrical domain where signal conditioning, transmission, and display occur.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition** — A transducer is a device that converts one form of energy into another. When the output is electrical, it is specifically called an **electrical transducer**. When the output is mechanical (displacement, force, or pressure), it is termed a **mechanical transducer** or **primary sensing element**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Universal Translator

Think of a transducer as a **universal translator** stationed at a customs checkpoint. A foreign traveler (the physical measurand) arrives speaking one language — say, the language of *heat* or *pressure*. The translator (the transducer) converts this foreign message into a language the central office (the control system or display unit) understands — typically the language of *volts and milliamps*.

Without this translator, the central office would be deaf and blind to the conditions in the field.

### 1.3 Classification Overview

Transducers are broadly classified into two engineering categories based on the form of their output energy:

| Category | Output Form | Typical Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Transducer** | Displacement, force, pressure | Primary sensing stage (input element) |
| **Electrical Transducer** | Voltage, current, resistance, charge | Secondary stage (signal conditioning-ready) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Distinction Matters in KTU Exams** — Mechanical transducers almost always act as the *primary element* (first stage) and frequently feed an electrical transducer as the *secondary element*. For example, a Bourdon tube (mechanical) might drive an LVDT (electrical), forming a complete pressure transmitter.

### 1.4 Physical Constants Referenced in This Module

- Gauge factor of a typical strain gauge: **G ≈ 2.0**
- Poisson's ratio for steel: **ν ≈ 0.30**
- Temperature coefficient of resistance for platinum (RTD): **α ≈ 3.85 × 10⁻³ /°C**
- Standard reference temperature for RTDs: **T₀ = 0 °C (273.15 K)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Transducers

Mechanical transducers are devices that convert the measurand into a mechanical output — typically a displacement, a force, or a pressure change. They are the workhorses of the *primary sensing stage* and are usually paired with an electrical transducer downstream.

### 2.1.1 Bourdon Tube
A curved tube of elliptical cross-section. When internal pressure increases, the tube tends to straighten. The tip displacement is proportional to the applied pressure.

- **Working principle:** Pressure → Elastic deformation → Tip displacement
- **Range:** 0.6 bar to 7000 bar
- **Common types:** C-type, helical, spiral

### 2.1.2 Bellows
A corrugated cylindrical element that elongates axially when internal pressure is applied.

- **Working principle:** Pressure → Axial extension
- **Sensitivity:** Much higher than Bourdon tube for low pressures
- **Typical range:** Up to ~7 bar

### 2.1.3 Diaphragm
A thin circular plate (flat or corrugated) clamped at its edges. Pressure difference across it causes center deflection.

- **Working principle:** Differential pressure → Center deflection (y)
- **Formula (flat circular, small deflection):**
$y = \dfrac{3}{16} \cdot \dfrac{(1-\nu^2)}{E \cdot t^3} \cdot (R^4) \cdot \Delta P$

where $E$ = Young's modulus, $t$ = thickness, $R$ = radius, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio.

### 2.1.4 Cantilever / Spring Element
A beam fixed at one end. An applied force causes deflection at the free end.

- **Working principle:** Force → Linear displacement
- **Deflection formula:**
$y = \dfrac{F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$

where $I$ = moment of inertia of the beam cross-section.

### 2.1.5 Manometer (U-Tube)
A fluid column device that balances an unknown pressure against a known column height.

- **Working principle:** Pressure → Liquid column height
- **Formula:**
$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$

where $\rho$ = fluid density, $g$ = gravitational acceleration, $h$ = height difference.

---

## 2.2 Electrical Transducers

Electrical transducers convert the measurand (or a mechanical signal from a primary element) directly into an electrical quantity: resistance, inductance, capacitance, voltage, or current.

### 2.2.1 Resistive Transducers

#### Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)
Metal wire (typically platinum) whose resistance varies linearly with temperature.

$$R_t = R_0 [1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$$

Higher-order Callendar–Van Dusen form:

$$R_t = R_0 (1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$$

where $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$, and $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient of resistance.

#### Thermistor
A semiconductor device with strong negative (NTC) or positive (PTC) temperature coefficient.

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

where $B$ = characteristic constant (typically 3000–5000 K).

#### Strain Gauge
A foil or wire grid whose resistance changes with mechanical strain.

$$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$

Differentiating and applying strain $\epsilon = \Delta L / L$:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \epsilon - 2\nu\epsilon$$

The **gauge factor** is defined as:

$$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\epsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\epsilon}$$

### 2.2.2 Inductive Transducers — Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)
A transformer with one primary and two secondary windings on a movable core. Displacement of the core produces a differential output voltage.

For small displacements, the output is linear:

$$V_o = K \cdot V_s \cdot x$$

where $K$ is sensitivity, $V_s$ is the excitation voltage, and $x$ is core displacement from null.

### 2.2.3 Capacitive Transducers
A capacitor whose capacitance varies with displacement, area change, or dielectric change.

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

Three modes of operation:
1. **Variable distance (d)** — most sensitive
2. **Variable area (A)** — linear
3. **Variable dielectric ($\varepsilon_r$)** — used for level measurement

### 2.2.4 Piezoelectric Transducers
Certain crystals (quartz, barium titanate) generate charge when mechanically stressed — the direct piezoelectric effect.

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

$$V = \frac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$$

where $d$ = charge sensitivity (pC/N), $C_p$ = crystal capacitance, $F$ = applied force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piezoelectric transducers CANNOT measure static quantities.** They respond only to dynamic changes (vibration, acceleration), because accumulated charge leaks away over time.

### 2.2.5 Photovoltaic / Photoelectric Transducers
Light incident on a PN junction produces a voltage (photovoltaic mode) or varies current (photoconductive mode).

$$V_{oc} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Transducer | Output Quantity | Governing Equation | Key Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bourdon Tube | Tip displacement $x$ | $x \propto \Delta P$ | Pressure $\Delta P$ |
| Diaphragm | Center deflection $y$ | $y = \dfrac{3(1-\nu^2)R^4}{16Et^3} \Delta P$ | $E, t, R, \nu$ |
| Cantilever beam | Deflection $y$ | $y = \dfrac{FL^3}{3EI}$ | $F, L, E, I$ |
| Manometer | Height $h$ | $P = \rho g h$ | $\rho, g, h$ |
| RTD | Resistance $R_t$ | $R_t = R_0[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$ | $R_0, \alpha, T$ |
| Thermistor (NTC) | Resistance $R_T$ | $R_T = R_0 \exp\left[B\left(\tfrac{1}{T} - \tfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | $B, T, T_0$ |
| Strain Gauge | $\Delta R / R$ | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \epsilon$ | Gauge factor $G$, strain $\epsilon$ |
| LVDT | Output voltage $V_o$ | $V_o = K V_s x$ | $K, V_s, x$ |
| Capacitive | Capacitance $C$ | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $\varepsilon_0, \varepsilon_r, A, d$ |
| Piezoelectric | Charge $Q$ | $Q = d \cdot F$ | $d, F$ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Typical Transducer Combination |
|---|---|
| **Oil & Gas Process Plants** | Bourdon tube + LVDT (pressure transmitter) |
| **Automotive Weigh Bridges** | Cantilever beam + Strain gauge (load cell) |
| **HVAC & Refrigeration** | RTD or Thermistor (temperature) |
| **Vibration Monitoring of Turbines** | Piezoelectric accelerometer |
| **Tank Level Measurement** | Capacitive probe with variable dielectric |
| **Industrial Weighing Scales** | Diaphragm + Strain gauge bridge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight** — In real industrial transmitters, you almost NEVER see a single transducer. You always see a **mechanical-to-electrical cascade**: e.g., pressure → Bourdon tube (mechanical) → LVDT core (electrical) → 4–20 mA current loop output.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Strain Gauge Gauge Factor (Complete)

The strain gauge is one of the most heavily tested topics in KTU university examinations. We now derive the gauge factor equation rigorously.

### Step 1: Starting Resistance Equation

A strain gauge is a metallic conductor of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$. Its resistance is given by:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

### Step 2: Apply Small Differential Changes

When the gauge is strained, all three quantities change. Taking the natural log of both sides:

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

Differentiating both sides:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

### Step 3: Express Changes in Terms of Strain

By definition, axial strain $\epsilon = \dfrac{dL}{L}$.

The lateral strain is governed by Poisson's ratio $\nu$:

$$\frac{dr}{r} = -\nu \epsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = -2\nu \epsilon$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \epsilon - (-2\nu \epsilon)$$

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \epsilon + 2\nu \epsilon$$

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \epsilon$$

### Step 4: Define the Gauge Factor

The gauge factor is defined as the ratio of fractional resistance change to applied strain:

$$G = \frac{dR / R}{\epsilon}$$

Therefore, dividing through by $\epsilon$:

$$G = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{1}{\epsilon} \cdot \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

For metallic strain gauges, the piezoresistive term $\dfrac{1}{\epsilon} \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$ is small but not negligible. For constantan, it is near zero, giving $G \approx 2\nu + 1 \approx 1.6$. For platinum alloys, $G \approx 2.0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LVDT Output Voltage (Excitation Model)

### Step 1: Setup

The LVDT has a primary winding excited by $V_s$ and two identical secondary windings $S_1$ and $S_2$ connected in series opposition. A movable core couples flux between the windings.

### Step 2: EMF Induced in Each Secondary

Let $N_p, N_s$ be turns and $\phi_1, \phi_2$ be the fluxes linked by $S_1$ and $S_2$. The induced EMFs are:

$$E_1 = -N_s \frac{d\phi_1}{dt} \quad ; \quad E_2 = -N_s \frac{d\phi_2}{dt}$$

### Step 3: Differential Output

Because the secondaries are connected in opposition, the net output is:

$$V_o = E_1 - E_2$$

For a sinusoidal excitation $V_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, the mutual inductance between primary and each secondary depends linearly on core position $x$. Assuming linearity near the null point:

$$V_o = V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot \left[ M_1(x) - M_2(x) \right]$$

### Step 4: Linear Approximation

Near the central (null) position, the difference in mutual inductance is proportional to $x$:

$$M_1(x) - M_2(x) = K \cdot x$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{V_o = K \cdot V_s \cdot x}$$

where $K$ is the LVDT sensitivity (V/V/m) and $x$ is displacement from null.

### Step 5: Phase Information

The **sign** of $x$ is encoded in the **phase** of $V_o$ relative to $V_s$. When the core moves toward $S_1$, $V_o$ is in phase with $V_s$; when it moves toward $S_2$, $V_o$ is 180° out of phase. This is how an LVDT determines the *direction* of displacement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Diaphragm Deflection (Flat Circular Plate)

### Step 1: Geometry and Loading

A circular diaphragm of radius $R$, thickness $t$, clamped at its edge, is subjected to a uniform differential pressure $\Delta P$.

### Step 2: Plate Equation

For small deflections, classical plate theory gives the deflection $y(r)$ at radius $r$:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left[ r \frac{d}{dr} \left( \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dy}{dr}\right) \right) \right] = \frac{\Delta P}{D}$$

where $D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ is the **flexural rigidity**.

### Step 3: Integration (Boundary Conditions: $y = 0$ and $dy/dr = 0$ at $r = R$)

Solving with appropriate boundary conditions yields the center deflection:

$$\boxed{y_{max} = \frac{3}{16} \cdot \frac{(1 - \nu^2)}{E \cdot t^3} \cdot R^4 \cdot \Delta P}$$

### Step 4: Sensitivity Analysis

- Doubling radius $R$ increases sensitivity by $2^4 = 16\times$ — *most powerful design lever*.
- Doubling thickness $t$ reduces sensitivity by $2^3 = 8\times$.
- Doubling Young's modulus $E$ halves sensitivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway** — To make a more sensitive pressure diaphragm, make it **larger in diameter** and **thinner**, using a low-$E$ material.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — RTD Temperature Calculation

**Problem:** A platinum RTD has $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $T_0 = 0\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\ /\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Find its resistance at $T = 150\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Use the linear approximation.

### Solution

Using the linear RTD equation:

$$R_t = R_0[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$$

Substituting:

$$R_t = 100 \left[ 1 + (3.85 \times 10^{-3})(150 - 0) \right]$$

$$R_t = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.5775 \right]$$

$$R_t = 100 \times 1.5775$$

$$\boxed{R_t = 157.75\ \Omega}$$

This value is consistent with the international standard IEC 60751 for PT100 sensors.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Capacitive Displacement Sensor

**Problem:** A parallel-plate capacitive transducer has $A = 25\ \text{mm}^2$ and nominal gap $d = 0.5\ \text{mm}$. The dielectric is air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$). Find the capacitance and the sensitivity $\dfrac{dC}{dd}$.

### Solution

Nominal capacitance:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(25 \times 10^{-6})}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C = \frac{2.214 \times 10^{-16}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.43 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{F}$$

$$C \approx 0.443\ \text{pF}$$

Sensitivity (differentiating $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$):

$$\frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2} = -\frac{C}{d}$$

$$\frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{4.43 \times 10^{-13}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = -8.85 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F/m}$$

The negative sign indicates that capacitance *decreases* as gap *increases* — an inverse, non-linear (1/d) relationship.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
