# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Definition and Classification `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a transducer. Distinguish between active and passive transducers with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**
A transducer is a device that converts energy from one form to another, typically converting a physical measurand into an electrical signal.

**Active (self-generating) transducers** produce an electrical output *without* requiring an external excitation source. Energy conversion happens directly.
- Examples: Piezoelectric crystal, thermocouple, photovoltaic cell.

**Passive transducers** require an external excitation (voltage or current source). The measurand *modulates* an electrical parameter (R, L, or C).
- Examples: RTD (R changes), LVDT (L changes), capacitive displacement sensor (C changes).

*(Examiner note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the distinction with examples.)*

---

### Question 2 — Bourdon Tube Working `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a C-type Bourdon tube used for pressure measurement.**

**Model Answer:**
A C-type Bourdon tube is a curved tube of elliptical cross-section, bent into a C-shape and closed at one end. When the fluid whose pressure is to be measured enters the open end, the internal pressure tends to straighten the curved tube (because an elliptical cross-section becomes more circular under pressure, increasing its cross-sectional area). This causes the closed tip to displace by an amount proportional to the applied pressure.

The tip is mechanically linked to a pointer via a rack-and-pinion or lever mechanism, producing a readable pressure indication. The relationship between pressure and tip displacement is approximately linear over the working range. Typical operating range is 0.6 bar to 7000 bar depending on tube material and geometry.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks) — Strain Gauge and RTD `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Derive the expression for the gauge factor of a strain gauge. A metal strain gauge has a nominal resistance of 120 Ω and gauge factor 2.1. If it is subjected to a strain of 1000 micro-strain, calculate the change in resistance.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Starting from $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ and taking differentials:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting $\Delta L / L = \epsilon$ and $\Delta A / A = -2\nu\epsilon$:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \epsilon + 2\nu\epsilon$$

**Step 3:** Defining gauge factor $G = (\Delta R / R) / \epsilon$:

$$G = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{1}{\epsilon} \cdot \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical calculation with $R = 120\ \Omega$, $G = 2.1$, $\epsilon = 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}$:

$$\Delta R = G \cdot \epsilon \cdot R = 2.1 \times 10^{-3} \times 120$$

$$\Delta R = 0.252\ \Omega$$

**Answer:** $\Delta R = 0.252\ \Omega$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the working principle of an RTD. A platinum RTD has $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at 0 °C. If $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\ /\ ^\circ\text{C}$, find its resistance at 250 °C. Mention two advantages and two disadvantages of RTDs.**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
An RTD operates on the principle that the electrical resistance of a pure metal increases linearly with temperature, due to increased lattice vibration scattering of conduction electrons. Platinum is the preferred metal because of its chemical stability, linear response, and high melting point.

**Resistance Calculation:**

$$R_t = R_0[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$$

$$R_t = 100 [1 + (3.85 \times 10^{-3})(250 - 0)]$$

$$R_t = 100 [1 + 0.9625]$$

$$\boxed{R_t = 196.25\ \Omega}$$

**Advantages:**
1. Higher accuracy and better linearity than thermocouples and thermistors.
2. Excellent stability and repeatability over long periods (drift < 0.05 °C/year for PT100).

**Disadvantages:**
1. Lower sensitivity compared to thermistors (small $\Delta R$ per °C).
2. Requires lead-wire compensation (3-wire or 4-wire bridge) due to lead resistance errors.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — LVDT and Piezoelectric `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With a block diagram, explain the construction and working of an LVDT. Derive the expression for its output voltage. Why is the phase of the output important?**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
An LVDT consists of:
- One **primary winding** (P) wound centrally on a hollow cylindrical former.
- Two identical **secondary windings** ($S_1$ and $S_2$) wound on either side of the primary.
- A movable **ferromagnetic core** that slides axially inside the former.

**Working:**
When the primary is energized by an AC excitation $V_s$, alternating flux is produced. The flux linking with $S_1$ and $S_2$ depends on the core position. The two secondaries are connected in series opposition.

**Derivation:**
Let $M_1$ and $M_2$ be mutual inductances between primary and the two secondaries.

$$V_{S1} = j\omega M_1 V_s / j\omega L_p \quad ; \quad V_{S2} = j\omega M_2 V_s / j\omega L_p$$

Net output (series opposition):

$$V_o = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = \frac{M_1 - M_2}{L_p} V_s$$

Near the null position, $M_1 - M_2 \propto x$, so:

$$\boxed{V_o = K V_s x}$$

**Phase Importance:**
The phase of $V_o$ relative to $V_s$ indicates the **direction** of core displacement:
- Core displaced toward $S_1$: $V_o$ in phase with $V_s$.
- Core displaced toward $S_2$: $V_o$ is 180° out of phase.
- At null: $V_o = 0$.

This makes the LVDT a **bidirectional** displacement sensor.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the piezoelectric effect. Derive the expression for charge sensitivity. Mention two industrial applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Direct Piezoelectric Effect:**
Discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880, certain asymmetric crystals (quartz, Rochelle salt, barium titanate, PZT) generate an electric charge on their surfaces when subjected to mechanical stress.

**Derivation:**
Let $d$ be the **charge sensitivity** (in coulombs per newton), and $F$ be the applied force. The generated charge is:

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

The voltage developed across the crystal (with capacitance $C_p$) is:

$$V = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$$

Since $C_p = \varepsilon A / t$, we can also write the **voltage sensitivity** $g$:

$$g = \frac{d}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot t}{\varepsilon A}$$

$$\boxed{V = g \cdot t \cdot \frac{F}{A} = g \cdot t \cdot \sigma}$$

where $\sigma$ = applied stress (N/m²).

**Important Limitation:**
Piezoelectric transducers measure only **dynamic** quantities (vibration, dynamic pressure, acceleration). They cannot measure static forces because the generated charge leaks away through the crystal's finite resistance over time.

**Industrial Applications:**
1. **Accelerometers** — for vibration monitoring in turbines, motors, and bearings.
2. **Ultrasonic transducers** — for non-destructive testing and medical imaging.
3. **Dynamic pressure sensors** — in engine cylinder pressure measurement.
4. **Microphones and acoustic pickups**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Active vs Passive** — Many students incorrectly classify an LVDT as "active" because it produces a voltage. **Reality:** LVDT is *passive* — it requires an external AC excitation. Only self-generating devices (piezoelectric, thermocouple, photovoltaic) are active.
>
> 2. **Piezoelectric "Static Force" Error** — A frequent KTU question tests whether you know piezoelectric sensors CANNOT measure static quantities. Writing "piezoelectric pressure gauge" without this caveat costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Missing Sign Convention in LVDT** — When solving problems, always specify that the *phase* encodes direction. Just stating $|V_o| = KV_s x$ loses the directional information.
>
> 4. **RTD Non-Linearity** — At very high temperatures (> 600 °C), the linear RTD equation becomes inaccurate. The Callendar–Van Dusen equation must be used. Forgetting this loses 1 mark in numerical questions.
>
> 5. **Strain Gauge Bridge Wiring** — Students often forget that a single active strain gauge has very low output (~mV); practical systems use a **Wheatstone bridge** with 1, 2, or 4 active gauges. Drawing only the gauge without the bridge costs marks.
>
> 6. **Units Confusion** — Always write units: $R$ in ohms, $T$ in Kelvin for thermistor equation, $T$ in °C for linear RTD equation, $\epsilon$ dimensionless for strain.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Definition**: Transducer = energy form converter; in instrumentation, typically physical → electrical.
- **Two families**: Mechanical (primary, output is displacement/force) and Electrical (secondary, output is V/I/R).
- **Active vs Passive**: Active = self-generating (no excitation); Passive = needs excitation (RTD, LVDT, capacitive).
- **Bourdon Tube**: Pressure → tip displacement. C-type, helical, spiral variants.
- **Bellows**: Higher sensitivity than Bourdon at low pressures.
- **Diaphragm**: $y_{max} \propto R^4 / t^3$; very sensitive to radius.
- **Cantilever**: $y = F L^3 / (3 E I)$; foundation of load cells.
- **Manometer**: $P = \rho g h$; simplest pressure measurement.
- **RTD**: $R_t = R_0 [1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$; platinum PT100 standard; high accuracy.
- **Thermistor**: $R_T = R_0 \exp[B(1/T - 1/T_0)]$; highly non-linear, high sensitivity, semiconductor.
- **Strain Gauge**: $G = (1 + 2\nu) + (\Delta\rho/\rho)/\epsilon$; typical $G \approx 2.0$; bridge-based readout.
- **LVDT**: $V_o = K V_s x$; phase gives direction; rugged, infinite resolution.
- **Capacitive**: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$; three modes (d, A, $\varepsilon_r$).
- **Piezoelectric**: $Q = dF$; dynamic-only, no static measurement.
- **Photovoltaic**: $V_{oc} \propto \ln(I_L)$; used in light meters and solar cells.
- **Industrial Pattern**: Always expect *mechanical-to-electrical cascade* in transmitter questions.

### Key Engineering Numbers to Memorize

- Platinum RTD $\alpha$ = **3.85 × 10⁻³ /°C**
- Typical strain gauge $G$ ≈ **2.0**
- Poisson's ratio of steel $\nu$ ≈ **0.30**
- Standard reference $T_0$ = **0 °C** (273.15 K)
- Permittivity of free space $\varepsilon_0$ = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**

### Exam Strategy — How to Score Full Marks

1. Always start with a **neat labeled diagram** — 1 to 2 marks reserved for it.
2. Quote the **defining equation** with all variables defined.
3. Show **step-by-step substitution** in numerical problems; do not skip arithmetic.
4. End with a **boxed answer** for clarity.
5. For 14-mark questions, mention at least **two practical examples** and **two limitations** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Density Measurement Using Constant Volume Hydrometer and Air Pressure Balance Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Density Measurement Using Constant Volume Hydrometer and Air Pressure Balance Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Industrial Need for Density Measurement

**Density** ($\rho$) is defined as the mass per unit volume of a substance, expressed in **kg/m³** (SI) or **g/cm³** (CGS). In the process industries, density is a critical quality variable because it directly correlates with concentration, composition, and purity of liquids and slurries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 (Density / Specific Gravity)**
> According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBBT724)** syllabus, students must be able to explain, sketch, and derive the working equations for the **Constant Volume Hydrometer** and the **Air Pressure Balance** method. These two techniques represent the *classical (gravitational)* and *pneumatic (manometric)* families of density measurement, respectively.

**Specific Gravity (SG)** is a dimensionless ratio:

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{\text{liquid}}}{\rho_{\text{water at } 4^\circ C}}$$

where $\rho_{\text{water at } 4^\circ C} = \mathbf{1000 \ kg/m^3}$.

---

### 1.2 Constant Volume Hydrometer — Formal Definition

A **Constant Volume Hydrometer** (also known as a **Weight Hydrometer** or **Westphal Balance type**) is a buoyancy-based densitometer in which a plummet (displacer) of *fixed volume* $V_d$ is fully immersed in the test liquid up to a *fixed reference mark*. The depth of immersion is kept constant; **density is inferred from the additional weights required on a balance pan to restore equilibrium**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "constant volume"?** In the standard variable-depth hydrometer, the immersed volume changes with density. Here, we deliberately keep $V_d$ constant by always sinking the plummet to the same index mark, and we read density through the *mass compensation* on the pan.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Constant Volume Hydrometer

Imagine pushing a sealed, hollow glass ball (a *plummet*) under water with your finger. The deeper you push, the more the water pushes back. To keep your finger at a *fixed depth*, you have to push harder in **salt water** (denser) than in **fresh water** (less dense). The "extra push" you feel is exactly proportional to the density of the liquid.

In the constant volume hydrometer, the **"extra push"** is provided by adding small calibrated **weights** to the pan. The heavier the weights needed, the denser the liquid. The plummet stays at the same depth — only the weights change.

---

### 1.4 Air Pressure Balance Method — Formal Definition

The **Air Pressure Balance Method** (also called the **Air Purge Method**, **Bubbler Method**, or **Differential Manometer Method**) is a manometric technique in which **low-flow-rate air is bubbled** through two vertical columns of liquid — one containing a *reference fluid* of known density $\rho_{ref}$ and the other containing the *sample fluid* of unknown density $\rho_s$. A differential pressure transmitter reads the pressure difference between the bottoms of the two columns; this differential is directly proportional to the difference in densities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "air pressure balance"?** The bubbling air acts as a *pneumatic cushion* that transmits the hydrostatic head from the bottom of each column to the differential manometer. Because the air flow is kept *just high enough* to maintain bubbling, the pressure drop in the air lines is **negligible**, ensuring that the manometer reads the true hydrostatic difference.

### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy — Air Pressure Balance Method

Picture two identical drinking straws standing upright in two glasses — one filled with water, the other with honey. If you blow gently through both straws from the top, the pressure you must exert at the top to push a bubble out of the honey glass is **higher** than that needed for the water glass — because the denser honey column has more "weight" pushing down on the air.

Now measure that *pressure difference* with a U-tube manometer, and you can compute exactly how much denser the honey is than the water. This is the essence of the Air Pressure Balance method.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Module Link:** This topic links directly to Module 1 (Pressure Measurement) because the differential manometer here is the same instrument used for orifice / venturi flow, and to Module 3 (Level Measurement) because the bubbler principle is identical to a dip-tube level measurement.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Constant Volume Hydrometer

**Mechanical Construction:**
- A **plummet** (a heavy, sealed, precision-machined body) of known volume $V_d$ and known dry weight $W_p$.
- A thin suspension wire connecting the plummet to a **balance pan** suspended from a sensitive beam balance or electronic force sensor.
- A **fixed index mark** etched either on the suspension wire or on a guide tube. The plummet is always lowered until this mark aligns with the liquid surface — guaranteeing a constant immersed volume $V_d$.
- A set of **calibrated standard weights** to be placed on the pan.

**Step-by-Step Operating Logic:**

1. The plummet is suspended in air, and the balance is **zeroed** (or tared). The dry weight reading is $W_p$.
2. The plummet is lowered into the test liquid until the index mark coincides with the free liquid surface.
3. Due to buoyancy, the apparent weight on the pan *decreases* by an amount equal to the weight of liquid displaced: $F_b = \rho_s \, V_d \, g$.
4. The operator (or automatic servo) **adds calibrated weights** $W_a$ to the pan until the beam is re-balanced.
5. The added weight exactly equals the buoyancy loss: $W_a = \rho_s \, V_d \, g$.

**The "Why" Behind the Constant Volume Choice:**
- A standard hydrometer's scale is **non-linear** and short-range. Industrial liquids span 600 – 2500 kg/m³, requiring multiple hydrometers.
- By making the *volume* the fixed quantity, we obtain a **linear relationship** between density and weight, enabling a single instrument with interchangeable plummets of different $V_d$ to cover wide ranges.

---

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Air Pressure Balance Method

**Constructional Setup:**
- A **regulated, low-pressure air supply** (typically 0.2 – 0.5 bar above process pressure) feeds a **rotameter** or precision needle valve to limit flow.
- The air stream splits into two paths through **identical flow-restrictors**, ensuring equal, low flow rates.
- Each path terminates at the **bottom of a vertical column**: the **reference column** (containing a fluid of known density $\rho_{ref}$, often water or another standard liquid) and the **sample column** (containing the process fluid of unknown density $\rho_s$).
- **Pressure tappings** at the air-injection point of each column route to a **differential pressure (DP) cell** or U-tube manometer.
- The vertical liquid column length is identical ($L$) for both sides.

**Step-by-Step Operating Logic:**

1. Air is admitted at a *very low flow rate* — just enough to sustain a slow stream of bubbles from the bottom of each column.
2. At the air injection point, the **air pressure equals the hydrostatic head** of the liquid column above plus the surface (vapour) pressure. The surface pressure is essentially equal in both columns because they are both vented (or both connected to a common gas space).
3. Because the air flow rate is low and identical, the **frictional pressure drop** in the two air lines is identical and cancels out in the differential measurement.
4. The differential pressure cell therefore reads **only the difference in hydrostatic heads**:

$$\Delta P = (\rho_s - \rho_{ref}) \, g \, L$$

5. From this single measurement, $\rho_s$ is computed linearly.

**Why Low Flow Rate?**
If flow is high, the air's own dynamic head and line friction add error. If flow is zero, the bubbles stop and the system plugs. The "**purge flow**" is the engineering sweet spot — typically a few bubbles per second.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units (SI) | Boundary / Limiting Condition |
|---|----------|---------------|-----------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | $W_a = \rho_s \, V_d \, g$ | Density from added weight | $\rho_s$ in kg/m³, $V_d$ in m³, $W_a$ in N | Valid only when plummet is fully immersed to index mark |
| 2 | $\rho_s = \dfrac{W_p + W_a}{V_d \, g}$ | Density from total weight on pan | kg/m³ | $W_p$ = dry weight of plummet; total downward force must balance buoyancy |
| 3 | $F_b = \rho_s \, V_d \, g$ | Buoyancy force on plummet | N (Newton) | Archimedes' principle |
| 4 | $\Delta P = (\rho_s - \rho_{ref}) \, g \, L$ | DP cell output for Air Pressure Balance | Pa (N/m²) | Equal column length $L$; equal air flow in both lines |
| 5 | $\rho_s = \rho_{ref} + \dfrac{\Delta P}{g \, L}$ | Density computation from $\Delta P$ | kg/m³ | $\rho_{ref}$ usually water ($\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³) |
| 6 | $\Delta P_{\text{manometer}} = (\rho_s - \rho_{ref}) \, g \, L$ | U-tube manometer reading (in metres of manometer fluid) | m | Convert Pa to m of fluid: $h_m = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho_m \, g}$ |
| 7 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho_w}$ | Specific Gravity (dimensionless) | — | $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ at 4 °C |
| 8 | Range $= \dfrac{W_{a,\max} - W_{a,\min}}{V_d \, g}$ | Measurable density range of a single plummet | kg/m³ | Determined by smallest and largest standard weights |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In every density-related derivation, the KTU answer-script evaluator looks for *three* explicit elements: (i) the **Archimedes buoyancy statement**, (ii) the **force / pressure balance equation**, and (iii) the **final isolated expression for $\rho_s$**. Missing any one of these costs 2–3 marks out of 7.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Constant Volume Hydrometer:** Used in **dairy industry** (cream/milk fat testing via Gerber & Westphal balances), **battery acid** concentration checks, **petroleum** API gravity estimation in laboratories, and **brewery** wort density measurement.
- **Air Pressure Balance Method:** Used in **mining slurry pipelines**, **wastewater treatment** (sludge density), **pulping** (black liquor density in paper mills), **chlor-alkali plants** (brine concentration), and **caustic / acid storage tanks** where the liquid is too corrosive for direct contact sensors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Constant Volume Hydrometer

We start from the principle that, at equilibrium, the **net downward force** equals the **net upward buoyancy force**.

**Step 1 — Identify all downward forces (acting on the beam balance through the pan and plummet):**
- Weight of plummet in air: $W_p$ (N)
- Weight of added standard masses on the pan: $W_a$ (N)
- Total downward force:

$$F_{\text{down}} = W_p + W_a$$

**Step 2 — Identify the upward force:**
By Archimedes' principle, the upward buoyancy force on the fully immersed plummet equals the weight of the displaced liquid:

$$F_{\text{up}} = \rho_s \, V_d \, g$$

where $\rho_s$ is the density of the sample liquid (kg/m³), $V_d$ is the immersed volume of the plummet (m³), and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity (m/s²).

**Step 3 — Apply static equilibrium:**

$$F_{\text{down}} = F_{\text{up}}$$

$$W_p + W_a = \rho_s \, V_d \, g$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the unknown density $\rho_s$:**

$$\rho_s = \frac{W_p + W_a}{V_d \, g}$$

This is the **primary working equation** for the constant volume hydrometer.

**Step 5 — Equivalent form for weight-only operation (when plummet is pre-tared):**
If the balance is **tared** (zeroed) with the plummet hanging in air, then the balance reads only the apparent weight loss, which is exactly equal to the buoyancy force. Therefore the added weights directly give:

$$W_a = \rho_s \, V_d \, g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_s = \frac{W_a}{V_d \, g}$$

This second form is the most commonly used in KTU numerical problems.

**Step 6 — Sensitivity & Range Analysis:**
The measurable density span for a given plummet is:

$$\Delta\rho = \frac{W_{a,\max}}{V_d \, g} - \frac{W_{a,\min}}{V_d \, g}$$

Larger $V_d$ → higher sensitivity (more buoyancy force per unit density change), but smaller range. Smaller $V_d$ → wider range, lower sensitivity. Plummets are therefore *interchangeable* to cover different ranges.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Air Pressure Balance Method

**Step 1 — Define column geometry:**
Both columns are vertical, of equal immersed length $L$ (m), measured from the air injection point at the bottom to the free liquid surface at the top. Air enters at the bottom of each column and bubbles slowly upward.

**Step 2 — Write the pressure at the bottom of the reference column:**
Let $P_{\text{top}}$ be the (common) gas pressure just above the liquid surface in both columns. For an open vented system, $P_{\text{top}} \approx P_{\text{atm}}$; for a closed tank, both columns share the same vapour-space pressure.

Pressure at the bottom of the reference column = top pressure + hydrostatic head of reference liquid:

$$P_{\text{bottom,ref}} = P_{\text{top}} + \rho_{ref} \, g \, L$$

**Step 3 — Write the pressure at the bottom of the sample column:**
Similarly, using the sample density $\rho_s$:

$$P_{\text{bottom,sample}} = P_{\text{top}} + \rho_s \, g \, L$$

**Step 4 — Form the differential pressure measured by the DP cell / manometer:**

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{bottom,sample}} - P_{\text{bottom,ref}}$$

Substituting the two expressions:

$$\Delta P = \bigl(P_{\text{top}} + \rho_s \, g \, L\bigr) - \bigl(P_{\text{top}} + \rho_{ref} \, g \, L\bigr)$$

The $P_{\text{top}}$ terms cancel exactly:

$$\Delta P = \rho_s \, g \, L - \rho_{ref} \, g \, L$$

$$\Delta P = (\rho_s - \rho_{ref}) \, g \, L$$

**Step 5 — Isolate the unknown sample density $\rho_s$:**

$$\rho_s = \rho_{ref} + \frac{\Delta P}{g \, L}$$

This is the **master equation** for the Air Pressure Balance method.

**Step 6 — Convert manometer reading (in mm of water / mercury) to Pa:**
A U-tube manometer with manometer fluid of density $\rho_m$ and deflection $h_m$ (m) gives:

$$\Delta P = \rho_m \, g \, h_m$$

Substituting into the master equation:

$$\rho_s = \rho_{ref} + \frac{\rho_m \, h_m}{L}$$

This form is very convenient for KTU numerical problems where the reading is in **mm of water column**.

**Step 7 — Special case: Reference fluid is air (used for low-density liquids or gases):**
If $\rho_{ref} = \rho_{\text{air}} \approx 1.2$ kg/m³ (negligible), the equation reduces to:

$$\rho_s \approx \frac{\Delta P}{g \, L}$$

with the column length chosen sufficiently long to amplify the small $\Delta P$ into a measurable manometer deflection.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Constant Volume Hydrometer | Air Pressure Balance Method |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Primary principle** | Archimedes buoyancy on a fixed-volume plummet | Hydrostatic head comparison via air purge |
| **Measurand** | Weight $W_a$ (mechanical/electronic) | Differential pressure $\Delta P$ |
| **Output type** | Discrete (weights) → continuous (electronic load cell) | Continuous pneumatic / electronic DP signal |
| **Range extension** | Interchangeable plummets of different $V_d$ | Variable column length $L$ |
| **Best for** | Laboratory, manual, or batch density checks | Continuous in-line slurry / corrosive liquid density |
| **Contact with liquid** | Plummet must be corrosion-resistant | Only the bubbler tip contacts the liquid |
| **Limitation** | Manual operation; solid-laden liquids foul the plummet | Requires clean, dry air supply; column must be vertical |
| **Typical accuracy** | ± 0.1 % of reading (lab grade) | ± 1 % of full scale (industrial grade) |
| **KTU weightage** | 7–14 marks (favoured for derivation questions) | 7 marks (favoured for descriptive + numerical) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Constant Volume Hydrometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AirCheck[Liquid Sample in Container]
    Plummet[Plummet of Known Volume Vd and Dry Weight Wp]
    Wire[Suspension Wire]
    IndexMark[Fixed Index Mark on Wire]
    Beam[Precision Beam Balance / Load Cell]
    Pan[Balance Pan]
    Weights[Calibrated Standard Weights Wa]
    Reading[Density Output rho equals Wa over Vd times g]

    AirCheck --> Plummet
    Plummet --> Wire
    Wire --> IndexMark
    Wire --> Beam
    Beam --> Pan
    Pan --> Weights
    Weights --> Reading
    IndexMark -.aligns with liquid surface.-> AirCheck
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Air Pressure Balance Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AirSupply[Clean Dry Air Supply]
    Regulator[Pressure Regulator]
    Filter[Filter and Moisture Separator]
    Rotameter[Rotameter for Low Flow Setting]
    RefColumn[Reference Column with Reference Liquid density rho ref]
    SampleColumn[Sample Column with Process Liquid density rho s]
    RefBubble[Air Bubbles Rise Through Reference Liquid]
    SampleBubble[Air Bubbles Rise Through Sample Liquid]
    TapRef[Pressure Tap at Bottom of Reference Column]
    TapSample[Pressure Tap at Bottom of Sample Column]
    DPMeter[Differential Pressure Cell or U Tube Manometer]
    Output[Density Output rho s equals rho ref plus deltaP over g times L]

    AirSupply --> Regulator
    Regulator --> Filter
    Filter --> Rotameter
    Rotameter --> RefColumn
    Rotameter --> SampleColumn
    RefColumn --> RefBubble
    SampleColumn --> SampleBubble
    RefBubble --> TapRef
    SampleBubble --> TapSample
    TapRef --> DPMeter
    TapSample --> DPMeter
    DPMeter --> Output
```

### 4.3 Process Schematic — Typical Industrial Installation of Air Pressure Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Tank[Process Tank or Pipeline]
    RefVessel[Reference Vessel with Known Liquid]
    Manifold[Air Distribution Manifold]
    DPTrans[Differential Pressure Transmitter]
    Indicator[Density Indicator or Controller]

    Tank --> SampleBubble1[Bubbler Probe 1 Inside Process Liquid]
    RefVessel --> SampleBubble2[Bubbler Probe 2 Inside Reference Liquid]
    SampleBubble1 --> Manifold
    SampleBubble2 --> Manifold
    Manifold --> DPTrans
    DPTrans --> Indicator
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each rectangular block represents a physical component or measurement node. Solid arrows indicate the *flow of fluid or signal*; dotted arrows (in diagram 4.1) indicate a *physical alignment reference* between the index mark and the free liquid surface.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define a *constant volume hydrometer* and explain why the immersed volume of the plummet is kept fixed during measurement.

**Model Answer:**
A constant volume hydrometer is a buoyancy-based densitometer in which a plummet of known volume $V_d$ is fully immersed in the test liquid up to a fixed reference mark. The density of the liquid is determined from the additional weights $W_a$ required on the pan to keep the plummet at the constant immersion depth.

The immersed volume is kept fixed because, by Archimedes' principle, the buoyancy force $F_b = \rho_s \, V_d \, g$ becomes a **linear function** of density when $V_d$ is constant. This linearity makes the instrument's calibration uniform, simplifies range switching (by swapping plummets), and avoids the non-linear scale compression seen in variable-depth hydrometers.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State the principle of the *Air Pressure Balance method* of density measurement. Why is the air flow rate maintained at a very low value?

**Model Answer:**
**Principle:** Air is bubbled slowly through two vertical columns of equal length $L$ — one containing a reference liquid of known density $\rho_{ref}$ and the other containing the sample liquid of unknown density $\rho_s$. The differential pressure between the bottoms of the two columns is measured and is given by $\Delta P = (\rho_s - \rho_{ref}) \, g \, L$, from which $\rho_s$ is computed.

**Why low flow rate:** A low (purge) flow rate is maintained so that (a) the frictional pressure drop in the two air lines is identical and cancels out in the differential measurement, (b) the dynamic head of the rising air column does not add an error term, and (c) the air does not agitate the liquid surface, which would distort the hydrostatic head.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Part B Module-2 questions carry **14 marks** with an internal choice. The standard split is **(a) 7 marks** (descriptive + derivation) and **(b) 7 marks** (numerical or extended discussion). The model answers below include full working.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **constant volume hydrometer**. Derive the expression relating the density of the liquid to the weights added on the pan. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
The instrument consists of (i) a heavy, sealed plummet (displacer) of precisely known volume $V_d$ and dry weight $W_p$, typically made of glass or stainless steel; (ii) a fine suspension wire attached to the plummet at one end and to a precision beam balance / electronic load cell at the other; (iii) a fixed index mark (notch or ring) etched on the wire; (iv) a balance pan for placing calibrated standard weights; and (v) a sample container holding the test liquid.

**Working:**
1. The plummet is hung in air, and the balance is tared (zeroed). The reading corresponds to $W_p$.
2. The plummet is lowered into the test liquid until the index mark just coincides with the free liquid surface. The immersed volume is now exactly $V_d$.
3. The buoyant force pushes the plummet upward, lightening the apparent weight on the pan.
4. Standard weights $W_a$ are placed on the pan until the beam balances again.
5. The added weights give the buoyancy loss, which equals the weight of displaced liquid.

**Derivation:**

At equilibrium, the upward buoyancy equals the total downward force on the plummet-pan assembly:

$$\rho_s \, V_d \, g = W_p + W_a$$

Solving for $\rho_s$:

$$\rho_s = \frac{W_p + W_a}{V_d \, g}$$

If the balance is pre-tared with the plummet in air, $W_p$ is cancelled and:

$$\rho_s = \frac{W_a}{V_d \, g}$$

This is the working equation. *(Full marks require the diagram, three labelled parts, and both equilibrium steps.)*

---

**(b)** A constant volume hydrometer uses a plummet of volume $V_d = 50 \text{ cm}^3$ and dry weight $W_p = 0.85 \text{ N}$. Calculate **(i)** the density of a liquid in which an additional weight of $0.39 \text{ N}$ restores balance, and **(ii)** the range of densities that can be measured if the available standard weights range from $0.05 \text{ N}$ to $0.95 \text{ N}$. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_d = 50 \text{ cm}^3 = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$
- $W_p = 0.85 \text{ N}$
- $W_a = 0.39 \text{ N}$ (for part i)
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Part (i):** Density of the unknown liquid.

Using the working equation (with $W_p$ included):

$$\rho_s = \frac{W_p + W_a}{V_d \, g}$$

$$\rho_s = \frac{0.85 + 0.39}{5 \times 10^{-5} \times 9.81}$$

$$\rho_s = \frac{1.24}{4.905 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_s \approx 2528 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Part (ii):** Density range with weights from $0.05 \text{ N}$ to $0.95 \text{ N}$.

**Lower bound** ($W_{a,\min} = 0.05 \text{ N}$):

$$\rho_{\min} = \frac{W_p + W_{a,\min}}{V_d \, g} = \frac{0.85 + 0.05}{4.905 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.90}{4.905 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1835 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Upper bound** ($W_{a,\max} = 0.95 \text{ N}$):

$$\rho_{\max} = \frac{W_p + W_{a,\max}}{V_d \, g} = \frac{0.85 + 0.95}{4.905 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1.80}{4.905 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3670 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\text{Range} = 1835 \text{ to } 3670 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Air Pressure Balance method** of density measurement. Derive the relation between the measured differential pressure and the unknown density. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
The setup consists of a clean, dry, regulated air supply feeding a common manifold, which splits into two identical flow paths through needle valves or rotameters. Each path terminates at the bottom of a vertical column. The **reference column** contains a liquid of known density $\rho_{ref}$ (typically water), and the **sample column** contains the process liquid of unknown density $\rho_s$. Both columns have identical liquid height $L$ above the air injection point. Pressure tappings at the bottom of each column are connected to the **high-pressure** and **low-pressure** ports of a differential pressure (DP) cell or a U-tube manometer.

**Working:**
1. Low-pressure air (slightly above process pressure) is admitted through the needle valves, producing a slow stream of bubbles in each column.
2. At the air injection point, the air pressure equals the hydrostatic head of the liquid column above plus the (common) top gas pressure.
3. Because the air flow is identical and very low, the line friction and dynamic head are equal on both sides and cancel in the differential reading.
4. The DP cell therefore reads only the difference in hydrostatic heads of the two liquids over the equal column length $L$.

**Derivation:**

Pressure at the bottom of the reference column:

$$P_{\text{ref}} = P_{\text{top}} + \rho_{ref} \, g \, L$$

Pressure at the bottom of the sample column:

$$P_{\text{sample}} = P_{\text{top}} + \rho_s \, g \, L$$

Differential pressure:

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{sample}} - P_{\text{ref}} = (\rho_s - \rho_{ref}) \, g \, L$$

Solving for $\rho_s$:

$$\rho_s = \rho_{ref} + \frac{\Delta P}{g \, L}$$

The DP cell's electrical / pneumatic output is therefore a **linear** function of the sample density — a major advantage for transmitter scaling.

---

**(b)** In an Air Pressure Balance density measurement system, the reference column contains water at 25 °C ($\rho_{ref} = 997 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The DP cell reads $\Delta P = 6.20 \text{ kPa}$. The column length is $L = 1.50 \text{ m}$. Calculate **(i)** the density of the sample liquid, **(ii)** the specific gravity, and **(iii)** the equivalent manometer deflection if a U-tube manometer using mercury ($\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$) were used instead of the DP cell. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\rho_{ref} = 997 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\Delta P = 6.20 \text{ kPa} = 6200 \text{ Pa}$
- $L = 1.50 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (for part iii)

**Part (i):** Sample density.

$$\rho_s = \rho_{ref} + \frac{\Delta P}{g \, L}$$

$$\rho_s = 997 + \frac{6200}{9.81 \times 1.50}$$

$$\rho_s = 997 + \frac{6200}{14.715}$$

$$\rho_s = 997 + 421.34$$

$$\boxed{\rho_s \approx 1418.3 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Part (ii):** Specific gravity.

$$SG = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_w} = \frac{1418.3}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{SG \approx 1.418}$$

**Part (iii):** Equivalent mercury manometer deflection.

For a U-tube manometer, $\Delta P = \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h_{Hg}$, so:

$$h_{Hg} = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho_{Hg} \, g} = \frac{6200}{13600 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_{Hg} = \frac{6200}{133416} \approx 0.0465 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_{Hg} \approx 46.5 \text{ mm of Hg}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions:** $V_d$ must be in m³ (not cm³) when $g$ is in m/s². Marks lost: **1–2**.
> 2. **Omitting $W_p$:** When the balance is *not* tared in air, students often write $\rho_s = W_a/(V_d g)$ and miss the plummet's dry weight $W_p$. Always check the wording. Marks lost: **2**.
> 3. **Sign convention in Air Pressure Balance:** If the sample is **lighter** than the reference, $\Delta P$ becomes **negative**; students sometimes report a negative density. The correct handling is to *re-define* which side is high-pressure, or use the absolute-value form with a labelled sign convention. Marks lost: **2–3**.
> 4. **Using $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ without stating the temperature:** Examiner expects a mention of "at 4 °C" or the temperature used. Marks lost: **0.5–1**.
> 5. **Skipping the assumption of equal column length $L$:** In derivation, the cancellation of $P_{\text{top}}$ assumes both columns have identical liquid height above the air injection point. Marks lost: **1–2**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Density** $\rho$ = mass / volume; **Specific Gravity** $SG = \rho / \rho_w$ where $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ at 4 °C.
- **Constant Volume Hydrometer** keeps the **immersed volume $V_d$** fixed; density is read from the **weights $W_a$ added to the pan** to restore balance.
- Master equation: $\rho_s = (W_p + W_a) / (V_d \, g)$, or with tared balance: $\rho_s = W_a / (V_d \, g)$.
- Interchangeable plummets of different $V_d$ give **different measurement ranges**.
- **Air Pressure Balance Method** keeps the **column length $L$** fixed; density is read from the **differential pressure $\Delta P$** measured between the bottoms of the two columns.
- Master equation: $\rho_s = \rho_{ref} + \Delta P / (g \, L)$.
- Air flow must be **low and equal on both sides** to cancel line friction and dynamic head errors.
- The output is **linear** in $\rho_s$, making the Air Pressure Balance method ideal for **continuous industrial** density measurement.
- Conversion to manometer deflection: $\Delta P = \rho_m \, g \, h_m$, hence $h_m = \Delta P / (\rho_m \, g)$.
- Buoyancy is always an **upward force** equal to the weight of fluid displaced (Archimedes).
- Both methods rely on **hydrostatic / gravitational equilibrium**, not on electrical or optical properties of the fluid.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Density Detector and Gas Specific Gravity Measuring System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Gas Density Detector and Gas Specific Gravity Measuring System ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Gas Density **Gas Density ($\rho_g$)** is formally defined as the mass of a gas contained per unit volume of the gas. The SI unit is **kg/m³**, while in process industries it is often expressed in **g/L**, **lb/ft³**, or **Amagat units**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *"Gas density is the mass per unit volume of a gas, typically evaluated at standard reference conditions of $0^\circ C$ and 1.013 bar (STP)."* Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$\rho_g = \frac{m}{V} \quad \left[ \text{kg/m}^3 \right]$$ Where $m$ is the mass of the gas and $V$ is the volume it occupies. Using the Ideal Gas Law ($PV = nRT$ and $n = m/M$): $$\rho_g = \frac{PM}{RT}$$ Where $P$ is absolute pressure, $M$ is molar mass (kg/mol), $R$ is the universal gas constant (**8.314 J/(mol·K)**), and $T$ is absolute temperature. ### 1.2 Gas Specific Gravity **Specific Gravity (SG)** of a gas is the dimensionless ratio of the gas density to the density of a reference gas (usually dry air) measured at identical pressure and temperature conditions. $$SG = \frac{\rho_{gas}}{\rho_{air}}$$ > [!NOTE] > - For dry air at STP, $\rho_{air} = \textbf{1.293 kg/m}^3$ (also widely approximated as **1.2 kg/m³** at 20°C and 1 atm). > - When measured against dry air, the term is called **Relative Gas Density (RGD)** or **Gas Specific Gravity**. > - In some industries (e.g., natural gas), the reference is air at **15.6°C and 101.325 kPa** per ISO 6976. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have two identical balloons of the same size. One is filled with air, and the other with a denser gas like carbon dioxide ($CO_2$). Although they look identical in size, the $CO_2$ balloon feels **heavier** in your hand. That is essentially what a Gas Density Detector (GDD) does — it "weighs" the gas by detecting the **buoyancy force** or **mass-flow torque** it produces, much like your hand feels the weight difference. A Gas Specific Gravity Meter then compares this "weight" against a known reference gas (air) to give a ratio. ### 1.4 Why Measure Gas Density / Specific Gravity? - **Composition Indication:** In binary gas mixtures (e.g., $N_2 + CO_2$ or $H_2 + N_2$), specific gravity directly indicates composition. - **Quality Control:** In natural gas pipelines, SG is monitored to determine heating value and Wobbe Index. - **Combustion Control:** Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio in burners depends on fuel gas density. - **Safety / Leak Detection:** Sudden density changes may indicate contamination or leaks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The most common industrial Gas Density Detector is the **Gas Density Balance** manufactured by **Mobrey (Emerson)**, **L&J Engineering**, and **Endress+Hauser**. The principle is essentially a **buoyancy-driven pressure comparator**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Gas Density Measurement Methods Industrial methods for measuring gas density and specific gravity can be classified as: | S.No. | Method | Principle | Typical Application | |:-----:|:-------|:----------|:--------------------| | 1 | **Gas Density Balance** | Buoyancy comparison between sample and reference gas columns | Custody transfer, pipeline monitoring | | 2 | **Centrifugal Type** | Torque required to rotate an impeller at constant speed | Combustion air, flue gas | | 3 | **Displacement (Plumb Bob) Type** | Buoyancy force on a displacer in gas | Laboratory-grade SG meters | | 4 | **Hot-Wire / Thermal Type** | Heat loss depends on gas density and thermal conductivity | Binary gas analysis | | 5 | **Effusion / Effusiometric** | Rate of gas flow through orifice (Graham's law) | Light gas detection | | 6 | **Vibrating Element (CFT)** | Resonant frequency shift of vibrating tube | Reference-class densitometers | | 7 | **Refractometric / Optical** | Refractive index correlation with density | Specialty gases | ### 2.2 Gas Density Balance (Buoyancy Comparator) – Working Theory A **Gas Density Balance** is a continuous specific gravity meter. Its core principle: - Two vertical columns of equal height, **H**, are exposed at their lower ends to a common gas pressure. - One column contains the **sample gas**; the other contains the **reference gas** (typically dry air sealed in a chamber). - Due to differing densities, the **hydrostatic pressure head** at the top of each column differs. - A sensitive differential pressure sensor (manometer, diaphragm, or LVDT) measures $\Delta P$. - The output is directly proportional to $(\rho_{gas} - \rho_{ref}) \cdot g \cdot H$. ### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operating Logic 1. **Sample inlet** admits the process gas into the measuring column at a constant flow. 2. The **reference chamber** is sealed with dry air at a known reference condition. 3. Both columns vent to a common low-pressure or atmospheric header at the top. 4. The **pressure imbalance** at the top of the columns is captured by a sensitive differential transducer. 5. The transducer's output (current 4–20 mA or pneumatic 3–15 psi) is calibrated to read **specific gravity** directly. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |:-:|:--------|:------------|:------| | 1 | $\rho_g = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$ | Ideal gas density | kg/m³ | | 2 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{gas}}{\rho_{air}}$ | Specific Gravity (dimensionless) | – | | 3 | $\Delta P = (\rho_{gas} - \rho_{air}) \cdot g \cdot H$ | Pressure imbalance in density balance | Pa | | 4 | $SG = 1 + \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot H}$ | SG from pressure difference | – | | 5 | $P = k \cdot \rho_g \cdot N^3 D^5$ | Power required by centrifugal impeller (approx.) | W | | 6 | $\tau = C_t \cdot \rho_g \cdot N^2 D^5$ | Torque proportional to gas density | N·m | | 7 | $\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{\sum y_i M_i P}{RT}$ | Density of a gas mixture using mole fractions $y_i$ | kg/m³ | | 8 | $SG_{mix} = \sum y_i \cdot SG_i$ | Specific gravity of ideal gas mixture | – | > [!NOTE] > **Important Boundary Condition:** Equation (4) above assumes the two columns are at the **same temperature and base pressure**. In a sealed-reference design, the reference air is typically maintained at $20^\circ C$ and 1 atm — any temperature drift of the sample line causes error unless compensated. ### 2.5 Centrifugal Gas Density Detector — Working Theory In this method, a small **centrifugal blower / impeller** is rotated in the gas at a constant speed $N$. The **torque** required to maintain this constant speed is directly proportional to the mass of gas handled, and hence to gas density. The motor current (which is proportional to torque) is sensed and used as a measure of $\rho_g$: $$\tau \propto \rho_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{motor} = k_1 + k_2 \cdot \rho_g$$ Where $k_1$ and $k_2$ are instrument constants determined by calibration with two gases of known density (typically air and $N_2$). ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Natural gas custody transfer** — billing depends on heating value, which is a function of SG. - **Hydrogen-cooled turbogenerators** — monitoring $H_2$ purity (SG ≈ 0.07) inside the casing. - **Blast furnace gas analysis** — $CO$ and $CO_2$ rich gases monitored continuously. - **LPG vapor detection** — butane/propane vapor SG > 1 (heavier than air), critical for leak safety. - **Combustion air** monitoring in power plant boilers for excess air optimization. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Difference in a Gas Density Balance **Step 1 — Setup:** Consider two vertical columns of identical height $H$, both open at the bottom to a common gas pressure $P_b$ and vented at the top. **Step 2 — Hydrostatic Pressure Equation:** The pressure at the top of a column filled with a gas of density $\rho$ is given by the basic hydrostatic relation: $$P_{top} = P_b - \rho \cdot g \cdot H$$ **Step 3 — Apply to both columns:** For the sample gas column (density $\rho_g$): $$P_{top,\,gas} = P_b - \rho_g \cdot g \cdot H$$ For the reference air column (density $\rho_{air}$): $$P_{top,\,air} = P_b - \rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot H$$ **Step 4 — Differential Pressure:** Subtract the two equations: $$P_{top,\,gas} - P_{top,\,air} = \rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot H - \rho_g \cdot g \cdot H$$ $$\Delta P = (\rho_{air} - \rho_g) \cdot g \cdot H$$ Or equivalently (in magnitude form, with sample density greater than air): $$\boxed{\Delta P = (\rho_g - \rho_{air}) \cdot g \cdot H}$$ **Step 5 — Express in Specific Gravity:** Substituting $\rho_g = SG \cdot \rho_{air}$: $$\Delta P = (SG \cdot \rho_{air} - \rho_{air}) \cdot g \cdot H = \rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot H \cdot (SG - 1)$$ Rearranging for Specific Gravity: $$\boxed{SG = 1 + \frac{\Delta P}{\rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot H}}$$ This is the **master equation** the differential pressure transducer is calibrated to read. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Centrifugal Type Density Detector **Step 1 — Impeller Power Relation:** For a centrifugal impeller rotating in a gas, the power required is given by affinity laws: $$P = C_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$$ Where $C_p$ is a power coefficient, $N$ is rotational speed (rev/s), and $D$ is impeller diameter. **Step 2 — Torque From Power:** Since $P = \tau \cdot \omega = \tau \cdot 2\pi N$: $$\tau = \frac{C_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5}{2\pi N} = C_t \cdot \rho \cdot N^2 \cdot D^5$$ Where $C_t = C_p / 2\pi$. **Step 3 — At Constant Speed:** When $N$ and $D$ are fixed, $\tau \propto \rho$. Hence: $$\tau = k \cdot \rho_g$$ **Step 4 — Specific Gravity:** Taking the ratio with reference air torque $\tau_{air}$: $$SG = \frac{\rho_g}{\rho_{air}} = \frac{\tau}{\tau_{air}}$$ This means the **SG is simply the torque ratio** — a beautifully direct result. $$\boxed{SG = \frac{\tau_{sample}}{\tau_{air}}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A gas density balance has two columns of height $H = 0.60$ m. The reference is dry air at $20^\circ C$ ($\rho_{air} = 1.20$ kg/m³). A differential pressure transducer reads $\Delta P = 15$ Pa (sample gas denser than air). Calculate: (i) the gas density, and (ii) the specific gravity. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Apply the master equation for SG:** $$SG = 1 + \frac{\Delta P}{\rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot H}$$ Substituting values $g = 9.81$ m/s²: $$SG = 1 + \frac{15}{1.20 \times 9.81 \times 0.60}$$ Compute the denominator: $$1.20 \times 9.81 \times 0.60 = 7.0632$$ Then: $$SG = 1 + \frac{15}{7.0632} = 1 + 2.1234 = 3.1234$$ **Step 2 — Gas Density:** $$\rho_g = SG \times \rho_{air} = 3.1234 \times 1.20 = 3.748 \,\, \text{kg/m}^3$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** The gas density is approximately **3.75 kg/m³** and its specific gravity is **≈ 3.12** (consistent with a heavy gas like $SO_2$, $Cl_2$, or a high-molar-mass hydrocarbon vapor). --- ### 3.4 Numerical Example: Centrifugal Type **Problem:** A centrifugal gas density meter is calibrated with air ($\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³) and nitrogen ($\rho_{N_2} = 1.15$ kg/m³). At calibration, motor torques are $\tau_{air} = 1.8$ N·m and $\tau_{N_2} = 1.725$ N·m. When measuring an unknown gas, the torque reads $\tau_x = 2.25$ N·m. Find the specific gravity of the unknown gas. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Verify the linear relation:** From the calibration data: $$\frac{\tau_{N_2}}{\tau_{air}} = \frac{1.725}{1.8} = 0.9583$$ $$\frac{\rho_{N_2}}{\rho_{air}} = \frac{1.15}{1.2} = 0.9583$$ The ratios match — confirming the linear torque-density relation. **Step 2 — Specific Gravity of Unknown Gas:** $$SG_x = \frac{\tau_x}{\tau_{air}} = \frac{2.25}{1.8} = 1.25$$ $$\boxed{SG_x = 1.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_x = 1.25 \times 1.2 = 1.50 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Gas Density Balance (Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the functional flow of a typical industrial Gas Density Balance / Specific Gravity Meter. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Process Gas Inlet
Constant Pressure & Flow] --> B[Sample Column
Height H sealed at top
Density rho_gas] C[Sealed Reference Chamber
Dry Air at 20 deg C
Density rho_air] --> D[Reference Column
Same Height H] B --> E[Top Pressure Port A
P_top_gas] D --> F[Top Pressure Port B
P_top_air] E --> G[Differential Pressure
Transducer deltaP Sensor] F --> G G --> H[Signal Conditioning
Amplifier and Lineariser] H --> I[Microprocessor Unit
Temperature Compensation] I --> J[Display Unit
SG or kg per cubic m readout] I --> K[Output Transmitter
4 to 20 mA HART Signal] K --> L[PLC / DCS / SCADA
Control Room] L --> M[Alarm and Trip Logic
Hi Hi SG or Lo Lo SG] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Centrifugal Gas Density Detector ```mermaid flowchart LR G1[Process Gas
Enters Chamber] --> H1[Centrifugal Impeller
Driven by Motor] H1 --> H2[Gas Discharged
Through Vents] M1[Constant Speed Motor
Driven by VFD] --> H1 H1 --> T1[Torque Sensor
Measures Shaft Torque] T1 --> S1[Signal Conditioner
Lineariser] S1 --> D1[Display
Density or SG] S1 --> O1[Current Output
4 to 20 mA] O1 --> C1[DCS / Recorder] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic of Pressure Difference Formation in a Gas Density Balance ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Column_Sample direction TB SP[Top Port A
P_top_gas] --> CS[Sample Gas Column
height H meters
density rho_g] CS --> BP[Common Bottom
P_b isobaric] end subgraph Column_Reference direction TB RP[Top Port B
P_top_air] --> CR[Reference Air Column
height H meters
density rho_air] CR --> BP end BP --> BS[Common Manifold
Process Inlet] SP --> DP[Differential Pressure
Transducer deltaP] RP --> DP DP --> RD[Readout
SG Indicator] ``` ### 4.4 Description of the Functional Flow 1. **Inlet Block A** admits the process gas at regulated pressure and flow to ensure a **steady-state density sample**. 2. **Sample Column B** is exposed to the gas for the full column height $H$. 3. **Reference Chamber C** is sealed and contains dry air at calibration conditions — this acts as the **density standard**. 4. The differential pressure transducer **G** senses $\Delta P$ between the two top ports. 5. **Signal conditioner H** and **temperature compensator I** convert the raw voltage to a standardized 4–20 mA HART signal. 6. The output drives the **DCS L**, where **alarm M** logic trips on out-of-limit SG. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Specific Gravity of a Gas" and explain how it is related to gas density. Mention the standard reference conditions.* **Model Answer:** Specific gravity of a gas is defined as the ratio of the density of the gas to the density of dry air measured at identical pressure and temperature conditions. $$SG = \frac{\rho_{gas}}{\rho_{air}}$$ It is a **dimensionless quantity**. The standard reference is dry air at **15.6 °C and 101.325 kPa** (per ISO 6976) or at **0 °C and 1.013 bar** (per old STP convention). Since it is a ratio, temperature and pressure cancel out only if both gases are at the same condition. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *State and explain the working principle of a gas density balance.* **Model Answer:** A gas density balance works on the **buoyancy comparison principle**. Two vertical columns of equal height are connected at the bottom to a common gas supply. One column carries the **sample gas**; the other contains **sealed reference air**. Because of differing densities, the hydrostatic pressure at the top of each column differs by: $$\Delta P = (\rho_{gas} - \rho_{air}) \, g \, H$$ A sensitive **differential pressure transducer** measures $\Delta P$ and converts it into an output signal proportional to the specific gravity of the sample gas. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A [14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **construction and working of a Gas Density Balance** used for continuous specific gravity measurement. Discuss the role of the reference chamber and the differential pressure sensor. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A gas density balance has a column height of 0.8 m. The reference is dry air at 25 °C ($\rho_{air} = 1.184$ kg/m³). The differential pressure sensor reads 25 Pa with the sample gas flowing. Compute the **(i)** specific gravity and **(ii)** density of the sample gas. **[7 Marks]** ##### Model Solution **(a) Construction and Working** A gas density balance is composed of the following key parts: 1. **Sample Column:** A vertical tube of height $H$ (typically 0.5–1 m) through which the process gas flows continuously. The gas enters at the bottom and exits at the top. 2. **Reference Column / Sealed Chamber:** A second identical vertical column sealed at the top, containing **dry air at calibration conditions** (e.g., 20 °C, 1 atm). This chamber acts as the density standard. 3. **Common Manifold / Bottom Header:** Connects the two columns at the base, ensuring the bottom pressure $P_b$ is identical for both columns. 4. **Differential Pressure Transducer:** A high-sensitivity sensor (e.g., capacitance-type or piezoresistive) measures the **pressure difference $\Delta P$** between the two top ports. 5. **Signal Conditioning Electronics:** Linearises the $\Delta P$ to a 4–20 mA output and applies **temperature compensation** to the reference chamber. 6. **Display / Transmitter Unit:** Reads out **SG** (dimensionless) or **density** (kg/m³) on a digital indicator. **Working Equation:** $$\Delta P = (\rho_{gas} - \rho_{air}) \, g \, H \quad \Rightarrow \quad SG = 1 + \frac{\Delta P}{\rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot H}$$ When $\rho_{gas} > \rho_{air}$ (e.g., $CO_2$), $\Delta P$ is positive; when $\rho_{gas} < \rho_{air}$ (e.g., $H_2$, $He$), $\Delta P$ reverses polarity. The instrument can therefore handle both light and heavy gases. **Role of Reference Chamber:** - Provides a **stable, known density benchmark** independent of process variations. - Eliminates the need for a vacuum or absolute pressure reference. - Allows **continuous in-situ calibration** by simply knowing $\rho_{air}$ at chamber temperature. **Role of Differential Pressure Sensor:** - Converts the **hydrostatic imbalance** into an electrical signal. - Resolution better than 1 Pa is required to detect SG changes of 0.001 in a 1 m column. --- **(b) Numerical Solution** **Given:** - $H = 0.8$ m - $\rho_{air} = 1.184$ kg/m³ at 25 °C - $\Delta P = 25$ Pa - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1 — Compute the denominator of SG formula:** $$\rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot H = 1.184 \times 9.81 \times 0.8 = 9.2912 \,\,\text{Pa}$$ **Step 2 — Specific Gravity:** $$SG = 1 + \frac{25}{9.2912} = 1 + 2.6906 = 3.6906$$ $$\boxed{SG \approx 3.69}$$ **Step 3 — Gas Density:** $$\rho_{gas} = SG \times \rho_{air} = 3.6906 \times 1.184 = 4.370 \,\,\text{kg/m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\rho_{gas} \approx 4.37 \,\,\text{kg/m}^3}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert $\Delta P$ correctly or mixing up pressure units. Always confirm: 1 mmH₂O = 9.806 Pa. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $\rho_{air} = 1.293$ kg/m³ (STP) when the problem specifies 25 °C. **Always use the given temperature's air density**. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing $SG = \rho_{air} / \rho_{gas}$ (inverted) — loses 1 mark minimum. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Omitting units in the final answer — KTU examiners specifically deduct for missing units in numerical problems. --- #### **Question B [14 Marks] (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Explain the **working of a Centrifugal Gas Density Detector**. Derive the relationship between torque and gas density. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A centrifugal gas density detector is calibrated with air ($\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³, torque $\tau_{air} = 1.5$ N·m) and $CO_2$ ($\rho_{CO_2} = 1.85$ kg/m³, torque $\tau_{CO_2} = 2.31$ N·m). When measuring flue gas, the torque is 1.78 N·m. Determine: **(i)** the calibration constant, and **(ii)** the specific gravity of the flue gas. **[7 Marks]** ##### Model Solution **(a) Working of a Centrifugal Gas Density Detector** A small **centrifugal impeller** is mounted inside a chamber through which the sample gas flows. The impeller is driven by a **constant-speed motor** (often regulated by a VFD or feedback loop). **Operating Principle:** As the impeller rotates at speed $N$, it transfers momentum to the gas molecules. The **torque** required on the shaft is equal to the rate of change of angular momentum of the gas. Using dimensional analysis and the affinity laws for centrifugal machines: $$P = C_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$$ Since power = torque × angular velocity, i.e., $P = \tau \cdot (2\pi N)$: $$\tau = \frac{C_p}{2\pi} \cdot \rho \cdot N^2 \cdot D^5 = C_t \cdot \rho \cdot N^2 \cdot D^5$$ For a fixed geometry ($D$ = constant) and constant speed ($N$ = constant): $$\boxed{\tau = k \cdot \rho}$$ Where $k = C_t \cdot N^2 \cdot D^5$ is the **instrument constant**. **Practical Implementation:** - A **torque sensor** (strain gauge on the motor shaft, or a slip-ring-based dynamometer) measures $\tau$. - The output is **temperature-compensated** and linearised. - Suitable for **flue gas, combustion air, and stack monitoring**. **Advantages:** - No moving seals or gas-liquid interfaces. - Fast dynamic response (milliseconds). - Suitable for wet, dirty, or corrosive gases (with material selection). **Limitations:** - Sensitive to flow pulsations. - Requires periodic recalibration if the impeller wears. - Inaccuracy at very low densities (< 0.1 kg/m³). --- **(b) Numerical Solution** **Given calibration data:** | Gas | Density (kg/m³) | Torque (N·m) | |:----|:---------------:|:------------:| | Air | 1.20 | 1.50 | | CO₂ | 1.85 | 2.31 | | Flue gas | ? | 1.78 | **Step 1 — Find the Calibration Constant $k$:** Using air calibration: $$k_1 = \frac{\tau_{air}}{\rho_{air}} = \frac{1.50}{1.20} = 1.25 \,\,\text{N·m·m}^3/\text{kg}$$ Using $CO_2$ calibration: $$k_2 = \frac{\tau_{CO_2}}{\rho_{CO_2}} = \frac{2.31}{1.85} = 1.2486 \,\,\text{N·m·m}^3/\text{kg}$$ Average calibration constant: $$k = \frac{k_1 + k_2}{2} = \frac{1.25 + 1.2486}{2} = 1.2493 \,\,\text{N·m·m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Step 2 — Specific Gravity of Flue Gas:** $$SG_{flue} = \frac{\tau_{flue}}{\tau_{air}} = \frac{1.78}{1.50} = 1.1867$$ $$\boxed{SG_{flue} \approx 1.187}$$ **Step 3 — Density of Flue Gas (cross-check):** $$\rho_{flue} = \frac{\tau_{flue}}{k} = \frac{1.78}{1.2493} = 1.4248 \,\,\text{kg/m}^3$$ $$\rho_{flue} = SG \times \rho_{air} = 1.187 \times 1.20 = 1.424 \,\,\text{kg/m}^3 \quad \checkmark$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Centrifugal Type):** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing the **affinity laws** for pumps/fans with the linear torque-density relation. The key is to **fix N and D** before writing $\tau = k\rho$. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **temperature compensation** aspect — the impeller chamber must be temperature-controlled, otherwise $k$ drifts. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Not verifying the calibration linearity — KTU values the step where students **check $k_1 \approx k_2$** to validate the model. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gas Density & Specific Gravity** - **Gas Density ($\rho_g$):** Mass per unit volume. SI unit: **kg/m³**. From ideal gas law: $\rho_g = PM/RT$. - **Specific Gravity (SG):** Dimensionless ratio $\rho_{gas} / \rho_{air}$. Reference is **dry air** at standard conditions. - **Standard Air Density:** **1.293 kg/m³** (at 0 °C, 1 atm) or **1.20 kg/m³** (at 20 °C, 1 atm). Always read the problem statement carefully. - **Master Equation of Gas Density Balance:** $SG = 1 + \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot H}$ - **Hydrostatic Pressure Drop:** $\Delta P = (\rho_{gas} - \rho_{air}) \cdot g \cdot H$ — sign tells you whether the gas is heavier (+) or lighter (−) than air. - **Centrifugal Type:** $\tau = k \cdot \rho_g$ — torque is directly proportional to density. $SG = \tau_{sample} / \tau_{air}$. - **Reference Chamber:** Sealed dry air — provides a **stable benchmark**; not affected by process upsets. - **Differential Pressure Sensor:** Resolution < 1 Pa is needed for SG sensitivity of 0.001 in a 1 m column. - **Applications:** Natural gas custody transfer, $H_2$ purity in generators, combustion control, LPG vapor detection, blast furnace gas analysis. - **Common Methods to Remember (in order of KTU exam frequency):** Gas Density Balance > Centrifugal > Displacement > Thermal > Effusion > Vibrating Tube. - **KTU High-Yield Numerical Setup:** Typically 7-mark problems use $H = 0.5$–$1.0$ m and $\Delta P = 10$–$50$ Pa — result SG usually in 0.5–5 range. - **Sign Convention for SG:** $SG > 1$ means gas is heavier than air; $SG < 1$ means gas is lighter (e.g., $H_2 \approx 0.07$). - **Mixing Rule (Bonus):** $SG_{mix} = \sum y_i \cdot SG_i$ for an ideal gas mixture, where $y_i$ are mole fractions.

topics.title: Flow Measurement Using Head Type Flowmeters: Orifice Meter, Venturi Meter, Flow Nozzle, and Pitot Tube
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flow Measurement Using Head Type Flowmeters: Orifice Meter, Venturi Meter, Flow Nozzle, and Pitot Tube

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **head-type flowmeter** is a differential-pressure-producing primary flow measurement device in which a deliberate restriction (constriction) is introduced into a flow conduit, causing a measurable drop in static pressure that is functionally related (by Bernoulli's principle and the continuity equation) to the volumetric or mass flow rate of the fluid passing through it.

Per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEBBT724)**, the four canonical head-type flowmeters studied under Module 3 are:

1. **Orifice Meter** (Concentric, Eccentric, and Segmental types)
2. **Venturi Meter**
3. **Flow Nozzle**
4. **Pitot Tube** (often classified as a velocity-sensing probe, but functionally uses differential head)

The fundamental governing relationship for all head-type meters is derived from **Bernoulli's equation** coupled with the **continuity equation**, yielding a generalized flow equation:

$$Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2g \, \Delta h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

where $C_d$ is the **discharge coefficient**, $A_1$ and $A_2$ are the upstream and throat (vena contracta) cross-sectional areas, and $\Delta h$ is the manometric head difference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the derivation of flow equations from first principles (Bernoulli + Continuity), the physical significance of $C_d$, $C_v$ (velocity coefficient), and $C_c$ (contraction coefficient), and the comparative engineering trade-offs (permanent pressure loss, accuracy, cost, suitability for clean vs. dirty fluids) between the four devices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are **partially blocking a garden hose with your thumb** to spray water farther. The harder you press (more constriction), the higher the speed of the water leaving the nozzle and the greater the pressure drop you can feel just behind your thumb.

A **head-type flowmeter works on exactly the same principle**:

- The **flowing fluid** = the water in the hose
- The **constriction** (orifice, venturi throat, nozzle) = your thumb
- The **pressure drop** measured across the constriction = the squirt speed indicator
- The **flow rate** we want to measure = the amount of water coming out per second

The key engineering insight: by measuring **how much the pressure drops** across a known constriction, we can calculate **how fast the fluid must be moving** to satisfy the laws of mass and energy conservation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Why "Head"?** In fluid mechanics, pressure is frequently expressed as the height of a fluid column (e.g., cm of water, mm of mercury). This is called "head." Manometers and differential pressure transmitters literally convert pressure differences into "head" differences, which is why these devices are historically called "head-type" flowmeters. Standard head units: **m of water, cm of Hg, or Pa (N/m²)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following values must be committed to memory for KTU numerical problems:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Density of water at 4 °C | $\rho_w$ | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Density of mercury at 0 °C | $\rho_{Hg}$ | **13600** | kg/m³ |
| Specific weight of water | $\gamma_w$ | **9810** | N/m³ |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101325** | Pa |
| Standard discharge coefficient range (orifice) | $C_d$ | **0.6 – 0.65** | dimensionless |
| Standard discharge coefficient (venturi) | $C_d$ | **0.95 – 0.98** | dimensionless |
| Standard discharge coefficient (nozzle) | $C_d$ | **0.96 – 0.99** | dimensionless |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Physics — Step-by-Step Logic

All four head-type flowmeters derive their working principle from the same pair of fundamental equations. Let us walk through the **"Why"** and **"How"** of each.

### Step 1 — Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

For an incompressible fluid in a closed conduit:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

**Why?** Because the same volume of fluid entering per second must exit per second (mass cannot be created or destroyed in the flow path).

**How does this help?** It links the upstream velocity $V_1$ to the throat velocity $V_2$ purely through the known geometry $A_1$ and $A_2$.

### Step 2 — Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy)

Applied between an upstream section (point 1) and the throat (point 2), both lying on the same horizontal datum ($z_1 = z_2$):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Why?** The total mechanical energy of the fluid is conserved (we ignore friction for the ideal case).

**How does this help?** It shows that the velocity increase at the throat **must** be paid for by a static pressure decrease — and this pressure drop is what we measure.

### Step 3 — Combining the Two Equations

Substituting $V_1 = V_2 (A_2/A_1)$ into Bernoulli's equation and rearranging:

$$V_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$

The theoretical (ideal) volumetric flow is:

$$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2g \, \Delta h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

### Step 4 — Real-World Correction Using $C_d$

In practice, the actual flow is less than the theoretical because of:

- **Vena contracta effect** — the jet continues to contract downstream of the orifice, reaching a minimum area smaller than the orifice itself. This is governed by the **coefficient of contraction** $C_c = A_{vc}/A_o$.
- **Viscous (frictional) losses** — characterized by the **coefficient of velocity** $C_v = V_{actual}/V_{theoretical}$.
- The combined correction is the **discharge coefficient** $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$.

Therefore:

$$Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2g \, \Delta h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

## 2.2 Device-Specific Working Principles

### 2.2.1 Orifice Meter

A thin, flat, concentric circular plate with a precisely machined hole (typically $d/D \approx 0.5$ for maximum $C_d$) is clamped between flanges. The fluid accelerates through the hole, forming a **vena contracta** (a minimum cross-section) about 0.35D to 0.7D downstream of the plate, where static pressure is minimum.

**Tap locations (very high-yield for KTU):**

| Tap Type | Location | Symbol | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Corner taps | Flush with the orifice plate faces | — | Used for small pipes, $D < 50$ mm |
| D and D/2 taps | 1D upstream and D/2 downstream | — | Most common, recommended by ISO 5167 |
| Flange taps | 25.4 mm (1 inch) from plate faces | — | Standard for $D \geq 50$ mm |

### 2.2.2 Venturi Meter

Invented by **Clemens Herschel (1887)**, the venturi consists of three sections:

1. **Convergent cone** (angle ≈ 21° ± 2°): smoothly accelerates the flow.
2. **Throat** (cylindrical, length ≈ 1 throat diameter): pressure is measured here.
3. **Divergent cone** (angle ≈ 5° to 7°): *gradually* recovers most of the kinetic energy as static pressure.

**Why the smooth shape?** The gradual contraction avoids vena contracta, and the gradual expansion drastically reduces the **permanent pressure loss** (typically 10–15% of the differential, vs. 40–60% for an orifice). This is the venturi's main engineering advantage — it saves pumping energy.

### 2.2.3 Flow Nozzle

A **flow nozzle** is essentially a venturi with the divergent section removed — a smooth bell-shaped inlet that ends abruptly. It has a higher $C_d$ than an orifice (less vena contracta loss) and produces less permanent pressure drop than an orifice but more than a venturi.

- **ISA 1932 nozzle**: standardized profile used in Europe.
- **Long-radius nozzle**: standardized profile used in the USA.

**Engineering use:** Best for high-velocity steam and superheated fluids where erosion of a sharp-edged orifice would be a problem.

### 2.2.4 Pitot Tube

Unlike the previous three, the **pitot tube** does not constrict the entire flow. It is a small L-shaped or coaxial probe inserted into the flow. It has two pressure ports:

- **Stagnation port (impact port)**: faces the flow, measures **stagnation (total) pressure** $P_0 = P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$.
- **Static port**: perpendicular to the flow, measures **static pressure** $P$.

The pitot tube measures **point velocity**, not bulk flow. To get the volumetric flow, one must either:

1. Multiply point velocity by the cross-sectional area (approximate, only for uniform profiles), or
2. Use a **pitot traverse** — measure at several points across the pipe and integrate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the pitot-static tube with a venturi. Remember: the **venturi measures volumetric flow** by introducing a constriction; the **pitot tube measures point velocity** by sampling stagnation and static pressures.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2g \Delta h}$ | General head-type flow equation | All four meters |
| 2 | $\Delta h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = h \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)$ | Manometric head from differential manometer | Converting manometer reading to head |
| 3 | $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{2g \, \Delta h}$ | Simplified form (valid for $A_2 \ll A_1$) | Orifice, nozzle with $d/D \le 0.5$ |
| 4 | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | Definition of discharge coefficient | Orifice analysis |
| 5 | $V = C_v \sqrt{2g \, \Delta h}$ | Actual velocity at vena contracta | Orifice, pitot |
| 6 | $A_{vc} = C_c \cdot A_o$ | Vena contracta area | Orifice |
| 7 | $P_0 - P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | Pitot tube dynamic pressure | Pitot |
| 8 | $V_{pitot} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$ | Velocity from pitot reading | Pitot |
| 9 | $m = \rho \cdot Q$ | Mass flow rate | All meters |
| 10 | $Re_D = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Pipe Reynolds number | To select $C_d$ from ISO 5167 charts |
| 11 | $\Delta P_{permanent} = \dfrac{(1 - C_d^2)}{C_d^2} \cdot \Delta P$ | Permanent pressure loss | Energy audit calculations |
| 12 | $NPSH_{available} = NPSH_{required} + \Delta P_{losses}$ | Net Positive Suction Head (pump cavitation check) | Plant engineering |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Orifice meters** dominate oil & gas custody transfer and water distribution because they are cheap, easy to install, and standards (ISO 5167, AGA 3) allow traceable, legally defensible measurement.
- **Venturi meters** are used in **boiler feedwater**, **cooling water** of large power plants, and **chlorinated water** lines in chemical plants where low permanent pressure loss directly reduces pumping cost.
- **Flow nozzles** are preferred for **high-pressure steam** in thermal power stations (Rajasthan, NTPC, KSEB plants) because they resist erosion and have predictable $C_d$ at high Re.
- **Pitot tubes** are used in **aircraft airspeed indicators**, **HVAC duct balancing**, **stack emission velocity** measurement, and **river/canal flow gauging** (pitot-static tubes mounted on boats).
