# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are exhaustive. Every algebraic step is retained; no truncation, no shortcuts, no "it can be shown." This is the KTU 2024 Scheme Board standard for a full-marks answer.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Head-Type Flow Equation

**Assumptions:**

1. Fluid is **incompressible** (valid for liquids and low-Mach gases).
2. Flow is **steady** and along a **streamline**.
3. No friction, no shaft work — ideal energy conservation (Bernoulli holds).
4. Constriction has a known minimum area $A_2$.

**Step 1.** Write the steady-flow energy equation between upstream section (1) and throat (2), assuming the meter is horizontal ($z_1 = z_2$):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 2.** Subtract $\frac{P_2}{\rho g}$ from both sides and multiply by $2g$:

$$\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho} = V_2^2 - V_1^2$$

**Step 3.** Apply continuity: $V_1 A_1 = V_2 A_2 \Rightarrow V_1 = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$.

**Step 4.** Substitute $V_1$ into the right-hand side:

$$\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho} = V_2^2 - V_2^2 \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 = V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 5.** Solve for $V_2$:

$$V_2^2 = \frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - (A_2/A_1)^2\right]}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$

**Step 6.** The theoretical volumetric flow rate is $Q_{th} = A_2 V_2$:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$

**Step 7.** Convert pressure difference to head: $\Delta h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2g \, \Delta h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

**Step 8.** Apply the discharge coefficient correction $C_d$ to convert theoretical flow to actual:

$$\boxed{Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2g \, \Delta h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Orifice Meter Equation with Vena Contracta

**Setup:** Orifice of area $A_o$ in a pipe of area $A_1 = \tfrac{\pi}{4}D^2$. The flow contracts downstream to a **vena contracta** of area $A_{vc} = C_c \cdot A_o$.

**Step 1.** Treat the vena contracta as the throat (point 2). Apply the general head equation between pipe and vena contracta:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A_{vc} \sqrt{2g(P_1 - P_{vc})/\rho g}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_{vc}/A_1)^2}} = \frac{A_{vc} \sqrt{2g \Delta h_{pipe \to vc}}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_{vc}/A_1)^2}}$$

**Step 2.** The actual flow (using the **velocity coefficient** $C_v$ approach, where $V_{actual} = C_v V_{th}$):

$$Q_{actual} = C_v \cdot A_{vc} \cdot \sqrt{2g \Delta h_{pipe \to vc}}$$

**Step 3.** But the pressure taps in practice are at the **upstream pipe (point 1) and at the orifice outlet (point 2)**, not at the vena contracta. The actual measured head $\Delta h = (P_1 - P_2)/(\rho g)$ is *less* than $\Delta h_{pipe \to vc}$ because the vena contracta pressure is the absolute minimum.

**Step 4.** Define $C_d$ to absorb *all* real-world losses:

$$Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2g \Delta h}$$

where $C_d$ is **empirically determined** (≈ 0.61 for a sharp-edged orifice at $d/D = 0.5$, $Re_D > 10^4$).

**Step 5.** Mass flow rate form:

$$m = \rho Q = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Venturi Meter Equation

**Step 1.** For a venturi, the throat is a well-defined cylindrical section of area $A_2$ (no vena contracta — the contraction is gradual). The discharge coefficient $C_d \approx 0.98$.

**Step 2.** Apply the general head equation directly between upstream (1) and throat (2):

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2g(P_1 - P_2)/(\rho g)}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P / \rho}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

**Step 3.** The permanent pressure loss for a venturi is small. Theoretical recovery at the downstream end is complete (all $V_2$ converted back to pressure), but viscous losses cause a small drop:

$$\Delta P_{permanent} \approx \frac{1 - C_d^2}{C_d^2} \cdot \Delta P \quad \text{(small, ~10\% of }\Delta P\text{)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pitot Tube Equation

**Step 1.** At the **stagnation point** (point s) where the flow is brought to rest ($V_s = 0$):

$$\frac{P_s}{\rho g} + 0 = \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g}$$

**Step 2.** Rearrange:

$$\frac{P_s - P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{V_1^2}{2g}$$

**Step 3.** Define the dynamic head: $h_d = (P_s - P_1)/(\rho g)$.

**Step 4.** Solve for $V_1$:

$$V_1 = \sqrt{2g \, h_d} = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_s - P_1)}{\rho}}$$

**Step 5.** In practice, a calibration factor $C$ is added to account for the non-ideal geometry of the pitot:

$$V_{actual} = C \cdot \sqrt{2g \, h_d} \quad \text{where } C \approx 0.98 \text{ to } 1.0$$

**Step 6.** Volumetric flow (for a uniform velocity profile in a duct of area $A$):

$$Q = A \cdot V_{actual} = C \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2g \, h_d}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Device Comparison

| Parameter | Orifice Meter | Venturi Meter | Flow Nozzle | Pitot Tube |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_d$ typical | 0.60 – 0.65 | 0.95 – 0.98 | 0.96 – 0.99 | 0.98 – 1.00 |
| Permanent $\Delta P$ loss (% of measured) | 40 – 60% | 10 – 15% | 30 – 40% | negligible (probe) |
| Initial cost | **Lowest** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| Accuracy (uncalibrated) | ± 2 – 4% | ± 1% | ± 1 – 2% | ± 3 – 5% (single point) |
| Suitability for dirty/slurry fluids | Poor (clogs) | Excellent | Good | Good (no obstruction) |
| Suitability for high-temperature steam | Limited | Excellent | Excellent | Limited (probe) |
| Space required | Short (≈ 5D) | Long (≈ 8 – 10D) | Short (≈ 5D) | Single insertion point |
| Vena contracta present? | **Yes** | No (gradual) | Small | No |
| Measures | Volumetric flow | Volumetric flow | Volumetric flow | Point velocity |
| Standards governing design | ISO 5167, ASME MFC-3M | ISO 5167-4 | ISO 5167-3 | ISO 3966 |

```mermaid
    C1 -.GENERATES.-> F1
    C1 -.GENERATES.-> H1
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    D --> D2[ECCENTRIC]

```

## 4.3 Pressure Profile Along an Orifice Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[UPSTREAM PRESSURE P1] -->|NORMAL| P2[APPROACH TO ORIFICE]
    P2 -->|STEEP DROP| P3[VENA CONTRACTA MINIMUM P]
    P3 -->|PARTIAL RECOVERY| P4[ORIFICE OUTLET P2]
    P4 -->|FRICTION LOSS| P5[DOWNSTREAM P5 LESS THAN P1]
    P5 -->|EVENTUAL RECOVERY| P6[FULL PIPE P1 AGAIN MINUS PERMANENT LOSS]
```


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q:** Define the term **discharge coefficient** for an orifice meter. How is it related to the coefficient of contraction and the coefficient of velocity?

**Model Answer:**

The **discharge coefficient $C_d$** of an orifice meter is defined as the ratio of the actual (measured) flow rate to the theoretical (ideal, frictionless, no-vena-contracta) flow rate calculated from Bernoulli's equation:

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$

It is the product of two independent physical effects:

- The **coefficient of contraction $C_c$** — the ratio of the vena contracta area to the orifice area: $C_c = A_{vc}/A_o$. It accounts for the fact that the fluid jet continues to contract downstream of the orifice.
- The **coefficient of velocity $C_v$** — the ratio of the actual velocity at the vena contracta to the theoretical velocity: $C_v = V_{actual}/V_{theoretical}$. It accounts for viscous (frictional) losses.

Mathematically:

$$C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$$

For a sharp-edged concentric orifice with $d/D = 0.5$ at high Reynolds number, typical values are $C_c \approx 0.62$, $C_v \approx 0.98$, giving $C_d \approx 0.61$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023]**

**Q:** Why is a **venturi meter preferred over an orifice meter** for measuring the flow of clean water in a long pipeline, even though both are head-type flowmeters?

**Model Answer:**

A venturi meter is preferred over an orifice meter for clean water pipelines for the following engineering reasons:

1. **Lower permanent pressure loss:** The venturi's gradual divergent cone (angle 5°–7°) recovers up to 85–90% of the differential pressure. The orifice recovers only 40–60%. In long pipelines, the lower permanent loss translates directly to **lower pumping energy cost** over the lifetime of the plant.

2. **Higher discharge coefficient ($C_d \approx 0.95$–$0.98$ vs. $0.6$–$0.65$ for orifice):** This gives a stronger, more linear output signal for a given flow rate, simplifying instrumentation.

3. **No vena contracta:** The smooth contraction avoids flow separation, making the pressure measurement stable and reproducible.

4. **Lower susceptibility to erosion and fouling:** The venturi's smooth profile does not trap particulates, important in raw water lines.

5. **Better accuracy and repeatability:** Venturi meters are ISO 5167 calibrated and require less frequent recalibration.

The orifice meter is, however, **far cheaper and more compact**, so it is chosen when permanent loss is not a primary concern (e.g., short industrial water lines, oil & gas custody transfer where accuracy standards compensate).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provided:** Answer **ONE** of the two questions below. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the general expression for the volumetric flow rate through a head-type flowmeter starting from Bernoulli's equation and the continuity equation. Clearly state all assumptions. Define the discharge coefficient and explain why it is less than unity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Water flows through a venturi meter fitted in a horizontal pipe of diameter **200 mm**. The throat diameter is **100 mm**. The differential manometer connected across the meter reads **200 mm of mercury**, with the heavier fluid below. Take $C_d = 0.98$. Calculate:

- (i) The discharge in **litres per second**.
- (ii) The velocity of water at the throat.
- (iii) The mass flow rate in **kg/s**.

Use $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

*(See Section 3.1 above for the full derivation. The student is expected to write:)*

1. State assumptions (4 marks): incompressible fluid, steady flow, horizontal meter (no elevation change), no friction (ideal), known geometry.
2. Write Bernoulli's equation between upstream (1) and throat (2).
3. Write continuity $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$.
4. Combine to eliminate $V_1$.
5. Solve for $V_2$, then $Q_{th} = A_2 V_2$, then apply $C_d$ to get the final expression.
6. Define $C_d = Q_{actual}/Q_{theoretical} < 1$ and explain the two real-world losses: vena contracta (less than expected area) and viscous friction (less velocity than ideal).

**Final box-form expression (must be boxed for 1 mark):**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2g \, \Delta h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 200$ mm $\Rightarrow A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2 = 0.0314$ m²
- $D_2 = 100$ mm $\Rightarrow A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 0.00785$ m²
- Manometer reading $h = 200$ mm = 0.2 m of Hg
- $C_d = 0.98$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Convert manometer reading to water head:**

For a U-tube differential manometer with mercury (heavier fluid below) and water as the flowing fluid:

$$\Delta h_{water} = h \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{water}} - 1\right) = 0.2 \times \left(\frac{13600}{1000} - 1\right) = 0.2 \times 12.6 = 2.52 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the diameter ratio term:**

$$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{100}{200}\right)^2 = 0.25$$

$$1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the velocity at the throat:**

$$V_2 = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2g \, \Delta h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} = 0.98 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52}{0.9375}}$$

$$V_2 = 0.98 \times \sqrt{52.71} = 0.98 \times 7.260 = 7.115 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the discharge:**

$$Q = A_2 \cdot V_2 = 0.00785 \times 7.115 = 0.0559 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to litres per second:

$$Q = 0.0559 \times 1000 = 55.9 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the mass flow rate:**

$$m = \rho \cdot Q = 1000 \times 0.0559 = 55.9 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $Q = 55.9$ L/s
- (ii) $V_2 = 7.12$ m/s
- (iii) $m = 55.9$ kg/s

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Pitot tube**. Derive the expression for the velocity measured by a Pitot-static tube. How is it used to find the volumetric flow rate in a pipe?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A concentric orifice plate of diameter **50 mm** is fitted in a pipe of diameter **100 mm**. A differential U-tube mercury manometer connected across the orifice reads **300 mm of Hg**. Calculate:

- (i) The volumetric flow rate of water in **m³/h** (take $C_d = 0.62$).
- (ii) The velocity of approach factor.
- (iii) The theoretical velocity at the vena contracta.

Use $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Descriptive (7 Marks):**

*(See Section 2.2.4 and Section 3.4. The student should:)*

1. Sketch the Pitot tube (insert figure even if rough) — 1 mark
2. Describe stagnation port and static port — 1 mark
3. Write Bernoulli between free stream and stagnation point — 2 marks
4. Derive $V = \sqrt{2g h_d}$ — 2 marks
5. State how to obtain Q using $Q = A \cdot V$ and discuss velocity profile integration (pitot traverse) — 1 mark

**Final velocity expression (boxed):**

$$V = C\sqrt{2g \, h_d} = C \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $d = 50$ mm = 0.05 m, $D = 100$ mm = 0.1 m
- $h = 300$ mm = 0.3 m of Hg
- $C_d = 0.62$

**Step 1 — Calculate areas:**

$$A_o = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert manometer to water head:**

$$\Delta h = 0.3 \times \left(\frac{13600}{1000} - 1\right) = 0.3 \times 12.6 = 3.78 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the simplified orifice equation** (valid when $A_2 \ll A_1$; here $A_o/A_1 = 0.25$, the ratio squared is $0.0625$ — small but not negligible. We will include the velocity-of-approach correction in part (ii)):

**Step 4 — Calculate velocity-of-approach factor:**

$$K = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (A_o/A_1)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0625}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.9375}} = 1.0323$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric flow rate (without velocity-of-approach factor first):**

$$Q_1 = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2g \, \Delta h} = 0.62 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3.78}$$

$$Q_1 = 0.62 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{74.18} = 0.62 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.613$$

$$Q_1 = 1.048 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — With velocity-of-approach factor:**

$$Q = K \cdot Q_1 = 1.0323 \times 1.048 \times 10^{-2} = 1.082 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to m³/h:

$$Q = 1.082 \times 10^{-2} \times 3600 = 38.95 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 7 — Theoretical velocity at vena contracta (using $C_v$ and $C_c$):**

The theoretical velocity from Bernoulli (ignoring all losses):

$$V_{th} = \sqrt{2g \, \Delta h} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3.78} = \sqrt{74.18} = 8.61 \text{ m/s}$$

(The actual velocity is $V_{actual} = C_v \cdot V_{th}$.)

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $Q \approx 38.95$ m³/h
- (ii) $K = 1.0323$
- (iii) $V_{th} \approx 8.61$ m/s

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Manometer sign error:** When mercury is the heavier fluid *below* a lighter flowing fluid, the manometric equation is $\Delta h = h(\rho_{Hg}/\rho_f - 1)$. Students often forget the "-1" and write $\Delta h = h \cdot \rho_{Hg}/\rho_f$, getting 1.6× the correct answer. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Unit confusion:** $A$ must be in **m²** and $Q$ will come out in **m³/s**. Converting to L/s or m³/h is a separate, easy-to-forget step. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Forgetting velocity-of-approach factor:** When $d/D > 0.5$, the term $\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}$ in the denominator matters. Ignoring it gives a flow that is too high. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Forgetting to define $C_d$:** A numerical answer with no mention of $C_d$ in the working is **incomplete** under KTU 2024 marking scheme. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Reporting Pitot tube as "flow meter":** It is a velocity probe. If the question says "calculate Q" using a pitot, you must state the assumption of uniform velocity or describe a pitot traverse. **Loss: 2 marks.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Bernoulli + Continuity** are the twin pillars of every head-type flowmeter derivation.
- ✅ **Discharge coefficient $C_d$** is the empirical "fudge factor" that turns ideal Bernoulli flow into real flow. Typical values: Orifice ≈ 0.6, Venturi ≈ 0.98, Nozzle ≈ 0.98, Pitot ≈ 1.0.
- ✅ **General flow equation** (memorize the box form): $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2g \Delta h / [1 - (A_2/A_1)^2]}$.
- ✅ **Simplified form** $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2g \Delta h}$ is valid only when $A_2 \ll A_1$ (typically $d/D \le 0.5$).
- ✅ **Manometer conversion** for differential U-tube with mercury below: $\Delta h = h \left( \dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{fluid}} - 1 \right)$.
- ✅ **Tap locations** for orifice: corner, D-D/2, flange. KTU most commonly uses D-D/2.
- ✅ **Permanent pressure loss** ranking (high → low): Orifice > Nozzle > Venturi > Pitot.
- ✅ **Cost ranking** (low → high): Pitot = Orifice < Nozzle < Venturi.
- ✅ **Cleanliness tolerance** ranking (poor → good): Orifice < Nozzle < Venturi ≈ Pitot.
- ✅ **Pitot tube** measures **point velocity**, not flow. Use a **traverse** for accurate $Q$ in non-uniform profiles.
- ✅ **Venturi** has a gradual convergent (21°) and gradual divergent (5°–7°) cone — no vena contracta, low permanent loss.
- ✅ **Flow nozzle** is a venturi with the divergent section removed.
- ✅ **Velocity of approach factor** $K = 1/\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}$ is needed when $d/D > 0.5$.
- ✅ **Mass flow rate** $m = \rho Q$ (in kg/s when $\rho$ in kg/m³ and $Q$ in m³/s).
- ✅ **Standards** to remember for viva: ISO 5167 (orifice, venturi, nozzle), ISO 3966 (pitot), AGA 3 (orifice, gas), ASME MFC-3M (orifice).
- ✅ **Reynolds number** $Re_D = \rho V D / \mu$ governs the value of $C_d$ in ISO 5167 charts. For $Re > 10^4$, $C_d$ is approximately constant.
- ✅ **Cavitation check:** The downstream pressure $P_2$ must be greater than the fluid's vapor pressure $P_v$ to avoid cavitation. KTU viva favourite.
- ✅ **Units discipline:** Always SI (Pa, m³/s, kg/s). Convert manometer mm → m before substitution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Board Tip:** The single most important 2-mark concept in this entire module is the **derivation of the general head-type flow equation** from Bernoulli + Continuity. If you can write this derivation cold, in 5 minutes, on the answer sheet — you have secured 80% of the marks for this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Channel Meters: Weirs and Flumes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Open Channel Meters: Weirs and Flumes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Open Channel?

An **open channel** is any conduit in which liquid flows with a free surface exposed to the atmosphere. Unlike closed-pipe flow, the pressure at the surface equals atmospheric pressure, and the driving force is **gravity acting on the hydraulic head difference** between two points along the channel.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Standard Textbook):**
> An open channel flow meter is a primary device that measures the flow rate of liquids (most commonly water) in open conduits by relating the flow to a measurable static head — typically created by a constriction or obstruction in the channel.

Open channel meters are essential in:
- Water treatment plants
- Sewage systems
- Irrigation canals
- Industrial effluent discharge
- River and dam flow monitoring

---

## 1.2 The Two Principal Families

Open channel flow meters fall into two functional categories:

| Category | Method of Operation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Weirs** | A physical obstruction (plate or notch) raises the water level, and the **head (H)** over the crest is measured | Small to medium flow rates, clean liquids |
| **Flumes** | A specially shaped constriction accelerates flow and lowers the upstream water level; **head (Ha)** is measured at a standard point | Larger flow rates, sewage, dirty water, slurries |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dam and Slide" Model

Imagine a wide, shallow stream flowing down a gentle slope.

1. **The Weir Analogy**: Place a small dam (a wooden plank with a notch cut into it) across the stream. The water cannot pass except through the notch. The depth of water piling up behind the plank is the **head (H)**. Deeper pile → more water → bigger flow. The weir is essentially a **"depth-to-flow translator."**

2. **The Flume Analogy**: Now imagine narrowing the stream into a smooth, throat-shaped slide (like a Venturi tube lying on its side, but open at the top). The water speeds up through the throat and the upstream level drops slightly. Measure the level at a specific upstream point, and you can compute flow. The flume is a **"constriction-to-flow translator."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The fundamental relationship in ALL open channel meters is **Q = f(H)** — flow rate is *always* an explicit function of a single measurable static head. This is why a simple **level sensor** (float, bubbler, ultrasonic, radar) is the typical secondary instrument.

---

## 1.4 Why Open Channel Measurement Matters in Industrial Instrumentation

- **Cost-effective**: No moving parts submerged; minimal pressure drop.
- **Handles solids and debris**: Flumes (especially Parshall) tolerate suspended matter.
- **Self-averaging**: The free surface smooths out turbulence before reaching the measuring section.
- **Standardization**: ISO 1438, ISO 9826, and ASTM standards govern weir/flume dimensions and calibration.

---

## 1.5 Primary Variables and Symbols

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| $Q$ | Volumetric discharge (flow rate) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| $H$ | Head over weir crest / upstream depth | $\text{m}$ |
| $L$ | Length of weir crest | $\text{m}$ |
| $P$ | Height of weir crest above channel bed | $\text{m}$ |
| $B$ | Width of channel approach | $\text{m}$ |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | $\mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$ |
| $C_d$ | Coefficient of discharge | dimensionless |
| $\theta$ | V-notch angle | degrees or radians |
| $H_a$ | Head at the Parshall flume gauge point | $\text{m}$ |
| $W$ | Throat width of Parshall flume | $\text{m}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydraulic Foundation

All weir and flume equations derive from a single physical principle: **Torricelli's Law** (1651), which states that the velocity of a fluid exiting through an orifice under a head $H$ is:

$$v = \sqrt{2gH}$$

Integrating this velocity across the flow area of the weir/flume gives the **theoretical (ideal) discharge**, which is then reduced by an empirical **coefficient of discharge ($C_d \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 0.98$)** to account for:
- Energy losses (viscous, turbulent)
- Velocity of approach
- Surface tension and nappe contraction

---

## 2.2 Classification of Weirs

### A. By Shape of the Notch

| Weir Type | Notch Geometry | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular (Suppressed)** | Full-width rectangular slot | Wide channels, large $Q$ |
| **Rectangular (Contracted)** | Narrower than channel (Francis/Bazin) | Standard laboratory use |
| **Triangular (V-Notch)** | V-shaped, angles 30°, 60°, 90° | Small $Q$, high accuracy |
| **Trapezoidal (Cipolletti)** | Sides slope 1 horizontal : 4 vertical | Field irrigation use |
| **Broad-Crested** | Flat horizontal crest of finite width | Large flows, robust construction |

### B. By Crest Sharpness

- **Sharp-crested weir**: Crest edge thickness $\leq 2$ mm; nappe springs free (used as flow standard).
- **Broad-crested weir**: Crest width $\geq 3H_{\max}$; flow passes through critical depth (used for flow control).

---

## 2.3 Rectangular Weir — Derivation of the Theoretical Equation

Consider a rectangular notch of width $L$, with water standing to a head $H$ above the crest. Consider an elemental horizontal strip of width $L$ and thickness $dh$ at depth $h$ below the free surface.

The strip area: $dA = L \, dh$
The strip velocity (by Torricelli): $v = \sqrt{2gh}$
Elemental discharge: $dQ = v \, dA = L\sqrt{2gh}\,dh$

Integrate from $h=0$ (crest level) to $h=H$ (free surface):

$$Q_{th} = \int_{0}^{H} L\sqrt{2g}\,h^{1/2}\,dh = L\sqrt{2g}\left[\frac{2}{3}h^{3/2}\right]_{0}^{H}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3}\,L\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}}$$

Apply the discharge coefficient $C_d$ for actual flow:

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{2}{3}\,L\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$$

---

## 2.4 Triangular (V-Notch) Weir — Derivation

For a V-notch of angle $\theta$, consider an elemental circular strip at depth $h$ below the apex (free surface).

Width of the strip at depth $h$ from apex: $w = 2(h\tan(\theta/2))$
Area: $dA = 2(h\tan(\theta/2))\,dh$
Velocity: $\sqrt{2gh}$

$$Q_{th} = \int_{0}^{H} 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\,h^{3/2}\,dh = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\left[\frac{2}{5}h^{5/2}\right]_{0}^{H}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{4}{5}\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}}$$

With discharge coefficient:

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{4}{5}\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$$

---

## 2.5 The Cipolletti (Trapezoidal) Weir

The trapezoidal weir with side slopes of **1 horizontal : 4 vertical** combines the advantages of rectangular and triangular weirs. Side slope angle $\theta$ satisfies $\tan(\theta/2) = 1/4$, so the triangular correction exactly compensates for the end contractions of the rectangular portion, eliminating the need for a separate contraction correction.

$$Q = 1.839 \, L \, H^{3/2} \quad (\text{for } L \text{ in m, } H \text{ in m, } Q \text{ in m}^3/\text{s})$$

This is a **Cipolletti constant** derived from $C_d = 0.63$ and $g = 9.81$ m/s².

---

## 2.6 Francis and Bazin Empirical Formulas

For practical measurement with rectangular weirs, two classical empirical formulas are universally tested:

**Francis Formula** (rectangular, with 2 end contractions):
$$Q = 1.84 \left(L - 0.2H\right) H^{3/2}$$

**Bazin Formula** (rectangular, suppressed weir):
$$Q = m\,L\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$$

where $m$ is the Bazin coefficient (typically $0.40$ to $0.45$).

---

## 2.7 Flumes — General Principle

A flume is a **short, open-channel constriction** with a specifically contoured cross-section. The most common is the **Parshall Flume** (1926), patented by Ralph Parshall.

**Operating principle:**
1. Water enters at the converging section.
2. Critical (choked) flow occurs at the throat.
3. As the flow passes the throat into the diverging section, a hydraulic jump forms.
4. The upstream depth $H_a$ (measured at the gauge point, located at $2W/3$ upstream of the throat entrance, where $W$ = throat width) is uniquely related to discharge.

The Parshall flume discharge equation is **empirical and depends on throat size $W$:**

$$Q = C\,H_a^{n}$$

where $C$ and $n$ are constants tabulated for each standard flume size ($W = 1\text{ in}, 3\text{ in}, 6\text{ in}, 9\text{ in}, 1\text{ ft}, 1.5\text{ ft}, 2\text{ ft}, \ldots, 50\text{ ft}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Parshall flumes are popular in industry:**
> - Self-cleaning (passes debris and silt)
> - Low head loss (~25% of weir)
> - High accuracy ($\pm 3\%$ for free flow, $\pm 5\%$ for submerged flow)
> - Standardized dimensions; no field calibration needed

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Device | Discharge Equation | Variables / Range | Typical $C_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rectangular Weir (Suppressed) | $Q = C_d \cdot \frac{2}{3}L\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ | $L$, $H$ in m; $Q$ in m³/s | **0.60 – 0.66** |
| 2 | Rectangular Weir (Contracted) — Francis | $Q = 1.84(L - 0.2H)H^{3/2}$ | $L$, $H$ in m | (implicit 0.623) |
| 3 | V-Notch Weir (90°) | $Q = 2.362\,H^{5/2}$ | $H$ in m, $Q$ in m³/s | **0.58 – 0.61** |
| 4 | V-Notch Weir (General) | $Q = C_d \cdot \frac{4}{5}\tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$ | $\theta$ in rad | **0.58 – 0.61** |
| 5 | Cipolletti Weir | $Q = 1.839\,L\,H^{3/2}$ | $L$, $H$ in m | **0.63** |
| 6 | Broad-Crested Weir | $Q = 1.70\,L\,H^{3/2}$ | $L$, $H$ in m | (effective ≈1.70) |
| 7 | Parshall Flume | $Q = C\,H_a^{\,n}$ | $H_a$ in m; $W$ determines $C$, $n$ | Empirical |
| 8 | Venturi Flume | $Q = C_d \cdot A_t \sqrt{2g(H_a - H_b)}$ | $H_a - H_b$ = head difference | **0.95 – 0.98** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on units in imperial formulas:** The constants 1.84, 2.362, 1.839 are derived assuming $H$ in **metres** and $Q$ in **m³/s**. If $H$ is in feet and $Q$ in cfs, the constants change (e.g., Francis becomes $Q = 3.33(L - 0.2H)H^{3/2}$).

---

## 2.9 Engineering Selection Criteria

When choosing between a weir and a flume for an industrial application:

| Parameter | Prefer Weir | Prefer Flume |
|---|---|---|
| Solids / debris content | Low | High |
| Head loss required | Acceptable | Must be minimized |
| Accuracy at low flows | V-notch excellent | Moderate |
| Installation cost | Low | Higher (precise geometry) |
| Standardization | Varies | Parshall is fully standardized |
| Silt / grease buildup | Common problem | Self-cleaning |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation — Rectangular Weir

### Step 1: Setup the Geometry

Place the rectangular weir of crest length $L$ in a vertical plane. The free surface of water upstream stands at a height $H$ above the crest. The channel is wide enough that $P$ (crest height above bed) is small compared to $H$ (velocity-of-approach neglected in this derivation).

### Step 2: Define Elemental Strip

Consider an elemental horizontal strip at depth $h$ measured downward from the free surface (so $h = 0$ at the surface and $h = H$ at the crest). The strip has:
- Vertical position: depth $h$ below free surface
- Thickness: $dh$
- Width: $L$
- Area: $dA = L \, dh$

### Step 3: Velocity of Water Through the Strip

By **Torricelli's Theorem** (a special case of Bernoulli's equation with the free surface as the upper stagnation point and the strip as the exit point, with negligible atmospheric pressure differential and small velocities at the surface):

$$v(h) = \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 4: Elemental Discharge

$$dQ = v \cdot dA = L\sqrt{2gh}\,dh$$

### Step 5: Integrate Over the Full Head

Total theoretical discharge:

$$Q_{th} = \int_{0}^{H} L\sqrt{2g}\,h^{1/2}\,dh$$

Pull constants out:

$$Q_{th} = L\sqrt{2g}\int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2}\,dh$$

Use $\int h^{1/2} dh = \frac{2}{3}h^{3/2}$:

$$Q_{th} = L\sqrt{2g} \cdot \left[\frac{2}{3}h^{3/2}\right]_{0}^{H}$$

Evaluate limits:

$$Q_{th} = L\sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{2}{3}\left(H^{3/2} - 0^{3/2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3}L\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}}$$

### Step 6: Apply Discharge Coefficient

Real flow loses energy to friction, turbulence, nappe contraction, and viscous shear. We introduce $C_d < 1$:

$$Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot \frac{2}{3}L\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$$

### Step 7: Velocity-of-Approach Correction (Advanced)

If $v_a = Q/A_{channel}$ is non-negligible, add $\frac{v_a^2}{2g}$ to $H$:

$$Q = \frac{2}{3}C_d\,L\sqrt{2g}\left[\left(H + \frac{v_a^2}{2g}\right)^{3/2} - \left(\frac{v_a^2}{2g}\right)^{3/2}\right]$$

---

## 3.2 Complete Derivation — V-Notch Weir

### Step 1: Geometry of the V-Notch

The notch is an inverted V with apex pointing downward. The half-angle at the apex is $\theta/2$. At depth $h$ below the free surface (which lies at the same level as the apex — actually at height $H$ above the apex), the width of the elemental horizontal strip is:

$$w(h) = 2(H - h)\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

Wait — let us redefine $h$ as the vertical distance measured from the **apex** upward. Then $h$ ranges from $0$ (at apex) to $H$ (at free surface), and the strip width at height $h$ is:

$$w(h) = 2\,h\,\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

### Step 2: Elemental Area and Velocity

$$dA = w(h)\,dh = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) h \, dh$$

The velocity at this strip (since water falls from the free surface a height $h$ below it):

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 3: Elemental Discharge

$$dQ = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) h \cdot \sqrt{2gh}\,dh = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\,h^{3/2}\,dh$$

### Step 4: Integrate

$$Q_{th} = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\int_{0}^{H} h^{3/2}\,dh$$

Use $\int h^{3/2} dh = \frac{2}{5}h^{5/2}$:

$$Q_{th} = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{2}{5}\left[H^{5/2} - 0\right]$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{4}{5}\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}}$$

### Step 5: Apply $C_d$

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{4}{5}\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$$

For a 90° V-notch ($\theta = 90°$), $\tan(45°) = 1$, so:

$$Q = \frac{4}{5}C_d\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$$

With $C_d = 0.611$ and $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$Q = (0.611)(0.8)(\sqrt{19.62})\,H^{5/2} = (0.611)(0.8)(4.429)\,H^{5/2}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 2.165\,H^{5/2} \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{s, with } H \text{ in m})}$$

Some KTU textbooks round this to **2.362** by using slightly different $C_d$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Rectangular Weir

**Problem:** Water flows over a suppressed rectangular weir of crest length $L = 1.20$ m. The measured head is $H = 0.25$ m. Take $C_d = 0.62$.

**Solution:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{2}{3} L \sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$$

Substitute:

$$Q = 0.62 \times \frac{2}{3} \times 1.20 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.25)^{3/2}$$

Compute step by step:

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294\ \text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

$$(0.25)^{3/2} = \sqrt{0.25^3} = \sqrt{0.015625} = 0.125\ \text{m}^{3/2}$$

Assemble:

$$Q = 0.62 \times 0.6667 \times 1.20 \times 4.4294 \times 0.125$$

$$Q = 0.62 \times 0.6667 \times 0.6638$$

$$Q = 0.2743\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.274\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 274\ \text{litres/second}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — 90° V-Notch

**Problem:** A 90° V-notch weir carries water with head $H = 0.20$ m. Use $C_d = 0.61$.

**Solution:**

$$Q = \frac{4}{5}C_d\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$$

$$Q = 0.8 \times 0.61 \times 4.4294 \times (0.20)^{5/2}$$

$(0.20)^{5/2} = 0.20^2 \times \sqrt{0.20} = 0.04 \times 0.4472 = 0.01789\ \text{m}^{5/2}$

$$Q = 0.8 \times 0.61 \times 4.4294 \times 0.01789$$

$$Q = 0.8 \times 0.61 \times 0.07926$$

$$Q = 0.03869\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 38.7\ \text{litres/second}}$$

---

## 3.5 Comparative Accuracy Analysis

A key KTU conceptual point: **Why does the V-notch weir give better accuracy at low flows?**

Sensitivity of $Q$ to measurement error $\Delta H$ is:

$$\frac{dQ}{dH} \propto H^{1/2} \quad \text{(rectangular)}$$

$$\frac{dQ}{dH} \propto H^{3/2} \quad \text{(V-notch)}$$

For small $H$, a small head measurement error $\Delta H$ causes a smaller fractional error in the rectangular case but a *proportionally larger* fractional error in the V-notch. However, what matters more is the **absolute range of $H$** covered. The V-notch spans a wider practical $H$ range (good resolution at low $Q$), whereas the rectangular weir saturates quickly.

In practice: at $Q < 50$ L/s, use a V-notch. At $Q > 50$ L/s, use a rectangular or Cipolletti weir.

---

## 3.6 Derivation — Parshall Flume Discharge Equation

The Parshall flume is empirical, so its discharge equation is determined by **curve fitting** to experimental data. For the standard free-flow regime (downstream water level below the throat level — submergence $< 70\%$):

$$Q = C\,H_a^{\,n}$$

For example, for a 1-foot ($W = 0.305$ m) Parshall flume, the standard calibrated constants are:

$$Q = 0.992\,H_a^{1.55} \quad (Q \text{ in cfs, } H_a \text{ in ft})$$

In SI units:

$$Q = 0.381\,H_a^{1.55} \quad (Q \text{ in m}^3/\text{s, } H_a \text{ in m})$$

For a 2-foot ($W = 0.610$ m) flume:

$$Q = 2.06\,H_a^{1.59} \quad (Q \text{ in cfs, } H_a \text{ in ft})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Submerged flow correction:** If the submergence ratio exceeds 70%, a single $H_a$ measurement is insufficient. Both upstream ($H_a$) and downstream ($H_b$) heads must be measured and a correction factor $S$ applied:
> $$S = \frac{H_b}{H_a}, \quad Q_{corrected} = \frac{Q_{free}}{\text{correction chart}}$$
> This is the standard source of **submerged-flow error** in industry.

---

## 3.7 Summary Table — Error Sources in Open Channel Measurement

| Error Source | Magnitude | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Head measurement uncertainty ($\pm 1$ mm) | 1.5 – 3% of $Q$ | Use ultrasonic/radar level sensor |
| Velocity of approach | 1 – 5% of $Q$ | Keep $P \geq 2H_{\max}$ |
| Weir plate deformation | 2 – 10% | Use stainless steel plate |
| Tailwater submergence (weir) | Major | Maintain free-falling nappe |
| Parshall submergence > 70% | Major | Two-head measurement |
| Silt / algae on crest | 5 – 30% | Routine maintenance schedule |
| Temperature-induced $g$ variation | < 0.1% | Negligible |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
