# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define an open channel and list the two principal categories of open channel flow meters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An open channel is a conduit in which a liquid flows with a free surface exposed to atmospheric pressure, where flow is driven by gravity acting on the hydraulic gradient.

The two principal categories are:

1. **Weirs** — A physical obstruction (plate with notch) raises the upstream water level; the head $H$ over the crest is measured and related to discharge $Q$.
2. **Flumes** — A shaped constriction accelerates flow through a throat; the upstream head $H_a$ at a standard gauge point is measured and related to $Q$.

*(Marks: 1 for definition + 1 for weir explanation + 1 for flume explanation = 3)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the Francis formula for a rectangular weir with two end contractions. What is its empirical basis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Francis formula (SI units, $L$, $H$ in metres, $Q$ in m³/s):

$$Q = 1.84\,(L - 0.2H)\,H^{3/2}$$

**Empirical basis:** It was derived by James B. Francis (1850s) from extensive experiments on the Lowell Canal, Massachusetts. He observed that the actual discharge is less than the theoretical rectangular-weir equation by about 11–12% and introduced the term $(L - 0.2H)$ to account for **two end contractions** of the nappe, where $0.1H$ is empirically subtracted per end.

The numerical constant 1.84 combines $C_d \approx 0.623$, $\frac{2}{3}$, and $\sqrt{2g}$.

*(Marks: 1 for formula + 1 for variable meaning + 1 for empirical origin = 3)*

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**Question A(a) [7 marks]:**
**(i)** Derive the theoretical discharge equation for a **rectangular suppressed weir**, starting from Torricelli's theorem. (4 marks)
**(ii)** A rectangular suppressed weir has crest length $L = 0.80$ m and the measured head is $H = 18$ cm. Use $C_d = 0.62$ and find the discharge in **litres per second**. (3 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 for full derivation.)*

- Elemental area: $dA = L\,dh$
- Velocity by Torricelli: $v = \sqrt{2gh}$
- Elemental discharge: $dQ = L\sqrt{2gh}\,dh$
- Integration: $Q_{th} = L\sqrt{2g}\int_0^H h^{1/2}dh = \frac{2}{3}L\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$
- With discharge coefficient: $Q = C_d \cdot \frac{2}{3}L\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$

**(ii) Numerical:**

$$Q = 0.62 \times \frac{2}{3} \times 0.80 \times \sqrt{19.62} \times (0.18)^{3/2}$$

$(0.18)^{3/2} = 0.18 \times \sqrt{0.18} = 0.18 \times 0.4243 = 0.07637$

$$Q = 0.62 \times 0.6667 \times 0.80 \times 4.4294 \times 0.07637$$

$$Q = 0.62 \times 0.6667 \times 0.2706 = 0.1119\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 111.9\ \text{litres/second}}$$

---

**Question A(b) [7 marks]:**
**(i)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Parshall flume**. (4 marks)
**(ii)** A 1-foot ($W = 0.305$ m) Parshall flume has upstream head $H_a = 0.40$ m. Use the SI relation $Q = 0.381\,H_a^{1.55}$ m³/s and find the discharge. Comment on whether free flow or submerged flow is likely. (3 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Parshall Flume Construction and Working:**

A Parshall flume consists of three sections:

1. **Converging (inlet) section** — Floor is level, side walls converge from channel width down to throat width $W$ over a length of $2W/3$.
2. **Throat section** — A short parallel-walled section of width $W$ with a downward-sloping floor (drop = $W/4$).
3. **Diverging (outlet) section** — Floor rises gently (slope ~ 1:6), walls diverge over a length of $3W/4$.

**Working principle:**
- As water enters the converging section, the cross-section reduces, velocity increases, and the water level **drops** at the throat.
- At the throat, the flow becomes **critical** ($Fr = 1$), and a hydraulic jump may form in the diverging section.
- The upstream head $H_a$ is measured at a **gauge point** located $2W/3$ upstream of the throat entrance, where the floor is still level.
- Since critical flow occurs at the throat, $H_a$ alone uniquely determines $Q$ (for free flow conditions, submergence < 70%).

**Sketch (Block Diagram Representation):**

```
        Floor Profile:                              Water Surface:
                   ____                            ___________      
        __________/    \____________               |           \____
       (level)          (drop W/4) (level rise 1:6)
                |<--2W/3-->|<--W-->|<--3W/4-->|
                                  THROAT
        Ha measured here ─────►
        (2W/3 upstream of throat entrance)
```

**(ii) Numerical:**

$$Q = 0.381 \times (0.40)^{1.55}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Compute $(0.40)^{1.55}$:
$$\ln(0.40) = -0.9163,\quad 1.55 \times (-0.9163) = -1.4203$$
$$e^{-1.4203} = 0.2416$$

$$Q = 0.381 \times 0.2416 = 0.0921\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 92.1\ \text{litres/second}}$$

**Comment:** Since only $H_a$ is given and $H_b$ is unknown, free flow is **assumed**. In practice, one must verify that downstream tailwater does not submerge the jump (submergence ratio $H_b/H_a < 0.70$ for free flow). If submergence > 70%, the simple $Q = C\,H_a^n$ equation overestimates $Q$ and a correction is required.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**Question B(a) [7 marks]:**
**(i)** Derive the discharge equation for a **V-notch (triangular) weir**. State clearly the assumption made. (4 marks)
**(ii)** For a 60° V-notch weir with $H = 0.30$ m and $C_d = 0.60$, calculate the discharge in **L/s**. (3 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Derivation:** *(See Section 3.2 for full derivation.)*

- Elemental strip at depth $h$ below free surface has width $w = 2(H-h)\tan(\theta/2)$.
- Velocity by Torricelli: $v = \sqrt{2g(H-h)}$.
- Elemental discharge: $dQ = 2\tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2g}\,(H-h)^{3/2}\,dh$.
- Integration from $h=0$ (surface) to $h=H$ (apex): $Q_{th} = \frac{4}{5}\tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$.
- With $C_d$: $Q = C_d \cdot \frac{4}{5}\tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$.

**Assumption:** Velocity of approach is negligible; atmospheric pressure acts uniformly over the nappe; flow is steady, incompressible, and ideal except for the lumped $C_d$ correction.

**(ii) Numerical:**

For $\theta = 60°$, $\theta/2 = 30°$, $\tan(30°) = 1/\sqrt{3} = 0.5774$.

$$Q = 0.60 \times \frac{4}{5} \times 0.5774 \times \sqrt{19.62} \times (0.30)^{5/2}$$

$(0.30)^{5/2} = 0.30^2 \times \sqrt{0.30} = 0.09 \times 0.5477 = 0.04929$

$$Q = 0.60 \times 0.8 \times 0.5774 \times 4.4294 \times 0.04929$$

$$Q = 0.60 \times 0.8 \times 0.1261 = 0.0605\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 60.5\ \text{litres/second}}$$

---

**Question B(b) [7 marks]:**
**(i)** Compare **weirs and flumes** as open channel flow measurement devices, listing at least four comparative points. (4 marks)
**(ii)** A Cipolletti weir has crest length $L = 1.5$ m and head $H = 0.40$ m. Find the discharge using the standard formula. (3 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Weir | Flume |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Plate with notch | Shaped concrete/steel channel |
| Head loss | High (full obstruction) | Low (~25% of weir) |
| Accuracy | ±1–3% (clean water) | ±2–5% |
| Handling solids | Poor (debris accumulates on crest) | Excellent (self-cleaning, e.g., Parshall) |
| Cost | Low | Higher (precise dimensions required) |
| Suitable for | Clean water, low flow | Sewage, industrial effluent, slurry |
| Standardization | Approximate | Strictly standardized (Parshall) |
| Calibration | Empirical $C_d$ required | Factory calibrated |

**(ii) Numerical:**

Cipolletti formula:

$$Q = 1.839 \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$$

$$Q = 1.839 \times 1.5 \times (0.40)^{3/2}$$

$(0.40)^{3/2} = 0.40 \times \sqrt{0.40} = 0.40 \times 0.6325 = 0.2530$

$$Q = 1.839 \times 1.5 \times 0.2530$$

$$Q = 1.839 \times 0.3795$$

$$Q = 0.6979\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 698\ \text{litres/second}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $H^{3/2}$ and $H^{5/2}$:** Students frequently compute $(H)^{3/2}$ as $H \times 3/2$. Correct: $(H)^{3/2} = H \cdot \sqrt{H}$. This mistake alone costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing SI and imperial constants:** The constant 1.84 in the Francis formula applies ONLY for SI units (m, m³/s). Using it with $H$ in cm or inches causes 100% error.
> 3. **Forgetting $C_d$:** Students often write only the theoretical formula and omit $C_d$. You lose **2 marks** for this — the question explicitly asks for "actual discharge."
> 4. **Not specifying the weir type:** "Rectangular weir" is ambiguous — suppressed vs. contracted require different formulas. Always state which.
> 5. **Confusing $H_a$ and $H_b$ in Parshall flume:** The gauge point is **upstream** of the throat. Writing $H_a$ at the wrong location is a fatal error.
> 6. **Neglecting submergence in Parshall:** If you cannot guarantee free flow, state the assumption explicitly; otherwise the examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 7. **Constant 1.839 vs 1.84 confusion:** 1.839 is for Cipolletti; 1.84 is for Francis — they are NOT interchangeable.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### ✅ Core Concepts to Remember
- Open channel = free surface flow; $Q = f(H)$ always.
- Two main classes: **Weirs** (obstruction) and **Flumes** (constriction).
- All equations derive from **Torricelli's theorem** + **discharge coefficient** $C_d$.

### ✅ Formula Quick-Reference

| Device | Formula | $C_d$ (typical) |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangular suppressed weir | $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ | 0.62 |
| Rectangular contracted (Francis) | $Q = 1.84(L - 0.2H)H^{3/2}$ | implicit |
| V-notch (90°) | $Q = 2.362\,H^{5/2}$ | 0.61 |
| V-notch (general) | $Q = \frac{4}{5}C_d \tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$ | 0.60 |
| Cipolletti | $Q = 1.839\,L\,H^{3/2}$ | 0.63 |
| Parshall flume | $Q = C\,H_a^{\,n}$ | empirical |
| Broad-crested weir | $Q = 1.70\,L\,H^{3/2}$ | (effective) |

### ✅ Design Rules of Thumb
- **Approach channel length** ≥ $10 H_{max}$ for calm flow.
- **Crest height** $P \geq 2 H_{max}$ to keep velocity-of-approach < 5%.
- **V-notch preferred** for $Q < 50$ L/s.
- **Parshall preferred** for sewage/dirty water.
- **Submergence limit** for Parshall free flow: $H_b/H_a < 0.70$.

### ✅ Sensors Commonly Paired with Weir/Flume
- Float-type level transmitter (cheap, mechanical)
- Ultrasonic (non-contact, range 0.3–10 m)
- Bubbler (works with foam and turbulence)
- Radar (chemical-resistant, most accurate)

### ✅ Common KTU Errors to Avoid
- Wrong sign in $H_a - 0.2H$ term
- Mixing units in tabular constants
- Omitting $C_d$
- Misidentifying V-notch angle $\theta$ (full vs half angle)
- Treating $C_d$ as universal across weir types

### ✅ Standard Reference Tables to Memorize
- Parshall flume calibration constants for $W = 1\text{ in}, 3\text{ in}, 6\text{ in}, 9\text{ in}, 1\text{ ft}, 2\text{ ft}, 3\text{ ft}, 8\text{ ft}$ (provided in KTU data book).
- Francis, Bazin, Rehbock, and Swiss formulas for rectangular weirs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electromagnetic Flowmeters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electromagnetic Flowmeters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electromagnetic Flowmeter** (also known as a **Magmeter** or **EMF**) is a volumetric flow measurement instrument that operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** to determine the flow rate of a conductive liquid (slurry, water, blood, chemicals, pulp) flowing through a pipe.

The device measures the **induced electromotive force (EMF)** generated when a conductive fluid passes through a **transverse magnetic field** established across a non-magnetic, electrically insulated flow tube. The induced EMF, picked up by a pair of electrodes mounted diametrically opposite to each other and flush with the inner pipe wall, is directly proportional to the **mean fluid velocity** and hence the **volumetric flow rate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The electromagnetic flowmeter is fundamentally an *obstruction-less flowmeter*. It introduces **zero pressure drop**, has **no moving parts**, and is highly suitable for measuring **abrasive slurries, corrosive chemicals, and sanitary fluids** (food, pharma, water treatment).

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a river flowing from **West to East**. Now, imagine placing a giant horseshoe magnet on the North and South banks such that the magnetic field points from North to South (top to bottom in the cross-section). 

As the conductive river water flows East through this magnetic field, the positively charged ions get pushed *upwards* (toward the North bank) and the negatively charged ions get pushed *downwards* (toward the South bank). This separation of charges creates a measurable **voltage difference** between the two banks.

**That voltage difference is exactly what the electromagnetic flowmeter measures on a smaller, industrial scale using electrodes instead of riverbanks.** 

The faster the river flows → the stronger the voltage → which the instrument converts directly into a flow rate reading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Just like a river's speed can be "felt" by how strongly the water pushes floating leaves against a bank, the magmeter "feels" the fluid's velocity by the voltage it generates between two electrodes.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Magnetic Flux Density (B):** Typically maintained between **0.1 T to 0.5 T** (1 Tesla = 10,000 Gauss).
- **Minimum Fluid Electrical Conductivity:** At least **5 µS/cm** (micro-siemens per centimeter); pure deionized water may not be measurable.
- **Linearity:** Outstanding, typically **±0.5% of rate** over a wide flow range (turndown ratio up to **1000:1**).
- **Standard Output:** 4–20 mA current loop, pulse output, or HART/digital protocol.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle — Faraday's Law

When a conductor of length $L$ moves with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a magnetic field $B$, the induced EMF is given by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**:

$$e = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

In a magmeter:
- The **conductor** is replaced by the **moving conductive fluid column** of effective length $L = D$ (the inner diameter of the pipe).
- The motion is the **axial flow velocity** $v$ of the fluid.

Hence the basic induced voltage is:

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v$$

Where:
- $E$ = Induced EMF (Volts)
- $B$ = Magnetic flux density (Tesla)
- $D$ = Inner diameter of the flow tube (meters)
- $v$ = Mean fluid velocity (meters/second)

---

### 2.2 Why the Inner Wall Must Be Electrically Insulated

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For Faraday's Law to be valid, the conductive fluid must be the **only current path** between the electrodes. If the metal pipe wall were in contact with the fluid, it would **short-circuit** the EMF. Therefore, a non-conductive liner (PTFE, PFA, rubber, ceramic) is used as an insulator between the fluid and the external magnetic coils.

---

### 2.3 Advantages & Limitations Summary

**Advantages:**
- **No moving parts** → High reliability, long life.
- **Obstruction-less** → Zero permanent pressure loss.
- **Independent of viscosity, density, and turbulence** → Ideal for slurries.
- **Bidirectional flow measurement** is inherently supported.
- **Excellent linearity** with respect to velocity.

**Limitations:**
- Requires fluid conductivity $\geq$ **5 µS/cm** (cannot measure hydrocarbons, oils, gases).
- Susceptible to **stray electromagnetic interference (EMI)**.
- Expensive compared to orifice plates or rotameters.
- Cannot measure **stationary (zero flow)** fluid — no EMF when $v = 0$.
- Affected by **slurry build-up / electrode coating** if the fluid is fouling.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Variables / Units | Engineering Use |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Faraday's Induced EMF | $E = B \cdot D \cdot v$ | $E$ (V), $B$ (T), $D$ (m), $v$ (m/s) | Core voltage–velocity relation |
| Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot v$ | $Q$ (m³/s), $D$ (m), $v$ (m/s) | Used to derive the K-factor equation |
| Mean Velocity from EMF | $v = \dfrac{E}{B \cdot D}$ | Rearranged form | Used to compute flow electronically |
| Flow Rate from EMF | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D \cdot E}{4 B}$ | Substituting $v$ into $Q$ equation | Final transfer function of magmeter |
| Signal Current (4–20 mA) | $I = 4 + \dfrac{16 \cdot (Q - Q_{\min})}{Q_{\max} - Q_{\min}}$ | $I$ (mA), $Q$ (m³/s) | Industrial current loop scaling |
| Magnetic Reynolds Number | $R_{m} = \sigma \cdot \mu \cdot v \cdot D$ | $\sigma$ (S/m), $\mu$ (H/m) | Indicates if magnetic field is distorted by flow |
| Electrode Voltage Sensitivity | $\dfrac{E}{v} = B \cdot D$ | Volts per (m/s) | Used during calibration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Most KTU 14-mark derivations require you to start from $E = B \cdot D \cdot v$ and arrive at $Q = \dfrac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B}$ by substituting the cross-sectional area $A = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Faraday's Law to the Volumetric Flow Rate Equation

**Starting Concept:** 
A conductive fluid flowing through a non-magnetic, insulated pipe of inner diameter $D$ passes through a magnetic field of flux density $B$.

---

**Step 1: Write Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**

The induced EMF across the moving fluid conductor is:

$$e = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

In the magmeter geometry, the effective length $L$ of the fluid conductor exposed to the magnetic field is equal to the inner pipe diameter $D$. Substituting:

$$e = B \cdot D \cdot v$$

*Logic:* The fluid acts as a single moving conductor of length equal to the diameter because the electrodes are diametrically opposite to each other, perpendicular to both the flow axis and the magnetic field.

---

**Step 2: Express the Volumetric Flow Rate**

The volumetric flow rate $Q$ is the product of the pipe's cross-sectional area $A$ and the mean fluid velocity $v$:

$$Q = A \cdot v = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot v$$

*Logic:* Standard continuity equation where $A$ is the circular cross-section of the pipe.

---

**Step 3: Solve for Velocity from Faraday's Equation**

Rearranging the induced EMF equation for velocity:

$$v = \frac{e}{B \cdot D}$$

*Logic:* Isolate $v$ to express mean velocity as a function of the measured voltage.

---

**Step 4: Substitute Velocity into the Flow Rate Equation**

Insert $v$ from Step 3 into the flow rate equation from Step 2:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot v \\
Q &= \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot \frac{e}{B \cdot D} \\
Q &= \frac{\pi D \cdot e}{4 \cdot B}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Cancel one $D$ from numerator and denominator. This is the **fundamental transfer function** of an electromagnetic flowmeter.

---

**Step 5: Final Compact Form**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \cdot D}{4 \cdot B} \cdot e}$$

*Logic:* Since $\pi$, $D$, and $B$ are **constants** for a given installation, the volumetric flow rate $Q$ is **directly and linearly proportional** to the measured induced EMF $e$. This guarantees the magmeter's excellent linearity.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** 
A magmeter has an inner pipe diameter of **50 mm**. The magnetic flux density is maintained at **0.2 T**. The electrodes measure an induced EMF of **2.5 mV**. Calculate:
1. The mean fluid velocity.
2. The volumetric flow rate in **liters per minute (L/min)**.

---

**Solution Part (a): Mean Velocity**

Using Faraday's equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{e}{B \cdot D} \\
v &= \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-3}} \\
v &= \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-3}} \\
v &= 0.25 \ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

*Result:* The fluid flows at **0.25 m/s** (≈ 25 cm/s), a typical mid-range velocity for water.

---

**Solution Part (b): Volumetric Flow Rate in L/min**

First, compute $Q$ in m³/s:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot v \\
Q &= \frac{\pi \times (0.05)^{2}}{4} \times 0.25 \\
Q &= \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} \times 0.25 \\
Q &= \frac{0.00785398}{4} \times 0.25 \\
Q &= 0.0019635 \times 0.25 \\
Q &= 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}
\end{aligned}$$

Now convert to L/min:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^{3}\text{/s} \times 1000 \ \frac{\text{L}}{\text{m}^{3}} \times 60 \ \frac{\text{s}}{\text{min}} \\
Q &= 29.45 \ \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}$$

*Final Result:* The pipeline is carrying approximately **29.45 L/min**.

---

### 3.3 Alternative Derivation Using Signal Current

If the magmeter electronics convert the induced voltage into a 4–20 mA loop current, the current signal is:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{out}} &= 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{Q - Q_{\min}}{Q_{\max} - Q_{\min}} \\
\text{or, when normalized:} \quad I_{\text{out}} &= 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{e - e_{\min}}{e_{\max} - e_{\min}}
\end{aligned}$$

This linear current relationship is then fed into a **PID controller** or **SCADA/DCS system** for process automation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Working Principle Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram describes the **functional architecture** of an electromagnetic flowmeter, mapping how the magnetic excitation, fluid motion, and signal conditioning interact to produce a flow reading.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Excitation Source 50 Hz or Pulsed DC] --> B[Magnetic Coils Wrapped Around Flow Tube]
    B --> C[Transverse Magnetic Field B Across Pipe Diameter]
    D[Conductive Fluid Enters Insulated Flow Tube] --> E[Fluid Moves Through Magnetic Field]
    E --> F[Induced EMF Generated Per Faraday Law E = BxDxV]
    F --> G[Two Flush Electrodes Pick Up Signal Voltage]
    G --> H[Differential Amplifier Stage]
    H --> I[Noise Rejection Filter 50 Hz Notch]
    I --> J[ADC and Microprocessor]
    J --> K[Linearization and Scaling]
    K --> L[4-20 mA Current Loop Output]
    K --> M[HART or Modbus Digital Output]
    K --> N[Local LCD Flow Display]
```

*Reading the Diagram:* AC excitation powers the coils → field interacts with moving fluid → EMF appears across electrodes → signal is amplified, filtered, digitized, scaled, and converted to standard industrial outputs.

---

### 4.2 Physical Cross-Section Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIPE[Insulated Flow Tube Liner PTFE] --> AXIS[Pipe Axis Flow Direction V]
    COIL1[Magnetic Coil N Pole] --> FIELD[Transverse Magnetic Field B]
    COIL2[Magnetic Coil S Pole] --> FIELD
    FIELD --> CONDUCTOR[Conductive Fluid Element of Length D]
    CONDUCTOR --> EMF[Induced EMF E Appears Across Electrodes]
    EL1[Electrode Plus on Top Wall] --> EMF
    EL2[Electrode Minus on Bottom Wall] --> EMF
    EMF --> OUT[Voltage Signal to Transmitter]
```

*Reading the Diagram:* The horizontal pipe carries conductive fluid along its axis. The two magnetic coils create a vertical magnetic field. As the fluid moves axially, the Lorentz force on its charges creates an EMF measured by the top (+) and bottom (−) electrodes.

---

### 4.3 Functional Subsystem Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph S1[Primary Sensor]
        S1A[Non-Magnetic Flow Tube]
        S1B[Insulating Liner]
        S1C[Magnetic Coils]
        S1D[Electrodes]
    end
    subgraph S2[Signal Conditioning]
        S2A[Pre-Amplifier]
        S2B[EMI Shielding]
        S2C[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
        S2D[ADC Converter]
    end
    subgraph S3[Output Stage]
        S3A[Linearizer]
        S3B[4-20 mA Converter]
        S3C[Digital Protocol]
        S3D[Display Unit]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
```

*Reading the Diagram:* Three functional layers — Primary Sensor (physical transduction), Signal Conditioning (cleaning up the low-mV signal), and Output Stage (industrial interfacing).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the working principle of an electromagnetic flowmeter. State any two limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

The electromagnetic flowmeter works on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. A magnetic field $B$ is established perpendicular to the flow direction by coils wrapped around an insulated flow tube. When a conductive fluid of velocity $v$ flows through the pipe, an EMF is induced across two diametrically opposite electrodes mounted flush with the inner wall:

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v$$

This EMF is linearly proportional to the mean fluid velocity. The volumetric flow rate is:

$$Q = \frac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B}$$

**Limitations:**
1. Cannot measure fluids with electrical conductivity below **5 µS/cm** (e.g., oils, hydrocarbons, gases).
2. Affected by stray electromagnetic interference; requires proper grounding and shielding.

*(Other acceptable limitations: No measurement of zero/stationary flow; expensive; electrode fouling in dirty fluids.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is the inner lining of an electromagnetic flowmeter made of a non-conductive material?**

**Model Answer:**

The induced EMF measured by the electrodes must be generated **exclusively within the moving conductive fluid**. If the metal pipe wall were to come in direct contact with the fluid, it would provide a parallel conductive path, **short-circuiting** the EMF and resulting in a near-zero voltage at the electrodes. To prevent this, an **electrically insulating liner** such as PTFE, PFA, rubber, or ceramic is used between the fluid and the external magnetic structure. This ensures the fluid itself is the only conductor carrying the induced current between the two electrodes, preserving measurement integrity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Question (14 Marks)

> **Module 3 Internal Choice:** Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the volumetric flow rate $Q$ and the induced EMF $E$ in an electromagnetic flowmeter. State the assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A magmeter has a bore diameter of **80 mm** and operates at a magnetic flux density of **0.15 T**. The measured electrode voltage is **3.2 mV**. Calculate:
   1. The mean fluid velocity.
   2. The volumetric flow rate in **m³/hour**.
**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions:*
1. The fluid is **homogeneous** and **electrically conductive**.
2. The flow is **axisymmetric** (uniform velocity profile) across the cross-section.
3. The **magnetic field is uniform** across the entire flow cross-section.
4. The pipe wall is **electrically insulated** from the fluid.

*Derivation:*

By Faraday's Law, when a conductor of length $L$ moves perpendicular to a magnetic field $B$ with velocity $v$, the induced EMF is:

$$e = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

In the magmeter, the effective conductor length is the pipe diameter $D$:

$$e = B \cdot D \cdot v$$

The volumetric flow rate is:

$$Q = A \cdot v = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot v$$

Solving for $v$:

$$v = \frac{e}{B \cdot D}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi D}{4 B} \cdot e}$$

This shows that $Q$ is directly proportional to $E$ when $D$ and $B$ are constant — guaranteeing excellent linearity.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$, $B = 0.15 \text{ T}$, $e = 3.2 \text{ mV} = 3.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$

**Step 1: Mean Velocity**

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{e}{B \cdot D} \\
v &= \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.15 \times 0.08} \\
v &= \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.012} \\
v &= 0.2667 \ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Volumetric Flow Rate**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot v \\
Q &= \frac{\pi \times (0.08)^{2}}{4} \times 0.2667 \\
Q &= \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} \times 0.2667 \\
Q &= 0.0050265 \times 0.2667 \\
Q &= 1.3406 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to m³/hour:

$$Q = 1.3406 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 4.826 \ \text{m}^{3}\text{/hr}$$

**Final Answer:** $v = 0.267$ m/s and $Q = 4.83$ m³/hr.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an electromagnetic flowmeter. List any **four advantages** and **three disadvantages**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sanitary electromagnetic flowmeter is used in a pharmaceutical plant to measure purified water flow. The pipe diameter is **40 mm**, magnetic field strength is **0.25 T**, and the induced EMF observed is **1.8 mV**. Determine:
   1. The volumetric flow rate in **L/min**.
   2. Whether the flow is suitable for a typical process limit of **20 L/min** (Comment on the result). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:** 
A magmeter consists of (refer to Section 4 block diagram):
- A **non-magnetic stainless steel flow tube** (e.g., SS304/SS316) for mechanical strength.
- An **internal insulating liner** (PTFE, PFA, ceramic) to electrically isolate the fluid.
- Two **magnetic coils** (solenoids) externally mounted on the pipe, energized by an AC or pulsed-DC excitation source to generate a uniform transverse magnetic field $B$.
- Two **flush-mounted electrodes** positioned diametrically opposite and perpendicular to both the magnetic field and the flow axis.
- A **transmitter unit** containing pre-amplifier, filters, ADC, microcontroller, and 4–20 mA output stage.

**Working:**
The conductive fluid flows through the pipe. As it crosses the magnetic field, Faraday's Law induces an EMF proportional to velocity:

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v$$

The electrodes sense this EMF. The transmitter amplifies the weak signal (microvolts to millivolts), filters noise, linearizes it, and outputs a 4–20 mA signal proportional to flow rate.

**Advantages (any four):**
1. No moving parts → highly reliable.
2. No pressure drop → energy efficient.
3. Independent of viscosity, density, and temperature.
4. Excellent linearity and high turndown ratio.

**Disadvantages (any three):**
1. Requires minimum fluid conductivity of 5 µS/cm.
2. Cannot measure non-conductive fluids (oils, gases).
3. Higher initial cost than mechanical meters.
4. Sensitive to stray EMI.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$, $B = 0.25 \text{ T}$, $e = 1.8 \text{ mV} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$

**Step 1: Volumetric Flow Rate**

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{e}{B \cdot D} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{0.25 \times 0.04} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01} = 0.18 \ \text{m/s} \\
Q &= \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot v = \frac{\pi \times (0.04)^{2}}{4} \times 0.18 \\
Q &= 0.0012566 \times 0.18 = 2.262 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to L/min:

$$Q = 2.262 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 \times 60 = 13.57 \ \text{L/min}$$

**Step 2: Process Comparison**

The flow rate of **13.57 L/min** is **below the process limit of 20 L/min**. 

**Comment:** The current flow is within safe operating limits but is **32% below the rated capacity**. The operator may either accept the lower throughput or increase upstream pressure / pump speed to bring the flow closer to the design value of 20 L/min.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert **mm → m** before plugging into $E = B \cdot D \cdot v$. Always check SI units first.
> 2. **Wrong Formula Choice (–2 marks):** Using $Q = A \cdot v$ without deriving the $E$-to-$Q$ relationship loses method marks. Always show the substitution explicitly.
> 3. **Ignoring Conductivity Limit (–1 mark):** Failing to mention the **5 µS/cm conductivity threshold** when discussing limitations.
> 4. **Missing the Insulation Logic (–1 mark):** Not explaining **why** the liner must be non-conductive. Examiners want conceptual depth, not just definitions.
> 5. **L/min vs m³/hr Confusion (–1 mark):** Always state the final flow rate in the units explicitly requested in the question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Operating Principle:** Electromagnetic flowmeters work on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.
- **Governing Equation:** $E = B \cdot D \cdot v$, leading to $Q = \dfrac{\pi D}{4B} \cdot E$.
- **No Moving Parts** and **No Pressure Drop** — two flagship advantages for board answers.
- **Minimum Fluid Conductivity:** **5 µS/cm** — cannot measure oils, hydrocarbons, or gases.
- **Construction Essentials:** Insulated liner (PTFE/PFA), magnetic coils, two flush-mounted electrodes.
- **Output Signal:** 4–20 mA current loop, pulse, or digital (HART/Modbus).
- **Applications:** Water treatment, chemical dosing, pulp & paper, food & pharma, slurries, blood flow measurement.
- **Sensitivity Factor:** $\dfrac{E}{v} = B \cdot D$ — used for calibration.
- **Cannot Measure Zero Flow:** When $v = 0$, $E = 0$ — therefore a magmeter always requires minimum flow.
- **Most Common Exam Mistake:** Forgetting to justify the **insulating liner**; examiners almost always allocate marks for this conceptual reasoning.
- **Valuation Keywords to Use:** "Faraday's Law," "Lorentz force," "induced EMF," "obstruction-less," "linear transfer function."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variable Area Meters: Rotameter and Cone and Float Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Variable Area Meters: Rotameter and Cone and Float Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable Area Meter (VA Meter):** A flow measurement device in which the **cross-sectional area of the flow stream varies** in response to the flow rate, while the **pressure drop across the metering element remains essentially constant**. The position of the float (or piston) becomes a direct, visible indicator of the volumetric or mass flow rate.

In contrast to **differential pressure (variable head) meters** — such as orifice plates, venturi tubes, and flow nozzles — where the area of the restriction is fixed and the pressure differential changes with flow, the Variable Area Meter holds the differential pressure nearly constant and lets the **annular flow area** expand or contract as the float seeks equilibrium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):** Students are expected to clearly distinguish between **Rotameters** (tapered-tube, gravity-balanced, generally glass-bodied) and **Cone-and-Float (Piston) type meters** (cylindrical tube, cone-shaped plunger, suitable for high-pressure industrial service).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **graduated conical cocktail glass** with a small cork floating inside. As you pour liquid (or any fluid) from the bottom upward, the cork rises. The **faster** you pour, the **higher** the cork climbs. Why? Because the upward rush of liquid pushes the cork up, and it stops only when the gap between the cork and the (now wider) glass walls is large enough for the liquid to slip past without lifting the cork any further.

That floating cork is the **float (bob)** of a **rotameter**, the tapered cocktail glass is the **metering tube**, and the height of the cork is the **flow-rate reading** etched on the scale.

Now imagine a more **industrial, robust version**: a vertical steel cylinder containing a precisely machined **cone (piston)**. Strong springs or magnetic coupling assist in its motion. The fluid flows upward through the narrow annular gap, lifting the cone against gravity (and spring force). This is the **Cone-and-Float type Variable Area Meter** — built for high pressure, opaque fluids, and electronic transmission.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *Area varies, head stays the same* → **Variable Area**. *Area stays the same, head varies* → **Variable Head (Differential Pressure)**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.806 \, \text{m/s}^2$ (**bold** in board answers).
- **Density of water at 4 °C:** $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$.
- **Typical accuracy of rotameter:** $\pm 2\%$ of full scale.
- **Typical accuracy of cone-and-float:** $\pm 1\%$ to $\pm 2\%$ of full scale.
- **Common float materials:** Stainless steel, tantalum, carbide, PTFE-coated.

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot

| Family | Sub-type | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Gravity-balanced** | Tapered-tube rotameter | Low-pressure liquids & gases, lab/process indication |
| **Spring-balanced** | Cylindrical piston / cone-and-float | High-pressure service, opaque fluids, electronic output |
| **Purged / Bypass** | Purge rotameter | Small flow indication in sample lines |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

**A. Rotameter (Tapered-Tube Float Meter)**

1. **Fluid entry:** The process fluid enters the **bottom** of a vertically mounted, **upward-tapering** (converging-diverging) precision-bore tube.
2. **Float engagement:** The fluid passes around a **calibrated float (bob)**, generating an upward drag force plus a buoyancy/pressure-difference force.
3. **Force balance:** The float rises until the **net downward gravitational force** (weight − buoyancy) is exactly balanced by the **net upward fluid force** (drag + pressure differential).
4. **Annular area increase:** As the float ascends into a wider section of the tube, the **annular area** $A_a$ between float outer surface and tube wall **increases**.
5. **Velocity decay:** Increased area → velocity drops → drag force decreases → float stabilises.
6. **Equilibrium height:** The vertical position $h$ of the float is a **monotonic, near-linear function of flow rate** for a properly designed meter.
7. **Reading:** Height is read against an **etched scale** on the tube, or sensed magnetically for electronic transmission.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does pressure drop stay nearly constant?** Because the float is designed so that the **buoyancy-cancelled weight** term $\Delta W = V_f \cdot g \cdot (\rho_f - \rho)$ dominates. As the float rises into a wider area, velocity drops and the drag force decreases, but the pressure-difference term across the float (which is essentially $\Delta P \cdot A_f$) keeps $\Delta P$ nearly constant at $\Delta P = (\rho_f - \rho)\,V_f\,g/A_f$.

**B. Cone-and-Float (Piston) Type Variable Area Meter**

1. The float is a **precision-machined cone or piston** that slides inside a **cylindrical bore**.
2. Fluid flows **upward** through the **annular gap** between cone and cylinder wall.
3. The cone rises against (a) gravity and (b) — in spring-loaded versions — a **calibrated restoring spring**.
4. The **annular area** $A_a$ increases as the cone moves up (cone tip is narrow, body widens).
5. At equilibrium: **Drag force + Pressure force = Weight − Buoyancy + Spring force (if any)**.
6. The cone's vertical position is read via:
   - Direct mechanical linkage to a pointer, **or**
   - **Magnetic coupling** to an external follower indicator, **or**
   - **LVDT / Hall-effect sensor** for 4–20 mA transmission.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All board answers must explicitly state the **assumptions** (steady flow, incompressible fluid, constant $C_d$) before substituting values.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Net effective weight of float | $\Delta W = V_f\,g\,(\rho_f - \rho)$ | N | $V_f$ = float volume, $\rho_f$ = float density, $\rho$ = fluid density |
| 2 | Pressure drop across float | $\Delta P = \dfrac{V_f\,g\,(\rho_f - \rho)}{A_f}$ | Pa | Holds **constant** in VA meters |
| 3 | Drag force on float | $F_d = C_d \, A_a \, \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | N | $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge |
| 4 | Annular flow area | $A_a = A_t - A_f$ | m² | $A_t$ = tube area at float position |
| 5 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q = C_d \, A_a \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d \rho}}$ | m³/s | General expression for VA meter |
| 6 | Simplified volumetric flow | $Q = C_d \, A_a \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | m³/s | Substitute $\Delta P$ from (2) |
| 7 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho \, Q = C_d \, A_a \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | kg/s | Mass-flow form |
| 8 | Linear velocity through annulus | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_a} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d \rho}}$ | m/s | Independent of float height (approx.) |
| 9 | Float height vs. flow | $h = h_0 + k \, Q$ | m | Empirical linear relation; $k$ = meter constant |
| 10 | Reynolds number for VA meter | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D_h}{\mu}$ | — | $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter of annulus |

> **Critical:** Avoid using the vertical pipe `$\vert$` symbol inside table cells. Use `$\mid$` or `$\vert$` instead.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rotameters:** Used in **chemical dosing skids**, **laboratory gas chromatography** carrier-gas flow, **oxygen flow in hospitals**, **water treatment** chemical injection lines, and **air sampling** in environmental monitoring.
- **Cone-and-float meters:** Used in **oil & gas well-head monitoring**, **high-pressure steam lines**, **refinery process streams**, **HP/LP air lines in power plants**, and **cryogenic LNG** transfer where glass rotameters would fail.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force Balance on a Rotameter Float

We consider a float in **steady-state equilibrium** inside a vertically mounted, upward-tapering tube. The fluid is assumed **incompressible** and the flow is **laminar-to-mildly-turbulent** with a constant discharge coefficient $C_d$.

**Step 1 — Identify all forces on the float.**

Let:
- $V_f$ = volume of float (m³)
- $A_f$ = maximum cross-sectional area of float (m²)
- $\rho_f$ = density of float material (kg/m³)
- $\rho$ = density of process fluid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (m/s²)
- $A_a$ = annular area between float and tube at the float's instantaneous position (m²)
- $v$ = mean velocity of fluid through the annulus (m/s)
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge (dimensionless)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure difference between bottom and top of float (Pa)

The four forces are:

1. **Weight of float (downward):**

$$W = V_f \, \rho_f \, g$$

2. **Buoyancy force (upward):**

$$F_b = V_f \, \rho \, g$$

3. **Pressure-difference force (upward)** — fluid below the float is at higher pressure than above:

$$F_p = \Delta P \, A_f$$

4. **Drag force due to fluid rushing past the float (upward):**

$$F_d = C_d \, A_a \, \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply steady-state equilibrium** (sum of vertical forces = 0; downward = upward).

$$W = F_b + F_p + F_d$$

Substitute:

$$V_f \, \rho_f \, g = V_f \, \rho \, g + \Delta P \, A_f + C_d \, A_a \, \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the drag term.**

$$C_d \, A_a \, \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = V_f \, \rho_f \, g - V_f \, \rho \, g - \Delta P \, A_f$$

**Step 4 — Recognise that $\Delta P \cdot A_f$ equals the net weight difference term** (a design feature of VA meters — the float is shaped so that buoyancy plus pressure difference equals the net gravitational force at zero flow). Hence the entire RHS simplifies to a single effective term:

$$C_d \, A_a \, \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for velocity $v$.**

$$v^2 = \frac{2 \, V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d \, A_a \, \rho}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d \, A_a \, \rho}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** $C_d$ and $A_a$ live in the denominator inside the square root because higher discharge efficiency and larger flow area both *reduce* the velocity needed to sustain the same float.

**Step 6 — Convert velocity to volumetric flow rate** using $Q = v \, A_a$.

$$Q = A_a \sqrt{\frac{2 \, V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d \, A_a \, \rho}}$$

$$Q = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, A_a \, V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d \, \rho}}$$

**Step 7 — Convert volumetric to mass flow rate** $\dot{m} = \rho \, Q$.

$$\dot{m} = \rho \sqrt{\frac{2 \, A_a \, V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d \, \rho}}$$

$$\dot{m} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \rho \, A_a \, V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d}}$$

> **Engineering note:** Because $A_a$ grows as the float rises, $Q$ does **not** increase linearly with $v$. The square-root dependence of $v$ on $1/A_a$ is precisely what gives the rotameter its **near-linear flow-vs-height** characteristic — a key insight KTU examiners love to test.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cone-and-Float Force Balance with Spring Loading

In a **spring-balanced** cone-and-float meter, an additional **spring force** $F_s = K_s (h - h_0)$ acts downward (opposing the float's upward motion), where $K_s$ is the spring constant (N/m) and $h - h_0$ is the cone displacement from its zero-flow reference position.

**Step 1 — Modified equilibrium equation:**

$$W = F_b + F_p + F_d + F_s$$

$$V_f \, \rho_f \, g = V_f \, \rho \, g + \Delta P \, A_f + C_d \, A_a \, \frac{\rho v^2}{2} + K_s (h - h_0)$$

**Step 2 — Express $\Delta P$ for a cone in a cylindrical tube.** For a piston-type float, the pressure drop is the sum of gravity, buoyancy, and spring terms divided by the piston head area:

$$\Delta P = \frac{V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho) + K_s(h - h_0)}{A_f}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve for $v$:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \left[ V_f \, g (\rho_f - \rho) + K_s (h - h_0) \right]}{C_d \, A_a \, \rho}}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = A_a \sqrt{\frac{2 \left[ V_f \, g (\rho_f - \rho) + K_s (h - h_0) \right]}{C_d \, A_a \, \rho}}$$

> **Engineering insight:** Spring loading **linearises** the meter response because the spring term grows linearly with $h$, partially compensating for the square-root $A_a$ dependence.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Rotameter vs. Cone-and-Float Comparison

| S/N | Parameter | Rotameter (Tapered Tube) | Cone-and-Float (Cylindrical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tube geometry | Upward-tapering cone (conical) | Straight cylinder |
| 2 | Float shape | Spherical / ellipsoidal / sharp-edged | Cone / piston / plunger |
| 3 | Restoring force | Gravity (plus buoyancy) | Gravity ± calibrated spring |
| 4 | Typical body material | Borosilicate glass, plastic, SS | SS, Monel, Hastelloy, PTFE-lined |
| 5 | Pressure rating | Low (typically ≤ 10 bar) | High (up to 600 bar) |
| 6 | Fluid clarity | Must be transparent (for visual) | Opaque fluids allowed |
| 7 | Output | Visual scale / magnetic follower | Pointer, 4–20 mA, HART, Fieldbus |
| 8 | Accuracy | ±2% FS | ±1% to ±2% FS |
| 9 | Turndown ratio | 10:1 | 10:1 to 20:1 |
| 10 | Cost | Low | Moderate to high |
| 11 | Installation | Vertical, inlet at bottom | Vertical, inlet at bottom |
| 12 | Typical applications | Lab dosing, gas sampling, medical O₂ | Refineries, chemical plants, HP steam |
| 13 | Calibration dependency | On fluid density & viscosity | Less density-sensitive (spring helps) |
| 14 | Maintenance | Float cleaning, scale readability | Spring fatigue, magnetic coupling wear |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem (KTU University Exam, June 2024 pattern):**
A rotameter is used to measure water flow. The float has a volume $V_f = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³, density $\rho_f = 8000$ kg/m³ (stainless steel), and maximum cross-sectional area $A_f = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m². The annular area at the float's equilibrium height is $A_a = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m². Take $C_d = 0.6$, $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, and $g = 9.81$ m/s². Calculate the **volumetric flow rate** $Q$.

**Step 1 — Compute the net effective weight term:**

$$\Delta W = V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho) = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times (8000 - 1000)$$

$$\Delta W = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times 7000 = 0.34335 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the velocity through the annulus:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta W}{C_d \, A_a \, \rho}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.34335}{0.6 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{0.6867}{0.24}} = \sqrt{2.86125} = 1.6915 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = v \, A_a = 1.6915 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 6.766 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to litres per minute (L/min) for board-friendly presentation:**

$$Q = 6.766 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 \times 60 = 40.6 \, \text{L/min}$$

> **Answer:** $Q \approx 40.6 \, \text{L/min}$.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Physical drawings of float shapes and tapered tubes are best represented here as **functional block-flow diagrams** describing the energy/force interactions, since the underlying geometry is complex. Mermaid is used to ensure clean, error-free rendering.

### 4.1 Rotameter — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID INLET AT BOTTOM] --> B[FLOAT RESTING AT LOWER STOP]
    B --> C[UPWARD FLUID DRAG ACTS ON FLOAT]
    C --> D[PRESSURE DIFFERENCE BUILDS UP ACROSS FLOAT]
    D --> E[NET DOWNWARD GRAVITY VS NET UPWARD FLUID FORCE]
    E --> F{FORCE BALANCE ACHIEVED}
    F -->|Yes| G[FLOAT RISES TO NEW EQUILIBRIUM HEIGHT H]
    F -->|No| C
    G --> H[ANNULAR AREA Aa INCREASES]
    H --> I[FLUID VELOCITY DECREASES]
    I --> J[DRAG FORCE DROPS]
    J --> F
    G --> K[HEIGHT H READ AGAINST SCALE]
    K --> L[FLOW RATE Q DISPLAYED OR TRANSMITTED]
```

### 4.2 Cone-and-Float Meter — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID ENTERS CYLINDRICAL BORE FROM BOTTOM] --> B[CONE FLOAT INITIALLY AT REST]
    B --> C[FLUID EXERTS UPWARD DRAG THROUGH ANNULUS]
    C --> D[PRESSURE DIFFERENCE ACTS UPWARD ON CONE HEAD]
    D --> E[SPRING FORCE PUSHES CONE DOWNWARD]
    E --> F{GRAVITY PLUS SPRING EQUALS FLUID FORCE}
    F -->|No| C
    F -->|Yes| G[CONE STABILISES AT HEIGHT H]
    G --> H[MAGNETIC COUPLING SENSES POSITION]
    H --> I[EXTERNAL FOLLOWER POINTER INDICATES FLOW]
    H --> J[LVDT OR HALL SENSOR GENERATES 4-20 mA OUTPUT]
    I --> K[DISPLAY OR DCS READOUT]
    J --> K
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Forces Acting on a Rotameter Float

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[WEIGHT W DOWNWARD] --> SUM{FORCE BALANCE NODE}
    F2[BUOYANCY Fb UPWARD] --> SUM
    F3[PRESSURE FORCE Fp UPWARD] --> SUM
    F4[DRAG FORCE Fd UPWARD] --> SUM
    SUM -->|Equilibrium Reached| OUT[FLOAT HOVERS AT HEIGHT H INDICATING FLOW RATE]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow — Selecting the Right VA Meter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[SELECT A VARIABLE AREA METER] --> Q1{IS FLUID VISUALLY CLEAR?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{PRESSURE BELOW 10 BAR?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{IS HIGH PRESSURE INVOLVED?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| PICK1[CHOOSE GLASS ROTAMETER]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{IS ELECTRONIC OUTPUT NEEDED?}
    Q3 -->|No| PICK2[CHOOSE METAL TUBE ROTAMETER]
    Q4 -->|Yes| PICK3[CHOOSE CONE AND FLOAT METER WITH 4-20 mA]
    Q4 -->|No| PICK4[CHOOSE CONE AND FLOAT METER WITH POINTER]
```

> **Diagram Note:** Each node ID is alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `F1`, `Q1`, etc.) and all labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without bold/italic markers inside quotes, in strict compliance with the Mermaid rendering safeguard.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`**  
> **Differentiate between a variable head meter and a variable area meter. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Variable Head (Differential Pressure) Meter | Variable Area Meter |
|---|---|---|
| Varying quantity | Pressure drop ($\Delta P$) changes with flow | Flow area ($A_a$) changes with flow |
| Constant quantity | Flow area (orifice/nuzzle bore is fixed) | Pressure drop across the float is nearly constant |
| Example | Orifice plate, Venturi tube, Flow nozzle | Rotameter, Cone-and-float meter |
| Typical reading | Differential manometer / DP transmitter | Float height or position sensor |

*(1 mark for the head-vs-area distinction, 1 mark for the constant-vs-varying quantity, 1 mark for the example pair.)*

> **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]`**  
> **State any three advantages of a rotameter over an orifice meter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. Rotameter has a **linear flow-vs-height relationship**, while an orifice meter has a **square-root** relationship requiring linearisation. *(1 mark)*
2. Rotameter is **self-indicating** — direct visual readout of float height; orifice meter requires a separate DP cell and manometer. *(1 mark)*
3. Rotameter has a **lower permanent pressure loss** because the float is streamlined and recovers most of the kinetic energy, whereas an orifice plate dissipates energy irreversibly. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Important KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question carries **7 + 7 = two sub-parts**. Internal choice means students answer either full Question A **or** full Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]`**

**A (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a rotameter**. Derive the expression for the volumetric flow rate through it.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (2 marks):** A rotameter consists of:
- A **vertically mounted, upward-tapering precision-bore tube** made of borosilicate glass (or metal for high-pressure service).
- A **calibrated float (bob)** whose density is greater than the fluid. Common float shapes: spherical, ellipsoidal, sharp-edged disc, or conical.
- **Upper and lower float stops** to limit travel.
- An **etched scale** on the tube (or external magnetic follower for metal tubes).
- Optional **magnetic coupling** for electronic output.

**Working (2 marks):** Fluid enters the bottom and flows upward, pushing the float. The float rises until the **net upward fluid force** (drag + pressure-difference) equals the **net downward gravity-buoyancy force**. The annular gap widens with height, so the float finds a unique equilibrium height for each flow rate.

**Derivation (3 marks):** Force balance gives:

$$V_f \, \rho_f \, g = V_f \, \rho \, g + \Delta P \, A_f + C_d \, A_a \, \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

Substituting $\Delta P \, A_f = V_f g (\rho_f - \rho)$ and solving:

$$Q = C_d \, A_a \sqrt{\frac{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d \, \rho}}$$

---

**A (b) [7 Marks]** A rotameter is used to measure the flow of oil (specific gravity 0.85, viscosity negligible). The float is made of stainless steel ($\rho_f = 8000$ kg/m³), has volume $V_f = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ and area $A_f = 1.6 \times 10^{-4}$ m². At a particular flow rate, the annular area is $A_a = 3.2 \times 10^{-4}$ m². Take $C_d = 0.65$ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². Determine (i) the **volumetric flow rate** in L/min and (ii) the **pressure drop** across the float in kPa.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the net effective weight of the float:**

$$\Delta W = V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho) = 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times (8000 - 850)$$

$$\Delta W = 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times 7150 = 0.2806 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the velocity through the annulus:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.2806}{0.65 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 850}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{0.5612}{0.1768}} = \sqrt{3.1742} = 1.7817 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = v \times A_a = 1.7817 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-4} = 5.701 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q = 5.701 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 \times 1000 = 34.2 \, \text{L/min}$$

**Step 4 — Pressure drop across the float:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho)}{A_f} = \frac{0.2806}{1.6 \times 10^{-4}} = 1753.75 \, \text{Pa} \approx 1.754 \, \text{kPa}$$

> **Final Answer:** (i) $Q \approx 34.2 \, \text{L/min}$; (ii) $\Delta P \approx 1.75 \, \text{kPa}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]`** (Internal Choice Alternative)

**B (a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable diagram, describe the **construction, working principle and advantages** of the **Cone-and-Float (Piston) type variable area meter**. Compare it with a rotameter.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (2 marks):** A cone-and-float meter has:
- A **vertical cylindrical precision bore** (instead of a tapered tube).
- A **cone-shaped (or piston-shaped) float** with the apex pointing downward, providing a **variable annular gap** as the cone rises.
- A **calibrated restoring spring** (in spring-balanced versions) for the float.
- **Magnetic coupling** between the internal float magnet and an external follower for non-intrusive position sensing.
- Optional **LVDT or Hall-effect sensor** for electronic 4–20 mA output.

**Working (2 marks):** Fluid entering the bottom flows upward through the annular gap between the cone and the cylinder wall. The upward drag and pressure forces lift the cone against gravity and the spring. At equilibrium, the cone hovers at a height that uniquely indicates the flow rate. The annular area increases as the cone rises because the cone body widens upward.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Handles **high pressures** (up to 600 bar) because the tube is metallic.
- Suitable for **opaque, dirty, or aggressive fluids**.
- Allows **electronic transmission** of flow.
- More robust and industrial.

**Comparison (1.5 marks):** See the analytical matrix in Section 3.3 — cylindrical vs. conical tube, high vs. low pressure, opaque vs. clear fluids, electronic vs. visual output.

---

**B (b) [7 Marks]** A **spring-loaded cone-and-float meter** measures the flow of a liquid of density $\rho = 900$ kg/m³. The cone has volume $V_f = 6 \times 10^{-6}$ m³, density $\rho_f = 7800$ kg/m³, and the spring constant is $K_s = 50$ N/m. At a flow rate $Q = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s, the cone lifts by $h - h_0 = 0.012$ m and the annular area is $A_a = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m². Take $C_d = 0.62$ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². Determine the **pressure drop** across the cone.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the gravity-buoyancy term:**

$$V_f g (\rho_f - \rho) = 6 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times (7800 - 900)$$

$$= 6 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times 6900 = 0.4061 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the spring force:**

$$F_s = K_s (h - h_0) = 50 \times 0.012 = 0.6 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the effective downward load:**

$$F_{eff} = 0.4061 + 0.6 = 1.0061 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the pressure drop** (force per unit float head area; we take $A_f \approx A_a$ for a near-cylindrical cone mid-section, and the problem implies this simplified model):

$$\Delta P = \frac{F_{eff}}{A_f} \approx \frac{1.0061}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2012.2 \, \text{Pa} \approx 2.01 \, \text{kPa}$$

> **Final Answer:** $\Delta P \approx 2.01 \, \text{kPa}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU board exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units.** Always write $Q$ in m³/s first, then convert to L/min or L/h. Examiners **deduct up to 1 mark** for missing or wrong units.
> 2. **Mixing up variable-area and variable-head formulas.** Do not substitute $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ from the orifice equation blindly. In a rotameter, $\Delta P$ is the *consequence*, not the cause — the *cause* is the float-weight imbalance.
> 3. **Ignoring fluid density correction.** If the calibration fluid differs from the operating fluid, a correction factor $K = \sqrt{(\rho_f - \rho_{cal})/(\rho_f - \rho_{op})}$ must be applied. Skipping this is a **favourite 2-mark trap**.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the assumption of incompressibility and constant $C_d$.** Examiners expect a line such as *"Assuming steady, incompressible flow and a constant discharge coefficient…"* before the substitution.
> 5. **Drawing the rotameter inverted.** The float must be shown **rising upward** with the **taper widening upward**. Reversed drawings lose **1 mark**.
> 6. **Not balancing dimensions.** If you write $V_f$ in cm³ and $\rho$ in kg/m³, your final units will not match. KTU examiners check dimensional consistency.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** VA meter = area varies, $\Delta P$ constant. (Opposite of DP meter.)
- ✅ **Rotameter:** Tapered tube, gravity-balanced, glass-bodied, low pressure, $\pm 2\%$.
- ✅ **Cone-and-Float:** Cylindrical bore, spring-assisted, metal-bodied, high pressure, $\pm 1$–$2\%$.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $Q = C_d \, A_a \sqrt{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho) / (C_d \rho)}$.
- ✅ **Key pressure formula:** $\Delta P = V_f g (\rho_f - \rho) / A_f$ — **independent of flow rate**.
- ✅ **Mass flow:** $\dot m = \rho Q = C_d A_a \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$.
- ✅ **Forces on float:** Weight (↓), Buoyancy (↑), Pressure-difference (↑), Drag (↑).
- ✅ **Equilibrium:** $\sum F_{down} = \sum F_{up}$.
- ✅ **Annular area:** $A_a = A_t - A_f$; grows with float height.
- ✅ **Float position ↔ Flow rate:** Direct, near-linear, monotonic.
- ✅ **Density correction factor:** $K = \sqrt{(\rho_f - \rho_{cal}) / (\rho_f - \rho_{op})}$ when fluid changes.
- ✅ **Cone-and-float extra force:** Spring force $F_s = K_s (h - h_0)$, helps linearisation.
- ✅ **Installation:** Always **vertical**, **inlet at bottom**, with adequate straight upstream pipe.
- ✅ **Limitations of glass rotameter:** Low pressure, transparent fluid needed, fragile.
- ✅ **Cone-and-float advantages over rotameter:** Industrial robustness, electronic output, high pressure.
- ✅ **KTU favourite line:** "The pressure drop across a variable area meter remains essentially constant irrespective of the flow rate."

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Flowmeters of Positive Displacement Type: Rotating Disk, Turbine Type, and Anemometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Flowmeters of Positive Displacement Type: Rotating Disk, Turbine Type, and Anemometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mechanical Flowmeters — The Bigger Picture

Flow measurement is one of the foundational pillars of industrial process control. Among the various families of flowmeters (differential pressure, electromagnetic, ultrasonic, Coriolis, etc.), **Mechanical Flowmeters** form a historically dominant and pedagogically important category because they convert a fluid's kinetic energy directly into a **mechanical motion** (rotation, oscillation, or deflection). The output can then be read locally on a register or converted into an electrical pulse train.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> A **Mechanical Flowmeter** is an instrument that measures volumetric or mass flow rate by employing moving mechanical elements that respond proportionally to the fluid velocity passing through a precisely defined cross-section. These meters are sub-classified into:
> 1. **Positive Displacement (PD) Meters** — fixed volume chambers
> 2. **Turbine Flowmeters** — angular momentum transfer
> 3. **Anemometers** — velocity measurement of gases
> 4. **Target / Drag-Force Flowmeters**
> 5. **Variable-Area (Rotameters)**

This module focuses on the first three, which together account for nearly **70 % of in-line industrial flow measurement installations**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "How Does a Mechanical Flowmeter 'Feel' the Fluid?"

Imagine you stand at the mouth of a garden hose with a small hand-held pinwheel. As water rushes out, the pinwheel spins. If you knew the precise geometry of the pinwheel blades and counted the revolutions per minute, you could *calculate* the volume of water that passed. **That is precisely how a turbine flowmeter works.**

Now imagine a more refined device: a closed cylinder with a precisely machined **nutating disk** inside. Each time fluid pushes the disk through one complete wobble, exactly **one fixed volume** (say 1 litre) has been delivered. Just like a gasoline pump at a petrol station — every click of the roller represents a known quantity. **That is a Positive Displacement flowmeter.**

Finally, think of a small four-cup rotor on a weather station roof. The wind blowing across the cups makes them rotate, and counting revolutions per second tells you wind speed. **That is an Anemometer.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinguishing Principle:**
> - **PD meters** measure by *counting fixed volumes* → best for **cumulative volume** (custody transfer of fuel, water, chemicals).
> - **Turbine meters** measure by *rotational velocity* → best for **clean, low-viscosity fluids** (petroleum, cryogenic liquids, water).
> - **Anemometers** measure *gas velocity* → used in HVAC, meteorology, and combustion air monitoring.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Recall Targets)

Every mechanical flowmeter is characterised by:

| Performance Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Industrial) |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $\varepsilon$ | ±0.5 % to ±2 % of reading |
| Repeatability | $r$ | ±0.05 % to ±0.2 % |
| Turn-down Ratio | $T_d$ | **10 : 1** (typical) |
| Pressure Loss | $\Delta P$ | 0.1 to 1 bar |
| Output | — | Pulses / unit volume |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask the difference between **Positive Displacement** and **Turbine** meters. The single crisp answer is: *PD meters are **volumetric integrators** (fixed volume chambers), whereas turbine meters are **kinematic transducers** (rotor speed ∝ flow velocity).*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Positive Displacement Flowmeters — Theory of Operation

The defining feature of a PD meter is the **fixed metrological volume ($V_d$)** of a measuring chamber. The fluid enters the chamber, mechanically displaces an internal element (disk, piston, gear, lobe), and then exits, having delivered *exactly* $V_d$ litres per displacement cycle.

### 2.1.1 The Rotating (Nutating) Disk Flowmeter

This is the most iconic PD design — the **disk inside a precisely machined spherical chamber** is tilted (nutates) about a central spindle such that the swept volume is geometrically fixed.

**Operational Steps (chronological per cycle):**

1. Fluid enters the chamber under inlet pressure $P_{in}$.
2. The pressure difference ($\Delta P = P_{in} - P_{out}$) creates a force on the disk.
3. The disk undergoes **nutation** — a compound motion combining precession and rotation about the spindle.
4. A measuring cam attached to the spindle converts nutation into a **linear reciprocating motion**.
5. This reciprocation drives the counter register via a magnetic coupling (preserves hermetic seal).
6. After **one full cycle**, a known volume $V_d$ has been discharged.

The cumulative volume is simply:

$$
V_{total} = N \cdot V_d
$$

where $N$ = total number of nutation cycles counted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the Rotating Disk Meter so popular for domestic water supply?**
> Because the metrological volume is **fixed by geometry alone** (chamber + disk dimensions), the calibration does not drift with viscosity or velocity within the design envelope. Hence, water boards worldwide rely on this meter for billing.

### 2.1.2 Working Equations — Rotating Disk

**Instantaneous Volumetric Flow Rate:**

$$
Q = V_d \cdot f
$$

where $f$ is the nutation frequency (cycles per second).

**Instantaneous Mass Flow Rate (using fluid density $\rho$):**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot V_d \cdot f
$$

**Mechanical Power Required to Drive the Disk:**

$$
P_{mech} = \Delta P \cdot Q = (P_{in} - P_{out}) \cdot V_d \cdot f
$$

### 2.1.3 Sources of Error in PD Meters

- **Leakage slip** — fluid bypasses the measuring chamber due to clearances → measured $V_d$ < true $V_d$.
- **Cavitation** — when outlet pressure falls below vapour pressure → causes noise, vibration, and damage.
- **Wear of chamber walls** — abrasive fluids erode the disk edge.
- **Temperature effects** — thermal expansion alters $V_d$.

## 2.2 Turbine Flowmeters — Theory of Operation

A turbine flowmeter contains a **multi-bladed rotor** mounted coaxially inside the pipe on a shaft supported by bearings. The fluid flowing axially strikes the angled blades and exerts a tangential force, causing the rotor to spin at an angular velocity $\omega$ proportional to the average fluid velocity $\bar{v}$.

### 2.2.1 The Governing Equation (Velocity–Frequency Relationship)

Let:
- $\bar{v}$ = average axial fluid velocity (m/s)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of rotor (rad/s)
- $D$ = rotor tip diameter (m)
- $K_f$ = meter K-factor (pulses/m³) — manufacturer calibration
- $f$ = pulse frequency (Hz)
- $\theta$ = blade angle with respect to the flow axis
- $A$ = pipe cross-section (m²)

The fundamental relationship derived from axial momentum balance is:

$$
\bar{v} = \frac{\omega \cdot D}{2 \tan\theta} + \text{slip correction}
$$

For ideal, slip-free flow, this simplifies to:

$$
Q = A \cdot \bar{v} = K \cdot f
$$

where $K$ is the **meter constant** (volume per pulse, e.g., litres/pulse).

### 2.2.2 The K-Factor (Linearisation Constant)

The **K-factor** is the number of pulses the meter's pick-off coil generates per unit volume of fluid. It is determined empirically during calibration:

$$
K_f = \frac{N_{pulses}}{V_{total}} \quad \text{[pulses/m}^3\text{]}
$$

Typical values: **K ≈ 100 to 10,000 pulses/litre** depending on rotor design.

**Linearised Volumetric Flow:**

$$
Q = \frac{f}{K_f}
$$

**Volumetric Flow from Angular Velocity:**

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \, D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\omega \, D}{2 \tan\theta}
$$

**Solving for $f$:**

$$
f = \frac{K_f \cdot Q}{1} = K_f \cdot A \cdot \bar{v}
$$

### 2.2.3 Reynolds Number Effects

Turbine meters are strongly sensitive to viscosity. The meter's K-factor varies with Reynolds number:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho \, \bar{v} \, D_h}{\mu}
$$

where $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter of the meter throat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For $Re > 10{,}000$, the flow is fully turbulent and K-factor is nearly **constant**. For $Re < 4{,}000$, viscosity causes rotor drag, lowering K significantly. Therefore turbine meters are specified for **high Reynolds number flows** (clean water, petroleum, LNG).

### 2.2.4 Mechanical Torque on the Turbine Rotor

$$
\tau = \rho \, Q \, (\bar{v} \, r_{avg} \, \sin\theta - \text{drag term})
$$

For steady state (torque balance with bearing friction $\tau_f$):

$$
\tau_{fluid} = \tau_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{\tau_{fluid} - \tau_f}{\text{damping coefficient}}
$$

## 2.3 Anemometers — Theory of Operation

An **anemometer** measures the **velocity of a gas** (typically air). Mechanical anemometers are sub-classified into:

| Type | Sensing Principle | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Cup Anemometer** | Rotation of hemispherical cups by wind drag | Pulses (frequency) |
| **Vane Anemometer** | Deflection / rotation of a flat vane | Angular displacement |
| **Hot-Wire (Thermal)** | Heat loss from heated wire | Resistance change |

### 2.3.1 Cup Anemometer — Linearised Theory

The cup rotor consists of 3 or 4 hemispherical cups mounted on horizontal arms. Wind striking the concave side produces greater drag than the convex side, creating a net torque that spins the rotor.

The empirical relationship:

$$
\bar{v} = a + b \cdot \omega
$$

where:
- $a$ = starting threshold (m/s) — minimum wind to overcome friction, typically **0.5 to 1 m/s**
- $b$ = slope (m per revolution)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rev/s or rad/s)

**Inverse Calibration (for pulse output):**

$$
\omega = K_a \, f_{pulse}
$$

$$
\bar{v} = a + b \, K_a \, f_{pulse}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Cup anemometers over-read in turbulent gusty conditions because the **drag on the concave cup** (high pressure) responds to $v^2$, while the **convex cup** responds weakly. The asymmetry biases the reading upward by **5–10 %**. This is called the *overspeeding error*.

### 2.3.2 Vane (Propeller) Anemometer

A small lightweight propeller with helically pitched blades rotates when air flows through. The pitch is chosen so that $\omega \propto \bar{v}$:

$$
\bar{v} = C_v \cdot \omega
$$

where $C_v$ is the vane calibration constant (m/rev). A tail fin aligns the rotor axis with the wind direction.

### 2.3.3 Pitot-Static vs. Anemometer — Distinction

| Feature | Pitot-Static Tube | Anemometer |
|---|---|---|
| Measures | Local point velocity | Average velocity over swept area |
| Output | Differential pressure $\Delta P$ | Rotational frequency / pulses |
| Sensitivity to direction | Cosine law (must align) | Less sensitive (cup) / Vane aligned |
| Typical use | Aircraft airspeed, ducts | Weather stations, HVAC ducts |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Following Table is the Authoritative Formula Reference for Board Exam Preparation.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cumulative volume (PD meter) | $V = N \, V_d$ | m³ | $N$ = cycle count |
| 2 | Instantaneous flow (PD) | $Q = V_d \, f$ | m³/s | $f$ in Hz |
| 3 | Mass flow (PD) | $\dot{m} = \rho \, V_d \, f$ | kg/s | $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 4 | Turbine volume per pulse | $K = V/N_{pulses}$ | m³/pulse | Empirical |
| 5 | Turbine instantaneous flow | $Q = f / K_f$ | m³/s | $K_f$ in pulses/m³ |
| 6 | Turbine frequency from velocity | $f = K_f \, A \, \bar{v}$ | Hz | $A$ = pipe area |
| 7 | Turbine velocity (ideal) | $\bar{v} = \omega D / (2 \tan\theta)$ | m/s | No-slip assumption |
| 8 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho \bar{v} D_h / \mu$ | dimensionless | $\mu$ = viscosity |
| 9 | Cup anemometer velocity | $\bar{v} = a + b \, \omega$ | m/s | $a$ = threshold |
| 10 | Vane anemometer velocity | $\bar{v} = C_v \, \omega$ | m/s | $C_v$ = m/rev |
| 11 | Power loss in meter | $P_{loss} = \Delta P \, Q$ | W | $\Delta P$ in Pa |
| 12 | Drag force on cup | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \, C_d \, A \, v^2$ | N | $C_d$ = drag coefficient |

> **Note on Notation:** When writing equations in your answer sheet, use $\vert x \vert$ for absolute values and never use raw vertical pipes inside markdown table cells (use $\mid$ instead).

## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **PD meters**: Custody transfer of petrol, diesel, LPG, domestic water billing (BS 5728 standard).
- **Turbine meters**: Aircraft fuel flow (Boeing/Airbus use turbine meters), refinery product lines, cryogenic LNG measurement, irrigation pipelines.
- **Cup anemometers**: Meteorological stations, airport wind monitoring (WMO standard).
- **Vane anemometers**: HVAC commissioning, cleanroom validation, fume hood face velocity measurement.

## 2.6 Comparative Selection Table (For KTU Exam)

| Property | PD Meter | Turbine Meter | Anemometer (Cup) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Best for | Custody transfer | Clean liquids | Gas velocity |
| Output | Pulses or pointer | Pulses | Pulses |
| Accuracy | ±0.1 % to ±0.5 % | ±0.5 % to ±1 % | ±3 % to ±5 % |
| Suitable for viscous fluids? | **Yes** (even up to 100 cP) | No (low viscosity only) | N/A (gas) |
| Pressure drop | High | Moderate | Negligible |
| Cost | High | Moderate | Low |
