The DP method *cancels* it automatically — never write it in the calibration range. > 3. **Confusing LRV/URV Sign:** In zero elevation, LRV is **negative**. Students often incorrectly make LRV = 0 and URV = Span. This is wrong; LRV must equal the actual DP at zero level. > 4. **Ignoring Temperature Compensation:** Examiners specifically test whether students recognize that a 4 m wet leg with steam at 200 °C will have $\rho_{wl}$ different from water at 25 °C. Always state the assumption of constant density. > 5. **Wrong Units in Current Calculation:** Span must be in **same units as LRV/URV** (both Pa or both kPa), or the 4–20 mA mapping will be wrong. Show unit cancellation explicitly. > 6. **Not Drawing the Vessel Sketch:** Examiners give **1–2 free marks** for a clearly labelled diagram. Always draw the vessel, taps, seal pot, and indicate $h$, $h_{wl}$, $\rho$, $\rho_{wl}$. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Master the canonical equation:** $\Delta P = \rho g h - \rho_{wl} g h_{wl}$ — it covers all wet-leg problems. - **Dry leg gives zero offset:** When LP leg is vented and contains only vapour, $\Delta P = \rho g h$ and no calibration adjustment is needed. - **Zero Suppression occurs when $\rho > \rho_{wl}$:** Process liquid heavier than sealing liquid → wet-leg offset is smaller → DP at zero level is positive (suppress it). - **Zero Elevation occurs when $\rho < \rho_{wl}$:** Process liquid lighter than sealing liquid → wet-leg offset is larger → DP at zero level is negative (elevate it). - **Span is always $\rho g H_{max}$:** Independent of wet-leg configuration; only LRV/URV shift. - **Vessel pressure cancels out:** This is the central advantage of the DP method over a simple gauge pressure transmitter. - **Bubble tube is for nasty fluids:** Corrosive, slurry, viscous — uses purge gas instead of direct contact. - **Temperature changes density:** A 60 °C rise can change span by 4–5 %, so always compensate in critical applications. - **Seal pots are mandatory** in wet-leg arrangements to maintain a stable sealing-liquid column. - **KTU exam tip:** Draw the vessel sketch first, label HP/LP taps and heights, then write equations — this 3-step ritual prevents sign errors and earns easy diagram marks. - **Units discipline:** Always work in SI (Pa, kg/m³, m, m/s²); convert only at the final answer if required. - **4–20 mA mapping formula:** $I = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P - \text{LRV}}{\text{Span}}$ — memorize this for all DP transmitter problems. - **Smart transmitters** (e.g., Rosemount 3051, Yokogawa EJX) offer **auto-compensation** for density, temperature, and static pressure — know this for viva questions.

topics.title: Solid Level Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solid Level Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solid Level Detection** is the measurement and monitoring of the level/height of bulk solid materials (powders, granules, pellets, flakes, lumps) contained in silos, hoppers, bunkers, and storage tanks. Unlike liquids, solids present unique challenges: they have irregular surfaces (angle of repose), varying bulk densities, dust generation, caking, bridging, and rat-holing.

**Solid Level Detectors** are broadly classified into two categories:

1. **Point Level Detectors (Level Switches):** Detect when the solid has reached a specific pre-set height (high-level or low-level alarm).
2. **Continuous Level Detectors (Level Transmitters):** Provide an analog output proportional to the actual height of the material surface in real-time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module covers *conductivity, capacitance, ultrasonic, radiation, and weight (load cell) methods* as the primary solid level detection techniques.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine filling a **transparent glass jar with sand** instead of water:
- Water forms a perfectly flat surface — easy to "see" the level.
- Sand forms a **cone-shaped heap** (angle of repose), and the surface keeps shifting.
- The sand may stick to the walls (caking), or create a hollow tunnel inside (rat-holing), even when the silo appears empty from above.
- Also, unlike water, sand grains are abrasive, dusty, and don't conduct electricity.

**The Intuition:** A solid level detector is essentially a "smart sensor" that solves these messy, real-world problems. For example:
- A **capacitance probe** acts like the **"smart ruler"** that detects sand by measuring how it changes the electrical charge around the probe.
- A **rotating paddle wheel** is like a **"windmill that gets stuck in the sand"** — when the paddle stops spinning, the alarm triggers.
- A **load cell** is like **"weighing the entire jar"** to figure out how full it is by calculating how much sand is inside.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board Exam Ready):** *Solid level detectors are devices used to sense the presence, absence, or height of bulk solid materials in vessels, employing techniques such as electrical conductance, capacitance variation, acoustic reflection, nuclear radiation absorption, and weight measurement.*

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

- **Range:** Typically **0.5 m to 70 m** depending on technique
- **Accuracy:** ±0.25% to ±2% of full scale
- **Response Time:** 1 second to 30 seconds (solids respond slower than liquids)
- **Operating Temperature:** **-40 °C to 200 °C** (varies with method)
- **Process Pressure:** Atmospheric to **10 bar**
- **Dielectric Constant of Solids (εr):** 1.5 (plastic pellets) to 80 (wet ores)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Solid Level Detectors

The detectors can be organized into a clear hierarchy based on the physical principle used:

| **S.No.** | **Principle** | **Type** | **Application** |
|:---------:|:--------------|:---------|:----------------|
| 1 | Conductance / Resistance | Point | Conductive solids, wet materials |
| 2 | Capacitance | Point / Continuous | Non-conductive bulk solids |
| 3 | Ultrasonic | Continuous | Large silos, dust-free environments |
| 4 | Radar (Microwave) | Continuous | Harsh environments, dusty silos |
| 5 | Nuclear (Radiation) | Point / Continuous | Extreme temperatures, corrosive media |
| 6 | Weight / Load Cell | Continuous | Tanks, hoppers, weigh feeders |
| 7 | Paddle Wheel / Rotating Paddle | Point | Free-flowing granular solids |
| 8 | Vibrating Fork (Tuning Fork) | Point | Fine powders, granular media |
| 9 | Laser | Continuous | Open containers, very accurate |
| 10 | Plumb Bob (Yo-Yo Sensor) | Continuous | Very tall silos (up to 70 m) |

## 2.2 Detailed Operational Concepts

### 2.2.1 Conductance / Resistance Level Detector

**How it works:** Two electrodes (a probe and the vessel wall) form a conductive path. When the solid material (which must be conductive or wet) touches the electrodes, current flows.

- **Principle:** Ohm's Law applied to bulk solids.
- **Limitation:** Requires conductive material (fails for dry plastic pellets, dry sand).
- **Output:** ON/OFF relay contact (point detection).

### 2.2.2 Capacitive Level Detector

**Principle:** A probe acts as one plate of a capacitor; the vessel wall acts as the other. The solid material (dielectric) between them changes the capacitance.

The capacitance of a cylindrical capacitor is given by:

$$C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(R/r)}$$

Where:
- $C$ = Capacitance in Farads (F)
- $\varepsilon_0$ = Permittivity of free space = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- $\varepsilon_r$ = Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of solid
- $L$ = Length of probe immersed in solid (m)
- $R$ = Radius of outer cylinder (vessel wall) (m)
- $r$ = Radius of inner probe (m)

**Why it works:** As the solid rises and covers more of the probe, the effective dielectric changes from air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$) to the solid material ($\varepsilon_r > 1$). The capacitance **increases linearly** with the level of the solid.

### 2.2.3 Ultrasonic Level Detector

**Principle:** A piezoelectric transducer emits high-frequency sound waves (typically **20 kHz to 200 kHz**) toward the solid surface. The waves reflect back, and the time-of-flight is measured.

**Why it works for solids:** Solids reflect sound waves. The transit time $t$ is directly proportional to distance $d$:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

Where:
- $d$ = Distance from sensor to solid surface (m)
- $v$ = Velocity of sound in air ≈ **343 m/s** (at 20 °C)
- $t$ = Time of flight (seconds)
- Division by 2 because sound travels **down and back** (round trip)

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation for Solids:** The irregular surface (angle of repose), dust, and foam cause false echoes. Requires sophisticated echo-processing algorithms (e.g., Sonic Intelligence by Siemens).

### 2.2.4 Nuclear (Radiation) Level Detector

**Principle:** A radioactive source (typically **Cesium-137** or **Cobalt-60**) emits gamma rays that pass through the vessel wall and the solid. A detector (Geiger-Müller tube or scintillation counter) on the opposite side measures the intensity of the transmitted radiation.

**Beer-Lambert Law (Exponential Attenuation):**

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho x}$$

Where:
- $I$ = Intensity of radiation reaching the detector (counts/s)
- $I_0$ = Initial radiation intensity (counts/s)
- $\mu$ = Mass absorption coefficient of the solid (m²/kg)
- $\rho$ = Bulk density of the solid (kg/m³)
- $x$ = Thickness of solid material in the path (m)

**Why it works:** When the solid fills the gap between source and detector, more material is in the path → more absorption → lower $I$. Empty vessel → full transmission → higher $I$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Nuclear for Solids?** It is the **only method** that works through metal walls, in extreme temperatures (up to **1000 °C**), corrosive chemicals, and abrasive materials without any contact with the process.

### 2.2.5 Weight / Load Cell Method

**Principle:** The entire vessel (silo) is mounted on load cells. The weight of the solid is measured directly. The level is then calculated using the cross-sectional area of the silo.

$$W = W_{vessel} + \rho_{bulk} \cdot A \cdot h$$

Solving for level $h$:

$$h = \frac{W - W_{vessel}}{\rho_{bulk} \cdot A}$$

Where:
- $W$ = Total weight measured by load cells (N or kg)
- $W_{vessel}$ = Tare weight of empty vessel (N or kg)
- $\rho_{bulk}$ = Bulk density of solid (kg/m³)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of silo (m²)
- $h$ = Height of solid level (m)

### 2.2.6 Paddle Wheel / Rotating Paddle Switch

**Principle:** A small paddle-wheel motor spins at constant speed (typically **1 to 5 RPM**) using a synchronous motor. When the solid material covers the paddle, it physically **stalls** the motor. The stall triggers a torque switch → output relay.

**Key Components:**
- Synchronous motor
- Magnetic clutch (protects motor)
- Paddle wheel (4 or 6 vanes)
- SPDT/DPDT relay output

### 2.2.7 Vibrating Fork (Tuning Fork) Level Switch

**Principle:** Two tuning forks (piezoelectric) vibrate at their **natural frequency** (typically **80 Hz to 400 Hz**). When solid material covers the fork, the resonant frequency **decreases** (or amplitude drops) because the added mass dampens vibration. The shift is detected electronically.

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}}$$

When solid adds mass $\Delta m$:

$$f_{n,loaded} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff} + \Delta m}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{n,loaded} < f_n$$

### 2.2.8 Laser Level Detector

**Principle:** A laser pulse (typically **905 nm** or **1550 nm** wavelength) is fired at the solid surface. The time-of-flight is measured. Because laser is collimated, it gives a very **narrow beam**, ideal for small containers or precise measurements.

**Advantage over Ultrasonic:** No false echoes from dust, side walls, or angle of repose.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Symbols** | **Units** | **Used For** |
|:-----:|:------------|:------------|:----------|:-------------|
| 1 | $C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(R/r)}$ | Capacitance vs. probe immersion | Farads (F) | Capacitance level |
| 2 | $d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | Time-of-flight distance | meters (m) | Ultrasonic / Radar / Laser |
| 3 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho x}$ | Radiation attenuation | counts/s | Nuclear level |
| 4 | $h = \frac{W - W_{vessel}}{\rho_{bulk} \cdot A}$ | Level from weight | m | Load cell method |
| 5 | $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}}$ | Resonant frequency | Hz | Vibrating fork |
| 6 | $\Delta C = C_{solid} - C_{air} = C_0(\varepsilon_r - 1)$ | Change in capacitance | F | Capacitance calibration |
| 7 | $v = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | Speed of sound in air vs. temp | m/s | Ultrasonic correction |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Pitfall:** Never write `|x|` inside markdown tables. Use `\vert x \vert` or `x` (with context) to avoid breaking table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| **Industry** | **Solid Material** | **Preferred Detector** |
|:-------------|:-------------------|:-----------------------|
| Cement | Clinker, cement powder | Paddle wheel, Radar |
| Food & Beverage | Flour, sugar, grain | Vibrating fork, Capacitance |
| Pharmaceutical | Powders, tablets | Capacitance, Laser |
| Mining | Coal, ores, sand | Nuclear, Radar |
| Plastics | Pellets, granules | Capacitance, Ultrasonic |
| Chemical | Catalysts, fertilizers | Nuclear, Weight |
| Power Plants | Fly ash, coal bunker | Paddle, Ultrasonic, Radar |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Capacitance Level Detector — Output Voltage Equation

**Given:** A cylindrical probe of radius $r$ and length $L$ is partially immersed in a bulk solid with dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$. The vessel wall acts as the outer cylinder of radius $R$.

**Objective:** Derive the relationship between probe immersion depth $h$ and output voltage $V_{out}$.

### Step 1: Express Capacitance in Air (Empty Vessel)

When the vessel is empty, the dielectric between the probe and vessel wall is air with $\varepsilon_r = 1$:

$$C_{air} = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot 1 \cdot L}{\ln(R/r)} = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R/r)}$$

**Logic:** Air has $\varepsilon_r = 1$, which is the reference dielectric.

### Step 2: Express Capacitance with Solid Present

When the solid rises to a height $h$ on the probe, two capacitors are formed **in parallel**:
- Section of length $h$ filled with solid (dielectric $\varepsilon_r$)
- Section of length $(L - h)$ filled with air (dielectric = 1)

$$C_{solid} = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r h}{\ln(R/r)} + \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (L - h)}{\ln(R/r)}$$

**Logic:** The two sections share the same voltage (probe and wall are equipotential surfaces) → they add directly.

### Step 3: Simplify the Equation

Factor out common terms:

$$C_{solid} = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R/r)} \left[ \varepsilon_r h + (L - h) \right]$$

Expanding the bracket:

$$C_{solid} = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R/r)} \left[ L + h(\varepsilon_r - 1) \right]$$

**Logic:** This is a linear function of $h$.

### Step 4: Compute Change in Capacitance

$$\Delta C = C_{solid} - C_{air} = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R/r)} \left[ L + h(\varepsilon_r - 1) \right] - \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R/r)}$$

$$\Delta C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R/r)} \cdot h(\varepsilon_r - 1)$$

**Logic:** The difference is directly proportional to immersion depth $h$.

### Step 5: Apply AC Bridge / Oscillator Circuit

The capacitance change is converted to voltage using an AC bridge. The output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = K \cdot \Delta C = K \cdot \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1)}{\ln(R/r)} \cdot h$$

Where $K$ is the bridge sensitivity (V/F).

**Final Linearized Equation:**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = m \cdot h + V_0}$$

Where:
- $m = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 K (\varepsilon_r - 1)}{\ln(R/r)}$ (sensitivity, V/m)
- $V_0$ = Zero-level offset voltage

## 3.2 Derivation: Ultrasonic Distance Calculation with Temperature Compensation

**Given:** Sound travels at velocity $v$ in air. Temperature $T$ affects $v$.

**Objective:** Find the corrected distance formula for an ultrasonic solid level sensor.

### Step 1: Standard Time-of-Flight Formula

For a sensor at the top of a silo, the sound travels down to the solid surface and reflects back:

$$t = \frac{2d}{v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

### Step 2: Incorporate Temperature Effect

The velocity of sound in air is a function of temperature:

$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T$$

Where $T$ is in °C.

### Step 3: Substituting the Temperature-Corrected Velocity

$$d = \frac{(331.4 + 0.6T) \cdot t}{2}$$

### Step 4: Account for Silo Height (Level Calculation)

If the silo has total height $H$ and the sensor is at the top, then the level of solid is:

$$h = H - d = H - \frac{(331.4 + 0.6T) \cdot t}{2}$$

**Logic:** Subtract empty distance from total height to get the level.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A silo has a height of **10 m**. An ultrasonic sensor at the top measures a time-of-flight of **45 ms**. The temperature inside is **35 °C**. Calculate the level of solid in the silo.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Calculate velocity of sound at 35 °C:

$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 35$$

$$v = 331.4 + 21 = 352.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the distance from sensor to solid surface:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2} = \frac{352.4 \times 45 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{15.858}{2} = 7.929 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the level of solid:

$$h = H - d = 10 - 7.929 = 2.071 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** The solid level is **2.071 m** from the bottom of the silo.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** If the silo were empty, the time-of-flight would be $t = 2H/v = 20/352.4 = 56.75$ ms. Our value of 45 ms is less, indicating material is present. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Capacitance** | **Ultrasonic** | **Nuclear** | **Load Cell** | **Paddle Wheel** |
|:--------------|:---------------:|:--------------:|:-----------:|:-------------:|:----------------:|
| **Type** | Point/Cont. | Continuous | Point/Cont. | Continuous | Point |
| **Accuracy** | ±1-2% | ±0.25% | ±1% | ±0.1% | (Switch) |
| **Range** | 0-30 m | 0-60 m | 0-30 m | Full silo | (Switch) |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High | High | Low |
| **Maintenance** | Low | Medium | Low (no contact) | High (mech.) | Medium |
| **Dust Tolerance** | Good | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| **Temp Limit** | 200 °C | 150 °C | 1000 °C | 100 °C | 200 °C |
| **Contact with Process** | Yes | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Best For** | Powders, pellets | Liquids mostly | Harsh env. | Total inventory | Free-flowing solids |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Universal Solid Level Detection System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Process Vessel / Silo Containing Bulk Solid] --> B[Solid Level Sensor Probe]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Circuit]
    C --> D[Microcontroller / Signal Processor Unit]
    D --> E[Display Unit HMI / SCADA]
    D --> F[Alarm Relay Output]
    D --> G[Analog Output 4-20 mA]
    F --> H[High Level Alarm]
    F --> I[Low Level Alarm]
    G --> J[Control Room PLC DCS]
    D --> K[Power Supply Unit 24 V DC]
```

**Interpretation:** The bulk solid in the silo creates a measurable physical change (capacitance, attenuation, weight, etc.) at the probe. The sensor converts this into a low-level electrical signal. The signal conditioning circuit amplifies and filters it. The microcontroller processes the signal and produces three outputs: a local display, a relay output for alarms, and a standard 4–20 mA current loop for the control system. Power is supplied via a regulated 24 V DC supply.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Capacitive Level Detector — Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Probe Electrode] --> B[Capacitance Sensing Network]
    C[Vessel Wall Electrode] --> B
    B --> D[AC Bridge Circuit]
    D --> E[Phase Sensitive Detector]
    E --> F[DC Amplifier Stage]
    F --> G[Microcontroller ADC]
    G --> H[Threshold Comparator]
    H --> I[Relay Output ON OFF]
    G --> J[4-20 mA Current Loop Output]
```

**Interpretation:** The probe and the vessel wall form the two plates of a capacitor. The dielectric between them (air or solid) determines the capacitance. An AC bridge circuit excites the capacitor with a high-frequency signal (typically 50 kHz to 1 MHz). A phase-sensitive detector converts the capacitance change to a DC voltage. A microcontroller then linearizes the signal and drives the relay and 4–20 mA output.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Nuclear Radiation Level Detector

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Radioactive Source Cs-137 or Co-60] --> B[Gamma Ray Beam Collimator]
    B --> C[Process Vessel with Solid Material]
    C --> D[Attenuated Gamma Rays Exit Vessel]
    D --> E[Scintillation Detector or GM Tube]
    E --> F[Pulse Counter Circuit]
    F --> G[Microcontroller for Processing]
    G --> H[Output Display and Relay]
```

**Interpretation:** A radioactive source emits gamma rays that are collimated (focused) into a narrow beam. The beam passes through the vessel wall and the solid material. The intensity of the beam is reduced exponentially based on the amount of solid in its path (Beer-Lambert Law). The detector (scintillation or Geiger-Müller) counts the arriving pulses. A microcontroller computes the level and provides output.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Paddle Wheel Level Switch — Internal Construction

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Power Supply 110/230 V AC or 24 V DC] --> B[Synchronous Motor]
    B --> C[Magnetic Clutch Coupling]
    C --> D[Paddle Wheel Shaft]
    D --> E[Paddle Wheel with Vanes]
    E --> F[Torque Sensor on Shaft]
    F --> G[Detection Circuit]
    G --> H[Relay Contact Output]
    B -. Free Rotation when Air .-> E
    E -. Stall when Solid Touches .-> F
    F --> I[Alarm Indicator LED]
```

**Interpretation:** A synchronous motor drives a paddle wheel through a magnetic clutch (which protects the motor from mechanical shock). When the solid material covers the paddle, it stalls the rotation. The clutch slips, and the torque is detected by a sensor. The detection circuit triggers the relay and an LED indicator.

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Ultrasonic Level Detection Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Microcontroller] --> B[Pulse Generator 40 kHz]
    B --> C[Piezoelectric Transducer]
    C --> D[Acoustic Wave Travels Through Air]
    D --> E[Hits Solid Surface and Reflects]
    E --> F[Returns to Transducer]
    F --> G[Time of Flight Measurement]
    G --> H[Distance Calculation using v x t / 2]
    H --> I[Echo Processing Algorithm]
    I --> J[Level Output in Meters]
    J --> K[HMI Display]
    J --> L[4-20 mA to PLC]
```

**Interpretation:** A microcontroller generates a high-frequency electrical pulse (typically 40 kHz) that excites a piezoelectric transducer. The transducer converts the electrical pulse into a mechanical acoustic wave that travels through the air, hits the irregular surface of the solid, and reflects back. The transducer receives the echo and converts it back to an electrical signal. The time-of-flight is measured, and the distance is calculated. Advanced echo-processing algorithms filter out false echoes from side walls and dust.

## 4.6 Functional Comparison Chart

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Solid Level Detection] --> B[Point Detection Switch]
    A --> C[Continuous Detection Transmitter]
    B --> D[Conductance]
    B --> E[Capacitance Point]
    B --> F[Paddle Wheel]
    B --> G[Vibrating Fork]
    B --> H[Nuclear Point]
    C --> I[Capacitance Continuous]
    C --> J[Ultrasonic]
    C --> K[Radar Microwave]
    C --> L[Load Cell Weight]
    C --> M[Nuclear Continuous]
    C --> N[Plumb Bob Yo-Yo]
    C --> O[Laser]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Q: List any three solid level detection methods suitable for bulk solids. State one limitation of ultrasonic level measurement for solids.**

**Model Answer:**

Three solid level detection methods are:
1. **Capacitance method** — Uses the change in dielectric constant between a probe and vessel wall.
2. **Ultrasonic method** — Uses time-of-flight of high-frequency sound waves reflected from the solid surface.
3. **Nuclear (Radiation) method** — Uses gamma ray attenuation through the solid material.

**Limitation of Ultrasonic for Solids:** Ultrasonic sensors are highly sensitive to **dust, irregular surfaces (angle of repose), and foaming**. The rough, cone-shaped surface of bulk solids causes scattering and false echoes, leading to inaccurate readings. Additionally, high-temperature environments alter the velocity of sound, requiring compensation.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Q: What is the principle of a vibrating fork level switch? How does it detect a solid material?**

**Model Answer:**

A vibrating fork level switch operates on the principle of **change in resonant frequency** of a piezoelectric tuning fork.

**Working Principle:**
- A pair of piezoelectric elements drives a tuning fork at its **natural resonant frequency** (typically 80 Hz to 400 Hz).
- When the solid material **covers** the fork, the effective mass of the fork increases by $\Delta m$.
- This lowers the resonant frequency: $f_{n,loaded} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff} + \Delta m}}$.
- The frequency shift is detected by an electronic circuit, which triggers a relay output.
- The fork is self-cleaning — vibrations prevent dust build-up.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **capacitive method of solid level measurement**. Derive the relationship between capacitance and the level of solid. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A capacitive level sensor has a probe radius of **5 mm** and is mounted in a vessel of inner radius **50 mm**. The probe length is **2 m**. When the solid level is **1.5 m**, calculate the change in capacitance. Take $\varepsilon_r$ of solid = 4, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m. (7 Marks)

#### Solution to (a):

The **capacitive level measurement** works on the principle of variation of capacitance between a probe (electrode) and the vessel wall (which acts as the second electrode), with the solid material acting as the dielectric.

**Construction:**
- A vertical metal probe (insulated from the vessel wall) is installed in the silo.
- The vessel wall acts as the reference electrode.
- The bulk solid between them acts as the dielectric medium.

**Working:**
- When the vessel is empty, the dielectric is air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$).
- When solid rises and covers the probe, the dielectric changes to the solid material ($\varepsilon_r > 1$).
- Since $C \propto \varepsilon_r$, the capacitance increases linearly with the level of solid.
- The capacitance change is measured using an AC bridge or oscillator circuit and converted to a DC voltage signal.

**Derivation (Already shown in Section 3.1):**

$$\Delta C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1)}{\ln(R/r)} \cdot h$$

**Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 — Block Diagram of Capacitive Level Detector)

#### Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $r = 5$ mm = $0.005$ m
- $R = 50$ mm = $0.05$ m
- $L = 2$ m
- $h = 1.5$ m
- $\varepsilon_r = 4$
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m

**Step 1:** Compute the geometric factor $\frac{1}{\ln(R/r)}$:

$$\ln(R/r) = \ln(50/5) = \ln(10) = 2.3026$$

**Step 2:** Apply the capacitance change formula:

$$\Delta C = \frac{2 \pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times (4 - 1)}{2.3026} \times 1.5$$

$$\Delta C = \frac{2 \pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3 \times 1.5}{2.3026}$$

**Step 3:** Numerator calculation:

$$2 \pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 5.563 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$5.563 \times 10^{-11} \times 3 \times 1.5 = 2.503 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 4:** Final division:

$$\Delta C = \frac{2.503 \times 10^{-10}}{2.3026} = 1.087 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to pF:

$$\boxed{\Delta C = 108.7 \text{ pF}}$$

**Verification:** This is a typical value for industrial capacitance probes, confirming the result is realistic.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **nuclear (radiation) method of solid level measurement** with a neat block diagram. State two advantages and two disadvantages. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A silo of **6 m diameter** is fitted with a load cell weighing system. The empty silo weighs **5000 kg** and the loaded silo weighs **18000 kg**. If the bulk density of the solid is **1200 kg/m³**, determine the level of the solid in the silo. (7 Marks)

#### Solution to (a):

The **nuclear (radiation) level detector** uses the attenuation of gamma rays by the solid material to determine the level.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3 — Nuclear Radiation Level Detector)

**Components:**
1. **Radioactive source** (Cesium-137 or Cobalt-60) — sealed in a lead container with a collimated opening.
2. **Radiation detector** (Geiger-Müller tube or scintillation counter) — mounted on the opposite side of the vessel.
3. **Pulse counter** — counts the pulses from the detector.
4. **Microcontroller** — processes the count and computes the level.

**Working:**
- Gamma rays from the source travel through the vessel wall and solid material.
- The intensity is reduced exponentially as per the **Beer-Lambert Law**:
$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho x}$$
- Empty vessel → minimal absorption → high count rate → "empty" indication.
- Filled vessel → maximum absorption → low count rate → "full" indication.
- The level can be detected as a point (high/low alarm) or continuously (using a long source and detector strip).

**Advantages:**
1. **No contact** with the process — ideal for abrasive, corrosive, and high-temperature solids (up to 1000 °C).
2. **Works through metal walls** — no need to cut openings in the vessel.
3. Unaffected by dust, foam, and angle of repose.
4. Long-term stability with no moving parts.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Hazardous radiation** — requires strict licensing, safety protocols, and operator training.
2. **Expensive** — both initial cost and decommissioning of radioactive source.
3. Radiation level decreases over time (source half-life), requiring periodic replacement.
4. Calibration depends on bulk density, which may vary for the same solid.

#### Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- Silo diameter $D = 6$ m
- Empty weight $W_{vessel} = 5000$ kg
- Loaded weight $W = 18000$ kg
- Bulk density $\rho_{bulk} = 1200$ kg/m³

**Step 1:** Calculate the cross-sectional area of the silo:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 6^2}{4} = \frac{36\pi}{4} = 9\pi = 28.274 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the mass of the solid:

$$m_{solid} = W - W_{vessel} = 18000 - 5000 = 13000 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the volume of the solid:

$$V = \frac{m_{solid}}{\rho_{bulk}} = \frac{13000}{1200} = 10.833 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the level of the solid:

$$h = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{10.833}{28.274} = 0.3832 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h = 0.3832 \text{ m} \approx 38.32 \text{ cm}}$$

**Verification:** Volume check: $V = A \times h = 28.274 \times 0.3832 = 10.83$ m³ ✓ Mass check: $m = V \times \rho = 10.83 \times 1200 = 12996 \approx 13000$ kg ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Capacitance Q:** Students often forget to apply the **parallel capacitor model** for the air section. The two sections (filled and empty) are in **parallel**, not series. Marks deducted: 2.
> 2. **Ultrasonic Q:** Always mention **time-of-flight** principle. Do NOT confuse it with radar (which uses electromagnetic waves). Marks deducted: 1–2.
> 3. **Nuclear Q:** Must state **Beer-Lambert Law** with units. Examiner expects the formula on the answer sheet. Marks deducted: 2.
> 4. **Load Cell Q:** Use $\rho_{bulk}$ (bulk density), NOT true density. The vessel's cross-section must be computed correctly from diameter. Marks deducted: 1–2.
> 5. **Common Mistake:** Students confuse **level** (m) with **volume** (m³) or **mass** (kg). Always state units clearly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📌 Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Solid Level Detection** = measurement of bulk solid height/presence in vessels.
- Two categories: **Point (switch)** vs **Continuous (transmitter)**.
- **Capacitance:** $C = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L}{\ln(R/r)}$; linear with level; needs $\varepsilon_r > 1$.
- **Ultrasonic:** $d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$; non-contact; poor with dust and irregular surfaces.
- **Nuclear:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu \rho x}$; non-contact; works in harsh environments; **safety-critical**.
- **Load Cell:** $h = \frac{W - W_{vessel}}{\rho_{bulk} A}$; high accuracy; measures total inventory.
- **Paddle Wheel:** Stall detection at low RPM (1–5); free-flowing solids only.
- **Vibrating Fork:** Frequency shift due to mass loading; self-cleaning.
- **Radar:** Uses **EM waves** (not sound); immune to dust, vapor, and temperature.
- **Laser:** Very narrow beam, high accuracy, expensive.
- **Plumb Bob (Yo-Yo):** Mechanical sensor lowered from top; for very tall silos (up to 70 m).

### 🎯 KTU Board Exam Key Points

1. **Always state the principle first**, then explain working.
2. **Draw a block diagram** for every method — it fetches 2–3 marks.
3. **Use formulas** with proper symbols and units.
4. **Mention applications** — minimum one industry example.
5. **Compare** methods using a table for higher marks.
6. **Numerical problems** — show step-by-step substitution; never skip a step.

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- Confusing **capacitance** with **conductance** methods.
- Using **speed of light** for ultrasonic (it's sound, not EM wave).
- Forgetting to **square the radius** in vessel area calculation ($A = \pi r^2$, not $\pi d$).
- Using **true density** instead of **bulk density** in load cell formula.
- Missing the **angle of repose** issue for solids (not for liquids).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moisture Content and Humidity: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Moisture Content and Humidity: Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Moisture Content — Formal Definition

In industrial process instrumentation, **Moisture Content** is formally defined as the quantitative measure of the amount of water (or any volatile liquid) present within a solid, liquid, or gaseous substance, expressed as a ratio of the mass of water to the mass of the dry reference material.

$$M = \frac{m_w}{m_{dry}} \times 100\%$$

where:
- $M$ = Moisture content (percentage, **% wb** or **% db**)
- $m_w$ = Mass of water present in the substance (**kg** or **g**)
- $m_{dry}$ = Mass of the bone-dry substance (**kg** or **g**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Industrial moisture measurement focuses on two bases:
> - **Wet Basis (% wb):** $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_{wet}} \times 100$ — commonly used in food, paper, and textile industries.
> - **Dry Basis (% db):** $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_{dry}} \times 100$ — preferred in chemical and drying operations.

---

### 1.2 Humidity — Formal Definition

**Humidity** is defined as the concentration of water vapor present in a gaseous mixture, most commonly in atmospheric air. Since air is a binary mixture of dry air and water vapor, humidity characterizes the thermodynamic state of this mixture from a moisture-load perspective.

Humidity is broadly classified into three primary categories recognized by the KTU instrumentation syllabus:

1. **Absolute Humidity (AH):** The actual mass of water vapor per unit volume of the moist air mixture. Units: **g/m³** or **kg/m³**.

$$AH = \frac{m_v}{V}$$

2. **Specific Humidity (SH) / Humidity Ratio (W):** The mass of water vapor per unit mass of dry air. Units: **kg water vapor / kg dry air** (dimensionless ratio).

$$W = \frac{m_v}{m_{da}}$$

3. **Relative Humidity (RH):** The ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor to the saturation vapor pressure at the same temperature, expressed as a percentage. Units: **% RH**.

$$RH = \frac{p_v}{p_s(T)} \times 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is RH bounded between 0% and 100%?**
> Because $p_v \leq p_s(T)$ by the laws of thermodynamics. At **dew point**, $RH = 100\%$, and any further cooling causes condensation (phase change of vapor into liquid water).

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Sponge Analogy for Moisture Content:**
> Imagine a kitchen sponge. The water trapped inside is the *moisture content*. If you squeeze it lightly, some water drips out — this is *free moisture*. The water that remains clinging to the sponge fibers, even after squeezing, is *bound moisture*. Industrial moisture sensors (like capacitive or NIR sensors) measure the *total moisture* because both free and bound water molecules affect the electrical/dielectric properties of the material.

> [!TIP]
> **The Crowded Room Analogy for Humidity:**
> Picture a closed room filled with people (water vapor molecules) and empty chairs (space available). 
> - **Absolute Humidity** = how many people are *actually* in the room. 
> - **Relative Humidity** = how full the room *feels* (people ÷ total chair capacity). 
> - On a hot day, the room can hold *more people* (higher saturation pressure $p_s$ rises with $T$), so even the same number of people makes the room feel less crowded (lower RH). This is why RH drops in the afternoon even when absolute moisture is constant.

---

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight of water | $M_w$ | **18.015 g/mol** |
| Molecular weight of dry air | $M_a$ | **28.97 g/mol** |
| Universal gas constant | $R_u$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Specific gas constant for water vapor | $R_v$ | **461.5 J/(kg·K)** |
| Specific gas constant for dry air | $R_a$ | **287.05 J/(kg·K)** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Reference temperature (IAPWS) | $T_0$ | **273.15 K (0°C)** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Two Separate Quantities?

Moisture content and humidity describe **physically different scenarios**:

- **Moisture content** describes water *trapped within or adsorbed onto* a solid/liquid bulk (e.g., soil, grain, paper, coal, petroleum).
- **Humidity** describes water *dispersed as vapor* in a gas stream (typically air).

Although both quantify water, their measurement techniques, units, and industrial relevance differ substantially. KTU 2024 scheme groups them together because many industrial plants (cement kilns, paper mills, dryers, HVAC) need to measure **both** simultaneously.

---

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown — Understanding the Humidity Family

**Step 1 — Saturation Vapor Pressure ($p_s$):**
The maximum partial pressure that water vapor can exert at a given temperature, before condensation begins. It is a strong, *non-linear* function of temperature (Clausius–Clapeyron relation).

**Step 2 — Partial Pressure of Vapor ($p_v$):**
The actual pressure contribution of water vapor in the moist air mixture, governed by Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures.

$$P_{total} = p_a + p_v$$

**Step 3 — Humidity Ratio (Specific Humidity):**
Derived from ideal gas law applied separately to dry air and water vapor. Since both occupy the same volume and temperature:

$$W = 0.622 \times \frac{p_v}{P_{total} - p_v}$$

**Step 4 — Relative Humidity:**
A *normalized* indicator — most useful because it directly correlates with human comfort, drying rates, and static electricity phenomena in industries.

**Step 5 — Dew Point Temperature ($T_{dp}$):**
The temperature at which the actual vapor would just begin to condense. It is the saturation temperature corresponding to $p_v$:

$$p_s(T_{dp}) = p_v$$

**Step 6 — Wet-Bulb Temperature ($T_{wb}$):**
The dynamic equilibrium temperature reached when water evaporates into unsaturated air, combining heat and mass transfer effects. Used in psychrometric calculations.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all** high-yield formulas for this topic. Memorize these for both **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** derivations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_{wet}} \times 100$ | % | $m_{wet} = m_w + m_{dry}$ |
| 2 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_{dry}} \times 100$ | % | Used in drying calculations |
| 3 | Conversion wb ↔ db | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$ | % | Critical relationship |
| 4 | Absolute humidity | $AH = \dfrac{m_v}{V}$ | kg/m³ | Density of water vapor |
| 5 | Specific humidity (W) | $W = 0.622 \times \dfrac{p_v}{P - p_v}$ | kg/kg da | $0.622 = M_w / M_a$ |
| 6 | Relative humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{p_v}{p_s(T)} \times 100$ | % | Bounded: 0 to 100% |
| 7 | Specific humidity in terms of $\phi$ | $W = 0.622 \times \dfrac{\phi \cdot p_s}{P - \phi \cdot p_s}$ | kg/kg da | Most used form |
| 8 | Degree of saturation | $\mu = \dfrac{W}{W_s}$ | ratio | $\leq 1$ |
| 9 | Dew point relationship | $p_s(T_{dp}) = p_v$ | Pa | Defines dew point |
| 10 | Enthalpy of moist air | $h = c_p \cdot T + W \cdot (h_{fg} + c_{pv} \cdot T)$ | kJ/kg da | Psychrometric formula |
| 11 | Saturation vapor pressure (Antoine) | $\log_{10} p_s = A - \dfrac{B}{C+T}$ | mmHg or kPa | Empirical, T in °C |
| 12 | Vapor pressure (Clausius–Clapeyron) | $\ln \dfrac{p_s(T_2)}{p_s(T_1)} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R_v} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$ | Pa | Theoretical basis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Specific humidity $W$ is the *carrier-friendly* quantity because it is conserved during heating/cooling of air (no condensation). Relative humidity $\phi$ is the *operator-friendly* quantity because it is intuitive (0–100%) and directly readable on industrial hygrometers.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Pharmaceuticals:** Moisture in granules must be controlled at **2–5% db** to ensure tablet integrity and dissolution rate.
- **Food Processing:** Wheat storage demands moisture **< 14% wb** to prevent fungal growth.
- **HVAC Systems:** Comfort control requires $40\% \leq RH \leq 60\%$ to prevent both dryness and microbial growth.
- **Power Plants:** Moisture in natural gas pipelines is measured in **ppm by volume** to prevent hydrate formation.
- **Cement Industry:** Kiln feed moisture must be **< 0.5%** to maintain combustion efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Specific Humidity (Humidity Ratio) $W$

**Starting Point — Ideal Gas Law applied to dry air and water vapor separately:**

For dry air: $p_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T$

For water vapor: $p_v \cdot V = m_v \cdot R_v \cdot T$

**Step 1 — Define specific humidity:**

$$W \triangleq \frac{m_v}{m_a}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the ideal gas relations into the ratio:**

$$W = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{\dfrac{p_v \cdot V}{R_v \cdot T}}{\dfrac{p_a \cdot V}{R_a \cdot T}}$$

The volume $V$ and temperature $T$ cancel out:

$$W = \frac{p_v \cdot R_a}{p_a \cdot R_v}$$

**Step 3 — Use the relation between gas constants:**

$$\frac{R_a}{R_v} = \frac{R_u / M_a}{R_u / M_v} = \frac{M_v}{M_a} = \frac{18.015}{28.97} \approx 0.622$$

This gives us the constant $\varepsilon = 0.622$.

**Step 4 — Apply Dalton's Law of partial pressures:**

$$p_a = P_{total} - p_v$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$W = 0.622 \times \frac{p_v}{P_{total} - p_v}$$

**Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\,W = 0.622 \times \frac{p_v}{P - p_v}\,}$$

This is the **fundamental psychrometric equation** used universally in KTU problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Specific Humidity in Terms of Relative Humidity

**Step 1 — Definition of RH:**

$$\phi = \frac{p_v}{p_s(T)} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; p_v = \phi \cdot p_s(T)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the $W$ equation:**

$$W = 0.622 \times \frac{\phi \cdot p_s(T)}{P - \phi \cdot p_s(T)}$$

**Step 3 — Verification (boundary condition):**
At saturation, $\phi = 1$:

$$W_s = 0.622 \times \frac{p_s}{P - p_s}$$

This is the *saturation humidity ratio*, used in drying-rate calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is the **single most important formula** for KTU 2024 numerical problems on this topic. Always ensure $\phi$ is entered as a decimal (0.5), NOT as a percentage (50).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Conversion Between Wet Basis and Dry Basis Moisture

**Step 1 — Definitions:**

$$M_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_{wet}} \times 100 = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_{dry}} \times 100$$

$$M_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_{dry}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio:**

$$\frac{M_{db}}{M_{wb}} = \frac{\dfrac{m_w}{m_{dry}}}{\dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_{dry}}} = \frac{m_w + m_{dry}}{m_{dry}} = 1 + \frac{m_w}{m_{dry}}$$

**Step 3 — Express $m_w / m_{dry}$ in terms of $M_{db}$:**

From $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_{dry}} \times 100$:

$$\frac{m_w}{m_{dry}} = \frac{M_{db}}{100}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$\frac{M_{db}}{M_{wb}} = 1 + \frac{M_{db}}{100}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $M_{db}$:**

$$100 \cdot M_{db} = 100 \cdot M_{wb} + M_{db} \cdot M_{wb}$$

$$M_{db}(100 - M_{wb}) = 100 \cdot M_{wb}$$

$$\boxed{\,M_{db} = \frac{100 \cdot M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}}\,}$$

Equivalently:

$$M_{wb} = \frac{100 \cdot M_{db}}{100 + M_{db}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Example — Worked Numerical (For Practice)

**Problem:** Moist air at **$T = 35°C$** and **$P = 101.325$ kPa** has a relative humidity of **60%**. Given $p_s(35°C) = 5.628$ kPa, find: (a) humidity ratio $W$, (b) dew point temperature (if $p_s(30°C) = 4.246$ kPa and $p_s(31°C) = 4.494$ kPa), and (c) absolute humidity.

**Solution (a) — Humidity Ratio:**

$$p_v = \phi \cdot p_s = 0.6 \times 5.628 = 3.377 \text{ kPa}$$

$$W = 0.622 \times \frac{3.377}{101.325 - 3.377} = 0.622 \times \frac{3.377}{97.948}$$

$$W = 0.02144 \text{ kg water / kg dry air} = 21.44 \text{ g/kg da}$$

**Solution (b) — Dew Point (interpolation):**

We need $T_{dp}$ such that $p_s(T_{dp}) = 3.377$ kPa.

From the data: $p_s(30°C) = 4.246$ kPa, $p_s(31°C) = 4.494$ kPa. But our value 3.377 kPa is *less* than 4.246, so it lies between 25°C and 30°C. Using more detailed steam tables or Antoine equation: $T_{dp} \approx 25.6°C$.

**Solution (c) — Absolute Humidity:**

$$AH = \frac{p_v \cdot M_w}{R_u \cdot T} = \frac{3377 \times 18.015}{8.314 \times 308.15} = \frac{60837}{2561.7} \approx 23.75 \text{ g/m}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification check:** $AH$ should be very close to $p_v \times 0.007$ (approx) at room temperature. Here $3377 \times 0.007 = 23.64$ — matches within 0.5%. ✓

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Degree of Saturation ($\mu$)

**Definition:**

$$\mu = \frac{W}{W_s}$$

Substituting the explicit formula for $W$:

$$\mu = \frac{0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_v}{P - p_v}}{0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_s}{P - p_s}} = \frac{p_v \cdot (P - p_s)}{p_s \cdot (P - p_v)}$$

This shows that $\mu$ and $\phi$ are related but **not equal**:

$$\mu = \frac{\phi \cdot (P - p_s)}{P - \phi \cdot p_s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students frequently write $\mu = \phi$. This is **wrong** — they are only approximately equal at low RH values (when $P \gg p_s$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define moisture content. Distinguish clearly between moisture content expressed on a wet basis and on a dry basis. Why is the dry basis preferred in industrial drying calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

**Moisture Content Definition:**
Moisture content is the quantity of water present in a material, expressed as a ratio of the mass of water to the mass of the dry material (or wet material, depending on the basis).

**Wet Basis:**
$$M_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_{wet}} \times 100\%$$
where $m_{wet} = m_w + m_{dry}$.

**Dry Basis:**
$$M_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_{dry}} \times 100\%$$

**Distinction:**

| Feature | Wet Basis | Dry Basis |
|---|---|---|
| Denominator | Includes water | Excludes water |
| Range | Always < 100% | Can exceed 100% |
| Convention | Quality control, food | Engineering, drying |

**Why dry basis is preferred in drying calculations:**
In drying operations, the *dry solid mass* remains constant throughout the process, while the wet mass continuously decreases. Using dry basis yields a linear relationship with evaporation rate and avoids mathematical singularities as moisture approaches zero. The mass of water evaporated per unit time is directly proportional to the change in $M_{db}$.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for distinction table, 1 for justification)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define absolute humidity, specific humidity, and relative humidity. State the SI units of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Absolute Humidity (AH):** The mass of water vapor contained in a unit volume of the moist air mixture.
   $$AH = \frac{m_v}{V} \quad [\text{SI Unit: kg/m}^3]$$

2. **Specific Humidity (W) / Humidity Ratio:** The mass of water vapor per unit mass of dry air.
   $$W = \frac{m_v}{m_{da}} \quad [\text{SI Unit: kg/kg dry air — dimensionless ratio}]$$

3. **Relative Humidity (RH, $\phi$):** The ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor to the saturation vapor pressure at the same dry-bulb temperature, expressed as a percentage.
   $$\phi = \frac{p_v}{p_s(T)} \times 100 \quad [\text{SI Unit: \% RH, dimensionless ratio}]$$

**Conversion relationship:**
$$W = 0.622 \times \frac{\phi \cdot p_s(T)}{P - \phi \cdot p_s(T)}$$

**(3 marks — 1 per definition with units)**

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the specific humidity (humidity ratio) of moist air in terms of partial pressure of water vapor and total pressure. State clearly the assumptions made in the derivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Moist air at a total pressure of **101.325 kPa** has a dry-bulb temperature of **40°C** and a relative humidity of **50%**. Given $p_s(40°C) = 7.384$ kPa, calculate:
- (i) Humidity ratio $W$
- (ii) Absolute humidity $AH$
- (iii) Dew point temperature, given $p_s(28°C) = 3.778$ kPa and $p_s(29°C) = 4.004$ kPa.

---

### **Model Solution A(a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Both dry air and water vapor obey the **ideal gas law**.
2. The gases are at the **same temperature and volume** (Dalton's law of partial pressures applies).
3. No chemical reaction occurs between dry air and water vapor.

**Derivation:**

Applying ideal gas law to water vapor: $p_v V = m_v R_v T$

Applying ideal gas law to dry air: $p_a V = m_a R_a T$

Dividing:

$$\frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{p_v \cdot R_a}{p_a \cdot R_v} = \frac{p_v}{p_a} \cdot \frac{M_v}{M_a} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_v}{p_a}$$

By Dalton's law: $p_a = P - p_v$

$$\boxed{W = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_v}{P - p_v}}$$

**(7 marks — 2 for assumptions, 5 for derivation)**

---

### **Model Solution A(b):**

**Given:** $P = 101.325$ kPa, $T = 40°C = 313.15$ K, $\phi = 0.5$, $p_s(40°C) = 7.384$ kPa

**(i) Humidity Ratio $W$:**

$$p_v = \phi \cdot p_s = 0.5 \times 7.384 = 3.692 \text{ kPa}$$

$$W = 0.622 \times \frac{3.692}{101.325 - 3.692} = 0.622 \times \frac{3.692}{97.633}$$

$$W = 0.622 \times 0.03782 = 0.02352 \text{ kg/kg da} = 23.52 \text{ g/kg da}$$

**(ii) Absolute Humidity $AH$:**

$$AH = \frac{p_v \cdot M_w}{R_u \cdot T} = \frac{3692 \times 18.015}{8.314 \times 313.15}$$

$$AH = \frac{66511}{2603.5} = 25.55 \text{ g/m}^3$$

**(iii) Dew Point Temperature $T_{dp}$:**

We need $T_{dp}$ such that $p_s(T_{dp}) = p_v = 3.692$ kPa.

**Linear interpolation** between 28°C (3.778 kPa) and 29°C (4.004 kPa):

But 3.692 kPa < 3.778 kPa, so $T_{dp} < 28°C$. Using Antoine's equation with constants $A = 8.07131$, $B = 1730.63$, $C = 233.426$ (water, $p$ in mmHg):

$$\log_{10} p_{mmHg} = 8.07131 - \frac{1730.63}{233.426 + T_{°C}}$$

Setting $p_{mmHg} = 3.692 \times 7.5006 = 27.69$ mmHg:

$$1.4422 = 8.07131 - \frac{1730.63}{233.426 + T_{°C}}$$

$$\frac{1730.63}{233.426 + T_{°C}} = 6.6291$$

$$233.426 + T_{°C} = 261.07 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; T_{dp} \approx 27.6°C$$

$$T_{dp} = 300.75 \text{ K} = 27.6°C$$

**(14 marks — 4 for (i), 4 for (ii), 6 for (iii) including interpolation)**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labeled block diagram, explain the psychrometric method of humidity measurement using dry-bulb and wet-bulb thermometers. Define psychrometric chart and list any four properties that can be read from it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A solid material weighing **120 kg** initially contains **30% moisture (wet basis)**. It is dried until the moisture content reduces to **8% (wet basis)**. Calculate:
- (i) The mass of water removed.
- (ii) The final mass of the dried product.
- (iii) The moisture content in **dry basis** at the start and end of the drying operation.

---

### **Model Solution B(a):**

**Block Diagram Description:**
A psychrometer consists of two identical thermometers mounted side by side:
- **Dry-Bulb Thermometer (DBT):** Measures the ambient air temperature directly.
- **Wet-Bulb Thermometer (WBT):** Has its bulb wrapped in a muslin wick saturated with distilled water. As water evaporates from the wick into the unsaturated air, it absorbs latent heat from the bulb, lowering its reading.

The **psychrometric difference** $(T_{db} - T_{wb})$ is used in conjunction with the psychrometric chart to determine relative humidity, specific humidity, dew point, and enthalpy.

**Psychrometric Chart Definition:**
A psychrometric chart is a graphical representation of the thermodynamic properties of moist air, plotted with dry-bulb temperature on the abscissa (X-axis) and humidity ratio $W$ on the ordinate (Y-axis). Constant RH lines curve across the chart, along with constant wet-bulb, dew-point, and specific-volume lines.

**Four properties readable from the chart:**
1. Relative humidity (RH)
2. Specific humidity / humidity ratio (W)
3. Dew point temperature ($T_{dp}$)
4. Specific volume / enthalpy / wet-bulb temperature

**(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 2 for explanation, 1 for chart definition, 2 for four properties)**

---

### **Model Solution B(b):**

**Given:** Initial mass $m_1 = 120$ kg, $M_{wb,1} = 30\%$, $M_{wb,2} = 8\%$

**Step 1 — Find dry mass of the material (constant throughout drying):**

$$m_{dry} = m_1 \times (1 - 0.30) = 120 \times 0.70 = 84 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Find initial mass of water:**

$$m_{w,1} = 120 - 84 = 36 \text{ kg}$$

**(i) Mass of water removed:**

Initial water = 36 kg.

Final mass $m_2$ satisfies: $m_2 = 84 + m_{w,2}$ and $M_{wb,2} = 0.08 = \dfrac{m_{w,2}}{m_2}$

$$0.08 \cdot m_2 = m_{w,2} = m_2 - 84$$

$$0.92 \cdot m_2 = 84 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; m_2 = 91.30 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{w,2} = 91.30 - 84 = 7.30 \text{ kg}$$

**Mass of water removed:**
$$\Delta m_w = 36 - 7.30 = 28.70 \text{ kg}$$

**(ii) Final mass of dried product:** $m_2 = 91.30$ kg

**(iii) Moisture content in dry basis:**

$$M_{db,1} = \frac{36}{84} \times 100 = 42.86\%$$

$$M_{db,2} = \frac{7.30}{84} \times 100 = 8.69\%$$

**Verification using conversion formula:**

$$M_{db,1} = \frac{100 \times 30}{100 - 30} = \frac{3000}{70} = 42.86\% \checkmark$$

$$M_{db,2} = \frac{100 \times 8}{100 - 8} = \frac{800}{92} = 8.70\% \checkmark$$

**(14 marks — 4 for setup, 3 for (i), 3 for (ii), 4 for (iii) including verification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **The $0.622$ trap:** Many students write $0.662$ or $0.622$ interchangeably. The correct ratio is $M_w / M_a = 18.015 / 28.97 = 0.622$. Marking penalty: **−1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **% vs decimal for $\phi$:** Entering $\phi = 50$ instead of $0.5$ yields numerically incorrect $W$ by a factor of 100. Always strip the percent sign before calculation.
> 3. **Wrong denominator in $M_{db}$:** The denominator is $m_{dry}$, NOT $m_{wet}$. This is the single most common KTU mistake. Penalty: **−2 marks**.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency in $AH$:** Mixing g/m³ with kg/m³ in a single numerical. Always state units explicitly in every line.
> 5. **Skipping assumptions in derivation:** The KTU 2024 scheme marks 2 marks *specifically* for assumptions in any derivation. Never skip them.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Moisture & Humidity Definitions**

- **Moisture Content** quantifies water *in solids/liquids*; **Humidity** quantifies water *as vapor in gases*. They are physically distinct.
- **Wet basis (wb)** uses total mass as denominator; **dry basis (db)** uses only dry mass as denominator. Conversion: $M_{db} = \dfrac{100 \cdot M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}}$.
- **Absolute Humidity** = mass of vapor per unit volume (g/m³ or kg/m³).
- **Specific Humidity / Humidity Ratio** $W$ = mass of vapor per mass of dry air (kg/kg da). **Most-used formula:** $W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_v}{P - p_v}$.
- **Relative Humidity** $\phi$ = ratio of actual vapor pressure to saturation vapor pressure at the same temperature, in %.
- **Dew Point** is the temperature at which condensation begins — find it by equating $p_s(T_{dp}) = p_v$.
- **The magic constant $0.622$** is the ratio of molecular weights of water vapor to dry air.
- **Saturation condition** corresponds to $\phi = 100\%$ and $W = W_s$.
- **Degree of saturation** $\mu = W / W_s$ is *not* equal to $\phi$ except at low humidity.
- **$W$ is conserved** during sensible heating/cooling of air (no condensation); $\phi$ is *not* conserved.
- **$p_s$ increases exponentially** with temperature (Clausius–Clapeyron). This is why a small temperature change causes a large RH drop.
- **Industrial applications:** Pharma granules (2–5% db), food storage (<14% wb), HVAC (40–60% RH), cement (<0.5%), natural gas pipelines (ppm-level moisture).
- **Key sensors to remember for next module topics:** Capacitive RH sensor, psychrometer (DBT/WBT), chilled mirror hygrometer, NIR moisture analyzer, Karl Fischer titrator.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moisture Content Determination by Thermal Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Moisture Content Determination by Thermal Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moisture Content Determination by Thermal Drying** is a primary, absolute (gravimetric) measurement technique in which the water present in a solid, liquid, or semi-solid material is removed by the application of heat, and the mass loss is interpreted as the moisture content of the sample. The method is standardized under ASTM E1756, ISO 712, and BIS 4333 (Part III) for cereals, food, pharma, cement, and chemical industries.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Thermal drying (also called Loss-On-Drying, LOD) is a direct method of moisture analysis in which a weighed sample is subjected to a controlled thermal environment (convection, infrared, microwave, or vacuum), and the moisture content is determined from the difference between the initial wet mass and the final dry mass, expressed as a percentage of either the wet sample (Wet Basis, %wb) or the dry solid (Dry Basis, %db).*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Wet Sponge Story"**
>
> Imagine you have a kitchen sponge that is completely soaked with water. The sponge feels heavy in your hand. Now, you place the sponge in a hot oven. After some time, the sponge becomes bone-dry and weighs significantly less.
>
> The **weight you lost** = the **water that evaporated**.
>
> This is exactly what thermal drying does! The instrument simply:
> 1. **Weighs** your sample when it is wet (Wet Mass, $M_w$).
> 2. **Heats** the sample until all water is driven out.
> 3. **Weighs** the sample again when it is bone-dry (Dry Mass, $M_d$).
> 4. **Subtracts** the two weights → the difference is the moisture.
>
> It is the **gold standard** of moisture measurement because it directly measures what we want to know — the mass of water — rather than inferring it from secondary properties (like electrical resistance or capacitance).

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions for KTU Lab & Industry Practice:**
> - **Standard Drying Temperature for Cereals/Food:** $103 \pm 2\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ (ISO 712)
> - **Standard Drying Temperature for Oils/Fats:** $105\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$
> - **Standard Drying Temperature for Dairy:** $102\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$
> - **Standard Drying Temperature for Pharma:** $105\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ (USP 731)
> - **Boiling Point of Water at 1 atm:** $100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($373.15\ \text{K}$)
> - **Latent Heat of Vaporization of Water:** $\approx 2257\ \text{kJ/kg}$ ($540\ \text{kcal/kg}$)
> - **Relative Humidity inside a Laboratory Oven:** typically maintained below $\mathbf{10\%}$ RH to prevent re-absorption.

---

## 1.4 The Two Reporting Bases — Why It Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A student can compute the moisture value correctly, but report it on the **wrong basis** and lose 2 full marks. The KTU examiner will explicitly check whether you have used **%wb** or **%db** as the question demands.

| Basis | Definition | Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Wet Basis (%wb)** | Moisture expressed as a percentage of the *original wet mass* | Food industry, agriculture, quality control reports |
| **Dry Basis (%db)** | Moisture expressed as a percentage of the *bone-dry solid mass* | Chemical engineering, drying-rate calculations, design of dryers |

---

## 1.5 Why Thermal Drying? Engineering Justification

> [!NOTE]
> **The Engineering Rationale:**
> 1. It is a **direct, absolute measurement** — no calibration curve required.
> 2. It gives the **true moisture content** (the reference against which all other methods are calibrated).
> 3. It is the **only method accepted in regulatory compliance** for pharma, food, and cement industries.
> 4. Modern infrared and microwave moisture balances reduce analysis time from **3–16 hours (conventional oven)** down to **5–20 minutes**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle

Thermal drying exploits the **phase change of water from liquid to vapor**. The energy supplied to the sample must satisfy two demands:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{total}} &= Q_{\text{sensible}} + Q_{\text{latent}} + Q_{\text{losses}} \\
Q_{\text{sensible}} &= m_s \cdot c_{p,s} \cdot (T_{\text{evap}} - T_{\text{initial}}) \\
Q_{\text{latent}} &= m_w \cdot L_v
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $m_s$ = mass of dry solid (kg)
- $c_{p,s}$ = specific heat capacity of solid (J/kg·K)
- $T_{\text{evap}}$ = evaporation temperature ($\approx 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ at 1 atm)
- $T_{\text{initial}}$ = starting temperature of sample
- $m_w$ = mass of water removed (kg)
- $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization ($\approx \mathbf{2.257 \times 10^6}\ \text{J/kg}$)

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Thermal Moisture Analyzer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Logic (the 6 steps that examiners love to draw in flowcharts):**

1. **Weighing Stage:** A clean, dry, empty sample pan is tared on a precision balance (typical readability: $0.0001\ \text{g}$ for lab, $0.001\ \text{g}$ for industrial).
2. **Sample Loading:** A representative sample ($2\ \text{g}$ to $10\ \text{g}$ typically) is uniformly spread on the pan to a thin layer ($\leq 2\ \text{mm}$) to maximize surface area.
3. **Heating Stage:** The sample is exposed to one of the thermal energy sources (convection, IR, MW, or vacuum).
4. **Mass Tracking:** The balance continuously logs the mass in real-time using a load cell or electromagnetic force restoration mechanism.
5. **Endpoint Detection:** Drying is terminated when the mass loss rate falls below a programmed threshold (e.g., $\leq 1\ \text{mg}$ change over $30\ \text{s}$ — called the "**auto-stop criterion**").
6. **Computation:** The instrument computes moisture content using the gravimetric difference.

---

## 2.3 Classification of Thermal Drying Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently asks: "Compare any two methods of moisture determination" — keep this table crystal clear.**

| Method | Energy Transfer Mechanism | Typical Drying Time | Typical Temp Range | Sample Form |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot-Air Convection Oven** | Convection (forced air) | 3–16 hours | $100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ – $160\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Granules, powders, sheets |
| **Vacuum Oven** | Conduction + reduced-pressure boiling | 4–24 hours | $60\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ – $100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Heat-sensitive, oils, pharma |
| **Infrared (IR) Moisture Balance** | Radiation ($1\ \mu\text{m}$ – $1000\ \mu\text{m}$) | 5 – 20 minutes | $100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ – $200\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Powders, pastes, food |
| **Microwave (MW) Moisture Balance** | Dielectric heating (volumetric) | 2 – 10 minutes | Internal heating up to $150\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Bulk solids, food, soil |
| **Thermogravimetric Analyzer (TGA)** | Programmed heating + mass log | 15 – 60 minutes | $25\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ – $1000\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Research, polymers, minerals |

---

## 2.4 Detailed Sub-Topic: Infrared (IR) Moisture Balance — Working Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU's most-asked thermal method in Module 4 — be thorough here.**

An **Infrared Moisture Analyzer** consists of:
- A **halogen/quartz IR emitter** (wavelength $\approx 1.0\ \mu\text{m}$ to $5.0\ \mu\text{m}$)
- A **precision weighing system** (typically a load cell or electromagnetic force balance)
- A **sample pan** (aluminum, $\varnothing 90\ \text{mm}$ typical)
- A **microprocessor** for data logging and auto-endpoint detection
- A **display unit** showing %MC, mass, drying curve

**Working Principle:**
The IR radiation penetrates the surface of the sample, is absorbed, and converted to heat. The water molecules absorb IR strongly (water has a strong absorption band near $3\ \mu\text{m}$ and $6\ \mu\text{m}$), causing rapid internal heating and vaporization. As water leaves, the sample mass decreases — the balance tracks this in real time.

---

## 2.5 Detailed Sub-Topic: Microwave Moisture Meter — Working Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **The Microwave method is special because it heats the sample *volumetrically* — meaning the entire bulk heats at once, not just the surface.**

- **Frequency used:** typically $2450\ \text{MHz}$ (ISM band), the same as a domestic microwave oven.
- **Dielectric heating mechanism:** Polar water molecules attempt to align with the rapidly alternating electric field. The friction between rotating dipoles generates heat *throughout* the bulk simultaneously.
- **Measurement types:**
  - **Attenuation type:** measures the loss of microwave power transmitted through the sample.
  - **Resonance / Shift type:** measures the shift in resonant frequency of a cavity loaded with the sample.
  - **Capacitive-type strip-line:** microwave passes through a strip-line; the change in impedance correlates to moisture.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Formula Sheet — memorize every entry below. Each formula has appeared in past papers.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture Content (Wet Basis) | $\%\text{wb} = \dfrac{m_w - m_d}{m_w} \times 100$ | dimensionless (%) | $m_w$ = wet mass, $m_d$ = dry mass |
| 2 | Moisture Content (Dry Basis) | $\%\text{db} = \dfrac{m_w - m_d}{m_d} \times 100$ | dimensionless (%) | Used in drying-rate engineering |
| 3 | Conversion: db → wb | $\%\text{wb} = \dfrac{\%\text{db}}{100 + \%\text{db}} \times 100$ | dimensionless (%) | Critical conversion |
| 4 | Conversion: wb → db | $\%\text{db} = \dfrac{\%\text{wb}}{100 - \%\text{wb}} \times 100$ | dimensionless (%) | Critical conversion |
| 5 | Mass of Water Removed | $m_{\text{water}} = m_w - m_d$ | g or kg | Direct gravimetric difference |
| 6 | Drying Rate | $N = -\dfrac{dm}{A \cdot dt}$ | kg/(m²·s) | Negative sign: mass decreases |
| 7 | Equilibrium Moisture Content | $M_{eq} = M_t \quad \text{at} \quad \dfrac{dm}{dt}=0$ | kg water/kg dry | Used in sorption isotherms |
| 8 | Latent Heat Requirement | $Q_L = m_w \cdot L_v$ | J | $L_v = 2.257\ \text{MJ/kg}$ at $100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| 9 | Time to Reach Constant Mass (Fick's law) | $t \approx \dfrac{x^2}{\pi^2 D_{AB}}$ | s | $x$ = sample thickness, $D_{AB}$ = diffusivity |
| 10 | Free Moisture Content | $M_f = M_t - M_{eq}$ | kg water/kg dry | Drives the drying process |
| 11 | Bone-Dry Mass Check (TGA) | Slope of TGA curve when $\le 0.1\%/\text{min}$ | — | Auto-stop criterion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Food industry:** moisture in grains, milk powder, snacks directly determines shelf-life and microbial growth.
> - **Pharma:** moisture in tablets and APIs affects stability, dissolution, and degradation kinetics.
> - **Cement & concrete:** water-to-cement ratio controls the strength of cured concrete.
> - **Paper industry:** moisture in pulp sheets affects calendering and printing quality.
> - **Soil mechanics:** moisture content governs bearing capacity, compaction, and slope stability.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Derivation of the Wet-Basis Moisture Equation

**Starting Premise:**
The moisture content is defined as the **ratio of the mass of water to the total mass of the wet sample**, expressed as a percentage.

**Step 1 — Identify the wet mass:**
The wet mass $m_w$ is the total mass of the sample as received (solid + water). Mathematically:

$$m_w = m_{\text{solid}} + m_{\text{water}}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the mass of water:**
When the sample is dried to constant mass, the remaining mass is the bone-dry solid $m_d = m_{\text{solid}}$. Therefore, the mass of water removed during drying is the difference:

$$m_{\text{water}} = m_w - m_d$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio (wet basis):**
By definition:

$$\%\text{wb} = \frac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_w} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expression for $m_{\text{water}}$:**

$$\%\text{wb} = \frac{m_w - m_d}{m_w} \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\%\text{wb} = \left(1 - \frac{m_d}{m_w}\right) \times 100}$$

> **Interpretation:** Notice the elegant equivalent form $\%\text{wb} = \left(1 - m_d/m_w\right) \times 100$ — this means the moisture content is the **fraction of mass lost** upon drying. KTU examiners sometimes give you the dry/wet mass ratio and expect you to use this shortcut.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Derivation of the Dry-Basis Moisture Equation

**Starting Premise:**
On a dry basis, moisture is expressed per unit mass of the **bone-dry solid**, not the original wet mass.

**Step 1 — Mass of water (same as before):**

$$m_{\text{water}} = m_w - m_d$$

**Step 2 — Reference mass is now $m_d$ (not $m_w$):**

$$\%\text{db} = \frac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_d} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$\%\text{db} = \frac{m_w - m_d}{m_d} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\%\text{db} = \left(\frac{m_w}{m_d} - 1\right) \times 100}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion Between Wet-Basis and Dry-Basis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a guaranteed 3–4 mark derivation in KTU ESE. Memorize the logic, not just the final result.**

**Given:**
$$\%\text{wb} = \frac{m_w - m_d}{m_w} \times 100 \quad \text{and} \quad \%\text{db} = \frac{m_w - m_d}{m_d} \times 100$$

**Step 1 — Form the ratio of the two:**

$$\frac{\%\text{db}}{\%\text{wb}} = \frac{(m_w - m_d)/m_d}{(m_w - m_d)/m_w} = \frac{m_w}{m_d}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel the common term $(m_w - m_d)$:**

$$\frac{\%\text{db}}{\%\text{wb}} = \frac{m_w}{m_d}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\%\text{wb}$ in terms of $\%\text{db}$:**

$$\%\text{wb} = \frac{\%\text{db}}{m_w / m_d}$$

**Step 4 — Use Step 2 to eliminate $m_w/m_d$:**

$$\%\text{wb} = \frac{\%\text{db}}{\%\text{db} / \%\text{wb}}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-substitute the ratio from Step 2:**

Since $m_w/m_d = 1 + \%\text{db}/100$, we get:

$$\%\text{wb} = \frac{\%\text{db}}{1 + \%\text{db}/100}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator by 100 to clear fractions:**

$$\boxed{\%\text{wb} = \frac{\%\text{db}}{100 + \%\text{db}} \times 100}$$

**Step 7 — Similarly invert to get db from wb:**

$$\boxed{\%\text{db} = \frac{\%\text{wb}}{100 - \%\text{wb}} \times 100}$$

> **Sanity Check (numerical):** If $\%\text{db} = 25$, then $\%\text{wb} = 25/125 \times 100 = 20\%$. ✓ This makes physical sense — dry basis is always *higher* than wet basis.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Drying Rate and the Constant-Rate Period

> [!NOTE]
> **From chemical engineering drying theory — directly relevant to industrial dryers.**

**Step 1 — Define the drying rate $N$:**

$$N = -\frac{dm}{A \cdot dt}$$

The negative sign indicates mass decreases with time.

**Step 2 — In the constant-rate period, the rate is governed by external mass transfer:**

$$N = k_y \cdot (H_s - H_a)$$

Where $k_y$ is the mass-transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s·$\Delta y$), $H_s$ is the humidity at the surface, and $H_a$ is the humidity of the bulk air.

**Step 3 — In the falling-rate period, moisture movement inside the solid (Fickian diffusion) dominates:**

$$N = -\frac{\rho_s \cdot D_{AB}}{x} \cdot \frac{dM}{dx}$$

Where $D_{AB}$ is the effective moisture diffusivity (m²/s) and $x$ is the position inside the solid.

**Step 4 — Integrating Fick's second law with the boundary condition $M(x, 0) = M_0$ and $M(x, t) = M_{eq}$ at the surface gives:**

$$\frac{M_t - M_{eq}}{M_0 - M_{eq}} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D_{AB} t}{4 L^2}\right]$$

For long times, the first term dominates, giving the asymptotic form:

$$\boxed{\frac{M_t - M_{eq}}{M_0 - M_{eq}} \approx \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\left[-\frac{\pi^2 D_{AB} t}{4 L^2}\right]}$$

Where $L$ is the half-thickness of the slab.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 1 (KTU-style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024 model]:**
> A wet sample of a pharmaceutical powder weighs $12.450\ \text{g}$. After drying in a hot-air oven at $105\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 4 hours, the mass stabilizes at $11.205\ \text{g}$. Calculate the moisture content on (i) wet basis and (ii) dry basis.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

$$m_w = 12.450\ \text{g}, \quad m_d = 11.205\ \text{g}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of water removed:**

$$m_{\text{water}} = m_w - m_d = 12.450 - 11.205 = 1.245\ \text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Moisture on wet basis:**

$$\%\text{wb} = \frac{1.245}{12.450} \times 100 = 10.0\%$$

**Step 4 — Moisture on dry basis:**

$$\%\text{db} = \frac{1.245}{11.205} \times 100 = 11.11\%$$

**Step 5 — Verification using conversion formula:**

$$\%\text{wb} = \frac{11.11}{100 + 11.11} \times 100 = \frac{11.11}{111.11} \times 100 = 10.0\%\ \checkmark$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 2 (KTU-style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 model]:**
> A grain sample has a moisture content of $14\%\ \text{wb}$. Express this on a dry basis. If a $500\ \text{kg}$ lot of this grain is dried to $8\%\ \text{wb}$, calculate the mass of water removed.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert $14\%\ \text{wb}$ to $\%\text{db}$:**

$$\%\text{db} = \frac{14}{100 - 14} \times 100 = \frac{14}{86} \times 100 = 16.28\%\ \text{db}$$

**Step 2 — Original mass of water in $500\ \text{kg}$ at $14\%\ \text{wb}$:**

$$m_{\text{water, initial}} = 0.14 \times 500 = 70\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of water in the dried lot at $8\%\ \text{wb}$:**

After drying, let the new wet mass be $m_w'$. Then:

$$0.08 = \frac{m_w' - m_d}{m_w'}$$

The dry mass $m_d$ is unchanged at $500 - 70 = 430\ \text{kg}$.

**Step 4 — Solve for $m_w'$:**

$$0.08 \, m_w' = m_w' - 430 \implies 430 = m_w' (1 - 0.08) = 0.92 \, m_w'$$

$$m_w' = \frac{430}{0.92} = 467.39\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Final moisture mass:**

$$m_{\text{water, final}} = 0.08 \times 467.39 = 37.39\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 6 — Water removed:**

$$\Delta m_{\text{water}} = 70 - 37.39 = 32.61\ \text{kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative shortcut method:** Some students use the **conservation of dry solid** to bypass step 4:
> $m_d = m_w (1 - \%\text{wb}/100)$
> $m_{d,\text{initial}} = 500 \times (1 - 0.14) = 430\ \text{kg}$
> $m_{w,\text{final}} = m_d / (1 - \%\text{wb}/100) = 430 / 0.92 = 467.39\ \text{kg}$
> Water removed = $500 - 467.39 = 32.61\ \text{kg}$ ✓

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks: "Which method is suitable for heat-sensitive materials?" The matrix below is the single best answer.**

| Sample Type | Recommended Method | Why | Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat-sensitive pharma | **Vacuum oven** | Lowers boiling point, no thermal degradation | Conventional oven at high T |
| Bulk soil/coal | **Microwave balance** | Volumetric heating, fast, penetrates bulk | Convection oven (too slow) |
| Food grains (rice, wheat) | **Convection oven at $103\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$** | ISO standard, regulatory acceptance | IR (non-uniform for thick layers) |
| Plastic pellets | **Infrared balance** | Fast, surface heating suitable for polymers | Vacuum (long time) |
| Volatile oils, fats | **Vacuum oven at $70\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$** | Prevents loss of volatile components | Conventional oven |
| Powders with sugar coating | **Infrared with halogen lamp** | Uniform IR absorption | Microwave (can char sugars) |
