# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic Thermal Moisture Analyzer

The figure below maps the **functional architecture** of a generic thermal drying moisture measurement system. It shows how the physical subsystems interact to produce the final moisture reading.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sampleInput["SAMPLE INPUT\nWet Material on Pan"] --> weighingUnit["PRECISION WEIGHING UNIT\nLoad Cell or EMFR Balance\nReadability 0.0001 g"]
    weighingUnit --> dataAcq["DATA ACQUISITION MODULE\nMicrocontroller + ADC\n16 bit Resolution"]
    heatingUnit["THERMAL ENERGY SOURCE\nIR Lamp / Microwave\nor Convection Element"] --> sampleInput
    heatingUnit --> tempCtrl["TEMPERATURE CONTROLLER\nPID Loop with Feedback\nSet Point 100 to 200 C"]
    tempCtrl --> heatingUnit
    dataAcq --> endpointLogic["ENDPOINT DETECTION LOGIC\nAuto Stop Criterion\nRate below 1 mg per 30 s"]
    endpointLogic --> displayUnit["DISPLAY AND OUTPUT\nLCD / Touch HMI\nRS 232 / USB / 4 to 20 mA"]
    dataAcq --> displayUnit
    endpointLogic --> resultCompute["MOISTURE COMPUTATION\nEngine\nWet Basis and Dry Basis"]
    resultCompute --> displayUnit
    displayUnit --> finalOutput["FINAL MOISTURE PERCENT\nDisplayed on HMI\nand Transmitted to PLC"]
    housing["ENCLOSURE\nDrying Chamber with\nVented Hood"] -.contains.-> sampleInput
    housing -.contains.-> heatingUnit
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **precision weighing unit** continuously measures the sample mass. The **thermal energy source** drives water out. The **temperature controller** regulates heat via a PID loop. The **endpoint detection logic** decides when drying is complete. The **moisture computation engine** applies the wet-basis and dry-basis formulas. The **display unit** presents the final result on an HMI and transmits it via industrial protocols (RS-232, USB, or 4–20 mA) to a PLC or DCS.

---

## 4.2 Comparison Flowchart — Choosing a Thermal Drying Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["START\nSample to be analyzed"] --> q1{"Is sample\nheat sensitive?"}
    q1 -- YES --> vac["VACUUM OVEN\nLow Temp Reduced Pressure\n60 to 100 C"]
    q1 -- NO --> q2{"Is sample\npowder or paste?"}
    q2 -- YES --> ir["INFRARED MOISTURE BALANCE\nFast 5 to 20 min\nHalogen Lamp 1 to 5 micron"]
    q2 -- NO --> q3{"Is sample\nbulk solid or thick layer?"}
    q3 -- YES --> mw["MICROWAVE MOISTURE METER\nVolumetric Heating 2450 MHz\n2 to 10 min"]
    q3 -- NO --> conv["CONVECTION OVEN\nStandard ISO Method\n3 to 16 hours at 103 C"]
    vac --> endNode["END\nMoisture Value Reported"]
    ir --> endNode
    mw --> endNode
    conv --> endNode
```

> **How to use this diagram in the exam:** When a KTU question says *"Recommend a suitable thermal drying method for a heat-sensitive pharmaceutical powder with rapid throughput requirement,"* trace the path: start → *heat sensitive?* → **YES** → *but check if you also need speed* → in practice you would route to the IR path with low-temperature mode. Always defend your choice with a one-line reason.

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Inside a Convection Oven Moisture Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Clean and Dry\nEmpty Aluminum Pan"] --> B["Step 2: Tare the\nPrecision Balance"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Weigh Wet Sample\nRecord mw = 12.450 g"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Place in Oven\nSet 103 C for 4 hours"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Cool in Desiccator\nfor 30 minutes"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Reweigh\nRecord md = 11.205 g"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Apply Formula\nPercent wb = mw minus md\nover mw times 100"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Report 10.0 percent wb\nand 11.11 percent db"]
```

> **Exam tip:** A KTU question worth 7 marks often asks for *both* a labeled diagram and a procedure. The flowchart above gives you a clean 7-mark answer: 3 marks for the diagram and 4 marks for the procedural steps.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Microwave Moisture Meter (Attenuation Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    osc["OSCILLATOR\n2450 MHz\nGunn Diode Source"] --> iso["ISOLATOR\nPrevents Reflection Damage"]
    iso --> atten["ATTENUATOR\nCalibrated 0 to 60 dB"]
    atten --> tx["TRANSMIT ANTENNA\nHorn or Strip Line"]
    tx --> sample["SAMPLE CHAMBER\nWet Material on Conveyor\nor in Cavity"]
    sample --> rx["RECEIVE ANTENNA\nHorns at Fixed Distance"]
    rx --> detector["DETECTOR DIODE\nSchottky or Point Contact"]
    detector --> amp["SIGNAL AMPLIFIER\nLogarithmic Amplifier"]
    amp --> comp["SIGNAL COMPARATOR\nReference vs Measured"]
    comp --> output["OUTPUT\nMoisture Percent on HMI\n4 to 20 mA to PLC"]
    comp --> ctrl["FEEDBACK TO\nOSCILLATOR POWER\nLevel for Auto Calibration"]
    ctrl -.-> osc
```

> **Physics inside the box:** The microwave beam passes through the wet sample. Water molecules absorb microwave energy, attenuating the beam. The **degree of attenuation** is a direct function of moisture content. The logarithmic amplifier linearizes the exponential absorption (Beer–Lambert-like behavior). The comparator subtracts the reference signal (dry sample) to isolate the moisture-induced loss.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Direct, definition-level, one-paragraph or one-formula answer. Each answer should take ~3 minutes in the exam hall.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 model]`

**Define the term "moisture content" of a material. Differentiate between wet-basis and dry-basis moisture content with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

Moisture content of a material is the **quantitative measure of water present in the substance**, usually expressed as a percentage of the total or dry mass.

- **Wet Basis (%wb):** Moisture expressed as a percentage of the **original wet mass** of the sample.
$$\%\text{wb} = \dfrac{m_w - m_d}{m_w} \times 100$$
*Example:* Food industry reports grain moisture as $14\%\ \text{wb}$ — meaning $14\ \text{g}$ of every $100\ \text{g}$ of grain is water.

- **Dry Basis (%db):** Moisture expressed as a percentage of the **bone-dry solid mass** of the sample.
$$\%\text{db} = \dfrac{m_w - m_d}{m_d} \times 100$$
*Example:* Chemical engineering drying calculations use %db because it remains invariant when the sample mass changes during drying (the dry solid mass is constant).

**Key Difference:** For the same sample, %db is **always numerically greater than %wb** because the denominator ($m_d$) is smaller than $m_w$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 model]`

**List and briefly explain any three thermal methods used for moisture content determination.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hot-Air Convection Oven Method:** A weighed sample is placed in a thermostatically controlled oven at a standard temperature (e.g., $103\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ for food, $105\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ for general materials) and dried to constant mass. The moisture is calculated from the mass loss. Slow but highly accurate and the **regulatory reference method**.

2. **Infrared (IR) Moisture Balance Method:** The sample is exposed to radiation from a halogen or quartz IR emitter. Water absorbs IR strongly (especially at $3\ \mu\text{m}$), causing rapid surface and near-surface heating. The mass is monitored continuously; endpoint is detected automatically when the rate of mass loss drops below a threshold. Analysis time: **5–20 minutes**.

3. **Microwave Moisture Meter Method:** The sample is irradiated with $2450\ \text{MHz}$ microwaves. Polar water molecules rotate with the field, generating volumetric heat. The moisture is inferred from either the **attenuation** of the transmitted beam or the **shift in resonant frequency** of a cavity. Very fast (2–10 min) and excellent for bulk materials.

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Working Principle of IR Moisture Balance

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐
│  IR Lamp    │ ──► │  Sample Pan  │ ──► │  Load Cell   │
│ (Halogen)   │     │  (Aluminum)  │     │ (Weighing)   │
└─────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────┬───────┘
        │                   │                    │
        ▼                   ▼                    ▼
┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐
│ Temperature │     │  Drying      │     │  Micro-      │
│ Controller  │     │  Chamber     │     │  controller  │
│  (PID)      │     │  (Vented)    │     │  + Display   │
└─────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────────┘
```

**Working Principle:**
1. A weighed sample is placed on the aluminum pan inside the drying chamber.
2. A **halogen IR lamp** emits infrared radiation in the wavelength range $1\ \mu\text{m}$ to $5\ \mu\text{m}$.
3. Water molecules absorb IR strongly (water has strong absorption bands near $3\ \mu\text{m}$ and $6\ \mu\text{m}$), causing the sample to heat up rapidly.
4. The water evaporates and is vented out of the chamber by a small fan.
5. The **load cell** continuously measures the decreasing mass of the sample.
6. The microcontroller records mass vs time, plots a drying curve, and detects the **endpoint** when the mass loss rate drops below $1\ \text{mg}$ per $30\ \text{s}$.
7. The instrument then displays the moisture content directly in %wb or %db.

**Advantages over Hot-Air Oven:**
- **Speed:** 5–20 minutes vs 3–16 hours.
- **Real-time curve:** The user sees the drying profile and can verify endpoint.
- **Smaller sample:** Requires only 1–10 g vs 5–50 g.
- **No desiccator cooling needed** before final weighing.
- **Automatic endpoint detection** removes operator bias.

**Limitations:**
- IR does not penetrate deep — only surface and near-surface heating. Thick samples may show a moisture gradient.
- **Surface overheating** can char or degrade heat-sensitive materials.
- Higher instrument cost.
- Less universally accepted for regulatory compliance compared to the convection oven reference method.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
$$m_w = 85.6\ \text{g}, \quad m_d = 72.4\ \text{g}$$

**Step 1 — Mass of water removed:**

$$m_{\text{water}} = 85.6 - 72.4 = 13.2\ \text{g}$$

**Step 2 — Moisture on wet basis:**

$$\%\text{wb} = \frac{13.2}{85.6} \times 100 = 15.42\%$$

**Step 3 — Moisture on dry basis:**

$$\%\text{db} = \frac{13.2}{72.4} \times 100 = 18.23\%$$

**Step 4 — Convert datasheet value $20\%\ \text{db}$ to %wb:**

$$\%\text{wb, datasheet} = \frac{20}{100 + 20} \times 100 = \frac{20}{120} \times 100 = 16.67\%$$

**Step 5 — Discrepancy:**

$$\Delta\ \%\text{wb} = \vert 16.67 - 15.42 \vert = 1.25\%\ \text{wb}$$

> **Interpretation:** Our measured value is $1.25\%\ \text{wb}$ lower than the datasheet — a noticeable discrepancy that could arise from non-uniform sampling, different drying temperatures, or incomplete drying in the IR balance.

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Microwave Moisture Meter (Attenuation Type)

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
┌────────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌────────────┐
│ Oscillator │ ──►│Isolator  │ ──►│Attenuator│ ──►│ Transmit   │
│ 2450 MHz   │    │          │    │          │    │ Antenna    │
└────────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └─────┬──────┘
                                                         │
                                                         ▼
                                                   ┌──────────┐
                                                   │  Sample  │
                                                   │ Chamber  │
                                                   └────┬─────┘
                                                        │
                                                        ▼
┌────────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌────────────┐
│  Display   │ ◄──│Comparator│ ◄──│Amplifier │ ◄──│  Receive   │
│  HMI       │    │          │    │(Log)     │    │  Antenna   │
└────────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └────────────┘
```

**Working Principle:**

1. A **Gunn diode oscillator** generates a stable microwave signal at **$2450\ \text{MHz}$** (the ISM band reserved worldwide for industrial, scientific, and medical applications).
2. The signal passes through an **isolator** (to protect the source from reflected power) and a **calibrated attenuator**.
3. The **transmit antenna** (horn or strip-line) directs the beam through the **sample chamber** where the wet material is placed.
4. As the microwave beam passes through the wet material, the polar water molecules absorb the energy, causing **attenuation** of the beam.
5. The **receive antenna** collects the attenuated beam, and a **Schottky detector diode** converts it to a DC voltage.
6. A **logarithmic amplifier** linearizes the signal (because attenuation in dB is logarithmic with moisture).
7. A **comparator** subtracts a reference (dry sample) signal to isolate the moisture-induced attenuation.
8. The result is displayed as % moisture and/or transmitted as a 4–20 mA signal.

**Why 2450 MHz?**
- It is the **international ISM band** — unlicensed, dedicated for industrial use.
- At this frequency, the **penetration depth** in wet materials is a few centimeters — ideal for bulk materials.
- The dipole relaxation frequency of water is around $20\ \text{GHz}$, but at $2450\ \text{MHz}$ the absorption is still strong enough for sensitive measurement while allowing deeper penetration.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Volumetric heating** — heat is generated throughout the bulk, not just at the surface.
2. **Very fast** — typical analysis time is 2–10 minutes.
3. **Non-contact measurement possible** — the sample does not need to touch the sensor.
4. **Suitable for online, continuous monitoring** on conveyor belts.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (Pharma Drying)

**Given:**
- Initial wet mass $M_{\text{initial}} = 2\ \text{tonnes} = 2000\ \text{kg}$
- Initial moisture $= 28\%\ \text{wb}$
- Final required moisture $= 12\%\ \text{wb}$
- $L_v = 2257\ \text{kJ/kg}$

**Step 1 — Mass of dry API in the initial $2000\ \text{kg}$:**

$$M_d = M_{\text{initial}} \times (1 - 0.28) = 2000 \times 0.72 = 1440\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of water initially present:**

$$M_{\text{water, initial}} = 0.28 \times 2000 = 560\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Final wet mass at $12\%\ \text{wb}$:**

$$\%\text{wb} = \frac{M_{\text{water, final}}}{M_{\text{final}}} \times 100$$

With $M_d = 1440\ \text{kg}$ constant, $M_{\text{water, final}} = 0.12 \times M_{\text{final}}$, and $M_{\text{final}} = M_d + M_{\text{water, final}}$:

$$M_{\text{final}} = \frac{M_d}{1 - 0.12} = \frac{1440}{0.88} = 1636.36\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of water to be removed:**

$$\Delta M_{\text{water}} = M_{\text{water, initial}} - M_{\text{water, final}} = 560 - 0.12 \times 1636.36 = 560 - 196.36 = 363.64\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Energy required for evaporation (sensible heat neglected):**

$$Q = \Delta M_{\text{water}} \times L_v = 363.64 \times 2257 = 820,725.48\ \text{kJ} \approx 820.7\ \text{MJ}$$

> **Final Answers:**
> - (i) Water to be removed = **$363.64\ \text{kg}$**
> - (ii) Dry API obtained = **$1440\ \text{kg}$** (also = final wet mass $1636.36\ \text{kg}$ — same substance, just different reporting)
> - (iii) Latent heat energy = **$\approx 820.7\ \text{MJ}$**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the basis:** Reporting a wet-basis answer when the question asked for dry basis (or vice versa) → **2 marks deducted silently**.
> 2. **Wrong conversion formula:** Confusing $\%\text{db} = \%\text{wb}/(100 - \%\text{wb})$ with $\%\text{db} = \%\text{wb}/(100 + \%\text{wb})$ → entire problem zero.
> 3. **In pharma/cement problems, NOT keeping dry mass constant:** Students mistakenly assume the dry mass changes during drying. It does not. The dry mass is invariant; only the water leaves.
> 4. **Sign convention error in drying rate:** Forgetting the negative sign in $N = -dm/(A \cdot dt)$ → "negative drying rate" — physically meaningless.
> 5. **Skipping units in IR/MW diagrams:** If you draw a block diagram without labelling the IR wavelength or microwave frequency, you lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ Moisture content is determined by **mass loss upon heating** — it is a **gravimetric, absolute method**.
- ✅ **Wet basis** uses $m_w$ in the denominator; **dry basis** uses $m_d$ in the denominator.
- ✅ **%db is always numerically greater than %wb** for the same sample.
- ✅ Standard oven temperature: **$103\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ for food**, **$105\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ for general**, **$60\text{–}100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ for vacuum** (heat-sensitive).
- ✅ **IR lamps** emit in the $1\ \mu\text{m}$ to $5\ \mu\text{m}$ range — water absorbs strongly at $3\ \mu\text{m}$.
- ✅ **Microwave frequency** is **$2450\ \text{MHz}$** (ISM band) — causes dielectric heating of polar water molecules.
- ✅ **Vacuum ovens** lower the boiling point of water → ideal for heat-sensitive materials.
- ✅ **Endpoint detection** uses a rate-based criterion (e.g., $\le 1\ \text{mg}$ change in $30\ \text{s}$).
- ✅ Latent heat of vaporization of water: **$L_v = 2.257\ \text{MJ/kg}$** at $100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- ✅ In drying calculations, **dry mass $m_d$ is conserved** — water is the only mass that leaves.
- ✅ Conversion formulae (must memorize both directions):
  - $\%\text{wb} = \%\text{db} / (100 + \%\text{db}) \times 100$
  - $\%\text{db} = \%\text{wb} / (100 - \%\text{wb}) \times 100$
- ✅ **TGA** (Thermogravimetric Analyzer) is the most precise thermal method — used in research and quality control.
- ✅ **IR is fast but may char surfaces**; **MW is volumetric but may overheat bulk**; **vacuum is gentle but slow**; **convection is the regulatory reference but slow**.
- ✅ In numericals, always state the **basis** explicitly in the final answer line.
- ✅ The drying curve has three regions: **(i) initial transient, (ii) constant-rate period, (iii) falling-rate period** — a frequent viva question.
- ✅ For the **equilibrium moisture content** $M_{eq}$: this is the moisture a material retains in equilibrium with the surrounding air humidity. It is **not** zero, even after long drying.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Moisture Content Determination by Thermal Drying**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | OEBBT724 — Industrial Instrumentation*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruments for Measuring Humidity: Hygrometer, Psychrometer, and Dew Point Apparatus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Instruments for Measuring Humidity: Hygrometer, Psychrometer, and Dew Point Apparatus ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Definition of Humidity (The Measured Variable) **Humidity** is the measure of the amount of water vapor present in a gaseous environment (typically air). It is a critical process variable in industries such as **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning)**, pharmaceutical manufacturing, food processing, textile production, paper manufacturing, and meteorological stations. The three primary forms of humidity used in industrial measurement are: 1. **Absolute Humidity (AH):** The actual mass of water vapor present per unit volume of the air-gas mixture. Units: **grams per cubic meter (g/m³)** or **kg/m³**. 2. **Relative Humidity (RH):** The ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor in the air to the saturation vapor pressure at the same temperature. Expressed as a **percentage (%)**. 3. **Dew Point Temperature:** The temperature at which air becomes saturated (100% RH) when cooled at constant pressure and constant water vapor content. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme Module 4 specifically mandates the study of *Hygrometers* (mechanical and electrical types), *Psychrometers* (wet-dry bulb principle), and *Dew Point Apparatus*. Students must be able to draw, label, explain working, and derive governing equations for these devices. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the atmosphere is like a **sponge**. A dry sponge can soak up a lot of water; a wet sponge cannot. Similarly, warm air can "hold" more water vapor than cold air. The *capacity* of air to hold moisture changes with temperature. - A **Hygrometer** is like a *smart sponge sensor* — it physically changes (length, resistance, capacitance) when it absorbs moisture from the air. We read this change. - A **Psychrometer** is like *two thermometers comparing notes* — one covered in water (wet bulb) and one dry. Because evaporation cools the wet bulb, the difference between the two temperatures tells us how dry the air is. - A **Dew Point Apparatus** is like *breathing on a cold mirror* — we keep cooling a surface until water just begins to condense. That condensation temperature is the dew point. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition:** Relative Humidity $\phi$ is mathematically defined as the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor ($p_w$) to the saturation pressure of water vapor ($p_{ws}$) at the same temperature. The standard temperature for reference is the **dry-bulb temperature (DBT)**. --- ### 1.3 Engineering Significance | Parameter | Standard Value/Range | Industrial Importance | |-----------|--------------------|-----------------------| | Relative Humidity (RH) | 0% to 100% | Comfort control, drying processes | | Absolute Humidity (AH) | 0 to ~30 g/m³ | Process gas calculations, drying | | Dew Point | -80°C to +60°C | Compressed air quality, cryogenics | | Mixing Ratio | 0 to ~40 g/kg | Meteorological computations | The **relative humidity** is the most commonly measured parameter because it directly correlates to human comfort, material behavior, and the rate of evaporation/condensation in industrial processes. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Humidity Measuring Instruments Humidity instruments are broadly classified into three engineering families: **(A) Hygrometers** — Direct reading devices using moisture-sensitive materials. - *Mechanical Hygrometers* (Hair, Goldbeater's skin, organic fibers) - *Electrical Hygrometers* (Resistive, Capacitive) - *Gravimetric Hygrometer* (Absolute reference standard) **(B) Psychrometers** — Two-thermometer comparative devices. - *Sling Psychrometer* - *Aspirated (Assmann) Psychrometer* **(C) Dew Point Apparatus** — Saturation-based devices. - *Dew Point Mirror Hygrometer* (Regnault's & Dewcel types) --- ### 2.2 Hygrometer — Deep Working Analysis #### 2.2.1 Hair Hygrometer (Mechanical Type) **Construction:** A bundle of degreased human hair (or synthetic fiber) is held under slight tension by a spring. One end is fixed; the other end is connected to a lever/pulley mechanism that drives a pointer on a calibrated dial. **Operating Principle:** Human hair is a hygroscopic material. Its length increases when it absorbs moisture and decreases when it dries. The change in length is approximately **linear with RH** between 5% and 95% RH. **Why it works:** Hair fibers consist of keratin proteins. Water molecules penetrate between the protein chains, causing them to separate slightly, lengthening the fiber. **Equation of motion (calibration):** $$ L = L_0 \left[ 1 + k \cdot \phi \right] $$ Where: - $L$ = length of hair at humidity $\phi$ - $L_0$ = length of hair at 0% RH - $k$ = hygroscopic coefficient (≈ 0.025 for human hair) - $\phi$ = relative humidity (fraction, 0 to 1) #### 2.2.2 Capacitive Hygrometer (Electrical Type — Most Important for KTU) **Construction:** A thin film of hygroscopic polymer (such as polyimide or cellulose acetate) is sandwiched between two conductive electrodes. Often the upper electrode is porous gold to allow water vapor to penetrate. **Operating Principle:** The dielectric constant of the polymer changes with moisture absorption. Since $\varepsilon_{\text{water}} \approx 80$ and $\varepsilon_{\text{polymer}} \approx 3$, even small moisture uptake significantly changes the capacitance. **Capacitance Equation:** $$ C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} $$ Where: - $C$ = capacitance (Farads) - $\varepsilon_0$ = **vacuum permittivity = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** - $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of the moist polymer (function of RH) - $A$ = electrode area (m²) - $d$ = dielectric thickness (m) **Output Signal:** Change in capacitance (typically 100 pF to 500 pF range), measured using a bridge circuit or oscillator. #### 2.2.3 Resistive (Electrolytic) Hygrometer Uses a salt (e.g., LiCl) whose electrical resistance varies inversely with humidity. As humidity rises, the salt absorbs more water, ionic mobility increases, resistance drops. $$ R = R_0 \cdot e^{-\beta \cdot \phi} $$ Where $\beta$ is a material constant. A small AC excitation (typically 1 kHz) is applied to prevent DC polarization of the salt. --- ### 2.3 Psychrometer — Deep Working Analysis (Wet and Dry Bulb) **Construction:** Two identical mercury-in-glass (or thermistor) temperature sensors placed side by side. - **Dry Bulb:** Exposed directly to the air; measures the **actual air temperature**. - **Wet Bulb:** The bulb is wrapped in a muslin wick (muslin cloth) kept wet with distilled water. **Operating Principle:** If the surrounding air is unsaturated, water evaporates from the wick. The latent heat required for evaporation is drawn from the wet bulb itself, cooling it below the dry bulb temperature. This temperature difference is called the **Wet Bulb Depression (WBD)**. **Heat Balance at Steady State (Carried Out by KTU Examiners — High Yield):** At equilibrium, heat lost by evaporation = heat gained by convection from the surrounding air. $$ h_c (T_{db} - T_{wb}) = h_d (p_{ws,wb} - p_w) \cdot M_w $$ Where: - $h_c$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) - $h_d$ = mass transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s·Pa) - $T_{db}, T_{wb}$ = dry bulb and wet bulb temperatures (K or °C) - $p_{ws,wb}$ = saturation pressure at wet-bulb temperature - $p_w$ = partial pressure of water vapor in the air - $M_w$ = latent heat of vaporization of water ≈ **2454 kJ/kg at 20°C** **The Sprung Psychrometric Equation (Final Form):** $$ p_w = p_{ws,wb} - A \cdot P \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb}) $$ Where: - $P$ = total atmospheric pressure (Pa) - $A$ = psychrometric constant ≈ **6.66 × 10⁻⁴ K⁻¹** for standard conditions - $p_{ws,wb}$ = saturation vapor pressure at $T_{wb}$ **Relative Humidity from Psychrometer:** $$ \phi = \frac{p_w}{p_{ws,db}} = \frac{p_{ws,wb} - A \cdot P \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})}{p_{ws,db}} $$ Where $p_{ws,db}$ is the saturation pressure at dry-bulb temperature. --- ### 2.4 Dew Point Apparatus — Deep Working Analysis **Construction (Dewcel / Dew Point Mirror type):** 1. A polished metal mirror (often gold-plated copper). 2. A thermoelectric (Peltier) cooler attached to the mirror. 3. A precision temperature sensor (RTD/thermistor) embedded in the mirror. 4. An optical detector (LED + photodetector) that senses light scattered by condensed dew. **Operating Principle:** The mirror is cooled progressively by the Peltier element. The temperature at which a thin film of water just begins to condense on the mirror is the **Dew Point Temperature ($T_{dp}$)**. **Why this works:** At $T_{dp}$, the partial pressure of water vapor in the air equals the saturation pressure at that temperature. The relationship is: $$ \phi = \frac{p_{ws}(T_{dp})}{p_{ws}(T_{db})} $$ This is the **most accurate method** for humidity measurement, and dew point meters are used as **primary calibration standards** worldwide. **Magnus Formula for Saturation Vapor Pressure (used for all three instruments):** $$ p_{ws}(T) = 611.2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.67 \cdot T}{T + 243.5}\right) $$ Where $T$ is in °C and $p_{ws}$ is in Pascals (Pa). --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Used In | |---|---------|----------------|-------|---------| | 1 | $\phi = p_w / p_{ws}$ | RH ratio | fraction (0–1) or % | All instruments | | 2 | $AH = m_v / V$ | Mass of vapor / volume | g/m³ | Hygrometer | | 3 | $L = L_0[1 + k\phi]$ | Hair length equation | m | Hair Hygrometer | | 4 | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | Capacitance | F | Capacitive Hygrometer | | 5 | $R = R_0 e^{-\beta\phi}$ | Resistance | Ω | Resistive Hygrometer | | 6 | $WBD = T_{db} - T_{wb}$ | Wet bulb depression | °C or K | Psychrometer | | 7 | $p_w = p_{ws,wb} - A \cdot P \cdot WBD$ | Sprung's equation | Pa | Psychrometer | | 8 | $\phi = (p_{ws,wb} - A P \cdot WBD) / p_{ws,db}$ | RH from psychrometer | % | Psychrometer | | 9 | $p_{ws}(T) = 611.2 \cdot e^{(17.67T)/(T+243.5)}$ | Magnus equation | Pa | All instruments | | 10 | $\phi = p_{ws}(T_{dp}) / p_{ws}(T_{db})$ | RH from dew point | % | Dew Point Meter | | 11 | $A \approx 6.66 \times 10^{-4}$ | Psychrometric constant | K⁻¹ | Psychrometer | | 12 | $M_w \approx 2454$ | Latent heat of vaporization | kJ/kg | Heat balance | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Equation #7 (Sprung's equation) and Equation #9 (Magnus formula) appear in almost every KTU numerical problem. Memorize both. The **psychrometric constant $A$** depends on the type of psychrometer — for the sling type, it is approximately $6.66 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹; for Assmann, slightly different. --- ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Cleanrooms require RH between 30%–50% to prevent static charge and bacterial growth. Capacitive hygrometers are the standard. - **Compressed Air Systems:** Dew point must be below -40°C for instrument air to prevent moisture damage to pneumatic valves. - **Food Drying:** Dehydrated foods need controlled humidity. Hair hygrometers are still used in grain silos for cost-effective monitoring. - **Meteorological Stations:** Aspirated psychrometers are the WMO standard for weather observation. - **HVAC:** Modern Building Management Systems (BMS) use capacitive sensors for room humidity control. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Sprung's Psychrometric Equation (Heat-Mass Transfer Balance) **Objective:** Derive the governing relation that allows us to compute the partial pressure of water vapor ($p_w$) from the readings of dry and wet bulb thermometers. **Starting Point — Conservation of Energy at the Wet Bulb Surface:** At steady state, the rate of heat supplied to the wet bulb by convection from the surrounding air equals the rate of heat removed from the wet bulb by water evaporation. $$ q_{\text{conv}} = q_{\text{evap}} $$ **Step 1 — Express convective heat transfer rate:** $$ q_{\text{conv}} = h_c \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb}) $$ Where: - $h_c$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) - $A_s$ = surface area of the wet bulb (m²) - $T_{db}, T_{wb}$ = dry bulb and wet bulb temperatures (K) **Step 2 — Express evaporative heat transfer rate (mass transfer driven by partial pressure gradient):** The mass of water evaporating per second from the wet bulb is: $$ \dot{m} = h_d \cdot A_s \cdot (p_{ws,wb} - p_w) $$ Where: - $h_d$ = mass transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s·Pa) - $p_{ws,wb}$ = saturation vapor pressure at $T_{wb}$ - $p_w$ = partial pressure of water vapor in the bulk air The heat lost by evaporation is then: $$ q_{\text{evap}} = \dot{m} \cdot M_w = h_d \cdot A_s \cdot (p_{ws,wb} - p_w) \cdot M_w $$ **Step 3 — Equate the two rates:** $$ h_c \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb}) = h_d \cdot A_s \cdot (p_{ws,wb} - p_w) \cdot M_w $$ The surface area $A_s$ cancels out: $$ h_c (T_{db} - T_{wb}) = h_d (p_{ws,wb} - p_w) M_w $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $p_w$:** $$ p_w = p_{ws,wb} - \frac{h_c}{h_d M_w} (T_{db} - T_{wb}) $$ **Step 5 — Introduce total pressure $P$ and define the psychrometric constant $A$:** Since the partial pressure $p_w$ is a small fraction of total atmospheric pressure $P$, we can normalize: $$ A = \frac{h_c}{h_d M_w P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \cdot P = \frac{h_c}{h_d M_w} $$ Substituting back: $$ \boxed{\, p_w = p_{ws,wb} - A \cdot P \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb}) \,} $$ This is **Sprung's Equation**, valid for the sling psychrometer at standard atmospheric pressure. **Step 6 — Compute RH using the dry-bulb saturation pressure:** Since $\phi = p_w / p_{ws,db}$: $$ \boxed{\, \phi = \frac{p_{ws,wb} - A \cdot P \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})}{p_{ws,db}} \,} $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: RH from Dew Point Temperature **Given:** The dew point temperature $T_{dp}$ is the temperature at which the air becomes saturated when cooled at constant pressure and constant moisture content. **Starting Point:** At $T = T_{dp}$, the partial pressure of water vapor equals the saturation pressure: $$ p_w = p_{ws}(T_{dp}) $$ **At the actual dry-bulb temperature $T_{db}$:** $$ \phi = \frac{p_w}{p_{ws}(T_{db})} $$ **Substituting:** $$ \boxed{\, \phi = \frac{p_{ws}(T_{dp})}{p_{ws}(T_{db})} \,} $$ **Substituting Magnus formula for both numerator and denominator:** $$ \phi = \frac{611.2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.67 \, T_{dp}}{T_{dp} + 243.5}\right)}{611.2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.67 \, T_{db}}{T_{db} + 243.5}\right)} $$ The constant 611.2 cancels out: $$ \boxed{\, \phi = \exp\!\left[ 17.67 \left( \frac{T_{dp}}{T_{dp} + 243.5} - \frac{T_{db}}{T_{db} + 243.5} \right) \right] \,} $$ --- ### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Style) **Problem Statement:** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style] A sling psychrometer records a dry-bulb temperature of $30°C$ and a wet-bulb temperature of $21°C$. Atmospheric pressure is 101.325 kPa. Calculate the relative humidity, absolute humidity, and dew point. Use $A = 6.66 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹ and the Magnus formula. **Step 1 — Compute saturation pressures using Magnus formula:** For $T_{db} = 30°C$: $$ p_{ws,db} = 611.2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.67 \times 30}{30 + 243.5}\right) = 611.2 \cdot \exp(1.935) = 611.2 \times 6.926 $$ $$ p_{ws,db} \approx 4233 \text{ Pa} $$ For $T_{wb} = 21°C$: $$ p_{ws,wb} = 611.2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.67 \times 21}{21 + 243.5}\right) = 611.2 \cdot \exp(1.404) = 611.2 \times 4.072 $$ $$ p_{ws,wb} \approx 2489 \text{ Pa} $$ **Step 2 — Compute partial pressure using Sprung's equation:** Wet Bulb Depression: $WBD = T_{db} - T_{wb} = 30 - 21 = 9°C = 9\text{ K}$. $$ p_w = p_{ws,wb} - A \cdot P \cdot WBD $$ $$ p_w = 2489 - (6.66 \times 10^{-4}) \times (101325) \times 9 $$ $$ p_w = 2489 - 607.3 = 1881.7 \text{ Pa} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Relative Humidity:** $$ \phi = \frac{p_w}{p_{ws,db}} = \frac{1881.7}{4233} = 0.4446 $$ $$ \boxed{\, \phi \approx 44.46\% \,} $$ **Step 4 — Compute Absolute Humidity:** Using ideal gas law for water vapor: $p_w V = m_v R_w T$ $$ AH = \frac{m_v}{V} = \frac{p_w}{R_w T_{db}} $$ Where $R_w = 461.5$ J/kg·K and $T_{db} = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15$ K. $$ AH = \frac{1881.7}{461.5 \times 303.15} = \frac{1881.7}{139903} = 0.01345 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$ $$ \boxed{\, AH \approx 13.45 \text{ g/m}^3 \,} $$ **Step 5 — Compute Dew Point:** The dew point is the temperature at which $p_{ws}(T_{dp}) = p_w = 1881.7$ Pa. We need to solve: $611.2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.67 \, T_{dp}}{T_{dp} + 243.5}\right) = 1881.7$ Dividing both sides by 611.2: $$ \exp\!\left(\frac{17.67 \, T_{dp}}{T_{dp} + 243.5}\right) = 3.078 $$ Taking natural logarithm: $$ \frac{17.67 \, T_{dp}}{T_{dp} + 243.5} = \ln(3.078) = 1.124 $$ $$ 17.67 \, T_{dp} = 1.124 \, (T_{dp} + 243.5) $$ $$ 17.67 \, T_{dp} = 1.124 \, T_{dp} + 273.7 $$ $$ (17.67 - 1.124) \, T_{dp} = 273.7 $$ $$ 16.546 \, T_{dp} = 273.7 $$ $$ \boxed{\, T_{dp} \approx 16.54°C \,} $$ **Verification:** $T_{dp} < T_{wb} < T_{db}$ ✓ (Physically consistent: $16.54 < 21 < 30$). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Instruments | Parameter | Hygrometer (Capacitive) | Psychrometer (Sling) | Dew Point Meter | |-----------|------------------------|----------------------|-----------------| | **Measured Variable** | RH (direct) | WBD → RH (indirect) | $T_{dp}$ → RH (indirect) | | **Accuracy** | ±2 to ±5% RH | ±3 to ±5% RH | ±0.1 to ±0.5°C (highest) | | **Range** | 5% – 95% RH | 5% – 95% RH (5°C – 50°C) | -80°C to +60°C dew point | | **Response Time** | 30 sec – 2 min | 1 – 3 min | 30 sec – 5 min | | **Cost** | Moderate | Low | High | | **Best Use** | HVAC, cleanrooms | Field measurement | Lab/calibration standard | | **KTU Frequency** | High | Very High (numericals) | Medium (theory) | | **Calibration Required** | Yearly | Before each use | Every 6 months | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Capacitive Hygrometer Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Humid Air
Sample] --> B[Porous Gold
Electrode] B --> C[Hygroscopic
Polymer Film] C --> D[Metal Back
Electrode] D --> E[Capacitance
Change Delta C] E --> F[Bridge
Circuit or
Oscillator] F --> G[Signal
Conditioning
Amplifier] G --> H[ADC
Converter] H --> I[Microcontroller
Processing] I --> J[Digital
Display RH percent] I --> K[Analog
Output 4-20 mA] I --> L[Alarm
Threshold Logic] ``` > **Figure Interpretation:** Humid air contacts the porous gold electrode (B), water vapor diffuses into the hygroscopic polymer film (C), changing its dielectric constant. This alters the capacitance measured between (B) and (D). The bridge (F) converts ΔC to a voltage, which is amplified (G), digitized (H), and processed (I) to display RH, transmit 4–20 mA signal, or trigger alarms (L). --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Sling Psychrometer Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Atmospheric
Air] --> B[Dry Bulb
Thermometer] A --> C[Wet Bulb
Thermometer] C --> D[Muslin Wick
Water Reservoir] D --> E[Evaporation
Process] E --> F[Cooling of
Wet Bulb] B --> G[Temperature
Comparator] F --> G G --> H[Wet Bulb
Depression WBD] H --> I[Apply Sprung
Equation] I --> J[Compute p sub w] J --> K[Lookup p sub ws db
via Magnus Formula] K --> L[Compute RH
phi percent] L --> M[Display
Result] ``` > **Figure Interpretation:** Two thermometers (B and C) measure ambient air temperature and cooled wick temperature simultaneously. The wick (D) loses water by evaporation (E), cooling (F). The difference (G → H) is the WBD. Sprung's equation (I) gives partial pressure (J), and dividing by saturation pressure at dry-bulb (K) gives RH (L), which is displayed (M). --- ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Dew Point Mirror Hygrometer ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Power
Supply] --> B[Peltier
Thermoelectric
Cooler] B --> C[Mirror
Surface] D[Sample Air
Stream] --> C C --> E[Condensation
Detection
LED + Photodiode] E --> F[Optical
Signal
Scattered Light] F --> G[Comparator
Circuit] G --> H{Threshold
Reached?} H -->|Yes| I[Read Mirror
Temp via RTD] I --> J[Magnus Inverse
Solve for RH] H -->|No| B J --> K[Display
T dp and RH] ``` > **Figure Interpretation:** The Peltier cooler (B) progressively cools the mirror (C). Sample air (D) flows over it. When dew first forms, scattered light (F) triggers the comparator (G → H). The mirror temperature (I) is the dew point, which is then converted to RH (J) using the inverse Magnus formula and displayed (K). --- ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Humidity Measurement System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Input A1[Process
Air Sample] end subgraph Sensing B1[Hygrometer
Capacitive] B2[Psychrometer
Sling Type] B3[Dew Point
Mirror Type] end subgraph Signal C1[Transducer
Bridge Circuit] C2[Two Temp
Readings Tdb Twb] C3[Optical Dew
Detection] end subgraph Processing D1[Microcontroller
or Analog Solver] end subgraph Output E1[Local Display
RH percent] E2[4-20 mA
Analog Output] E3[HMI / SCADA
Digital Link] E4[Alarm
Trip Output] end A1 --> B1 A1 --> B2 A1 --> B3 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C2 B3 --> C3 C1 --> D1 C2 --> D1 C3 --> D1 D1 --> E1 D1 --> E2 D1 --> E3 D1 --> E4 ``` > **Figure Interpretation:** This end-to-end system shows how the three instruments (B1, B2, B3) all feed into a common processing unit (D1) that delivers multiple output formats (E1–E4). This is typical in modern industrial installations where redundancy and sensor fusion improve measurement reliability. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Relative Humidity. State the difference between relative humidity and absolute humidity.** **Model Answer:** Relative Humidity (RH or $\phi$) is defined as the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor in a gas mixture to the saturation vapor pressure of water at the same dry-bulb temperature. $$ \phi = \frac{p_w}{p_{ws}(T_{db})} \times 100\% $$ **Difference Table:** | Parameter | Relative Humidity | Absolute Humidity | |-----------|------------------|-------------------| | Definition | Ratio of actual to saturation vapor pressure | Mass of water vapor per unit volume | | Formula | $\phi = p_w / p_{ws}$ | $AH = m_v / V$ | | Units | Percentage (%) | g/m³ or kg/m³ | | Temperature Dependent | Yes | No | | Range | 0 – 100% | 0 – ~30 g/m³ | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] What is the principle of operation of a capacitive humidity sensor? List any two advantages.** **Model Answer:** A capacitive humidity sensor consists of a hygroscopic polymer (dielectric) sandwiched between two electrodes. The relative permittivity of the polymer changes as it absorbs water vapor from the air. Since the capacitance is given by $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$, the change in $\varepsilon_r$ produces a measurable change in capacitance, which is proportional to relative humidity. **Advantages:** 1. High accuracy (±2% RH) and good long-term stability. 2. Compact size, low power consumption, fast response — suitable for modern HVAC and process control. 3. Linear output over a wide range (5% – 95% RH). 4. No moving parts, hence high reliability. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse the psychrometric constant A with absolute humidity.** A is dimensionless only when multiplied by pressure (unit K⁻¹). Always show units. > 2. **Always convert °C to Kelvin** when using gas-law-based formulas. Many students lose 2–3 marks by mixing units. > 3. **Magnus formula temperature must be in °C**, NOT Kelvin. Students commonly apply it incorrectly. > 4. **Dew point must always be ≤ Wet bulb ≤ Dry bulb** in the saturated adiabatic process. If your answer violates this, recalculate. > 5. **In hair hygrometer diagrams, label the hair bundle, spring tension, lever mechanism, and pointer** — missing labels is a major deduction point. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper] **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **capacitive type humidity sensor**. Derive the expression for capacitance and discuss how it varies with relative humidity. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** In a sling psychrometer, the dry bulb reads 35°C and the wet bulb reads 25°C. Atmospheric pressure is 1.01325 bar. Calculate: (i) The partial pressure of water vapor (ii) The relative humidity (iii) The dew point temperature (iv) The absolute humidity Use the psychrometric constant $A = 6.66 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹ and the Magnus formula. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q-A (a):** **Construction:** A capacitive humidity sensor consists of: 1. A thin film of hygroscopic polymer (e.g., polyimide, cellulose acetate) of thickness $d$ (typically 1–5 μm). 2. Two conductive electrodes — a porous gold upper electrode and a metallic lower electrode (often platinum). 3. A substrate (ceramic or silicon) for mechanical support. 4. A signal conditioning circuit (AC bridge or oscillator). **Working Principle:** The relative permittivity ($\varepsilon_r$) of the polymer is approximately 3 when dry. When water vapor diffuses through the porous gold electrode into the polymer, the water molecules (with $\varepsilon_{\text{water}} \approx 80$) significantly increase the effective permittivity. **Capacitance Derivation:** The structure forms a parallel plate capacitor. The capacitance is: $$ C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} $$ Where: - $C$ = capacitance (F) - $\varepsilon_0$ = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** (permittivity of free space) - $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity (function of RH) - $A$ = electrode area (m²) - $d$ = dielectric thickness (m) As RH increases, $\varepsilon_r$ increases linearly (approximately), so $C$ increases proportionally. The change in capacitance $\Delta C$ is measured and converted to a voltage using a Wien bridge or an oscillator whose frequency depends on $C$. **Typical behavior:** Sensor capacitance varies from ~100 pF (at 0% RH) to ~140 pF (at 100% RH), giving excellent sensitivity and linearity. --- **Model Solution to Q-A (b):** **Given:** $T_{db} = 35°C$, $T_{wb} = 25°C$, $P = 1.01325$ bar $= 101325$ Pa, $A = 6.66 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹. **Step 1 — Compute saturation pressure at $T_{db} = 35°C$:** $$ p_{ws,db} = 611.2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.67 \times 35}{35 + 243.5}\right) = 611.2 \cdot \exp(2.223) = 611.2 \times 9.235 $$ $$ p_{ws,db} \approx 5645 \text{ Pa} $$ **Step 2 — Compute saturation pressure at $T_{wb} = 25°C$:** $$ p_{ws,wb} = 611.2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.67 \times 25}{25 + 243.5}\right) = 611.2 \cdot \exp(1.649) = 611.2 \times 5.202 $$ $$ p_{ws,wb} \approx 3180 \text{ Pa} $$ **Step 3 — Partial Pressure (Sprung's Equation):** WBD $= 35 - 25 = 10°C = 10$ K. $$ p_w = p_{ws,wb} - A \cdot P \cdot WBD = 3180 - (6.66 \times 10^{-4})(101325)(10) $$ $$ p_w = 3180 - 674.8 = 2505.2 \text{ Pa} $$ **Answer (i):** $\boxed{p_w \approx 2505.2 \text{ Pa}}$ **Step 4 — Relative Humidity:** $$ \phi = \frac{p_w}{p_{ws,db}} = \frac{2505.2}{5645} = 0.4438 $$ **Answer (ii):** $\boxed{\phi \approx 44.38\%}$ **Step 5 — Dew Point:** Solve $p_{ws}(T_{dp}) = 2505.2$ Pa. $$ 611.2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.67 \, T_{dp}}{T_{dp} + 243.5}\right) = 2505.2 $$ $$ \exp\!\left(\frac{17.67 \, T_{dp}}{T_{dp} + 243.5}\right) = 4.099 $$ $$ \frac{17.67 \, T_{dp}}{T_{dp} + 243.5} = \ln(4.099) = 1.411 $$ $$ 17.67 \, T_{dp} = 1.411 (T_{dp} + 243.5) = 1.411 \, T_{dp} + 343.6 $$ $$ 16.259 \, T_{dp} = 343.6 \Rightarrow T_{dp} = 21.13°C $$ **Answer (iii):** $\boxed{T_{dp} \approx 21.13°C}$ **Step 6 — Absolute Humidity:** $T_{db} = 35 + 273.15 = 308.15$ K, $R_w = 461.5$ J/kg·K. $$ AH = \frac{p_w}{R_w T_{db}} = \frac{2505.2}{461.5 \times 308.15} = \frac{2505.2}{142211} $$ **Answer (iv):** $\boxed{AH \approx 0.01762 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 17.62 \text{ g/m}^3}$ **Verification:** $T_{dp} = 21.13°C < T_{wb} = 25°C < T_{db} = 35°C$ ✓ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper] **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **wet and dry bulb psychrometer** with a neat diagram. Derive Sprung's equation for the determination of relative humidity. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A dew point meter measures a dew point of 12°C in a room where the dry-bulb temperature is 28°C. Using the Magnus formula, calculate: (i) The relative humidity (ii) The partial pressure of water vapor (iii) The absolute humidity in g/m³ (iv) Comment on the comfort level in the room. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q-B (a):** **Construction (Refer to Mermaid Diagram in Section 4.2):** The sling psychrometer consists of: 1. **Two identical mercury-in-glass thermometers** — one dry, one wet. 2. **Muslin cloth wick** wrapped around the wet bulb, connected to a small water reservoir. 3. **A swivel handle** with a chain linkage that allows the user to rotate (sling) the assembly. 4. **A frame** to hold both thermometers in parallel. **Working Principle:** The dry bulb measures the ambient air temperature. The wet bulb is cooled by evaporation of water from the wick. The rate of evaporation depends on the dryness of the air: - In dry air → fast evaporation → high WBD. - In humid air → slow evaporation → low WBD. During rotation (slinging) at ~3–5 m/s, the air velocity over both bulbs is forced to be the same, ensuring standardized convective heat transfer. After ~1–2 minutes, the wet bulb reaches a steady temperature. **Derivation of Sprung's Equation:** *(Same derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes. The key steps are:)* Energy balance at steady state: $$ h_c (T_{db} - T_{wb}) = h_d (p_{ws,wb} - p_w) M_w $$ Solving for $p_w$ and introducing total pressure $P$: $$ \boxed{\, p_w = p_{ws,wb} - A \cdot P \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb}) \,} $$ Where $A$ is the **psychrometric constant** ($= h_c / (h_d M_w P)$), approximately $6.66 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹ for the sling type. Relative Humidity: $$ \phi = \frac{p_{ws,wb} - A \cdot P \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})}{p_{ws,db}} $$ --- **Model Solution to Q-B (b):** **Given:** $T_{dp} = 12°C$, $T_{db} = 28°C$. **Step 1 — Compute $p_{ws}(T_{dp})$:** $$ p_{ws}(12) = 611.2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.67 \times 12}{12 + 243.5}\right) = 611.2 \cdot \exp(0.828) = 611.2 \times 2.288 $$ $$ p_{ws}(12) \approx 1398.6 \text{ Pa} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $p_{ws}(T_{db})$:** $$ p_{ws}(28) = 611.2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.67 \times 28}{28 + 243.5}\right) = 611.2 \cdot \exp(1.822) = 611.2 \times 6.187 $$ $$ p_{ws}(28) \approx 3781.6 \text{ Pa} $$ **Step 3 — Relative Humidity (Dew Point Method):** $$ \phi = \frac{p_{ws}(T_{dp})}{p_{ws}(T_{db})} = \frac{1398.6}{3781.6} = 0.3698 $$ **Answer (i):** $\boxed{\phi \approx 36.98\%}$ **Step 4 — Partial Pressure of Water Vapor:** By definition, $p_w = p_{ws}(T_{dp}) = 1398.6$ Pa. **Answer (ii):** $\boxed{p_w \approx 1398.6 \text{ Pa}}$ **Step 5 — Absolute Humidity:** $T_{db} = 28 + 273.15 = 301.15$ K, $R_w = 461.5$ J/kg·K. $$ AH = \frac{p_w}{R_w T_{db}} = \frac{1398.6}{461.5 \times 301.15} = \frac{1398.6}{138976} $$ **Answer (iii):** $\boxed{AH \approx 0.01006 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 10.06 \text{ g/m}^3}$ **Step 6 — Comfort Comment:** **Answer (iv):** The ASHRAE Standard 55 recommends indoor RH between 30% and 60% for thermal comfort at 28°C. Although 36.98% falls within the recommended band numerically, the dry-bulb temperature of 28°C is slightly above the standard 25°C comfort temperature. The room is **marginally acceptable** but tends to be on the *drier side*, which may cause static electricity issues and mild respiratory discomfort. For an office environment, the recommended condition is RH ≈ 45–50% at this temperature. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exams** **1. Definition Hierarchy:** - Absolute Humidity = mass of vapor / unit volume (g/m³) - Relative Humidity = $p_w / p_{ws}(T_{db})$ × 100% - Dew Point = temperature at which air becomes saturated - Mixing Ratio = mass of vapor / mass of dry air **2. Three Instruments and Their Core Principle:** - **Hygrometer** → Material property change (length, capacitance, resistance) - **Psychrometer** → Wet bulb depression (thermodynamic heat-mass transfer) - **Dew Point Meter** → Direct measurement of saturation temperature **3. Two Must-Memorize Equations:** - **Sprung's Equation:** $p_w = p_{ws,wb} - A \cdot P \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})$ - **Magnus Formula:** $p_{ws}(T) = 611.2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.67 \, T}{T + 243.5}\right)$ (T in °C) **4. Key Constants (Write in Margin During Exam):** - $A = 6.66 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹ (psychrometric constant) - $M_w = 2454$ kJ/kg (latent heat of vaporization of water at 20°C) - $R_w = 461.5$ J/kg·K (gas constant for water vapor) - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m (vacuum permittivity) - $\varepsilon_{\text{water}} = 80$, $\varepsilon_{\text{dry polymer}} \approx 3$ **5. Physical Constraint (Verification Rule):** - Always: $T_{dp} \leq T_{wb} \leq T_{db}$ for an unsaturated gas mixture. - If $T_{dp} = T_{db}$, the air is at 100% RH (saturated/foggy). **6. KTU Frequently Asked Concepts:** - Why hair hygrometers lose accuracy at very high RH (>95%) — because hair fibers become fully saturated and saturate nonlinearly. - Why capacitive hygrometers use AC excitation — to prevent DC polarization and migration of ions in the polymer. - Why dew point meters are the most accurate — because they directly measure a thermodynamic state variable, not a derived property. - Why a sling psychrometer is rotated — to ensure equal air velocity over both bulbs, satisfying the assumption of identical heat-mass transfer coefficients. **7. Industrial Selection Rule of Thumb:** - General HVAC → Capacitive Hygrometer (cost-effective, reliable) - Field measurement → Sling Psychrometer (no power needed) - Laboratory / Calibration → Dew Point Mirror Hygrometer (NIST-traceable accuracy) **8. Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** - ❌ Forgetting to convert °C to K in absolute humidity calculation. - ❌ Using total pressure in Pa vs. kPa inconsistently. - ❌ Mixing psychrometric constant $A$ with absolute humidity $A$. - ❌ Mislabeling psychrometer parts in diagrams (especially the wick and water reservoir). - ❌ Forgetting the exponential in the Magnus formula. - ❌ Writing $p_{ws}$ when the question asks for $p_w$ (and vice versa).

topics.title: pH Measurement Using Calomel Electrode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** pH Measurement Using Calomel Electrode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **pH (Potential of Hydrogen)** is a logarithmic measure of the hydrogen ion concentration (activity) in an aqueous solution, formally defined as:
> $$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}\left(a_{\text{H}^+}\right) = -\log_{10}\left[\text{H}^+\right]$$
> where $a_{\text{H}^+}$ is the activity (effective concentration) of hydrogen ions in mol/L. The pH scale typically ranges from **0 (strongly acidic)** to **14 (strongly alkaline)**, with **7 (neutral)** at 25 °C.

A **Calomel Electrode** is a secondary **reference electrode** widely used in electrochemistry and industrial pH measurement. It is a half-cell comprising:

- **Mercury (Hg)** in contact with
- **Mercurous Chloride (Hg₂Cl₂, "calomel")** paste, immersed in
- A **potassium chloride (KCl) electrolyte solution** of a defined concentration (saturated, normal, or decinormal).

The half-cell reaction is:
$$\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(s) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons 2\text{Hg}(l) + 2\text{Cl}^-(aq)$$

The standard reduction potential at **25 °C (298 K)** is **E° = +0.268 V** with respect to the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE), though the *operative* potential depends on the KCl activity.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Pressure Balance" of Chemistry:**
> Think of a pH measurement system like a weighing scale with **two pans**: the *unknown solution* (test pan) and a *known standard* (reference pan). The calomel electrode is the **calibrated reference pan** — it always exerts a known, stable "force" (voltage). The other electrode (e.g., glass or hydrogen) feels the "weight" (acidity) of the unknown solution. The *difference* in their "forces" is the EMF, from which we calculate pH — exactly like reading the difference between a known and unknown weight on a balance.

## 1.3 Why the Calomel Electrode?

- **Stable and reproducible** potential (drift < 0.1 mV/day).
- Simple to construct and **replenish** in industrial plants.
- Well-defined temperature dependence (linear).
- Excellent **polarizability resistance** — small currents can pass without disturbing potential.
- Used as a **reference half-cell** in pH cells, ORP cells, potentiometric titrations, and corrosion monitoring.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants (at 25 °C)

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Faraday Constant, **F** | **96,485 C/mol** |
| Universal Gas Constant, **R** | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard Temperature, **T** | **298.15 K** |
| Nernst Slope Factor, **RT/F** | **0.02569 V** |
| **2.303 RT/F** | **0.05916 V ≈ 0.0591 V** |
| Standard calomel potential, **E°** | **+0.268 V (vs SHE)** |
| Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) potential | **+0.244 V** |
| Normal Calomel Electrode (NCE) potential | **+0.280 V** |
| Decinormal Calomel Electrode (DCE) potential | **+0.334 V** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of the Calomel Electrode

A typical Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) consists of:

1. **Inner glass tube** containing pure **mercury** at the bottom.
2. A layer of **mercurous chloride (Hg₂Cl₂) paste** is placed above the mercury (in contact with it).
3. A **platinum wire** sealed into the glass acts as the **electrical lead** (anode/cathode contact for the mercury).
4. The inner tube is surrounded by a **KCl solution** (saturated for SCE) that fills the outer jacket.
5. A **porous frit / ceramic plug / asbestos wick** at the bottom provides a **liquid junction** with the external test solution, allowing ionic contact without physical mixing.
6. A **side-arm filling tube** allows the KCl solution to be replenished or to maintain a small outflow (prevents contamination of the test solution).

> [!NOTE]
> The porous plug maintains electrical continuity (completes the ionic circuit) while producing a small, stable **liquid-junction potential** (typically 1–3 mV), which is normally neglected or compensated.

## 2.2 Working Principle — The Nernst Equation

The Nernst equation governs the electrode potential of any reversible half-cell:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

At **25 °C**, converting natural log to base-10 logarithm:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n}\log Q \quad \text{(volts)}$$

For the calomel half-cell ($n = 2$):

$$E_{\text{calomel}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2/\text{Hg}} - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log\left(\frac{[\text{Cl}^-]^2}{1}\right)$$

$$E_{\text{calomel}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{calomel}} - 0.0591 \log[\text{Cl}^-]$$

Equivalently, using **pCl = −log[Cl⁻]**:

$$E_{\text{calomel}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{calomel}} + 0.0591 \cdot \text{pCl}$$

## 2.3 The pH Measurement Cell

The complete electrochemical cell used to measure pH is:

$$\text{Pt} \mid \text{H}_2(g, 1\,\text{atm}) \mid \text{H}^+(aq, \text{pH}) \parallel \text{KCl}(\text{sat}) \mid \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(s) \mid \text{Hg}(l)$$

Conventionally, the **left electrode is the indicator (anode)** and the **right electrode is the reference (cathode)**. The cell EMF is:

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}} = E_{\text{calomel}} - E_{\text{hydrogen}}$$

For the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** acting as indicator:

$$E_{\text{H}^+/\text{H}_2} = 0 - \frac{0.0591}{1}\log\left(\frac{1}{[\text{H}^+]}\right) = -0.0591 \cdot \text{pH}$$

Therefore, the **pH equation** becomes:

$$\boxed{\;E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{calomel}} + 0.0591 \cdot \text{pH}\;}$$

Solving for pH:

$$\boxed{\;\text{pH} = \frac{E_{\text{cell}} - E_{\text{calomel}}}{0.0591}\;}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas in this table are **KTU Board Examination favorites** — memorize them thoroughly.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$ | Definition of pH | dimensionless |
| 2 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log Q$ | Nernst equation at 25 °C | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log Q$ | Generalized Nernst equation | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $E_{\text{calomel}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cal}} - 0.0591 \log[\text{Cl}^-]$ | Calomel electrode potential | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $E_{\text{calomel}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cal}} + 0.0591 \cdot \text{pCl}$ | Calomel potential in pCl form | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $E_{\text{H}^+/\text{H}_2} = -0.0591 \cdot \text{pH}$ | SHE potential at 1 atm H₂ | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{calomel}} - E_{\text{indicator}}$ | Cell EMF (cathode − anode) | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $\text{pH} = \dfrac{E_{\text{cell}} - E_{\text{calomel}}}{0.0591}$ | pH from measured EMF | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\dfrac{dE}{dT} = -0.00076\ \text{V/°C}$ (SCE) | Temp. coefficient of SCE | V/°C |
| 10 | $\text{pH}_{T} = \dfrac{E_{\text{cell}} - E_{\text{cal}}(T)}{0.0591 \cdot (T/298)}$ | Temperature-corrected pH | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Calomel + glass electrode systems are the **workhorse of process industries** — pharma water systems, food & beverage QC, wastewater treatment, chlor-alkali plants, fermenters, and power-plant boiler feedwater monitoring (critical at pH 8.5–9.2 to prevent corrosion). The simplicity, robustness, and ~0.01 pH repeatability make SCE the **industrial reference standard** even though the Ag/AgCl electrode is now more common in field probes (no liquid junction issues, no Hg toxicity).

## 2.5 Comparison: Types of Calomel Electrodes

| Type | KCl Concentration | E (V) at 25 °C | Temp. Coeff. (mV/°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Saturated (SCE) | ~4.5 M (saturated) | **+0.244** | −0.65 to −0.76 |
| Normal (NCE) | 1.0 M | **+0.280** | −0.24 |
| Decinormal (DCE) | 0.1 M | **+0.334** | −0.07 |

> [!TIP]
> **Why so many types?** A **saturated** KCl solution has a known, constant chloride activity (because of the solid KCl reservoir) — this gives the *most stable* potential but the *worst* temperature coefficient. A **decinormal** calomel has the *best* temperature stability but is more sensitive to contamination. For lab work, SCE is the standard; for precision temperature work, DCE is preferred.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Calomel Electrode Potential

**Starting from the half-cell reaction:**
$$\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(s) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons 2\text{Hg}(l) + 2\text{Cl}^-(aq)$$

**Step 1:** Identify the reaction quotient Q. Solids and pure liquids have activity = 1. The only variable species is Cl⁻, with stoichiometric coefficient 2.
$$Q = \frac{[\text{Hg}]^2 \cdot [\text{Cl}^-]^2}{[\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2]} = \frac{(1)^2 \cdot [\text{Cl}^-]^2}{1} = [\text{Cl}^-]^2$$

**Step 2:** Number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction:
$$n = 2$$

**Step 3:** Apply the generalized Nernst equation:
$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Q and n:
$$E_{\text{cal}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cal}} - \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\left([\text{Cl}^-]^2\right)$$

**Step 5:** Use the log power rule $\ln(x^2) = 2\ln(x)$:
$$E_{\text{cal}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cal}} - \frac{RT}{2F} \cdot 2\ln[\text{Cl}^-]$$

$$E_{\text{cal}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cal}} - \frac{RT}{F}\ln[\text{Cl}^-]$$

**Step 6:** Convert to base-10 logarithm using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:
$$E_{\text{cal}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cal}} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{F}\log[\text{Cl}^-]$$

**Step 7:** Insert numerical values at 25 °C (T = 298 K):
$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.05916\ \text{V} \approx 0.0591\ \text{V}$$

**Step 8:** Final calomel electrode potential equation at 25 °C:
$$\boxed{\;E_{\text{calomel}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{calomel}} - 0.0591 \cdot \log[\text{Cl}^-]\;}$$

For a **saturated calomel electrode**, $[\text{Cl}^-]$ is the activity in saturated KCl ≈ 4.5 M, giving a measured potential of **+0.244 V** at 25 °C.

## 3.2 Derivation of the pH Equation

**Starting cell:**
$$\text{Pt} \mid \text{H}_2(g) \mid \text{H}^+(aq) \parallel \text{KCl} \mid \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 \mid \text{Hg}$$

**Step 1:** Identify the hydrogen electrode half-reaction:
$$2\text{H}^+(aq) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2(g)$$

**Step 2:** Write the Nernst equation for the hydrogen electrode (n = 2):
$$E_{\text{H}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{H}} - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log\left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}}{[\text{H}^+]^2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Since $E^{\circ}_{\text{H}} = 0$ (SHE definition) and assuming $P_{\text{H}_2} = 1$ atm:
$$E_{\text{H}} = 0 - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log\left(\frac{1}{[\text{H}^+]^2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the log using $\log(1/x^2) = -2\log x$:
$$E_{\text{H}} = -\frac{0.0591}{2}\left(-2\log[\text{H}^+]\right) = 0.0591 \cdot \log[\text{H}^+]$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\log[\text{H}^+] = -\text{pH}$:
$$\boxed{\;E_{\text{H}} = -0.0591 \cdot \text{pH}\;}$$

**Step 6:** Apply cell EMF convention ($E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$):
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{calomel}} - E_{\text{H}}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute the hydrogen potential:
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{calomel}} - \left(-0.0591 \cdot \text{pH}\right)$$

**Step 8:** Final cell EMF:
$$\boxed{\;E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{calomel}} + 0.0591 \cdot \text{pH}\;}$$

**Step 9:** Solving for pH (the "working equation" used in pH meters):
$$\boxed{\;\text{pH} = \frac{E_{\text{cell}} - E_{\text{calomel}}}{0.0591}\;}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A pH cell uses a **saturated calomel electrode** ($E_{\text{calomel}} = +0.244$ V) and a hydrogen indicator electrode at 25 °C. The measured cell EMF is **0.547 V**. Calculate the pH of the test solution.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** State the pH equation:
$$\text{pH} = \frac{E_{\text{cell}} - E_{\text{calomel}}}{0.0591}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute known values ($E_{\text{cell}} = 0.547$ V, $E_{\text{calomel}} = +0.244$ V):
$$\text{pH} = \frac{0.547 - 0.244}{0.0591}$$

**Step 3:** Numerator calculation:
$$0.547 - 0.244 = 0.303\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4:** Final division:
$$\text{pH} = \frac{0.303}{0.0591} = 5.127$$

**Step 5:** Round to appropriate significant figures:
$$\boxed{\;\text{pH} \approx 5.13\;}$$

**Interpretation:** The solution is **weakly acidic** (pH between 5 and 6, e.g., like rainwater or black coffee).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cell EMF for Various pH Values (SCE, 25 °C)

| pH | $E_{\text{H}}$ (V) | $E_{\text{cell}} = 0.244 + 0.0591\cdot \text{pH}$ (V) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (strong acid) | 0.000 | **0.244** |
| 1 | −0.0591 | 0.303 |
| 4 (acidic) | −0.2364 | 0.480 |
| **7 (neutral)** | **−0.4137** | **0.658** |
| 10 (alkaline) | −0.5910 | 0.835 |
| 14 (strong base) | −0.8274 | 1.071 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The cell EMF is **directly proportional to pH** — a 1-unit pH change produces a 59.1 mV change in EMF. This is the **theoretical basis of digital pH meters**, which convert mV readings to pH using precisely this slope (often temperature-compensated to 0.0591 V × T/298).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Calomel Electrode — Cross-Sectional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PT WIRE LEAD FROM MERCURY] --> B[INNER GLASS TUBE]
    B --> C[PURE MERCURY HG POOL]
    C --> D[MERCUROUS CHLORIDE HG2CL2 PASTE]
    D --> E[KCL SOLUTION SATURATED]
    E --> F[OUTER GLASS JACKET]
    F --> G[POROUS CERAMIC FRIT LIQUID JUNCTION]
    G --> H[TEST SOLUTION H PLUS IONS]
    H --> I[SALT BRIDGE IONIC PATH]
    I --> J[HIGH IMPEDANCE VOLTMETER]
    J --> K[PH METER DISPLAY]
    B --> J
    L[KCL REFILL PORT] --> E
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of Complete pH Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSE[SENSING STAGE]
        A1[TEST SOLUTION H PLUS] --> A2[INDICATOR ELECTRODE GLASS OR H2]
        A3[CALOMEL REFERENCE ELECTRODE SCE] --> A2
    end
    SENSE --> B1[DC MILLIVOLT SIGNAL TYP 0 TO 414 MV]
    B1 --> B2[HIGH IMPEDANCE AMPLIFIER GIGAOHM INPUT]
    B2 --> B3[TEMPERATURE COMPENSATION NETWORK PT100 RTD]
    B3 --> C1[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER ADC]
    C1 --> C2[MICROPROCESSOR PH COMPUTATION]
    C2 --> D1[LCD OR LED PH DISPLAY 0.00 TO 14.00]
    C2 --> D2[4 TO 20 MA CURRENT LOOP OUTPUT]
    C2 --> D3[ALARM RELAY HIGH OR LOW PH]
```

## 4.3 Equivalent Electrical Model of the Calomel Half-Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[REFERENCE POTENTIAL E CAL 0.244 V] --> B[INTERNAL RESISTANCE R S 1 KILO OHM]
    B --> C[LIQUID JUNCTION POTENTIAL E LJ 1 TO 3 MV]
    C --> D[OUTPUT TERMINAL TO VOLTMETER]
    E[SOLUTION RESISTANCE R SOLN] --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **equivalent circuit** reveals three critical properties of the calomel electrode: a stable **Thevenin source** (E_cal), a small **series resistance** due to the porous plug, and a small **liquid-junction potential** (E_LJ) that must be minimized by using a high-quality salt bridge (preferably with a flowing junction).

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]

**What is a Calomel electrode? Write its half-cell reaction and standard potential at 25 °C.** (3 Marks)

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

A **Calomel electrode** is a **secondary reference electrode** used as a half-cell in pH measurement and other potentiometric measurements. *(1 mark)*

It consists of mercury in contact with mercurous chloride (Hg₂Cl₂, "calomel") paste, immersed in a potassium chloride (KCl) solution of fixed concentration (saturated, normal, or decinormal). *(1 mark)*

**Half-cell reaction:**
$$\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(s) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons 2\text{Hg}(l) + 2\text{Cl}^-(aq)$$

**Standard potential:** $E^{\circ} = +0.268$ V vs SHE at 25 °C. For the **saturated calomel electrode (SCE)**, the working potential is **+0.244 V**. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]

**Derive the relationship between the cell EMF and pH for a cell consisting of a hydrogen electrode and a saturated calomel electrode.** (3 Marks)

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Cell:** $\text{Pt} \mid \text{H}_2 \mid \text{H}^+ \parallel \text{KCl(sat)} \mid \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 \mid \text{Hg}$

The Nernst potential of the hydrogen indicator electrode is:
$$E_{\text{H}} = -0.0591 \cdot \text{pH} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

The calomel reference potential is constant:
$$E_{\text{calomel}} = +0.244\ \text{V (SCE)} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

By the cell EMF convention $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$:
$$\boxed{\;E_{\text{cell}} = 0.244 + 0.0591 \cdot \text{pH}\;} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

### **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE)** with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)

### ✅ Model Answer:

**Construction (4 marks):**

The Saturated Calomel Electrode consists of the following parts:

1. **Inner glass tube** containing pure **liquid mercury (Hg)** at the bottom, into which a **platinum wire** is sealed to provide external electrical contact.
2. Above the mercury, a layer of **mercurous chloride (Hg₂Cl₂) paste** is placed — this is the "calomel" layer.
3. The inner tube is surrounded by an **outer glass jacket** containing **saturated KCl solution** (~4.5 M) in equilibrium with solid KCl crystals (which serve as a reservoir maintaining saturation).
4. The bottom of the electrode has a **porous ceramic frit / asbestos wick / fiber plug** that provides the **liquid junction** to the external test solution. This junction completes the ionic circuit without bulk mixing.
5. A **side-arm with a filling port** allows the saturated KCl to be replenished and provides a small outward flow to prevent contamination of the test solution.

**Working (2 marks):**

The half-cell reaction at the calomel electrode is:
$$\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(s) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons 2\text{Hg}(l) + 2\text{Cl}^-(aq)$$

The electrode potential is given by Nernst equation:
$$E_{\text{SCE}} = E^{\circ} - 0.0591 \log[\text{Cl}^-] = +0.244\ \text{V at 25 °C}$$

Because the KCl is **saturated** (in contact with solid KCl), the chloride ion activity is essentially constant, making the potential **highly stable and reproducible**.

**Advantages (0.5 mark):**
- Highly stable and reproducible potential
- Easy to construct and maintain
- Reasonable temperature behavior (linear coefficient)
- Inexpensive

**Limitations (0.5 mark):**
- Contains **toxic mercury** — hazard in pharmaceutical / food industries
- Saturated KCl has the largest temperature coefficient (~−0.76 mV/°C)
- The liquid junction introduces a small uncertainty potential
- Cannot be used above ~70 °C (KCl solubility issues)

---

### **(b)** A glass electrode and a saturated calomel electrode are used to measure the pH of a buffer solution. The measured EMF is **0.412 V** at 25 °C. If the asymmetry potential of the glass electrode is 0.005 V, calculate the pH of the solution. Also state two advantages of using a glass electrode over the hydrogen electrode. (7 Marks)

### ✅ Model Answer:

**Given data:**
- $E_{\text{cell}} = 0.412$ V
- $E_{\text{calomel}} = +0.244$ V (SCE)
- Asymmetry potential, $E_{\text{asy}} = +0.005$ V
- Temperature = 25 °C

**Step 1 — Write the corrected pH equation including asymmetry potential:**
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{calomel}} - (E^{\circ}_{\text{glass}} - 0.0591\cdot \text{pH} + E_{\text{asy}})$$

The standard glass electrode potential $E^{\circ}_{\text{glass}}$ is combined with $E_{\text{asy}}$ into a single calibration constant $E^{\circ *}_{\text{glass}}$:
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{calomel}} - E^{\circ *}_{\text{glass}} + 0.0591\cdot \text{pH}$$

**Step 2 — Calibrate using the standard buffer method (standard pH practice).** For a *two-buffer calibration* with a known reference buffer of pH₁ (e.g., pH = 4.00), the EMF is $E_1$, and with pH₂ = 7.00, the EMF is $E_2$. The slope is computed from:
$$S = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{\text{pH}_2 - \text{pH}_1}$$

**Step 3 — For the standard calibrated glass electrode (ideal case, $E_{\text{asy}}$ incorporated in $E^{\circ *}_{\text{glass}}$):**
$$\text{pH} = \frac{E_{\text{cell}} - E_{\text{calomel}} + E^{\circ *}_{\text{glass}}}{0.0591}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting numerical values (assuming $E^{\circ *}_{\text{glass}}$ has been determined by calibration; here we illustrate the dominant contribution):**
$$\text{pH} \approx \frac{E_{\text{cell}} - E_{\text{calomel}} - E_{\text{asy}}}{0.0591} = \frac{0.412 - 0.244 - 0.005}{0.0591}$$

$$\text{pH} = \frac{0.163}{0.0591} = 2.758 \approx 2.76 \quad (4 \text{ marks for calculation})$$

**Step 5 — Advantages of glass electrode over hydrogen electrode (2 × 1 = 2 marks):**

1. **No external gas (H₂) required:** The glass electrode has an internal reference, so it can be used in closed systems, remote locations, and in vivo measurements. The hydrogen electrode requires a continuous supply of pure hydrogen gas and a platinized platinum surface.
2. **Not poisoned by contaminants:** The glass electrode is **insensitive to oxidizing/reducing agents, dissolved oxygen, sulfides, and heavy-metal ions**, which poison the platinum surface of a hydrogen electrode. It is also robust in strong acids and bases.
3. *(Bonus — optional third advantage)* Operates in the entire 0–14 pH range with **fast response** (< 1 s in modern probes).

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\text{pH} \approx 2.76}$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

### **(a)** Compare the **Saturated, Normal, and Decinormal Calomel Electrodes**. List three advantages and two limitations of the calomel electrode when used in industrial pH measurement. (7 Marks)

### ✅ Model Answer:

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | Saturated (SCE) | Normal (NCE) | Decinormal (DCE) |
|---|---|---|---|
| KCl concentration | ~4.5 M (sat.) | 1.0 M | 0.1 M |
| Potential vs SHE (25 °C) | +0.244 V | +0.280 V | +0.334 V |
| Temperature coefficient | −0.76 mV/°C (largest) | −0.24 mV/°C | −0.07 mV/°C (smallest) |
| Stability | Most stable (KCl solid reservoir) | Moderate | Least stable (sensitive to dilution) |
| Typical use | **Industrial & lab standard** | Lab reference | Precision temperature work |

**Three Advantages of Calomel Electrode (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **High potential stability** — small drift (< 0.1 mV/day) ensures reliable readings.
2. **Wide operating range** — works in solutions with pH 0 to 14, in presence of oxidizing/reducing agents (more robust than SHE).
3. **Low polarizability** — even when small measurement currents flow, the potential is not disturbed (essential for high-impedance voltmeters).

**Two Limitations (1 mark — 0.5 each):**

1. **Mercury hazard** — toxic; not preferred in food, pharma, or in-vivo applications.
2. **Liquid-junction potential** — the porous plug introduces a small, uncertain junction potential (1–3 mV) that affects accuracy at the 0.01 pH level.

---

### **(b)** A cell is constructed with a **hydrogen indicator electrode** and a **saturated calomel reference electrode** ($E_{\text{SCE}} = +0.244$ V). The measured cell EMF at 25 °C is **0.394 V**. Calculate:

  (i) The pH of the solution. (3 Marks)
  (ii) The hydrogen ion concentration, $[\text{H}^+]$. (2 Marks)
  (iii) Will the measured EMF increase or decrease if the temperature is raised to 50 °C? Justify. (2 Marks)

### ✅ Model Answer:

**(i) pH Calculation (3 marks):**

Using the pH equation:
$$\text{pH} = \frac{E_{\text{cell}} - E_{\text{calomel}}}{0.0591}$$

Substituting:
$$\text{pH} = \frac{0.394 - 0.244}{0.0591} = \frac{0.150}{0.0591}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{pH} = 2.538 \approx 2.54\;}$$

**(ii) Hydrogen ion concentration (2 marks):**

From the definition of pH:
$$[\text{H}^+] = 10^{-\text{pH}} = 10^{-2.54}$$

$$\boxed{\;[\text{H}^+] = 2.88 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol/L} = 2.88\ \text{mM}\;}$$

**(iii) Temperature effect (2 marks):**

At elevated temperature, **two competing effects** occur:
- The **Nernst slope** $2.303RT/F$ **increases** with T (from 0.0591 V at 25 °C to 0.0641 V at 50 °C).
- The **SCE potential** **decreases** (temp. coefficient ≈ −0.76 mV/°C × 25 °C rise = −19 mV → $E_{\text{SCE,50°C}} \approx 0.244 − 0.019 = 0.225$ V).

For a fixed pH, the cell EMF at 50 °C:
$$E_{\text{cell,50°C}} = 0.225 + 0.0641 \times 2.54 = 0.225 + 0.163 = 0.388\ \text{V}$$

**Net effect:** The EMF **decreases slightly** because the SCE potential drop (~19 mV) outweighs the slope increase. The pH meter must therefore apply **temperature compensation** to display the correct pH. *(1 mark justification, 1 mark conclusion)*

$$\boxed{\;E_{\text{cell}} \text{ decreases when temperature rises from 25 °C to 50 °C}\;}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Sign convention errors** (≈ 30% of students lose 2–3 marks here): When using $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{calomel}} - E_{\text{indicator}}$, always verify the **cathode** is the calomel (reference, higher potential) and **anode** is the indicator. Confusing this flips the sign and gives a **negative pH** — a guaranteed zero.
>
> 2. **Confusing $E^{\circ}$ with $E_{\text{calomel}}$ (working potential)**: The standard potential of the calomel half-reaction is **+0.268 V** (vs SHE), but the *operative* potential of an **SCE** is **+0.244 V**. Using 0.268 V instead of 0.244 V in the pH equation shifts the answer by ~0.4 pH units.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the 0.0591 factor (vs 0.0295)**: Since 2 electrons are involved in the calomel half-reaction, many students incorrectly write $0.0295 \log[\text{Cl}^-]$ — the **2 cancels** with the power of 2 in Q, giving 0.0591 in the final result. The full derivation is required by KTU to demonstrate this cancellation.
>
> 4. **No diagram = no full marks** for construction questions. A neat, **labelled cross-section** is mandatory — examiners specifically check for mercury, calomel paste, KCl, porous plug, and platinum lead labels.
>
> 5. **Unit errors in numericals**: Always state the **Nernst slope value with units (V)** explicitly, and **retain 3–4 significant figures** during intermediate steps. Rounding 0.0591 to 0.06 is a common mark-deduction.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 High-Yield Revision Checklist — Must memorize before KTU exam:**

- ✅ **pH definition:** $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$ (dimensionless, range 0–14)
- ✅ **Calomel half-reaction:** $\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons 2\text{Hg} + 2\text{Cl}^-$
- ✅ **Nernst equation (25 °C):** $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$
- ✅ **Three operative SCE potentials** (memorize all three):
  - SCE: **+0.244 V**
  - NCE: **+0.280 V**
  - DCE: **+0.334 V**
- ✅ **Standard calomel $E^{\circ} = +0.268$ V** (used in derivation only, not in numerical pH problems)
