- ✅ **Master pH equation:** $\text{pH} = \dfrac{E_{\text{cell}} - E_{\text{calomel}}}{0.0591}$
- ✅ **Cell EMF increases linearly with pH** — slope is 59.1 mV per pH unit at 25 °C
- ✅ **Temperature dependence:** Nernst slope ∝ T; SCE has negative temp. coefficient (decreases with T)
- ✅ **Calomel parts to draw:** Pt wire → Hg → Hg₂Cl₂ paste → KCl (sat) → porous frit → external solution
- ✅ **Advantages over SHE:** No H₂ gas needed; not poisoned; works in closed systems; robust
- ✅ **Limitations:** Hg toxicity; liquid-junction potential; SCE has highest T-coefficient
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Pharma water systems, food QC, wastewater treatment, chlor-alkali, power-plant boiler feedwater
- ✅ **Glass electrode = preferred modern indicator**; **Calomel = preferred reference** (vs Ag/AgCl in field probes due to toxicity)
- ✅ **Always use high-impedance voltmeter** ($> 10^{12}\ \Omega$) — otherwise electrode polarization shifts the reading
- ✅ **Nernst slope at 50 °C** = 0.0641 V (rounded); memorize: $0.0591 \times T/298$
- ✅ **Asymmetry potential** of glass electrode must be **calibrated out** with two standard buffers (typically pH 4, 7, 10)
- ✅ **Industrial pH measurement chain:** Sensing → Amplification → Temperature compensation → ADC → Microprocessor → Display + 4–20 mA output
- ✅ **Typical cell EMF range** for full pH scale (0–14) with SCE: **0.244 V to 1.071 V** (~827 mV span)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute strategy for KTU exam:** The most frequently asked 14-mark question pattern is **(a) construction/working with diagram + (b) numerical on pH calculation**. Master the pH equation derivation (Steps 1–9 in Section 3.2) cold — you can reproduce 5–7 marks within 3 minutes by writing it down verbatim. Always draw the **labelled cross-sectional diagram** of the calomel electrode, even if the question only asks for the working — KTU examiners award 2–3 marks for the figure alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composition Analysis Using Spectroscopic Methods: Absorption, Emission, and Mass Spectrometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Composition Analysis Using Spectroscopic Methods: Absorption, Emission, and Mass Spectrometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Spectroscopy — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spectroscopy** is the branch of analytical science that studies the interaction between **electromagnetic (EM) radiation** and matter, to identify chemical species (qualitative analysis) and/or determine their concentration (quantitative analysis) in a sample.

In the context of **Industrial Instrumentation**, spectroscopic analyzers are non-destructive (or minimally invasive) online/ offline analyzers deployed in:
- Petroleum refineries (sulfur in gasoline, octane rating)
- Pharmaceutical bulk-drug plants (API purity)
- Power plants (water/steam chemistry, oil condition monitoring)
- Steel & cement plants (composition of alloys, raw mix)
- Environmental monitoring (NOx, SOx, heavy metals in flue gas)

### 1.2 The Three Master Principles

| Principle | What happens inside the instrument | Best for detecting |
|---|---|---|
| **Absorption Spectrometry** | Sample **absorbs** specific photon energies from a polychromatic beam | Trace metals, organic functional groups, water purity |
| **Emission Spectrometry** | Excited atoms/ions **emit** characteristic photons when they fall back to ground state | Alkali/alkaline earth metals, plasma elements |
| **Mass Spectrometry** | Sample is ionized; ions are separated by their **mass-to-charge ratio (m/z)** | Isotopes, complex organic mixtures, ppm/ppb impurities |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module-4 focus is *composition analysis*, meaning the emphasis is on **what** is in a process stream and **how much** of it is present — NOT on imaging or biological assays.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The "Fingerprint of Atoms"

Imagine every chemical element is a **musical instrument** with a unique pitch:
- When you **shine white light** (all frequencies) through a sodium vapour, sodium atoms **swallow** only the **yellow-orange wavelengths (≈ 589 nm)**. The light coming out has two dark lines at exactly those wavelengths — this is its **absorption fingerprint**.
- If you instead **energise** sodium (heat it in a flame), it **spits out** light at the *same* two yellow-orange wavelengths — its **emission fingerprint**.
- A **mass spectrometer** is like a **post-office sorting room** that throws every ion onto a scale and counts how many weigh 23 amu, how many weigh 24 amu, etc. — its **mass fingerprint**.

Every molecule/atom has a unique spectral/mass signature, which is why spectroscopy is the gold standard for composition analysis.

### 1.4 The Electromagnetic Spectrum — The Engineer's Map

The frequency $f$, wavelength $\lambda$, wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}$, and photon energy $E$ are linked by Planck's relation:

$$E = h\,f = \dfrac{h\,c}{\lambda} = h\,c\,\tilde{\nu}$$

where:
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$ (**Planck's constant**)
- $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$ (**speed of light**)

| Spectral Region | Wavelength | Transitions probed | Typical Industrial Instrument |
|---|---|---|---|
| UV | 100–400 nm | Electronic (valence e⁻) | UV-Vis Spectrophotometer |
| Visible | 400–700 nm | Electronic | Colorimeter, Flame Photometer |
| IR | 0.7–1000 µm | Vibrational (molecular bonds) | FTIR, NDIR gas analyzer |
| Microwave | 1 mm–1 m | Rotational | Microwave moisture analyzer |
| X-Ray | 0.01–10 nm | Inner-shell electron | XRF, XRD |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Absorption Spectrometry — The Operational Logic

**Step-by-step physics:**
1. A polychromatic source (e.g., deuterium lamp for UV, tungsten-halogen for visible) emits a continuous spectrum.
2. The beam is split by a **monochromator** (prism or grating) so that a single wavelength $\lambda$ is selected.
3. The monochromatic beam is split into two parallel paths:
   - **Reference beam** → passes through pure solvent/blank.
   - **Sample beam** → passes through the analyte cell (cuvette).
4. Two detectors measure intensities $I_0$ (reference) and $I$ (sample).
5. The instrument computes **Absorbance** $A = \log_{10}(I_0/I)$ and **Transmittance** $T = I/I_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the log?** Human perception of brightness is logarithmic (Weber-Fechner law). More importantly, log converts a multiplicative attenuation (each infinitesimal layer absorbs a fraction) into a **linear** quantity proportional to concentration — this is what makes Beer-Lambert so powerful.

### 2.2 Beer-Lambert Law (The Heart of Absorption Analysis)

$$A = \varepsilon \, b \, c$$

where:
- $A$ = absorbance (dimensionless, also called optical density, OD)
- $\varepsilon$ = **molar absorptivity** or molar extinction coefficient (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹)
- $b$ = optical path length through the sample (cm)
- $c$ = molar concentration (mol/L)

The transmittance form is:

$$T = \dfrac{I}{I_0} = 10^{-\varepsilon b c} = 10^{-A}$$

### 2.3 Emission Spectrometry — The Operational Logic

**Step-by-step physics:**
1. Liquid sample is **aspirated** into a high-temperature source (flame ≈ 2000–3000 K, or inductively coupled plasma ≈ 6000–10 000 K).
2. Solvent evaporates → solid residue dissociates into **free atoms** (atomization).
3. Thermal collisions **excite** valence electrons to higher energy levels.
4. Excited atoms spontaneously fall back, emitting photons of wavelength $\lambda$ determined by:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{h\,c}{\Delta E}$$

5. A monochromator + detector measures the intensity, which is **proportional to concentration** (within linear range).

### 2.4 Mass Spectrometry — The Operational Logic

**Step-by-step physics:**
1. Sample is introduced (liquid via HPLC, gas via GC, solid via direct probe).
2. **Ion source** converts molecules into ions:
   - Electron Ionization (EI) — hard, 70 eV electrons knock out an electron
   - Chemical Ionization (CI) — soft, reagent gas transfers a proton
   - Electrospray Ionization (ESI) — soft, for biomolecules
   - Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) — for elemental mass spec
3. **Mass analyzer** separates ions by m/z using one of:
   - Magnetic sector (deflection in magnetic field)
   - Quadrupole (resonance in oscillating electric field)
   - Time-of-Flight (TOF — kinetic energy → flight time)
   - Ion trap (3D quadrupole field traps then ejects)
4. **Detector** (electron multiplier, Faraday cup) counts ions at each m/z.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorise for the university exam.**

| # | Equation | Symbol meaning | Where used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \varepsilon b c$ | Absorbance law | UV-Vis quantitation |
| 2 | $T = 10^{-A}$ | Transmittance ↔ Absorbance | Photometric calculations |
| 3 | $I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon b c}$ | Beer-Lambert intensity form | Quantitative analysis |
| 4 | $A_{mix} = \sum_i \varepsilon_i b c_i$ | Additivity of absorbance | Multi-component mixtures |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h\,c}{\Delta E}$ | Photon wavelength ↔ transition energy | Emission/Absorption peak ID |
| 6 | $\nu = \dfrac{R\,Z^2_{\text{eff}}}{n^2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n_1^2}-\dfrac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$ | Rydberg formula (H-like atoms) | Atomic spectroscopy |
| 7 | $r = \dfrac{m\,v}{q\,B}$ | Cyclotron radius in magnetic sector | Mass spectrometer |
| 8 | $\dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{B^2 r^2}{2\,V}$ | Mass-to-charge from sector MS | Magnetic sector MS design |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{m}{\Delta m} = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{r_1 + r_2}{r_2 - r_1}$ | Resolution of double-focusing MS | Mass spec performance |
| 10 | $t = L\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{2\,z\,e\,V}}$ | Time-of-flight in TOF-MS | TOF mass spectrometer |

**Constants to memorise:**
- **Planck's constant $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s**
- **Speed of light $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s**
- **Rydberg constant $R = 1.097 \times 10^{7}$ m⁻¹**
- **Avogadro's number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹**

### 2.6 Engineering Utility in Real Plants

- **Refineries:** NIR (near-infrared) absorption analyzers determine octane number, cetane number, Reid vapour pressure of crude in real time on a slipstream — saving millions in lab cost.
- **Pharma:** FTIR + mass spec together confirm identity & purity of APIs per **USP/EP** pharmacopoeia.
- **Power Plants:** On-line ICP-MS detects ppb-level wear metals (Fe, Cr, Ni) in turbine lube oil to predict failure.
- **Steel Plants:** OES (Optical Emission Spectrometer) gives full alloy composition in **<30 seconds** during steel tapping.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Beer-Lambert Law

We derive the absorbance as a function of concentration and path length by considering an infinitesimally thin slice of the absorbing medium.

**Start with the assumption:**
An infinitesimal thickness $\mathrm{d}x$ of the absorbing medium attenuates the beam intensity $I$ by an amount proportional to $I$ and to $\mathrm{d}x$.

**Step 1 — Set up the differential equation:**
The fraction of photons absorbed in the slice $\mathrm{d}x$ is proportional to the number of absorbing molecules encountered, which is proportional to the concentration $c$ and the slice thickness $\mathrm{d}x$. Hence:

$$-\mathrm{d}I = k\,c\,I\,\mathrm{d}x$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**
Move all intensity terms to the left and all length terms to the right:

$$\dfrac{\mathrm{d}I}{I} = -k\,c\,\mathrm{d}x$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides across the full cell of length $b$** (limits: $I_0$ to $I$ on the left, $0$ to $b$ on the right):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}I}{I} = -k\,c\int_{0}^{b}\mathrm{d}x$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) = -k\,c\,b$$

**Step 5 — Convert natural log to base-10 logarithm** by using $\ln x = 2.303\,\log_{10}x$ and absorbing $2.303$ into the constant:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \dfrac{k}{2.303}\,c\,b$$

**Step 6 — Identify the constant** as the molar absorptivity $\varepsilon = k/(2.303)$, and recognise the left side as absorbance $A$:

$$\boxed{A = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon\,b\,c}$$

This is the **Beer-Lambert Law** — a *linear* relationship between absorbance and concentration, valid when:
- $c$ is low (typically $< 0.01$ M)
- Monochromatic light is used
- No chemical association/dissociation of the analyte
- No scattering, no fluorescence from the sample

### 3.2 Quantitative Worked Example — Determination of Iron in Water

A water sample is treated with 1,10-phenanthroline to form a coloured complex with molar absorptivity $\varepsilon = 11{,}100\ \text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$ at 511 nm. A 1.00 cm cell is used. The measured absorbance is $A = 0.445$.

**Step 1 — Rearrange Beer-Lambert to solve for $c$:**

$$c = \dfrac{A}{\varepsilon\,b}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (note units):**

$$c = \dfrac{0.445}{(11{,}100\ \text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1})\,(1.00\ \text{cm})}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$c = 4.009 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mol/L}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mg/L** using atomic mass of Fe = 55.845 g/mol:

$$c_{\text{mg/L}} = 4.009 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mol/L} \times 55{,}845\ \text{mg/mol} = 2.24\ \text{mg/L}$$

This is well above the WHO drinking-water limit of 0.3 mg/L — **the water is non-compliant**.

### 3.3 Derivation of Quadrupole Mass Filter Operation

A quadrupole consists of four parallel cylindrical rods. Opposite rods are connected electrically; an RF voltage $V\cos(\omega t)$ superimposed on a DC voltage $U$ is applied.

**Step 1 — Equation of motion** (Mathieu equation) for an ion of mass $m$ and charge $e$ along the $x$-axis:

$$\dfrac{\mathrm{d}^2 x}{\mathrm{d}t^2} + \dfrac{2 e}{m r_0^2}\!\left(U - V\cos\omega t\right)x = 0$$

**Step 2 — Stability condition:** Ions survive only when the dimensionless parameters $a$ and $q$ lie within the first stability region of the Mathieu stability diagram:

$$a = \dfrac{4\,e\,U}{m\,r_0^2\,\omega^2}, \qquad q = \dfrac{2\,e\,V}{m\,r_0^2\,\omega^2}$$

**Step 3 — For a fixed $\omega$, $U$, $V$:** Only one specific m/z has a stable trajectory through to the detector. All others have unstable trajectories (oscillation amplitude grows exponentially) and strike the rods.

**Step 4 — Scanning $U$ and $V$** while keeping $U/V$ constant sweeps through the mass spectrum linearly.

**Step 5 — Resolution of a quadrupole MS:**

$$R = \dfrac{m}{\Delta m} \approx \text{constant} \quad \text{(typically unit resolution, } R \approx 1000\text{)}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Magnetic Sector Mass Spectrometer

A charged ion accelerated through potential $V$ acquires kinetic energy:

$$\frac{1}{2}mv^2 = z\,e\,V \quad\Longrightarrow\quad v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,z\,e\,V}{m}}$$

Entering a uniform magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to its velocity, it follows a circular path of radius $r$ where the centripetal force equals the Lorentz force:

$$\dfrac{m v^2}{r} = z\,e\,v\,B$$

**Eliminate $v$** by substituting the kinetic-energy expression:

$$\dfrac{m}{r}\cdot\dfrac{2\,z\,e\,V}{m} = z\,e\,v\,B$$

$$\dfrac{2\,V}{r} = v\,B \quad\Longrightarrow\quad v = \dfrac{2\,V}{r\,B}$$

Squaring and using $v^2 = 2z e V / m$:

$$\dfrac{4\,V^2}{r^2\,B^2} = \dfrac{2\,z\,e\,V}{m}$$

**Solve for m/z:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{B^2 r^2}{2\,e\,V}}$$

This is the **fundamental equation of the magnetic-sector mass spectrometer**. By varying $B$ (electromagnet) or $V$ at constant $r$ (fixed-radius instrument), ions of different m/z are sequentially focused onto the detector.

### 3.5 Time-of-Flight (TOF) Mass Spectrometer

The same accelerating potential $V$ gives all ions the **same kinetic energy**:

$$\frac{1}{2}m v^2 = z e V \quad\Longrightarrow\quad v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,z\,e\,V}{m}}$$

After drifting through a field-free tube of length $L$, the arrival time at the detector is:

$$\boxed{t = \dfrac{L}{v} = L\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{2\,z\,e\,V}}}$$

Lighter ions arrive first. The m/z ratio is recovered from the measured time $t$:

$$\dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{2\,e\,V\,t^2}{L^2}$$

### 3.6 Numerical Problem — TOF-MS

A TOF-MS has a drift length $L = 1.000$ m and an accelerating voltage $V = 5000$ V. Find the time taken by a singly-charged ion of mass 100 amu to reach the detector. ($1\ \text{amu} = 1.6605 \times 10^{-27}\ \text{kg}$.)

**Step 1 — Compute m in kg:**

$$m = 100 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} = 1.6605 \times 10^{-25}\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the velocity:**

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot (1)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(5000)}{1.6605 \times 10^{-25}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.602 \times 10^{-15}}{1.6605 \times 10^{-25}}} = \sqrt{9.647 \times 10^{9}} = 9.822 \times 10^{4}\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute flight time:**

$$t = \dfrac{L}{v} = \dfrac{1.000}{9.822 \times 10^{4}} = 1.018 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s} = 10.18\ \mu\text{s}$$

This ultra-fast transit is why **TOF-MS achieves > 30 000 spectra/second** — useful for GC×GC and imaging MS.

### 3.7 Limit-of-Detection Comparison Across Spectrometers

| Technique | Typical LOD | Linear Dynamic Range |
|---|---|---|
| UV-Vis Absorption | 10⁻⁶ M | 2–3 decades |
| AAS (flame) | 1 ppm | 2 decades |
| AAS (graphite furnace) | 1 ppb | 1 decade |
| ICP-OES (emission) | 1–10 ppb | 5–6 decades |
| ICP-MS | 1–10 ppt | 8 decades |
| FTIR | 0.1 % | 3 decades |
| NMR | 10⁻⁴ M | 1–2 decades |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ICP-MS dominates trace analysis:** it combines the atomisation efficiency of the 8000 K plasma with the elemental specificity and ultra-low background of mass spectrometry — pushing detection limits down to **parts-per-trillion (10⁻¹²)**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — UV-Vis Spectrophotometer (Double-Beam)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["L1 Light Source Deuterium or Tungsten Halogen"]
    MONO["M1 Monochromator Prism or Grating"]
    CHOP["CH Chopper Rotating Mirror Splits Beam"]
    SPLT["BS Beam Splitter"]
    REF["REF Reference Cuvette Pure Solvent"]
    SAMP["SMP Sample Cuvette Analyte Solution"]
    DREF["D1 Reference Detector Photodiode"]
    DSMP["D2 Sample Detector Photodiode"]
    AMP["AMP Log Ratio Amplifier Computes A log I0 by I"]
    DSP["DSP Microprocessor Display and Output"]

    SRC --> MONO
    MONO --> CHOP
    CHOP --> SPLT
    SPLT --> REF
    SPLT --> SAMP
    REF --> DREF
    SAMP --> DSMP
    DREF --> AMP
    DSMP --> AMP
    AMP --> DSP
```

**Functional description:** The chopper alternately routes the monochromatic beam through the reference and sample cuvettes. The two detectors produce AC signals whose log-ratio is the absorbance. Double-beam design **automatically cancels** lamp intensity drift, source aging, and solvent absorption — critical for long-term industrial use.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometer (AAS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HCL["HCL Hollow Cathode Lamp Element Specific"]
    MOD["MOD Modulator 285 Hz Chopper"]
    ATOM["ATOM Atomiser Flame or Graphite Furnace"]
    MONO2["M2 Monochromator"]
    PMT["PMT Photomultiplier Tube"]
    LOCK["LOCK Lock in Amplifier Tuned to 285 Hz"]
    READ["READ Readout Concentration Display"]

    HCL --> MOD --> ATOM --> MONO2 --> PMT --> LOCK --> READ
```

**Functional description:** The **hollow cathode lamp (HCL)** provides element-specific sharp line emission (e.g., 248.3 nm for Fe). After passing through the atomiser, only the *modulated* lamp signal is amplified by the lock-in — **flame emission noise and stray light are rejected**.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Mass Spectrometer (Generic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INJ["INJ Sample Inlet GC or LC or Direct Probe"]
    ION["ION Ion Source EI or ESI or ICP"]
    ACC["ACC Accelerator Lenses 0 to 10 kV"]
    ANA["ANA Mass Analyzer Magnetic Sector or Quadrupole or TOF"]
    DET["DET Detector Electron Multiplier or Faraday Cup"]
    REC["REC Recorder and Data System"]

    INJ --> ION --> ACC --> ANA --> DET --> REC
```

**Functional description:** Ions formed in the source are extracted, accelerated to a known kinetic energy, then separated in the analyzer according to their **m/z ratio**. Only one m/z at a time reaches the detector (for scanning instruments) or all simultaneously (for array detectors in TOF/Orbitrap).

### 4.4 Block Diagram — ICP Optical Emission Spectrometer (ICP-OES)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NEB["NEB Nebuliser Pneumatic or Ultrasonic"]
    SPR["SPR Spray Chamber"]
    TOR["TOR Torch Argon Plasma 8000 K"]
    POLY["POLY Polychromator Echelle Grating"]
    AR1["AR1 CID Array Detector Channel 1"]
    AR2["AR2 CID Array Detector Channel 2"]
    ARn["ARn CID Array Detector Channel n"]
    CPU["CPU Computer Multi element Display"]

    NEB --> SPR --> TOR --> POLY
    POLY --> AR1
    POLY --> AR2
    POLY --> ARn
    AR1 --> CPU
    AR2 --> CPU
    ARn --> CPU
```

**Functional description:** The argon plasma atomises and excites virtually **all elements** in the periodic table simultaneously. The echelle polychromator + CID array detector captures the entire spectrum in one reading — making ICP-OES a true **simultaneous multi-element analyzer** (up to 70 elements in < 60 s).

### 4.5 Spectral Coverage Diagram — Where Each Technique Operates

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["GAMMA Less than 0.01 nm"]
    B["X RAY 0.01 to 10 nm"]
    C["UV 100 to 400 nm"]
    D["VIS 400 to 700 nm"]
    E["NIR 0.7 to 5 micrometre"]
    F["MIR 5 to 30 micrometre"]
    G["FIR 30 to 1000 micrometre"]
    H["MICROWAVE More than 1 mm"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H
```

**Map of dominant industrial analyzers to spectral regions:**
- **X-ray (B):** XRF for sulfur-in-oil, lead in paint, alloy sorting
- **UV-Vis (C, D):** colorimeters, spectrophotometers, fluorimeters
- **NIR (E):** process analyzers for moisture, protein, octane number
- **MIR (F):** FTIR — functional-group identification, NDIR gas analyzers
- **Microwave (H):** moisture in solids/coal/cement
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
State and explain Beer-Lambert Law. List any **two** limitations of the law.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** "When a beam of monochromatic radiation passes through a homogeneous absorbing medium, the absorbance of the medium is directly proportional to the concentration of the absorbing species and the path length of the radiation through the medium." Mathematically:

$$A = \varepsilon b c$$

- **Limitations (2 × 1 = 2 marks):**
  1. **Deviation at high concentrations (> 0.01 M):** Intermolecular interactions change the absorptivity, breaking linearity. Absorbance vs. concentration curve flattens.
  2. **Stray-light and polychromatic radiation effects:** Real instruments have finite spectral bandwidth; if $\varepsilon$ varies strongly across the band, the Beer-Lambert law fails.
  3. *(Optional third for reference)* Chemical deviations occur when the analyte dissociates/associates with pH change.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
Differentiate between **flame emission spectrometry (FES)** and **atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS)** in terms of (i) principle, (ii) radiation source, (iii) typical application.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | FES / Flame Photometry | AAS |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Principle | Measures photons **emitted** by excited atoms in flame | Measures photons **absorbed** by ground-state atoms from HCL |
| (ii) Source | Flame itself is the excitation source | Hollow-cathode lamp (element-specific) |
| (iii) Application | Na, K, Li, Ca in clinical/soil samples | Trace metals (Fe, Cu, Zn, Pb) at ppm level |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **double-beam UV-Visible spectrophotometer**. Discuss the role of the monochromator and detector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sample of potassium permanganate solution shows an absorbance of 0.752 at 525 nm in a 1.00 cm cell. The molar absorptivity of KMnO₄ at 525 nm is $2040\ \text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$. Calculate the concentration of the solution in (i) mol/L and (ii) mg/L. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (already shown in §4.1) — 1 mark for the diagram.**
The major sub-systems are:

1. **Light Source (1 mark):** A combination of **deuterium lamp** (190–400 nm, UV) and **tungsten-halogen lamp** (350–2500 nm, visible). The lamp housing has a fused-silica or quartz window to transmit UV.

2. **Monochromator (2 marks):** A prism or, more commonly, a **reflection grating** mounted on a turntable. It disperses the polychromatic light and selects a narrow band ($\Delta\lambda \approx 1$–$2$ nm). Slit width controls the band-pass — narrower slit = better resolution but less energy.

3. **Beam splitter / Chopper (1 mark):** A rotating sector mirror alternately directs the monochromatic beam through the **reference** cell (pure solvent) and the **sample** cell (analyte). This allows simultaneous (or rapidly alternating) measurement of $I_0$ and $I$.

4. **Detector (1 mark):** A **photodiode**, **photomultiplier tube (PMT)**, or **CCD array**. PMT is preferred for low-light applications because of its internal gain ($\times 10^6$).

5. **Signal processing & display (1 mark):** Log-ratio amplifier computes $A = \log(I_0/I)$; a microprocessor drives the LCD/PC display, performs multi-wavelength scans, and outputs a 4–20 mA industrial signal.

**Advantages of double-beam design:** cancels lamp drift, compensates for solvent absorption, enables long-term stability.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $A = 0.752$, $b = 1.00\ \text{cm}$, $\varepsilon = 2040\ \text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$, $M_{\text{KMnO}_4} = 158.034\ \text{g/mol}$.

**Part (i) — Concentration in mol/L (3 marks):**

From Beer-Lambert law:

$$c = \dfrac{A}{\varepsilon\,b}$$

$$c = \dfrac{0.752}{(2040)(1.00)} = 3.686 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol/L}$$

**Part (ii) — Concentration in mg/L (4 marks):**

Convert using molar mass:

$$c_{\text{mg/L}} = c_{\text{mol/L}} \times M_{\text{g/mol}} \times 1000\ \dfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{g}}$$

$$c_{\text{mg/L}} = 3.686 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol/L} \times 158.034\ \text{g/mol} \times 1000\ \text{mg/g}$$

$$\boxed{c = 58.27\ \text{mg/L}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **quadrupole mass spectrometer** with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for the m/z ratio of ions transmitted. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A magnetic-sector mass spectrometer has a flight radius of $r = 12.00$ cm. With an accelerating voltage $V = 4000$ V and a magnetic field $B = 0.500$ T, find the m/z value of the ion reaching the detector. If the radius is varied to 12.05 cm while $V$ and $B$ are kept constant, what is the new m/z? Comment on the **resolution** of the instrument. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Quadrupole MS Working (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** A quadrupole mass filter consists of **four parallel metal rods** of radius $r_0$, arranged symmetrically around the ion path. Opposite rods are electrically connected; an RF voltage $V\cos\omega t$ is superimposed on a DC voltage $U$. Ions entering along the rod axis experience a hyperbolic electric field.

**Operation (3 marks):** The trajectory of an ion is described by the Mathieu equation. For given $U$, $V$, $\omega$, only ions of one specific m/z have stable trajectories (bounded oscillations) and reach the detector. All other m/z values have exponentially growing oscillations and are lost to the rods. By scanning $U$ and $V$ simultaneously (keeping $U/V$ constant), a **mass spectrum** is produced.

**m/z expression (2 marks):** From the stability limits of the Mathieu equation, the transmission condition gives:

$$\dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{4\,V}{r_0^2\,\omega^2} \cdot q_{\max}$$

where $q_{\max} \approx 0.706$ defines the apex of the first stability region. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{m}{z} \approx \dfrac{2.83\,V}{r_0^2\,\omega^2}}$$

---

**(b) Magnetic Sector Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply the magnetic sector equation (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{B^2 r^2}{2\,V}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values for the first radius (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{(0.500)^2(0.1200)^2}{2(4000)} = \dfrac{0.25 \times 0.0144}{8000}$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{m}{z}_1 = 4.50 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{kg/C}}$$

To express in atomic mass units per elementary charge: divide by $1.6605 \times 10^{-27}\ \text{kg/amu}$:

$$\dfrac{m}{z}_1 = \dfrac{4.50 \times 10^{-7}}{1.6605 \times 10^{-27}} = 2.71 \times 10^{20}\ \text{amu/C}$$

Since the elementary charge is $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, for $z = 1$:

$$m_1 = 2.71 \times 10^{20} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 43.4\ \text{amu}$$

(For $z=1$, this would correspond to a singly-charged ion of mass ≈ 43 amu.)

**Step 3 — Compute m/z at the new radius (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{m}{z}_2 = \dfrac{(0.500)^2(0.1205)^2}{2(4000)} = \dfrac{0.25 \times 0.014520}{8000} = 4.5375 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{kg/C}$$

**Step 4 — Resolution (2 marks):**

$$R = \dfrac{m_1}{\Delta m} = \dfrac{43.4}{43.4 - 43.5} \approx \text{very high (unit resolution easily achievable)}$$

A 0.05 cm change in radius separates ions that differ by ~1 amu, confirming that the **magnetic sector MS is a high-resolution instrument** ($R > 10{,}000$), ideal for isotope-ratio measurements and exact-mass determination.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Units trap:** In Beer-Lambert problems, forgetting the $1000$ factor when converting mol/L to mg/L costs **2 marks** in every calculation.
> 2. **Wrong constant:** Always verify whether the formula uses **$\varepsilon$** (molar absorptivity, with concentration in mol/L) or **$a$** (specific absorptivity, with concentration in g/L). Mixing them up is a guaranteed **-3 marks**.
> 3. **MS derivation:** When deriving $m/z = B^2 r^2 / 2V$, you **must** show the substitution step $v^2 = 2zeV/m$ into $mv^2/r = zevB$. Skipping this loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Block diagrams:** Drawing unlabelled boxes loses marks. Always label every block, including the **monochromator**, **detector type**, and **signal flow arrows**.
> 5. **AAS vs FES:** Confusing *flame photometer* (emission, used for Na/K) with *AAS* (absorption, used for transition metals) is a common 3-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Beer-Lambert Law** $A = \varepsilon b c$ is the **foundation** of all quantitative absorption analysis — valid only at low concentration with monochromatic light.
- **Transmittance** is $T = I/I_0 = 10^{-A}$; **Absorbance** is logarithmic; the two are related by $A = -\log_{10} T$.
- **UV-Vis** → electronic transitions; **FTIR** → vibrational transitions; **ICP-OES** → atomic emission in plasma; **AAS** → atomic absorption from HCL.
- **AAS requires a separate HCL for every element** — a major limitation. ICP-OES / ICP-MS can analyse **70+ elements** on one setup.
- **Magnetic sector MS** equation: $m/z = B^2 r^2 / 2V$. Resolution improves with larger radius.
- **TOF-MS** equation: $t = L\sqrt{m/(2zeV)}$. Lighter ions arrive first.
- **Quadrupole MS** transmits only one m/z at a time — robust, compact, but unit-resolution only.
- **FTIR advantage** over dispersive IR: **Felgett (multiplex) advantage** — all IR wavelengths measured simultaneously → much better SNR.
- **ICP-MS is the most sensitive** elemental technique (ppt level), but most expensive (≈ ₹1–2 crore) and needs high-purity argon.
- **Industrial considerations:** NIR analyzers are the workhorse for **on-line** process monitoring because fibre-optic probes can reach into reactors; FTIR is preferred for **off-line QC**; AAS/ICP-MS for **trace-level** environmental and pharmaceutical work.
- **Resolution $R$** is the key performance metric for any spectrometer: $R = \lambda/\Delta\lambda$ (optical) or $R = m/\Delta m$ (mass).
- **Wavenumber** $\tilde{\nu} = 1/\lambda$ is preferred over wavelength in IR spectroscopy because it is **directly proportional to energy**.
- **Why logarithm?** Because attenuation is multiplicative through each infinitesimal layer, but absorbance is the **linear additive** quantity — that is the deep mathematical reason Beer-Lambert is so elegant.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Solids by X-Ray Diffraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Solids by X-Ray Diffraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **X-Ray Diffraction (XRD)** is a non-destructive analytical technique used to characterize the crystalline structure, phase composition, lattice parameters, and crystallite size of solid materials. It relies on the constructive interference (diffraction) of monochromatic X-rays interacting with the regularly spaced atomic planes within a crystal lattice, governed by **Bragg's Law**: $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$.

**X-rays** are a form of **electromagnetic radiation** with wavelengths in the range of approximately **0.01 nm to 10 nm**, which is fortuitously comparable to the typical interatomic spacings in crystalline solids (~0.1 nm to 0.3 nm). This scale match is the fundamental physical reason why X-rays are the ideal probe for analyzing solids at the atomic level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** XRD is listed under *Module 4* as a primary instrumental method for solids analysis. Students must master both **qualitative phase identification** and **quantitative crystallographic parameter estimation**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tape Measure" View of Crystals

Imagine you have a wall covered in **identical, perfectly evenly spaced bricks**. Now suppose you shine a flashlight against this wall at various angles and observe the reflected light. Because the bricks are evenly spaced, certain angles will produce a bright, focused reflection (like a mirror), while other angles will produce a dim, scattered reflection.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of X-rays as an extremely tiny "tape measure" whose wavelength is almost exactly the same size as the spacing between atoms in a crystal. When the X-ray "tape measure" fits perfectly with the gap between atomic planes (i.e., the path difference equals an integer number of wavelengths), you get a **bright constructive interference peak** — this is the diffraction signal. By measuring *where* these peaks appear, scientists can deduce the exact spacing between atomic planes.

| Quantity | Real-World Tape Measure | X-Ray Diffraction |
|---|---|---|
| Probe | Physical ruler | **X-ray beam (λ ≈ 0.154 nm for Cu Kα)** |
| Object measured | Brick wall | **Crystal lattice (d-spacing)** |
| Detection | Visual reflection | **Geiger / Scintillation counter** |
| Information obtained | Brick size | **d-spacing, lattice parameter, phase, crystallite size** |

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and standard values are **must-remember** for KTU examinations:

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$
- **X-ray wavelength range:** **0.01 nm – 10 nm** (Hard X-rays for diffraction: **0.05 nm – 0.25 nm**)
- **Standard Cu Kα radiation:** $\lambda = 1.5406\ \text{\AA}$
- **Typical interatomic spacing:** **1 – 3 Å (0.1 – 0.3 nm)**
- **Bragg angle range used:** $2\theta \approx 10^\circ - 150^\circ$

---

## 1.4 Role of XRD in Industrial Instrumentation

> [!NOTE]
> XRD is a **primary characterization tool** in industries dealing with metals, ceramics, polymers, pharmaceuticals, semiconductors, and nanomaterials. It provides:
> - **Phase identification** (qualitative — which compound is present?)
> - **Lattice parameter measurement** (quantitative — how big is the unit cell?)
> - **Crystallite / particle size estimation** (via Scherrer equation)
> - **Residual stress analysis**
> - **Crystal structure determination** (cubic, tetragonal, hexagonal, etc.)
> - **Degree of crystallinity / amorphous content**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generation of X-Rays (Coolidge Tube)

Modern laboratory X-rays are produced in a **Coolidge X-ray tube**, which consists of:

1. **Cathode (filament):** Tungsten wire heated to ~2500 K → emits electrons via thermionic emission.
2. **Anode (target):** Water-cooled metal block (Cu, Mo, Co, Fe, Cr).
3. **High voltage:** 30–50 kV applied between cathode and anode → accelerates electrons.

When high-energy electrons strike the target, two types of X-ray spectra are produced:

| Spectrum Type | Origin | Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous (Bremsstrahlung)** | Deceleration of electrons in Coulomb field of target nuclei | Broad wavelength range; depends on accelerating voltage |
| **Characteristic (K, L, M series)** | Electron transitions between inner atomic shells of target atoms | Sharp peaks at specific wavelengths (e.g., Cu Kα at 1.5406 Å) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For diffraction experiments, we need **monochromatic** X-rays. This is achieved by using a **filter** (e.g., Ni filter for Cu Kα) or a **crystal monochromator** to isolate the Kα line.

---

## 2.2 Crystal Planes & Miller Indices

To describe the orientation of atomic planes in a crystal, we use **Miller Indices**, denoted as **(hkl)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Step-by-step construction of Miller Indices:**
> 1. Identify the intercepts of the plane on the **x, y, z** axes (in units of lattice parameter $a$).
> 2. Take the **reciprocals** of these intercepts.
> 3. Reduce to the **smallest integer ratio**.
> 4. Enclose in parentheses: $(h\ k\ l)$.

**Examples:**
- Plane intercepting axes at $(1, \infty, \infty)$ → $(1\ 0\ 0)$
- Plane intercepting axes at $(1, 1, 1)$ → $(1\ 1\ 1)$
- Plane intercepting axes at $(1/2, 1, \infty)$ → $(2\ 1\ 0)$

---

## 2.3 Bragg's Law — The Heart of XRD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bragg's Law (W.H. Bragg & W.L. Bragg, 1913):** When X-rays strike a set of parallel atomic planes with interplanar spacing $d$ at a glancing angle $\theta$, constructive interference occurs when:
> $$n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$$
> where $n$ is an integer (order of diffraction), $\lambda$ is the wavelength, and $\theta$ is the **Bragg angle**.

**Physical Interpretation:**
- X-rays are **partially reflected** by successive atomic planes.
- The **path difference** between reflections from successive planes is $2d\sin\theta$.
- For constructive interference, this path difference must equal $n\lambda$.

---

## 2.4 Interplanar Spacing for Cubic Crystals

For a **cubic crystal system** with lattice parameter $a$, the spacing between planes with Miller indices $(h\ k\ l)$ is:

$$d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^{2} + k^{2} + l^{2}}}$$

For other crystal systems:
- **Tetragonal:** $\frac{1}{d^{2}} = \dfrac{h^{2}+k^{2}}{a^{2}} + \dfrac{l^{2}}{c^{2}}$
- **Hexagonal:** $\dfrac{1}{d^{2}} = \dfrac{4}{3}\!\left(\dfrac{h^{2}+hk+k^{2}}{a^{2}}\right) + \dfrac{l^{2}}{c^{2}}$
- **Orthorhombic:** $\dfrac{1}{d^{2}} = \dfrac{h^{2}}{a^{2}} + \dfrac{k^{2}}{b^{2}} + \dfrac{l^{2}}{c^{2}}$

---

## 2.5 Selection Rules for Diffraction

Not all $(hkl)$ planes produce diffraction peaks — selection rules depend on the **Bravais lattice type**:

| Lattice Type | Allowed Reflections | Forbidden Reflections |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Cubic (SC)** | All $(hkl)$ | None |
| **Body-Centered Cubic (BCC)** | $h + k + l = \text{even}$ | $h + k + l = \text{odd}$ |
| **Face-Centered Cubic (FCC)** | $h, k, l$ all odd **or** all even | Mixed (e.g., $(1\ 0\ 0)$, $(1\ 1\ 0)$) |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | **Bragg's Law** | $\lambda$ in nm, $d$ in nm, $\theta$ in degrees |
| 2 | $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}}$ | Interplanar spacing (cubic) | $a$ in Å or nm |
| 3 | $\sin^{2}\theta = \dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{4a^{2}}(h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2})$ | Combined Bragg + cubic $d$ | Used for lattice parameter estimation |
| 4 | $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV} = \dfrac{12400}{V(\text{V})}\ \text{\AA}$ | Duane-Hunt limit (continuous X-ray cutoff) | $V$ = accelerating voltage |
| 5 | $D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$ | **Scherrer Equation** (crystallite size) | $K \approx 0.9$, $\beta$ = FWHM in radians |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\beta}{4\tan\theta}$ | **Strain** from line broadening | — |
| 7 | $\rho = \dfrac{n M}{N_{A} a^{3}}$ | **Density** of cubic unit cell | $n$ = atoms/cell, $M$ = molar mass |
| 8 | $\lambda = \dfrac{2a\sin\theta}{\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}}$ | Wavelength from known crystal | Used in X-ray spectroscopy |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** When using the Scherrer equation, $\beta$ must be in **radians**, not degrees! Convert using $\beta(\text{rad}) = \beta(\text{deg}) \times \pi/180$.

---

## 2.7 Experimental Methods of XRD

### 2.7.1 Laue Method (Single Crystal, Stationary)
- **Setup:** Polychromatic (white) X-ray beam + **stationary single crystal** + photographic film.
- **Output:** **Laue spots** arranged on the film in patterns that reveal the symmetry of the crystal.
- **Application:** Determining crystal orientation and symmetry.

### 2.7.2 Rotating Crystal Method (Single Crystal, Moving)
- **Setup:** Monochromatic X-ray beam + **rotating single crystal** + cylindrical photographic film.
- **Output:** Diffraction spots arranged in **layer lines**.
- **Application:** Determining unit cell parameters and structure.

### 2.7.3 Powder (Debye–Scherrer) Method (Polycrystalline)
- **Setup:** Monochromatic X-ray beam + **finely powdered (polycrystalline) sample** in a thin capillary tube + strip / cylindrical film.
- **Output:** **Concentric rings** (Debye rings) on the film — each ring corresponds to a specific $(hkl)$ family.
- **Advantage:** No need for a single crystal; works with bulk powder samples.

### 2.7.4 Powder Diffractometer (Modern, Most Common)
- **Setup:** Monochromatic X-ray beam + flat powder sample + **scintillation / solid-state detector** rotating on a goniometer circle.
- **Output:** Plot of **intensity vs. $2\theta$** (the famous XRD pattern with sharp peaks).
- **Advantage:** Quantitative, fast, automated peak detection.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where XRD matters in industry:**
> - **Metallurgy:** Identification of alloy phases (e.g., Fe-α vs Fe-γ).
> - **Pharmaceuticals:** Polymorph detection — critical because different crystal forms of the same drug have different bioavailabilities.
> - **Cement industry:** Quantitative phase analysis of alite, belite, aluminate phases.
> - **Semiconductor QA:** Verifying epitaxial layer crystallinity and strain.
> - **Nanotechnology:** Estimating nanoparticle size via Scherrer equation.
> - **Geology / mining:** Mineral identification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bragg's Law

**Statement to Prove:** When X-rays of wavelength $\lambda$ strike parallel atomic planes separated by distance $d$ at a glancing angle $\theta$, constructive interference occurs if $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$.

### Setting Up the Geometry

Consider two parallel atomic planes **P** (top) and **Q** (bottom) separated by perpendicular distance $d$. A monochromatic, parallel X-ray beam strikes the planes at a glancing angle $\theta$ (measured from the plane surface).

The incident rays are **AB** and **CD**, where:
- **AB** strikes the top plane **P** at point **B**.
- **CD** strikes the bottom plane **Q** at point **D**.

The diffracted rays emerge along **BF** and **DH**, making the same angle $\theta$ with the planes (law of reflection).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Drop perpendiculars from **B** onto the incident ray **CD** (meeting at point **G**) and from **D** onto the diffracted ray **BF** (meeting at point **H**).

**Step 2:** Identify the extra path travelled by ray **CD → D → DH** compared to **AB → B → BF**:

$$\text{Path difference} = (GD + DH)$$

**Step 3:** From the geometry of right triangles **GBD** and **BHD**, with $\angle GBD = \angle BHD = \theta$ and hypotenuse $BD = d/\sin\theta$:

$$GD = BD \sin\theta = \frac{d}{\sin\theta} \cdot \sin\theta$$

Wait — applying trigonometry correctly to the triangles formed by the perpendicular from **D** to the planes:

Since the angle between the incident ray and the plane is $\theta$, the angle between the incident ray and the **normal** is $(90^\circ - \theta)$.

$$GD = BD \cdot \cos(90^\circ - \theta) = BD \cdot \sin\theta$$

But from triangle **BDD'** (where **D'** is the projection of **D** onto plane P):

$$BD = \frac{d}{\sin\theta}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
GD &= BD \sin\theta = \frac{d}{\sin\theta} \cdot \sin\theta = d \cdot \frac{\sin\theta}{\sin\theta} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Let me redo this more carefully:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{From triangle geometry:} \quad BD &= \frac{d}{\sin\theta} \\
GD &= BD \cos\theta = \frac{d \cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \frac{d}{\tan\theta}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Similarly, for the reflected ray:

$$\begin{aligned}
DH &= BD \cos\theta = \frac{d \cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \frac{d}{\tan\theta}
\end{aligned}$$

Hmm — this is incorrect. Let me reconsider with the correct classical derivation:

For a ray striking at **glancing angle** $\theta$ (from the plane), the angle of incidence measured from the **normal** is $(90^\circ - \theta)$. The perpendicular distance between the two rays **CD** and **AB** before reflection is:

$$GD = CD' = d \sin\theta$$

After reflection, the perpendicular distance is:

$$DH = D'H = d \sin\theta$$

**Step 5:** Therefore the **total extra path length** for the lower ray:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= GD + DH = d\sin\theta + d\sin\theta \\
\Delta &= 2d\sin\theta
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** For **constructive interference**, this path difference must equal an **integer multiple of the wavelength**:

$$\Delta = n\lambda \quad \text{where}\quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 7:** Equating:

$$\boxed{\,n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta\,} \quad \text{— Bragg's Law}$$

**Step 8:** Validity range: Since $\vert\sin\theta\vert \leq 1$, we must have $n\lambda \leq 2d$, meaning XRD is only useful when $\lambda \leq 2d$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Interplanar Spacing for Cubic Systems

**Given:** A cubic unit cell with edge length $a$ and a plane with Miller indices $(h\ k\ l)$ having intercepts $\frac{a}{h}$, $\frac{a}{k}$, $\frac{a}{l}$ on the axes.

**To Prove:** $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}}$

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider the $(h\ k\ l)$ plane. Its equation in intercept form is:

$$\frac{x}{a/h} + \frac{y}{a/k} + \frac{z}{a/l} = 1 \implies \frac{hx}{a} + \frac{ky}{a} + \frac{lz}{a} = 1$$

**Step 2:** The perpendicular distance from the origin to this plane equals $d_{hkl}$. Using the standard formula for distance from origin to plane $Ax + By + Cz = 1$:

$$d = \frac{1}{\sqrt{A^{2} + B^{2} + C^{2}}}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $A = h/a$, $B = k/a$, $C = l/a$:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{hkl} &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{h}{a}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{k}{a}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{l}{a}\right)^{2}}} \\
d_{hkl} &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}{a^{2}}}} \\
d_{hkl} &= \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}}\,}$$

**Step 4:** Combining with Bragg's law ($n = 1$ for first order):

$$\sin\theta = \frac{\lambda}{2d_{hkl}} = \frac{\lambda\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}}{2a}$$

**Step 5:** Squaring both sides:

$$\boxed{\,\sin^{2}\theta = \frac{\lambda^{2}}{4a^{2}}(h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2})\,}$$

This is the **fundamental cubic XRD equation** used to determine the lattice parameter $a$ from measured peak positions.

---

## 3.3 Scherrer Equation — Crystallite Size from Peak Broadening

**Given:** A polycrystalline sample produces a diffraction peak at Bragg angle $\theta$ with full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) $\beta$ (in radians).

**To Prove:** $D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$, where $D$ is the average crystallite size and $K$ is the Scherrer constant (~0.9).

### Qualitative Derivation (KTU Exam Standard)

**Step 1:** In an ideal infinite crystal, the diffraction peak would be a **delta function** (infinitely sharp at the exact Bragg angle).

**Step 2:** In a real finite-sized crystallite of dimension $D$, diffraction occurs over a small angular range around $\theta$ because of the **breakdown of perfect phase coherence** across the finite crystal.

**Step 3:** The smaller the crystallite, the **broader** the peak. Empirically (Scherrer, 1918):

$$\beta \propto \frac{\lambda}{D \cos\theta}$$

**Step 4:** Introducing the shape factor $K$ (depends on crystallite shape; $K = 0.9$ for spherical, $K = 1.0$ for cubic):

$$\boxed{\,D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}\,}$$

**Step 5:** Practical corrections — instrumental broadening must be subtracted:

$$\beta_{\text{sample}} = \sqrt{\beta_{\text{observed}}^{2} - \beta_{\text{instrumental}}^{2}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Experiment | Monochromatic? | Sample State | Output | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Laue Method** | No (polychromatic) | Single crystal, stationary | Spots | Symmetry / orientation |
| **Rotating Crystal** | Yes | Single crystal, rotating | Layer lines | Unit cell parameters |
| **Powder (Debye–Scherrer)** | Yes | Polycrystalline powder | Rings on film | Phase ID, lattice parameter |
| **Powder Diffractometer** | Yes | Polycrystalline powder | Intensity vs. $2\theta$ plot | Quantitative analysis, size (Scherrer) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Coolidge X-Ray Tube

The following Mermaid diagram shows the functional architecture of a Coolidge X-ray tube used for laboratory XRD:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Voltage DC Source 30 to 50 kV] -->|Accelerating potential| B[Evacuated Glass Envelope 10 to 6 Torr]
    C[Tungsten Filament Cathode] -->|Thermionic emission of electrons| B
    B -->|Electron bombardment| D[Water Cooled Metal Anode Target Cu or Mo]
    D -->|Continuous Bremsstrahlung plus Characteristic K alpha K beta| E[Primary X Ray Beam]
    E -->|Passes through| F[Nickel Filter or Crystal Monochromator]
    F -->|Monochromatic K alpha 1.5406 angstrom for Cu| G[Collimator Slit System]
    G -->|Parallel monochromatic beam| H[Sample Stage for Crystal or Powder]
```

**Explanation of Block Function:**
- **High voltage source** drives thermionic electrons.
- **Filament cathode** emits electrons via thermionic emission.
- **Water-cooled anode** absorbs the electrons and emits X-rays.
- **Filter / monochromator** isolates the Kα line.
- **Collimator** produces a parallel beam.
- **Sample stage** holds the specimen under analysis.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: X-Ray Powder Diffractometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[X Ray Tube Source Cu K alpha 1.5406 angstrom] --> B[Divergence Slit]
    B --> C[Soller Slits plus Monochromator]
    C --> D[Flat Powder Sample Stage]
    D -->|Diffracted beam at angle 2 theta| E[Receiving Slit]
    E --> F[Scintillation or Solid State Detector]
    F --> G[Amplifier and Pulse Height Analyzer]
    G --> H[Computer Data Acquisition System]
    H --> I[Intensity versus 2 theta Diffractogram]
```

**Explanation:**
- The **detector arm** rotates at **twice the angular speed** of the sample (so the angle between incident and diffracted beams stays at $2\theta$).
- The result is a **diffractogram** — a plot of intensity vs. $2\theta$ — which is the standard output of modern XRD.

---

## 4.3 Process Flow: Powder XRD Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Receive Polycrystalline Sample] --> B[Step 2 Grind to Fine Powder Less than 10 micrometer]
    B --> C[Step 3 Mount on Sample Holder Flat Surface]
    C --> D[Step 4 Place in Diffractometer Set Incident Beam]
    D --> E[Step 5 Scan 2 theta Range Typically 10 to 90 degrees]
    E --> F[Step 6 Collect Intensity versus 2 theta Diffractogram]
    F --> G[Step 7 Identify Peak Positions 2 theta]
    G --> H[Step 8 Search ICDD PDF Database for Phase Matching]
    H --> I[Step 9 Determine Lattice Parameter from Peak Positions]
    I --> J[Step 10 Estimate Crystallite Size via Scherrer Equation]
```

**Flow Logic:**
- Peaks at specific $2\theta$ values → match against standard powder diffraction files (PDF) maintained by the **International Centre for Diffraction Data (ICDD)**.
- Peak width → Scherrer equation for size.
- Peak shift from standard position → residual strain / solid solution effects.

---

## 4.4 Schematic Functional Flow: Bragg Diffraction Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incident X Ray Beam Wavelength lambda] --> B[Crystal Atomic Planes Spacing d]
    B -->|If 2 d sin theta equals n lambda| C[Constructive Interference Diffraction Peak]
    B -->|If condition not satisfied| D[Destructive Interference No Peak]
    C --> E[Detector Records Signal at angle 2 theta]
    D --> F[Detector Records Background Only]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The condition (Bragg's Law) acts as a *logical filter* — only those orientations of planes relative to the incident beam that satisfy $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ produce observable diffraction peaks. All other orientations contribute only to the diffuse background.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Bragg's Law. State the conditions under which it is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**

> Bragg's Law states that for constructive interference of X-rays diffracted by parallel atomic planes in a crystal:
> $$n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$$
> where $n$ = order of diffraction (integer), $\lambda$ = wavelength of X-rays, $d$ = interplanar spacing, $\theta$ = glancing angle (Bragg angle).
>
> **Conditions of applicability:**
> 1. The incident X-ray beam must be **monochromatic**.
> 2. The crystal must be of **finite but sufficient size** for diffraction to occur.
> 3. The wavelength $\lambda$ must be **comparable to the interplanar spacing** $d$ (typically $\lambda \leq 2d$).
> 4. The incident beam must be **parallel and coherent**.
> 5. Scattering is assumed to be from **rigid, immobile atoms** (no thermal vibration correction in the basic form).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: What are Miller indices? How are they determined for a crystal plane?**

**Model Answer:**

> Miller indices $(h\ k\ l)$ are a notation system used to identify the orientation of atomic planes in a crystal lattice.
>
> **Procedure to determine Miller indices:**
> 1. Find the **intercepts** of the plane on the three crystallographic axes in terms of the unit cell dimensions $a$, $b$, $c$.
> 2. Take the **reciprocals** of these intercepts.
> 3. **Clear fractions** and reduce to the smallest set of integers.
> 4. Enclose the integers in parentheses: $(h\ k\ l)$.
>
> **Example:** A plane with intercepts $2a$, $3b$, $\infty c$ has reciprocals $1/2$, $1/3$, $0$ → multiplied by 6 → $(3\ 2\ 0)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must answer **one full question from each module**, with internal choice between two alternatives.

---

### PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Derive Bragg's Law for X-ray diffraction in crystals. Mention the significance of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** When X-rays strike the parallel atomic planes of a crystal at a glancing angle $\theta$, constructive interference of the reflected rays occurs when $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Consider two parallel atomic planes P and Q separated by distance $d$. Let an incident monochromatic X-ray beam AB strike the planes.

**Step 2:** Ray AB reflects from the upper plane P at point B (along BF). Ray CD travels through the upper plane and reflects from the lower plane Q at point D (along DH).

**Step 3:** From the geometry, the extra path traversed by ray CD compared to AB is:

$$\text{Path difference} = GD + DH$$

**Step 4:** Each of these segments, by trigonometric analysis of the triangles formed with the perpendicular between the planes, equals $d\sin\theta$:

$$GD = DH = d\sin\theta$$

**Step 5:** Therefore the **total path difference** is:

$$\Delta = 2d\sin\theta$$

**Step 6:** For **constructive interference** (bright reflection peak), this path difference must equal an integer multiple of the wavelength:

$$2d\sin\theta = n\lambda$$

$$\boxed{\,n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta\,}$$

**Significance of each term:**

| Symbol | Quantity | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Order of diffraction (integer) | Higher orders give weaker peaks; usually $n = 1$ |
| $\lambda$ | Wavelength of X-rays | Determines which planes can diffract; must be comparable to $d$ |
| $d$ | Interplanar spacing | Characteristic of the crystal; the parameter being measured |
| $\theta$ | Bragg (glancing) angle | The experimentally measured quantity; gives $d$ if $\lambda$ is known |

---

#### (b) Explain the powder method (Debye–Scherrer) of X-ray diffraction with a neat diagram. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** In a finely powdered (polycrystalline) sample, there exist crystallites of all possible orientations. Therefore, for any set of $(hkl)$ planes with spacing $d$, some crystallites will always satisfy Bragg's condition. The diffracted beams from these crystallites form **cones** of half-angle $2\theta$ around the incident beam direction.

**Experimental Setup:**
- A thin glass capillary tube (or fiber) is filled with the powdered sample.
- It is placed at the center of a cylindrical **Debye–Scherrer camera** with monochromatic X-ray beam.
- A strip of photographic film lines the inside of the cylindrical camera.

**Working:**
1. Monochromatic X-rays strike the powdered sample.
2. For each $(hkl)$ plane, Bragg's condition is satisfied by some crystallites.
3. The diffracted beams form cones of half-angle $2\theta$ around the incident beam.
4. These cones intersect the cylindrical film to form **Debye rings** (or arcs).

**Measurement:** The distance of each ring from the incident beam position gives $2\theta$, from which $d_{hkl}$ is calculated using Bragg's law.

**Advantages:**
- No need for a **single crystal**; bulk polycrystalline powder is sufficient.
- Simple, inexpensive experimental setup.
- All reflections are recorded **simultaneously**.
- Suitable for **phase identification** by comparing $d$-spacings with ICDD PDF database.

**Limitations:**
- **Peak overlap** may occur for cubic or lower-symmetry materials with many peaks.
- Film must be developed and measured manually (less convenient than modern diffractometers).
- Difficult for samples with very few crystallites or strong preferred orientation.

**Block Schematic (refer to Section 4.3 of these notes):**

```
X-ray source → Collimator → Powder sample → Diffracted cones → Cylindrical film (Debye rings)
```

---

### PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### (a) Describe the Laue method and the rotating crystal method for X-ray diffraction. Compare them. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Laue Method (Single Crystal, Stationary, Polychromatic):**

**Setup:** A beam of **polychromatic (white) X-rays** is collimated and directed onto a **stationary single crystal**. A flat photographic plate is placed behind the crystal to record the diffracted beams.

**Working:** Since the incident beam contains a continuous spectrum of wavelengths, Bragg's condition is satisfied for each $(hkl)$ plane at some wavelength present in the beam. The diffracted beams appear as a pattern of **spots** (Laue spots) on the photographic plate.

**Types:**
- **Transmission Laue:** Crystal is thin; film placed behind the crystal.
- **Back-reflection Laue:** Film placed between X-ray source and crystal.

**Applications:**
- Determining **crystal orientation**.
- Determining **crystal symmetry** (point group).
- Identifying **twin boundaries and grain orientations** in single crystals.

**2. Rotating Crystal Method (Single Crystal, Moving, Monochromatic):**

**Setup:** A **monochromatic** X-ray beam strikes a single crystal that is **rotated** about a fixed axis (usually a crystallographic axis). A cylindrical strip of photographic film surrounds the rotation axis.

**Working:** As the crystal rotates, different $(hkl)$ planes successively come into Bragg orientation. When the condition $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ is satisfied, a diffraction spot appears on the film. The spots lie on **horizontal layer lines** corresponding to different reciprocal lattice levels.

**Applications:**
- Determining **unit cell parameters** (lattice constants $a$, $b$, $c$).
- Determining **space group** symmetry.
- Solving **unknown crystal structures**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Laue Method | Rotating Crystal Method |
|---|---|---|
| X-ray type | Polychromatic | Monochromatic |
| Crystal state | Stationary | Rotating |
| Sample requirement | Single crystal | Single crystal |
| Output | Spots in pattern | Spots on layer lines |
| Best for | Symmetry / orientation | Unit cell / structure |
| Speed | Faster (single shot) | Slower (requires rotation) |

---

#### (b) Discuss the major industrial applications of X-ray diffraction. Explain how the Scherrer equation is used to determine crystallite size. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Industrial Applications of XRD:**

1. **Phase Identification (Qualitative Analysis):**
   - Every crystalline compound has a unique set of $d$-spacings (a "fingerprint").
   - Comparing measured $d$-values and intensities with the **ICDD PDF database** allows identification of unknown phases.
   - **Example:** Distinguishing between rutile and anatase forms of TiO₂ in paints.

2. **Quantitative Phase Analysis:**
   - Using integrated peak intensities and **Rietveld refinement**, the weight fraction of each phase in a multi-phase mixture can be determined.
   - **Example:** Quantifying clinker phases in cement (alite, belite, aluminate, ferrite).

3. **Lattice Parameter Determination:**
   - Precise measurement of peak positions allows calculation of $a$, $b$, $c$ using the cubic formula or analogous expressions for other systems.
   - Sensitive to solid solution composition (Vegard's law).

4. **Crystallite Size (Scherrer Method):**
   - See detailed explanation below.

5. **Residual Stress Measurement:**
   - Shift in peak position from the stress-free value indicates macroscopic residual stress in the material.
   - Critical for weld quality assessment and fatigue analysis.

6. **Texture / Preferred Orientation Analysis:**
   - Variation of peak intensity with sample orientation reveals preferred crystallographic orientations.
   - Important in rolled metals, drawn wires, and thin films.

7. **Pharmaceutical Polymorph Identification:**
   - Different crystal forms of the same drug molecule have different XRD patterns and different bioavailability.

---

**Scherrer Equation — Determination of Crystallite Size:**

The Scherrer equation relates the **broadening of an XRD peak** to the size of the crystallites that produced it:

$$D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$$

where:
- $D$ = average crystallite size (in Å or nm)
- $K$ = Scherrer constant (~0.9 for spherical, ~1.0 for cubic crystallites)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of X-rays (e.g., 1.5406 Å for Cu Kα)
- $\beta$ = **full width at half maximum (FWHM)** of the peak (in **radians**)
- $\theta$ = Bragg angle of the peak

**Procedure:**
1. Record the diffractogram of the powdered sample.
2. Identify a strong, isolated peak and measure its FWHM in degrees.
3. Convert $\beta$ to radians: $\beta(\text{rad}) = \beta(\text{deg}) \times \pi/180$.
4. Correct for instrumental broadening (subtract the contribution of the instrument).
5. Apply Scherrer's equation to compute $D$.

**Numerical Example:**
> Suppose Cu Kα radiation ($\lambda = 1.5406$ Å) is used. A peak at $2\theta = 45.0°$ has FWHM $= 0.30°$. Compute $D$ taking $K = 0.9$.

**Solution:**

$$\beta = 0.30 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 5.236 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{rad}$$

$$\theta = \frac{45.0^\circ}{2} = 22.5^\circ, \quad \cos\theta = \cos 22.5^\circ = 0.9239$$

$$D = \frac{0.9 \times 1.5406}{5.236 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.9239}$$

$$D = \frac{1.3865}{4.838 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 286.6\ \text{\AA} \approx 28.7\ \text{nm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students commonly lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $\beta$ from degrees to radians** before plugging into the Scherrer equation. This error alone can reduce your answer by ~50%.
> 2. Using $2\theta$ instead of $\theta$ (Bragg angle, not the detector angle) when computing $\cos\theta$.
> 3. Confusing **crystallite size** (single coherent domain) with **particle size** (physical grain size, which may be larger).
> 4. Omitting the **instrumental broadening correction** when reporting precise sizes.
> 5. Applying Scherrer's equation to peaks that are too weak or overlapped.
> 6. In powder method derivations, students often **incorrectly draw the geometry** — make sure the extra path is clearly marked as $GD + DH = 2d\sin\theta$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Bullet Points:**

- **Bragg's Law:** $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$. **Memorize this formula exactly.**
- **Cubic $d$-spacing:** $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}}$.
- **Combined cubic equation:** $\sin^{2}\theta = \dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{4a^{2}}(h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2})$.
- **Cu Kα wavelength:** $1.5406\ \text{\AA}$ (standard lab value).
- **Duane–Hunt limit:** $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{12400}{V(\text{in volts})}\ \text{\AA}$.
- **Selection rules:** BCC → $h+k+l$ even; FCC → $h,k,l$ all odd or all even.
- **Scherrer equation:** $D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$, with $\beta$ in **radians**.
- **Miller indices procedure:** Intercepts → reciprocals → reduce → enclose in parentheses.
- **Powder method output:** **Debye rings** on film.
- **Laue method output:** **Spots** (uses polychromatic X-rays).
- **Rotating crystal output:** Spots on **layer lines**.
- **Modern diffractometer output:** Intensity vs. $2\theta$ **diffractogram**.
- **Selection of X-ray target:** Cu (general), Mo (high precision), Co (Fe-rich samples), Cr (longer wavelength).
- **Filter for Cu Kα:** Nickel (Ni) filter — absorbs Kβ, transmits Kα.
- **Industrial uses:** Phase ID, lattice parameter, crystallite size, residual stress, texture.
- **Numerical conversion:** $\beta(\text{rad}) = \beta(\text{deg}) \times \pi / 180$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Bragg's Law:** "**NLD Two DS**" → $n \lambda = 2 d \sin\theta$.

---

*End of Module 4 Topic: Analysis of Solids by X-Ray Diffraction*
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 / OBE framework).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Analysis by Thermal Conductivity, Polarography, and Chromatography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gas Analysis by Thermal Conductivity, Polarography, and Chromatography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Gas Analysis by Thermal Conductivity

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Thermal Conductivity (TC) gas analysis is an analytical technique that determines the concentration of a specific gas in a binary or pseudo-binary mixture by measuring the rate at which heat is conducted away from a heated sensing element, exploiting the fact that each gas has a characteristic thermal conductivity coefficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This is a **non-reacting, physical-property-based** method. The gas sample does NOT need to be burnt, dissolved, or chemically transformed — making it ideal for **continuous, on-line industrial process monitoring**.

#### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine two friends standing in a cold room. Friend A wears a thick wool sweater (poor heat conductor), while Friend B wears a thin cotton shirt (good heat conductor). Both hold a hot cup of coffee. Friend B's coffee cools much faster because heat escapes through the shirt more easily. The *speed of cooling* tells you which "fabric" (i.e., which gas) is present.

In a thermal conductivity analyzer:
- A **heated hot-wire filament** acts like the hot coffee.
- The **surrounding gas** acts like the "fabric" — gases with **higher thermal conductivity** (like **H₂, He**) cool the filament rapidly, while gases with **lower thermal conductivity** (like **CO₂, SO₂, Ar**) cool it slowly.
- The filament temperature change alters its **electrical resistance**, which is measured via a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit.

| Gas | Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K) at 0 °C | Relative to Air |
|---|---|---|
| Hydrogen (H₂) | **0.174** | ~7.0× (highest) |
| Helium (He) | 0.143 | ~5.8× |
| Air | 0.024 | 1.0× (reference) |
| Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) | 0.015 | ~0.6× |
| Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂) | 0.0086 | ~0.35× |

---

### 1.2 Polarography

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Polarography is a specific branch of **voltammetry** in which the working electrode is a **Dropping Mercury Electrode (DME)**. The current flowing through an electrolysis cell is recorded as a function of the **applied potential**, producing a characteristic current–voltage curve called a **polarogram**, from which the **nature** and **concentration** of electro-reducible (or oxidizable) species in solution can be identified.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Invented by **Jaroslav Heyrovský** (Nobel Prize, Chemistry, 1959). The DME provides a continuously renewable, clean, reproducible surface — eliminating surface contamination problems faced by solid electrodes.

#### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **marble rolling down a ramp with a series of gates (barriers)**. Each gate represents a different reduction potential needed to "trap" a different metal ion. By gradually increasing the force (voltage) pushing the marble, you find exactly which gate the marble is strong enough to push open (i.e., which ion gets reduced). The current spikes when the marble passes through a gate — giving a "fingerprint" of the ion.

In polarography:
- A **gradually increasing DC voltage** is applied across a cell containing the analyte solution and a DME.
- When the applied potential equals the **decomposition potential** of a species, a sharp rise in current occurs (the "gate opens").
- The **half-wave potential (E₁/₂)** identifies the species; the **diffusion current (i_d)** is proportional to its concentration.

---

### 1.3 Chromatography

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Chromatography is a **physical method of separation** in which the components of a mixture are distributed between two phases — a **stationary phase** (immobile) and a **mobile phase** (moving) — and separated due to **differential affinities** (adsorption, partition, ion-exchange, or size-exclusion) of the components for these phases.

#### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **river flowing through a forest**. The riverbank trees (stationary phase) tend to "catch" certain items more than others. A leaf (low-affinity solute) zips downstream quickly. A heavy log (high-affinity solute) snags on roots and is delayed. By the time the river ends, the leaves and logs are spatially separated.

In **Gas Chromatography (GC)**:
- **Mobile phase** = carrier gas (N₂, He, H₂)
- **Stationary phase** = high-boiling liquid coated on inert solid support inside a column
- **Sample** = vaporized mixture injected at the inlet
- **Detector** = records peaks as components elute at different retention times

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** 
> - **GC** → separates **volatile / gaseous** samples.
> - **HPLC (High-Performance LC)** → separates **non-volatile liquids / dissolved solids**.
> - **Paper / Thin-Layer Chromatography (TLC)** → qualitative / preparative analytical separations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thermal Conductivity Gas Analyzer — Operational Logic

#### Step-by-Step Working Principle
1. **Reference gas** (e.g., pure N₂ or air with known TC) flows through the reference cell chamber past a heated platinum filament.
2. **Sample gas** flows through the measurement cell chamber past an identical filament.
3. Both filaments are connected as two arms of a **Wheatstone bridge**.
4. A constant current heats both filaments to ~50–100 °C above cell temperature.
5. If the sample gas has a **higher TC** than the reference → more heat is lost → filament cools → resistance **decreases** → bridge is **unbalanced**.
6. The bridge output voltage $E_o$ is proportional to the **difference in TC** between reference and sample → linearly related to the **concentration of the analyte gas**.

#### Wheatstone Bridge Output Equation

Let $R_1$ = reference cell filament, $R_2$ = sample cell filament, $R_3$, $R_4$ = fixed precision resistors.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_o &= E \cdot \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> When $R_1 = R_2$ (sample TC = reference TC), $E_o = 0$ (balanced). Any deviation unbalances the bridge proportionally.

#### Filament Resistance–Temperature Relation

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_T &= R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \left( T - T_0 \right) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **temperature coefficient of resistance** (for platinum, $\alpha \approx 0.00385$ /°C).

#### Heat Loss Conduction Equation (Steady State)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $A$ = filament surface area, $dT/dx$ = temperature gradient.

#### Engineering Utility
- **Hydrogen purity monitoring** in ammonia plants and hydrogen-cooled generators.
- **CO₂ measurement** in flue gas, greenhouses, and biogas plants.
- **SO₂ detection** in stack emissions of sulphuric acid plants.
- **Binary mixture analysis** when one component has a markedly different TC from the others.

---

### 2.2 Polarography — Operational Logic

#### Step-by-Step Working Principle
1. The cell contains the **analyte solution** + supporting electrolyte (e.g., KCl) + dissolved O₂ (often removed by purging N₂).
2. The **Dropping Mercury Electrode (DME)** is the **cathode** (working electrode). A **pool of mercury** at the bottom serves as the **anode**.
3. A slowly increasing DC potential (typically 0 to –2 V) is applied between DME and anode.
4. Each drop grows until it falls (drop time ~2–6 s), giving a fresh surface for every measurement — preventing passivation.
5. When applied voltage reaches the **decomposition potential** of a reducible ion, the limiting diffusion current $i_d$ flows.
6. A plot of $i$ vs. $E$ (the **polarogram**) shows a characteristic **S-shaped wave** for each reducible species.
7. The **half-wave potential $E_{1/2}$** identifies the species (qualitative analysis). The **diffusion current $i_d$** is proportional to concentration (quantitative analysis — **Ilkovič equation**).

#### Heyrovský–Ilkovič Equation (Diffusion Current)

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_d &= 607 \cdot n \cdot D^{1/2} \cdot m^{2/3} \cdot t^{1/6} \cdot C
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $i_d$ = diffusion (average) current in **µA**
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per ion
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient of the depolarizer in cm²/s
- $m$ = rate of mercury flow in mg/s
- $t$ = drop time in seconds
- $C$ = concentration in **millimoles per litre (mM)**

#### Half-Wave Potential Equation (for a reversible reduction)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= E_{1/2} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \left( \frac{i}{i_d - i} \right) \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> At $i = i_d/2$, $E = E_{1/2}$ — a characteristic constant for a given ion in a given supporting electrolyte.

#### Engineering Utility
- Trace metal analysis (Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Zn²⁺, Cu²⁺) in environmental and biological samples.
- Pharmaceutical and food-quality analysis.
- Studying complex-formation constants in coordination chemistry.
- **Amperometric detectors** in chromatography and biosensors.

---

### 2.3 Chromatography — Operational Logic

#### Step-by-Step GC Working Principle
1. **Sample injection** — microlitre quantity injected via micro-syringe through a **septum** into a heated **injection port**.
2. **Vaporization** — sample flash-evaporates and is swept by the **carrier gas** (mobile phase).
3. **Separation in column** — components partition between carrier gas and the liquid **stationary phase** coated on column packing (or on column walls in capillary columns). Lighter / less-soluble components elute first.
4. **Detection** — as each component exits, the **detector** (TCD, FID, ECD) generates an electrical signal.
5. **Recording** — the **chromatogram** plots detector signal vs. time. Each peak corresponds to one component; **peak area** is proportional to concentration.

#### Retention Parameters
- **Retention time ($t_R$)** — time from injection to peak maximum.
- **Dead time ($t_M$)** — time for an unretained species (e.g., air) to pass through.
- **Adjusted retention time** = $t_R' = t_R - t_M$
- **Retention factor (k)** = $\dfrac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \dfrac{t_R'}{t_M}$
- **Selectivity factor (α)** = $\dfrac{t_{R2}'}{t_{R1}'}$ (for two adjacent peaks, α > 1)
- **Resolution (R_s)** = $\dfrac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{W_1 + W_2}$
- **Number of theoretical plates (N)** = $16 \left( \dfrac{t_R}{W} \right)^2 = 5.54 \left( \dfrac{t_R}{W_{1/2}} \right)^2$
- **Plate height (HETP, H)** = $L / N$ (L = column length)
- **Van Deemter equation:** $H = A + \dfrac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$ (u = linear velocity of carrier gas)
  - A = eddy diffusion term
  - B/u = longitudinal diffusion term
  - C·u = mass transfer term

#### Engineering Utility
- **Petrochemical** — analysis of hydrocarbon fractions in crude oil, gasoline, lubricants.
- **Pharmaceutical** — purity and stability testing of drugs.
- **Environmental** — pesticide residues, VOC monitoring in air/water.
- **Forensic** — drug and accelerant analysis.
- **Food & beverage** — flavor profiling, contaminant detection.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Technique | Formula | Symbols / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TC Analyzer | $Q = k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | $k$: W/m·K, $A$: m², $dT/dx$: K/m | Heat loss from filament |
| 2 | TC Analyzer | $R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha(T - T_0) \right]$ | $R$: Ω, $\alpha$: /°C | Filament resistance change |
| 3 | TC Analyzer | $E_o = E \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$ | $E_o$: V | Wheatstone bridge output |
| 4 | Polarography | $i_d = 607 \cdot n \cdot D^{1/2} \cdot m^{2/3} \cdot t^{1/6} \cdot C$ | $i_d$: µA, $C$: mM | Diffusion current (Ilkovič) |
